Expansion: Benefits measurement establishes how intended outcomes will be quantified, tracked, and evaluated over time. In PMI practice, it supports benefits realization by linking expected value to metrics, baselines, and timing for review. It is used to determine whether project or program outputs are translating into the business outcomes promised in the business case.
Contrast: Benefits management — benefits measurement focuses on the metrics and assessment of benefits, while benefits management is the broader discipline of planning, tracking, and sustaining them.
Example: A customer-service transformation project measures average resolution time, satisfaction scores, and repeat-contact rate to verify whether the promised operational benefits are occurring.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The process of achieving and sustaining the measurable value that justified undertaking the project, program, or portfolio work.
Expansion: Benefits realisation connects delivery outputs to the outcomes and value the organisation expects to gain. In PMI usage, it is closely associated with programs and portfolios because benefits often emerge after individual project deliverables are produced and adopted. It requires identifying expected benefits, assigning ownership, measuring results over time, and adjusting work or operations to maximise value.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Benefits realisation is the disciplined management of how planned benefits are defined, tracked, transitioned, and confirmed. It focuses not just on producing deliverables, but on ensuring those deliverables create intended organisational outcomes.

Why it matters and where it applies: many projects finish on scope, schedule, and budget yet still fail to create meaningful value. Benefits realisation is especially important in strategic change, digital transformation, product, program, and portfolio environments where value depends on adoption, operational change, and time.

The most common misconception: that benefits are automatically realised once a project is completed. In practice, benefits often require business readiness, behavioural change, process integration, and post-project measurement before they can be confirmed.

How it shows up in real projects: teams define benefit metrics in business cases and benefit management plans, assign benefit owners, track leading and lagging indicators, and continue monitoring after delivery to verify that the expected value actually materialises.
Contrast: Outputs — outputs are the deliverables produced by the work; benefits realisation concerns the measurable outcomes and value created from using those deliverables.
Example: A CRM implementation project delivers the new platform on time, but benefits realisation is tracked through later improvements in sales-conversion rate, customer retention, and service response time.
Source: The Standard for Program Management, Fourth Edition, benefits management and benefits realization discussion; exact page citation not confirmed in available PMI source

Core Meaning: The process of ensuring that planned benefits are identified, executed, sustained, and measured from an initiative.
Expansion: Benefits realization is the disciplined management of intended value from a project, program, or portfolio investment. PMI treats it as more than tracking outputs, because value often emerges only when outcomes are adopted and sustained in operations. It links strategy to execution by defining expected benefits, assigning ownership, measuring achievement, and adjusting actions when benefits are at risk.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Benefits realization is the structured work of turning delivered capabilities into actual value. It includes defining benefits early, planning how they will be achieved, monitoring them over time, and confirming whether they materialize.

Why it matters and where it applies: organizations invest in change to create value, not merely to finish deliverables. Benefits realization is especially important in programs, portfolios, and strategy-driven projects where long-term outcomes depend on adoption, transition, and sustained use.

The most common misconception: that benefits are fully achieved when the project closes. In reality, many benefits emerge after handoff to operations and require ownership, change management, and measurement beyond the delivery phase.

How it shows up in real projects: a digital-transformation program tracks not only system deployment, but also customer adoption, process-cycle reduction, and revenue uplift for months after implementation to confirm the intended benefits are being achieved.
Contrast: Benefit — a benefit is a specific positive effect or gain; benefits realization is the ongoing process of making sure those gains actually occur and are sustained.
Example: A claims-automation initiative delivers new software, then monitors processing speed and rework rates over two quarters to verify the promised operational improvement has been realized.
Source: The Standard for Program Management, Glossary, benefits realization

Core Meaning: A proposal or offer submitted by a seller in response to a solicitation, usually for a price and specified terms.
Expansion: A bid is a seller's formal response to procurement solicitation, often used when requirements are clear and price competition is central. PMI distinguishes bids from proposals and quotations by the procurement context and the level of detail requested. Bids are evaluated during source selection to determine which seller best meets the buyer's needs.
Contrast: Proposal — a bid is typically a price-focused offer in response to a solicitation; a proposal may include broader technical and management information.
Example: A contractor submits a bid to build a warehouse after the buyer issues an invitation for bid.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Bid

Core Meaning: Procurement documents used to solicit responses from prospective sellers for project work, goods, or services.
Expansion: Bid documents are prepared by the buyer to communicate procurement requirements and invite sellers to submit offers or proposals. Depending on the procurement context, they may include specifications, statements of work, terms and conditions, evaluation criteria, and forms for seller response. In project management, they support a fair, structured sourcing process and provide a basis for comparing vendor submissions and selecting a seller.
Contrast: Procurement statement of work — bid documents are the full solicitation package sent to sellers, while the procurement statement of work is one component that describes the required deliverable or work.
Example: A construction project issues bid documents for electrical installation work, including technical drawings, contract terms, site conditions, and submission instructions for bidders.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition / PMI Lexicon — confirm exact citation in Infinity

Core Meaning: A meeting with prospective sellers before bid or proposal preparation to ensure a common understanding of the procurement requirement.
Expansion: A bidder conference is a procurement event held to explain requirements, answer questions, and reduce misunderstanding before sellers submit responses. It helps the buyer communicate expectations consistently and gives all invited sellers the same information at the same time. This supports fair competition and can improve the quality and comparability of submitted bids or proposals.
Contrast: Procurement negotiation — a bidder conference clarifies requirements before proposals are submitted; negotiation occurs later to reach agreement with a selected seller.
Example: A public sector project team holds a bidder conference for software vendors so each supplier hears the same explanation of integration, security, and service-level requirements before preparing proposals.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Glossary, bidder conference

Core Meaning: A condition in which an activity, task, or work item cannot proceed because a dependency, constraint, or impediment is preventing progress.
Expansion: Blocked is commonly used in project and agile contexts to indicate that work is waiting on something external or unresolved. It signals that the team needs help removing the obstacle before progress can continue. In practice, blocked items are often tracked so that impediments are visible and can be escalated quickly.
Contrast: Impediment — blocked describes the current state of work that cannot proceed; an impediment is the underlying obstacle causing that state.
Example: A developer's task is blocked because the security team has not yet approved access to the test environment.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A limiting point in a workflow, resource system, or process that constrains overall throughput.
Expansion: A bottleneck is the step, role, or constraint that restricts how fast work can move through the system as a whole. In project and delivery settings, it is used to diagnose delays, queue buildup, handoff friction, and uneven flow rather than simply to label any problem area. Managing a bottleneck improves overall system performance more effectively than optimising unconstrained parts of the work.
Contrast: Blocker — a bottleneck constrains flow for multiple items or the system overall, while a blocker stops a specific task or item from progressing until the obstacle is removed.
Example: In a product release project, security review becomes the bottleneck because every feature waits in queue for the same small specialist team before deployment can proceed.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A method of estimating duration or cost by aggregating estimates from the lowest-level components of the work breakdown structure.
Expansion: Bottom-up estimating starts with detailed estimates for individual work packages or activities and rolls them up to produce a higher-level total. PMI presents it as a more detailed estimating approach than top-down methods because it is built from the work itself rather than from broad analogies. It is useful when the scope is sufficiently defined and the team needs a more defensible estimate for planning and control.
Contrast: Analogous estimating — bottom-up estimating builds from detailed components; analogous estimating derives a figure from similar past work at a higher level.
Example: A software team estimates each feature, integration task, and test activity separately, then sums the results to produce the release estimate.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, bottom-up estimating

Core Meaning: A variation of brainstorming in which participants generate ideas individually and share them electronically.
Expansion: Brain-netting is used when a group wants the benefits of idea generation without requiring everyone to be in the same room at the same time. It can reduce dominance effects and allow quieter participants to contribute more freely.
Contrast: Brainstorming — brain-netting is typically asynchronous and electronic; brainstorming is usually a live group session led by a facilitator.
Example: A distributed project team uses a shared online board to collect risks and improvement ideas before a planning workshop.
Source: PMI Guide to Business Analysis, brain-netting

Core Meaning: An elicitation technique used in a group setting to quickly generate ideas on a specific topic.
Expansion: Brainstorming is a facilitated technique for producing a broad set of ideas in a short period of time. PMI discusses it especially in business analysis and stakeholder engagement contexts where teams need to surface options, requirements, risks, or causes quickly. Its value comes from divergence first and evaluation later, so participants can contribute without immediate filtering or debate.
Contrast: Workshop — brainstorming focuses on rapid idea generation; a workshop is a broader structured session that may include analysis, decisions, and planning.
Example: During requirements elicitation for a new customer portal, the facilitator leads a brainstorming session with service agents and users to surface needed features and pain points.
Source: The PMI Guide to Business Analysis, Glossary, brainstorming

Core Meaning: A structured idea-generation technique in which participants write ideas individually before sharing them with the group.
Expansion: Brainwriting helps teams generate a larger and more balanced set of ideas by giving each participant time to think before discussion begins. It is often used when a facilitator wants to avoid early anchoring or groupthink.
Contrast: Brainstorming — brainwriting emphasizes written individual contribution first; brainstorming emphasizes open verbal idea generation in the group.
Example: Before selecting a vendor, the team writes possible evaluation criteria on cards, then combines and discusses them as a group.
Source: PMI Guide to Business Analysis, brainwriting

Core Meaning: The approved estimate for the project, program, portfolio, or a component of that work.
Expansion: A budget is the authorised amount of money allocated to perform defined work. PMI uses the term as the approved estimate, which means it is more than an early calculation and becomes part of the basis for monitoring and control. Budgets may exist at multiple levels, from the total project to control accounts, work packages, or activities, depending on how the work is managed.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A budget is the approved financial expression of planned work. It converts estimates into an authorised spending framework against which actual cost performance can be tracked.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects need a clear spending boundary to support funding decisions, control, and reporting. Budgeting is central in cost management and is used across predictive, hybrid, and governed adaptive environments whenever financial accountability matters.

The most common misconception: that a budget is just an estimate. An estimate predicts likely cost, while a budget is the approved amount used for authorisation and control.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager rolls up labour, vendor, equipment, and contingency amounts into an approved project budget, then compares monthly actuals against that approved figure to identify variances and escalation needs.
Contrast: Estimate — an estimate forecasts probable cost; a budget is the approved amount authorised for the work.
Example: A data-center migration project receives an approved budget covering hardware, consulting support, testing, and cutover activities, and the team tracks actual spending against it each month.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, budget

Core Meaning: The sum of all approved budgets established for the work to be performed.
Expansion: Budget at Completion, or BAC, is the total planned value of the project or control account when all authorised work is included. It is a key earned value management reference point because it represents the full approved budget for the scope. Teams use it with cost and schedule performance data to forecast likely final cost and assess performance trends.
Contrast: Estimate at Completion — Budget at Completion is the approved total planned budget; Estimate at Completion is the current forecast of what the total cost will actually be.
Example: On an infrastructure upgrade, the team sets the BAC after finalising the cost baseline, then compares it with the running Estimate at Completion as performance data accumulates.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, budget at completion

Core Meaning: An estimate developed to establish or refine an approved budget for a project or component of work.
Expansion: A budgetary estimate is prepared early enough to support funding and cost planning decisions, but with less precision than later detailed estimates. In PMI usage, it helps set expectations for the likely cost range of the work and supports approval, prioritisation, or portfolio decisions. Its accuracy depends on the amount of scope definition, assumptions, and historical information available at the time.
Contrast: Definitive estimate — a budgetary estimate supports budgeting with a broader expected range, while a definitive estimate is developed later from more complete scope detail for tighter control.
Example: Before approving a warehouse-automation project, the sponsor uses a budgetary estimate based on conceptual design, benchmark data, and major cost assumptions to set an initial funding target.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, section 7.2 Estimate Costs, page 245

Core Meaning: A planned reserve of time, capacity, or other allowance inserted to absorb variability and protect delivery commitments.
Expansion: A buffer is used in project planning to reduce the impact of uncertainty on milestones, handoffs, or constrained resources. In PMI-aligned practice, it is a management device for protecting flow and commitments rather than hidden slack added arbitrarily to every task. Buffers are especially visible in scheduling approaches such as critical chain, but the concept also appears more broadly in contingency planning and operational coordination.
Contrast: Contingency reserve — a buffer is a planned protective allowance embedded in timing or flow, while contingency reserve is budget or schedule capacity set aside for identified risks.
Example: A project team places a two-day integration buffer before user acceptance testing so minor upstream delays do not automatically move the testing start date.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An organizational structure in which people are grouped by specialty into distinct functions, coordinated through formal rules and a clear hierarchy.
Expansion: Bureaucracy is one of Mintzberg's organizational structure forms and is characterized by standardized work, specialized roles, and strong procedural control. In project settings, it often appears in environments where authority, decision rights, and reporting lines are defined primarily by function rather than by temporary project needs. It can create clarity and consistency, but it may also slow cross-functional coordination and reduce flexibility when rapid adaptation is needed.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Bureaucracy is a formal organizational form built around specialization, hierarchy, and standardization. Work is divided into functions, governed by established procedures, and coordinated through managerial authority.

Why it matters and where it applies: project work is shaped by the organization that hosts it, so bureaucracy affects decision speed, escalation paths, and resource access. It commonly appears in large enterprises, government bodies, and regulated environments where consistency, control, and compliance are important.

The most common misconception: that bureaucracy simply means inefficiency or unnecessary paperwork. In fact, it is a legitimate structural design that can provide stability, accountability, and repeatability, though those strengths can become constraints when work demands agility or rapid cross-functional collaboration.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager in a functionally structured organization must secure approvals through department heads, follow formal change procedures, and coordinate work across specialized teams that report primarily to line managers rather than to the project.
Contrast: Projectized organization — bureaucracy groups people by function and governs work through formal hierarchy; a projectized organization centers authority and resources around projects.
Example: In a regulated pharmaceutical company, a packaging change project moves through formal quality, regulatory, engineering, and procurement departments, each with defined approval gates and specialist responsibilities.
Source: The Standard for Project Management and A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), section on organizational structure based on Mintzberg, bureaucracy

Core Meaning: A visual chart that shows work completed or work remaining over time so a team can track progress against an iteration, release, or project plan.
Expansion: A burn chart is a lightweight visual control used in adaptive delivery to show whether work is being completed at the expected pace. It commonly appears as a burndown chart, which tracks remaining work, or a burnup chart, which tracks completed work against total scope. Teams use it to inspect progress quickly, spot delivery risk early, and support forecasting conversations.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A burn chart plots work against time to make delivery progress visible. Depending on the chart type, it shows either how much work remains or how much has been completed.

Why it matters and where it applies: teams need a simple way to see whether they are on track within a sprint, release, or other short planning horizon. Burn charts support transparency, daily coordination, and evidence-based conversations about pace, scope, and predictability in agile and hybrid environments.

The most common misconception: that a burn chart is a performance score for individuals or a guarantee of delivery. In practice it is a team-level information radiator, and its usefulness depends on stable definitions of work, timely updates, and thoughtful interpretation.

How it shows up in real projects: a Scrum team reviews a sprint burndown during the daily stand-up, notices that remaining effort is not falling as expected, and then re-plans tasks, removes blockers, or renegotiates scope.
Contrast: Velocity — a burn chart shows progress over time within a timebox or release, while velocity measures the amount of work a team typically completes per iteration.
Example: A software team tracks remaining story points each day of a two-week sprint on a burndown chart to see whether the sprint goal is still achievable.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, burnup and burndown chart entries in glossary section

Core Meaning: A graphical representation of the work remaining versus the time left in an iteration, release, or project.
Expansion: A burndown chart visualizes how much work remains over time so a team can quickly see whether progress is tracking toward completion. It is commonly used in adaptive delivery settings such as iterations or releases to support transparency and short-cycle control. The chart is useful because it highlights pace, trend, and potential slippage without requiring a complex status narrative.
Contrast: Burnup chart — a burndown chart shows remaining work decreasing over time; a burnup chart shows completed work increasing and can also make scope growth more visible.
Example: A Scrum team tracks remaining story points each day of a two-week sprint on a burndown chart to see whether the sprint goal is still achievable.

Source: Agile Practice Guide, Glossary, burndown chart

Core Meaning: A graphical representation of work completed over time compared with the total scope.
Expansion: A burnup chart shows cumulative completed work rising over time against a line representing total scope. PMI highlights it as a simple visual tool for tracking progress in adaptive environments because it makes both delivery and scope change easier to see. This helps teams and stakeholders distinguish slow progress from expanding scope, which can be harder to interpret on a burndown chart alone.
Contrast: Burndown chart — a burnup chart emphasizes completed work and visibility of scope growth; a burndown chart emphasizes remaining work declining over time.
Example: A product team uses a burnup chart during a release to show completed features increasing while also revealing that added scope is pushing the target line upward.

Figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='40' y1='20' x2='40' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='160' x2='300' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><polyline points='44,60 170,60 170,42 296,42' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.6' stroke-dasharray='5 4'/><polyline points='44,158 92,140 140,110 188,96 236,70 296,46' stroke='#2F6F5E' stroke-width='2'/><text x='250' y='36' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10'>scope</text><text x='150' y='118' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='10'>completed</text><text x='170' y='182' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>sprint days</text></svg>
Figure.caption: Completed work rising toward total scope (which steps up when scope is added).

Source: Agile Practice Guide, Glossary, burnup chart

Core Meaning: The application of knowledge, skills, tools, and techniques to determine problems and opportunities and to identify and recommend viable solutions for meeting business needs.
Expansion: Business analysis connects organizational needs with project or product solutions by identifying problems, opportunities, requirements, and value expectations. PMI treats it as a disciplined practice that supports defining needs, recommending solutions, and enabling benefits realization across the life cycle. It is relevant in predictive, agile, and hybrid environments because decisions about requirements and value are needed regardless of delivery approach.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Business analysis is the work of understanding business needs, defining and managing requirements, assessing options, and recommending solutions that create value. It spans elicitation, analysis, traceability, validation of needs, and support for solution evaluation.

Why it matters and where it applies: many project failures come not from weak execution but from solving the wrong problem or defining the wrong requirements. Business analysis improves decision quality and stakeholder alignment in product development, transformation initiatives, process redesign, technology implementations, and benefits-focused programs.

The most common misconception: that business analysis is only documentation or only the responsibility of someone with the title business analyst. In practice, it is a set of responsibilities that may be shared among analysts, product owners, project managers, sponsors, and subject matter experts.

How it shows up in real projects: stakeholders are interviewed, current-state pain points are analysed, requirements are prioritised, acceptance criteria are defined, and solution options are compared against strategic goals, constraints, and expected benefits.
Contrast: Requirements — Business analysis is the broader practice of identifying needs and recommending solutions; requirements are the documented capabilities and conditions the solution must satisfy.
Example: Before launching an ERP implementation, the team analyses order-to-cash pain points, defines business requirements, evaluates solution options, and recommends the configuration that best supports the target operating model.
Source: The PMI Guide to Business Analysis, business analysis definition, p. 4

Core Meaning: A specific ability an organisation needs in order to achieve a business outcome or execute its strategy.
Expansion: Business capability describes what the organisation must be able to do, independent of any single process, system, or team structure. In PMI-related strategy and value discussions, it helps connect projects and programs to enduring organisational outcomes rather than just temporary deliverables. It is especially useful in transformation work because it frames change in terms of operating ability, such as fulfilling orders, managing risk, or personalising customer service.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Business capability is an expression of organisational ability, stated in terms of what the business can do rather than how a current process happens to do it. It is usually more stable than org charts, applications, or project phases, which makes it useful for strategic planning and change design.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects often produce outputs such as systems, policies, or training, but leaders need to understand what those outputs enable. Framing work around business capabilities helps prioritise investments, align cross-functional efforts, and evaluate whether change is building the organisation's real operating strength.

The most common misconception: that a business capability is just another name for a department, process, or software feature. It is distinct because it defines an enduring ability that may be supported by several processes, technologies, roles, and governance elements working together.

How it shows up in real projects: in a digital transformation, teams may map target capabilities such as customer onboarding, fraud detection, or omnichannel fulfilment, then use those capabilities to shape roadmaps, sequence projects, and measure value beyond individual deliverables.
Contrast: Business process — a business capability is the enduring ability the organisation needs; a business process is one specific flow of activities used to realise that ability.
Example: A bank funds several initiatives under the target business capability of digital customer onboarding, including identity verification, workflow redesign, policy updates, and staff training.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A documented justification for initiating a project, program, or other investment based on expected value, costs, risks, and alignment to strategy.
Expansion: A business case explains why an initiative should be undertaken by comparing the expected benefits with the required investment, major risks, and available alternatives. It supports decision making before significant resources are committed and often informs prioritisation in portfolios and governance reviews. While it may be updated over time, its central purpose is to demonstrate that the proposed work is worth doing and strategically aligned.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A business case is the rationale for investment in proposed work. It brings together the problem or opportunity, options, expected benefits, assumptions, costs, and risks into a decision-oriented argument.

Why it matters and where it applies: organizations use business cases to choose where to invest limited funds and attention. It is especially relevant at initiation, stage-gate review, portfolio selection, and whenever leaders need to test whether expected value still justifies continued effort.

The most common misconception: that the business case is just a financial spreadsheet. Financials matter, but a sound business case also addresses strategic fit, qualitative benefits, assumptions, dependencies, and the consequences of doing nothing.

How it shows up in real projects: before approving a CRM replacement project, leaders review a business case that compares vendor options, implementation cost, adoption risk, and expected gains in sales conversion and customer retention.
Contrast: Project charter — a business case justifies why the initiative should be approved; a project charter formally authorizes the project and defines initial authority and direction.
Example: A retailer proposes a warehouse modernisation project and uses a business case to show that faster fulfilment and lower picking errors justify the investment and transition risk.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), business case

Core Meaning: The internal and external conditions that influence an organization and shape the context in which projects operate.
Expansion: Business environment describes the wider organizational and market context surrounding project work, including internal structures and external forces such as regulation, competition, technology, and society. In PMI usage, it matters because projects do not operate in isolation; they are initiated, constrained, and evaluated within this broader context. The term is especially important when connecting projects to strategy, governance, value delivery, and organizational change.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Business environment is the total context around an organization and its work, including both internal conditions and external forces. It frames why projects are launched, how they are governed, and what outcomes are considered valuable.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters because shifts in policy, market conditions, leadership priorities, culture, or technology can change project priorities, risks, funding, and success criteria. It applies across portfolio, program, and project management, especially where strategic alignment and benefits realization are central.

The most common misconception: A common mistake is to treat business environment as only the internal company setting or only external market forces. In PMI thinking, it includes both, because projects are affected by and may also affect the broader environment around the organization.

How it shows up in real projects: A project may be accelerated because of competitive pressure, reshaped because of new regulation, or paused because of economic conditions. Teams experience business environment through governance decisions, stakeholder expectations, compliance demands, and shifting business priorities.
Contrast: Business function — business environment is the broader internal and external context; a business function is a specific organizational area that performs ongoing work.
Example: A hospital changes the scope of a patient-data project after new privacy rules and funding pressures alter the surrounding business environment.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Business environment, page citation not confirmed in current context

Core Meaning: A business function is a defined area of organizational activity that performs a particular set of ongoing responsibilities.
Expansion: In project management, business function refers to an organizational domain such as finance, operations, marketing, human resources, or procurement. PMI uses the term to distinguish ongoing functional responsibilities from temporary project work while also showing where project impacts, dependencies, and stakeholders sit. It is useful when planning cross-functional coordination, governance, ownership, and change adoption.
Contrast: Operations — a business function is the organizational area that owns work; operations are the continuing activities that area performs.
Example: In an ERP implementation, the procurement business function defines purchasing rules while the project team configures the new system to support them.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Business function, page citation not confirmed in current context

Core Meaning: A strategic visual framework used to describe how an organisation creates, delivers, and captures value across key business model elements.
Expansion: The business model canvas helps teams and sponsors structure discussion around customers, value propositions, channels, revenue, resources, activities, partnerships, and costs. In project and product settings it is often used to clarify the operating context for an initiative or test whether a proposed solution supports the intended value logic. It is useful in early concept development, innovation work, and business case discussions, but it is not a formal PMI glossary staple.
Contrast: Business case — the business model canvas maps how value is created and sustained at a system level, while the business case justifies a specific initiative or investment decision.
Example: Before launching a digital subscription service, a team uses a business model canvas to align on target customers, value proposition, partner ecosystem, and cost structure before finalising the project business case.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A business need is the problem to solve or opportunity to pursue that justifies initiating work.
Expansion: Business need is the underlying reason an organization undertakes a project, program, or other change effort. PMI treats it as the value-based rationale for action, linking the current state to a desired future state before any specific solution is chosen. It is foundational because it anchors business cases, charters, prioritization, benefits planning, and requirements development.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Business need is the gap, pain point, opportunity, or strategic driver that motivates investment in change. It explains why the work should exist before defining how the solution will be delivered.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters because clear business need supports better selection, sponsorship, prioritization, and success measurement. It appears early in business cases and charters, then continues to shape scope decisions, requirements, benefits, and stakeholder support.

The most common misconception: The most common confusion is treating business need as if it were already a requirement or chosen solution. A business need states the reason for action, while requirements and designs describe what must be built and how.

How it shows up in real projects: A project may start because customer onboarding takes too long, compliance risk is rising, or a new market opportunity appears. Teams refer back to the business need when resolving scope tradeoffs, evaluating change requests, and confirming whether the work still delivers value.
Contrast: Requirement — a business need explains why the work is undertaken; a requirement defines what the solution must provide.
Example: A retail bank launches a digital-identity project because it has a business need to shorten account opening time and reduce fraud exposure.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Business need, page citation not confirmed in current context

Core Meaning: A coordinated set of activities performed to produce a defined product, service, or result that delivers value to an organisation or its stakeholders.
Expansion: A business process is the repeatable flow of work through which an organisation operates and creates value. In PMI-aligned practice, projects often change, improve, automate, or enable business processes rather than existing as ends in themselves. Understanding the target process is essential for defining requirements, benefits, stakeholders, and success measures.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A business process is an organised sequence of tasks, decisions, inputs, and outputs that transforms work into a meaningful operational result. It can be end-to-end, cross-functional, or contained within one business unit.

Why it matters and where it applies: many projects are launched to create a new process or improve an existing one, such as onboarding, claims handling, procurement, or incident response. Clear process understanding helps teams identify requirements, handoffs, bottlenecks, controls, and the operational changes needed for adoption.

The most common misconception: that a business process is just a procedure or a documented workflow. In practice, it includes not only documented steps but also roles, business rules, systems, timing, metrics, and the value outcome the organisation is trying to achieve.

How it shows up in real projects: teams map the current state, define the future state, assess process capability or waste, align system requirements, and measure whether the new way of working improves cycle time, quality, compliance, or customer experience.
Contrast: Project management process — a business process runs the organisation's operational work, while a project management process is used to manage project work.
Example: A hospital project redesigns the patient-discharge business process to reduce delays by clarifying approvals, automating notifications, and standardising handoff steps.
Source: PMI Lexicon / Business Analysis for Practitioners — confirm exact citation in PMI source

Core Meaning: The net quantifiable benefit derived from a business endeavor, whether tangible, intangible, or both.
Expansion: Business value expresses the worthwhile benefit an organisation expects or realizes from an initiative. PMI frames it as net benefit, which means it goes beyond output delivery and considers the meaningful gain created for the enterprise or stakeholders. It can include financial return, strategic positioning, customer impact, capability improvement, compliance, or risk reduction, provided the benefit is tied to the endeavor.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Business value is the beneficial result an organization gains from work once costs and effort are considered. It links initiatives to meaningful outcomes rather than treating completion alone as success.

Why it matters and where it applies: project, program, and portfolio decisions are stronger when they are guided by expected value instead of activity volume. The concept is especially important in selection, prioritisation, benefits realization, and performance evaluation.

The most common misconception: that business value always means short-term financial profit. In practice it may also include strategic fit, customer retention, resilience, compliance, or capability gains that matter even when direct revenue is not immediate.

How it shows up in real projects: an organization approves a cybersecurity initiative not because it creates a sellable product, but because it reduces exposure, supports compliance, and protects customer trust, all of which contribute to business value.
Contrast: Benefit — a benefit is a specific positive effect or gain; business value is the net beneficial worth derived from the endeavor as a whole.
Example: A manufacturer funds predictive-maintenance software because the business value includes less downtime, lower repair cost, and more reliable customer delivery performance.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Glossary, business value

Core Meaning: The acquirer of products, services, or results for an organization.
Expansion: In procurement, the buyer is the party seeking to obtain goods or services from a seller. The buyer defines requirements, manages the solicitation and selection process, and administers the contract from the acquiring side.
Contrast: Seller — the buyer acquires and pays for the work; the seller provides the contracted product or service.
Example: A city transit project acts as the buyer when it issues a request for proposal and awards a contract for station security equipment.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Glossary, buyer

Core Meaning: Cadence is the regular rhythm or frequency at which project activities, events, or deliveries occur.
Expansion: Cadence gives work a predictable timing pattern, such as a two-week review cycle, a monthly steering meeting, or a fixed release rhythm. In PMI and agile usage, it supports coordination, inspection, planning, and stakeholder alignment by making key activities recur consistently. It is especially relevant in adaptive and iterative delivery, though it can also apply in predictive governance and reporting structures.
Contrast: Iteration — cadence is the repeating rhythm of work or events; an iteration is one time-boxed cycle that occurs within that rhythm.
Example: A delivery team uses a two-week cadence for planning, review, and retrospective meetings so stakeholders know exactly when to give feedback.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Cadence, page citation not confirmed in current context

Core Meaning: A representation of working and nonworking time that is used to plan and control when project activities and resources are available.
Expansion: In PMI practice, a calendar defines the time periods during which work can occur, such as business days, shifts, holidays, and exceptions. It is used in scheduling to calculate activity dates and resource availability rather than merely to display dates. Different calendars may exist for the project, for resources, or for specific activities when working patterns differ.
Contrast: Schedule — a calendar defines available working time; a schedule places activities and milestones onto that available time.
Example: A construction project uses one calendar for weekday office design work and a different calendar for six-day onsite installation crews.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / earlier PMI scheduling references; exact page citation requires source verification in PMI content

Core Meaning: The smallest unit of time used in scheduling or measuring schedule duration.
Expansion: Calendar units provide the basic time increments used to express activity durations, lags, and reporting periods, such as hours, days, or weeks. Using a consistent unit helps the schedule model remain coherent across planning and control.
Contrast: Work period — a calendar unit is the measurement increment for time, while a work period is a span in the resource or project calendar when work can actually occur.
Example: A project scheduler estimates a design review task as 3 working days, using days as the calendar unit throughout the schedule.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 698

Core Meaning: An ability that may be applied to achieve an intended result.
Expansion: Capability describes what an organisation, team, or system is able to do. In projects, it often refers to an enduring business ability created or improved through change.
Contrast: Competency — capability is an ability to achieve an outcome, while competency is a demonstrated level of proficiency in performing work.
Example: A claims-modernisation project gives an insurer the capability to process claims digitally rather than through paper forms.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Seventh Edition, section 2.4.3.1, page 33

Core Meaning: An evaluation of how well an organisation, function, team, or process can perform a required set of activities.
Expansion: Capability assessment examines current ability against defined criteria, maturity expectations, or target performance needs. In project, program, and organisational change contexts, it helps determine whether the enterprise can deliver, adopt, sustain, or improve the intended outcomes. It is often used to identify gaps in skills, governance, process discipline, technology, or operating readiness.
Contrast: Maturity assessment — capability assessment focuses on whether a specific ability exists and performs adequately, while maturity assessment typically evaluates the broader sophistication and consistency of a system or practice over levels.
Example: Before launching an enterprise AI program, leaders perform a capability assessment of data governance, technical skills, model oversight, and change readiness to identify gaps that could block delivery.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Capability development is the work of building or improving an organization's ability to perform effectively in a given area.
Expansion: Capability development focuses on strengthening what an organization can do, not just on producing a one-time deliverable. In PMI-aligned practice, it commonly appears in transformation, program, and strategic change efforts where the desired result is a sustained ability such as better analytics, stronger delivery discipline, or improved customer service. It often combines process change, tools, governance, training, and adoption activities to create lasting organizational performance.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Capability development is the intentional creation or improvement of an organizational ability. The target may be a business process, a technical discipline, a governance model, a service model, or a workforce skill base.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters because many investments aim not only to deliver outputs but to leave the organization more capable afterward. It is common in programs, PMO maturity efforts, digital transformation, and enterprise change where benefits depend on sustained organizational uptake.

The most common misconception: A common mistake is to treat capability development as only training people or installing a tool. In practice it usually requires coordinated change across process, roles, governance, technology, measures, and behavior.

How it shows up in real projects: An organization may launch multiple coordinated initiatives to build cybersecurity capability, data-governance capability, or agile delivery capability. Success is measured not only by completed deliverables but by whether the organization can reliably perform at a higher level afterward.
Contrast: Capability — capability development is the effort to build or improve an ability; capability is the resulting ability itself.
Example: A PMO runs a multi-team initiative to build agile delivery capability through coaching, workflow redesign, role clarification, and common metrics.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The amount of work that a resource, team, or system can realistically take on within a given time period.
Expansion: Capacity is a planning concept used to match demand with available capability, time, and constraints. In project environments it often informs forecasting, commitment decisions, iteration planning, and resource balancing. It is not just headcount; it reflects usable availability after accounting for skills, competing work, and practical limits.
Contrast: Capability — capacity is how much work can be taken on; capability is whether the needed skills or functional ability exist to do it well.
Example: A product team estimates that, after support duties and leave are considered, it can complete about thirty story points in the next iteration.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The movement of money into and out of a project, program, or organisation over time.
Expansion: Cash flow reflects when funds are received and when payments are made, not just the total budget or cost. In project management, it is used to understand funding needs, payment timing, and financial viability across the life of the work. Monitoring cash flow helps planners align expenditures, contractual payments, and resource commitments so that the project remains financially supportable.
Contrast: Cost baseline — cash flow tracks the timing of actual or planned money movement, while the cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget used to measure cost performance.
Example: An infrastructure project schedules major equipment payments in the second and third quarters and uses the cash-flow forecast to ensure funding is available before supplier invoices come due.
Source: PMI Lexicon — confirm exact citation in Infinity

Core Meaning: A factor or condition that produces an effect such as a risk, issue, or variance.
Expansion: Identifying the cause behind an effect is the basis of root-cause analysis and effective corrective action. Distinguishing causes from symptoms prevents teams from treating surface problems while the underlying driver persists.
Contrast: Effect — a cause is the originating factor; an effect is the resulting outcome it produces.
Example: Investigation finds the cause of repeated defects is an unclear requirement, not the developers' work.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; defined for glossary completeness

Core Meaning: A decomposition technique that helps trace an undesirable effect back to possible root causes.
Expansion: A cause-and-effect diagram organises possible sources of a problem so a team can explore why an issue is occurring rather than only reacting to symptoms. It is commonly used in quality management and risk analysis to structure thinking about categories such as methods, materials, equipment, people, or environment. The technique improves problem diagnosis and supports more targeted corrective or preventive action.
Contrast: Control chart — a cause-and-effect diagram helps identify possible reasons for a problem, while a control chart helps determine whether process performance is stable over time.
Example: After repeated defects in a manufacturing handoff, the team maps possible causes under training, tooling, documentation, and supplier inputs to identify the most likely root cause.

Figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 340 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='16' y1='100' x2='266' y2='100' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.6'/><polygon points='266,94 278,100 266,106' fill='currentColor'/><rect x='280' y='84' width='52' height='32' rx='4' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='306' y='104' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Effect</text><line x1='70' y1='44' x2='104' y2='100' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='170' y1='44' x2='204' y2='100' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='70' y1='156' x2='104' y2='100' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='170' y1='156' x2='204' y2='100' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='66' y='38' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>People</text><text x='166' y='38' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Process</text><text x='66' y='170' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Tools</text><text x='166' y='170' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Environment</text></svg>
Figure.caption: Causes grouped on bones; the problem (effect) at the head.

Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 698

Core Meaning: An act, process, or result of modifying something from one state to another within the project, product, or environment.
Expansion: In PMI usage, change is the movement from a current condition to a different condition that affects the work, its outputs, or the context around it. It may touch scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, risk, or stakeholder expectations. Because projects both create change and experience change, teams need disciplined ways to assess impact and respond without losing control.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Change is any alteration to a baseline, deliverable, requirement, process, or operating condition that shifts the project from what was previously understood or approved. Some changes are intentional and beneficial, while others are externally imposed.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects operate in environments where information evolves, stakeholders learn, and constraints shift. Change matters because unmanaged change can distort commitments, while well-managed change can improve value, reduce waste, and keep the work aligned with real needs.

The most common misconception: that change is automatically negative or equivalent to disorder. In PMI terms, change itself is neutral; the real issue is whether its impacts are identified, evaluated, approved as needed, and integrated coherently.

How it shows up in real projects: a customer asks for an added capability, a regulator introduces a new compliance rule, a supplier alters a component specification, or test results force a design revision.
Contrast: Change request — change is the actual modification or transition; a change request is the formal proposal to evaluate and authorize it.
Example: During a website redesign project, accessibility testing reveals that the planned navigation does not meet compliance needs, so the team revises the design and assesses impacts to schedule and effort.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, Glossary — Change

Core Meaning: A process through which modifications to documents, deliverables, or baselines are identified, documented, approved or rejected, and tracked.
Expansion: Change control is the formal mechanism for managing proposed changes to controlled aspects of a project. It makes the impact of a requested change visible before action is taken, rather than allowing work to drift informally. PMI treats it as an essential governance practice for protecting baseline integrity while still enabling necessary adaptation.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Change control is the structured process used to handle proposed modifications to project documents, deliverables, and baselines. It usually includes submitting a change request, assessing impacts, deciding whether to approve or reject it, and updating records and plans when a change is accepted.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects rarely unfold exactly as planned, so teams need a disciplined way to respond to new requirements, defects, risks, and external pressures. Change control matters most wherever commitments have been baselined and stakeholders need transparency on the consequences of altering them.

The most common misconception: that change control exists to stop change or add bureaucracy. Its actual purpose is to ensure change is intentional, evaluated, and aligned with governance rather than being introduced informally with hidden consequences.

How it shows up in real projects: a stakeholder asks for an added feature, the team analyses the effect on scope, cost, schedule, risk, and benefits, and the request is then approved, deferred, or rejected through the agreed process before implementation proceeds.
Contrast: Change request — a change request is the formal proposal for a modification, while change control is the process used to evaluate and govern that proposal.
Example: In a construction project, the client requests an upgraded facade material, and the project team routes the request through change control to assess procurement delay, added cost, and design impact before approval.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 696

Core Meaning: A formally constituted group responsible for reviewing, evaluating, approving, delaying, or rejecting changes to the project and recording and communicating those decisions.
Expansion: The Change Control Board, often shortened to CCB, is the governance body that decides how formal change requests will be handled. Its role is to ensure changes are considered consistently, with visibility into impacts on scope, schedule, cost, risk, and benefits. The board may include the sponsor, customer representatives, project manager, and subject matter experts depending on the project context.
Contrast: Change control — the Change Control Board is the decision-making body, while change control is the overall process used to manage proposed changes.
Example: During an ERP implementation, the CCB reviews a request to add a custom reporting feature and approves it only after confirming the schedule and budget impact is acceptable.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 696

Core Meaning: A component of project management planning that defines how changes will be identified, evaluated, approved, documented, and communicated.
Expansion: A change control plan sets the rules for managing proposed changes to baselines, scope, requirements, and other controlled project elements. It establishes the workflow, roles, approval thresholds, records, and governance needed so changes are assessed consistently rather than accepted informally. PMI treats change control as essential to maintaining alignment between delivery work, authorised scope, and decision rights.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A change control plan describes the process used to submit, review, decide, implement, and track change requests. It usually identifies who can request changes, who evaluates impacts, who approves them, and how decisions are recorded.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects operate in changing environments, but unmanaged change leads to scope creep, rework, cost growth, and conflicting stakeholder expectations. A defined change control approach is especially important where formal baselines, contractual commitments, regulatory obligations, or multiple delivery teams are involved.

The most common misconception: that change control exists to block change. In practice its purpose is to enable informed change by making impacts on scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk, and benefits visible before action is authorised.

How it shows up in real projects: when a stakeholder requests a new reporting feature, the project team documents the request, analyses impact on timeline and testing, routes it to the designated approval authority, updates controlled documents if approved, and communicates the decision to affected parties.
Contrast: Integrated change control — the change control plan defines how change will be governed, while integrated change control is the actual process of reviewing and deciding proposed changes.
Example: On an enterprise ERP implementation, the team uses the change control plan to route requested scope additions through impact analysis and change board approval before updating the schedule baseline.
Source: PMBOK Guide, project management plan components and Perform Integrated Change Control section

Core Meaning: A structured set of procedures, tools, records, and approval authorities used to manage changes to project documents, deliverables, and baselines.
Expansion: In PMI practice, change control governs how modifications are identified, documented, reviewed, approved or rejected, and communicated. A change control system is the practical mechanism that enables this governance through workflows, forms, logs, decision rules, and assigned authorities. It helps preserve baseline integrity while allowing justified changes to be evaluated in a controlled way.
Contrast: Change control board — the board is the decision-making body; the change control system is the full mechanism and process used to handle changes.
Example: A construction project routes a requested design revision through a formal change request form, impact analysis, approval review, decision log, and baseline update process.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / Process Groups: A Practice Guide, p. 94 and pp. 73-75

Core Meaning: A project document used to record and track changes and their status over time.
Expansion: A change log captures proposed changes, related decisions, status updates, and key traceability details throughout the project. It gives the team and stakeholders a visible history of what was raised, what was approved or rejected, and what has been implemented. In practice, it supports governance, communication, and auditability by keeping change information in one controlled record.
Contrast: Change request — a change request is the individual proposal; the change log is the cumulative record of change activity and status.
Example: On an infrastructure project, the team records each requested design adjustment in the change log, including the date submitted, impact summary, approval decision, and implementation status.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Project documents — Change log

Core Meaning: A comprehensive, cyclic, and structured approach for transitioning individuals, groups, and organizations from a current state to a future state with intended business benefits realized.
Expansion: PMI uses change management for the discipline focused on preparing, supporting, and enabling people to adopt a new future state. It addresses the human side of change through readiness, communication, engagement, learning, reinforcement, and resistance management. This makes it essential when project success depends not just on delivering an output, but on sustained adoption and behavioural or operational transition.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Change management is the structured practice of helping people and organizations move from an existing way of working to a new one. It is concerned with adoption, acceptance, and sustained use rather than only with delivering the technical solution.

Why it matters and where it applies: many projects fail to realize benefits not because the product was built incorrectly, but because people do not embrace it. It applies strongly in transformations, process redesign, digital implementations, reorganizations, and any initiative that requires stakeholder behaviour to change.

The most common misconception: that change management is the same thing as change control. Change control governs changes to project baselines and deliverables, while change management helps people adapt so the delivered change actually produces value.

How it shows up in real projects: stakeholder impact assessments, sponsorship alignment, communications planning, training, readiness checks, resistance responses, and measures of adoption after go-live.
Contrast: Change control — change management focuses on people, adoption, and transition; change control focuses on assessing and approving changes to project work and baselines.
Example: In an ERP implementation, the project includes role-based training, manager briefings, super-user support, and adoption metrics so staff can transition to the new operating model.
Source: The Standard for Change Management, Glossary — Change management

Core Meaning: A formal proposal to modify any document, deliverable, or baseline.
Expansion: A change request is the documented vehicle used to ask for a change in controlled project work or project management components. It may be triggered by a defect, stakeholder need, risk response, legal requirement, or new information. Once raised, it is evaluated through the project change control process before implementation is authorised.
Contrast: Issue — an issue is a current problem requiring action, while a change request is a formal proposal to alter a controlled project element.
Example: A regulatory update leads the project team to submit a change request to revise system requirements and add compliance testing activities.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 696

Core Meaning: A numbering or coding system used to monitor project costs by category.
Expansion: The chart of accounts structures cost information into defined categories so expenditures can be collected, tracked, and reported consistently. It supports budgeting, accounting, and cost control by aligning project financial data with the organization's accounting framework. In project practice, it helps teams see where money is being spent and compare actual costs with planned allocations.
Contrast: Code of accounts — a chart of accounts organizes financial cost categories, while a code of accounts is used to identify elements in the work breakdown structure.
Example: A facilities project tracks spending under separate account categories for internal labor, purchased materials, leased equipment, and subcontracted services.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Glossary — Chart of accounts

Core Meaning: A document issued by the project sponsor that formally authorizes the existence of a project and gives the project manager authority to apply organizational resources to project activities.
Expansion: A charter is the formal starting point that links a project to a business need, opportunity, or strategic objective. It typically names the project manager, states high-level scope and success criteria, and records key assumptions, constraints, and stakeholder expectations. In PMI usage, the project charter is not just an administrative formality; it is the authorizing instrument that legitimizes the work and clarifies who can make decisions early in the life cycle.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A charter is the formal authorization for a project. It establishes the project at a high level and gives the project manager authority to begin organizing and leading the work.

Why it matters and where it applies: Projects often fail early when people start work without clear sponsorship, purpose, or decision rights. The charter matters because it creates alignment on why the project exists, what success broadly looks like, and who is empowered to lead; it applies across predictive, adaptive, and hybrid environments even if the format varies.

The most common misconception: that the charter is just a summary version of the project management plan. It is not the detailed execution plan; it is the authorization and high-level framing document that exists before full planning is completed.

How it shows up in real projects: A sponsor approves a charter for a warehouse automation project, names the project manager, sets the target outcomes, notes funding limits and compliance constraints, and uses that document to initiate planning and stakeholder engagement.
Contrast: Project management plan — the charter authorizes the project and sets high-level direction; the project management plan explains how the project will be executed, monitored, and controlled.
Example: A hospital sponsor signs a project charter for an electronic records rollout that defines the business case, target go-live date, major risks, and the project manager's authority to coordinate IT, clinical, and vendor resources.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, project charter glossary entry, page 244

Core Meaning: A structured form used to collect and tally data consistently so that patterns, frequency, or defect types can later be analysed.
Expansion: A check sheet is one of the basic quality tools used to capture observations at the point of work in a simple, repeatable format. It supports fact-based quality analysis by making data collection visible and consistent across time, shifts, locations, or categories. In project settings it is commonly used for defect counts, error types, inspection findings, or recurring process issues.
Contrast: Checklist — a check sheet collects and tallies observed data; a checklist verifies whether required steps or items have been completed.
Example: A manufacturing project team uses a check sheet during inspections to count each occurrence of paint defects by defect category across production batches.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A disputed change, compensation request, or assertion of entitlement typically arising when parties cannot agree on contract terms or performance impacts.
Expansion: A claim arises when one contracting party believes it is entitled to additional time, money, or other relief and that entitlement is disputed by the other party. In project settings, claims often stem from scope changes, delays, differing interpretations of requirements, or unforeseen conditions. They are managed through contract administration processes and may proceed to negotiation, alternative dispute resolution, or legal action if unresolved.
Contrast: Change request — a change request seeks approval to modify the project baseline or deliverables, while a claim is a disputed contractual assertion about entitlement or responsibility.
Example: A contractor submits a claim for additional compensation after the client-directed redesign extends site work beyond the originally agreed completion period.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 699

Core Meaning: The process of identifying, documenting, reviewing, and resolving disputed contract changes or other contract-related assertions between buyer and seller.
Expansion: Claims administration is part of procurement and contract management when one party seeks compensation, schedule relief, or another remedy under the contract. It provides a structured way to log claims, examine supporting evidence, apply contract terms, and pursue resolution through negotiation, alternative dispute resolution, or other formal means. Effective claims administration protects project interests while reducing escalation and preserving commercial clarity.
Contrast: Change control — change control governs proposed modifications to project baselines, while claims administration handles disputed matters when the parties disagree on entitlement, responsibility, or compensation.
Example: A contractor submits a claim for additional payment after site conditions differ from the contract assumptions, and the project team reviews records, contract clauses, and schedule impacts before negotiating a resolution.
Source: PMBOK Guide, procurement management section; exact page citation not confirmed in available PMI source

Core Meaning: The act of grouping project-related information according to shared characteristics so it can be analyzed or managed more effectively.
Expansion: In project work, classification is used to organize items such as risks, requirements, costs, defects, or stakeholders by type or attribute. PMI uses the idea across many practices, but not as a distinct formal glossary concept with a dedicated project-management meaning.
Contrast: Categorization — classification is the act of systematically grouping items by shared attributes; categorization is a near-synonym often used more informally.
Example: A project team classifies risks as technical, external, organizational, and project management risks to support targeted response planning.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The process of completing each project procurement and formally closing the related contract.
Expansion: Close Procurements finalises the contract relationship after the seller has completed the work or the agreement has otherwise ended. It includes verifying that contractual obligations were met, resolving outstanding claims where applicable, and archiving procurement records. The process ensures the procurement is formally closed and the project has a complete contractual record.
Contrast: Control Procurements — Control Procurements manages procurement performance while the contract is active; Close Procurements formally completes and closes it.
Example: After a vendor delivers the final system release, receives final acceptance, and all invoices are settled, the project manager closes the contract and archives the procurement file.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Close Procurements; page citation not confirmed in available sources

Core Meaning: The process of finalizing all activities across all project management process groups to formally complete the project or phase.
Expansion: Close Project or Phase confirms that the work is finished, accepted, and ready for formal closure. It includes obtaining final acceptance, archiving project information, releasing resources, and documenting lessons learned. The process also ensures that remaining obligations are addressed and that the project or phase is closed in an orderly way. It provides a controlled endpoint rather than leaving work informally unfinished.
Contrast: Closing process group — Close Project or Phase is a specific process; the Closing Process Group is the broader category that contains closure activities.
Example: After a software rollout is accepted by the sponsor, the team archives documents, releases the team, and completes the formal closure review.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Close Project or Phase

Core Meaning: The process group concerned with formally completing a project, phase, or contractual effort and confirming that work is concluded.
Expansion: Closing is the set of activities used to formally end a project, phase, or contractual engagement in a controlled way. In PMI terms, it includes obtaining acceptance where required, transferring deliverables, finalising records, capturing lessons learned, and releasing resources or obligations. It matters because unfinished closure creates governance, audit, contractual, and operational risk even when delivery work appears complete. Closing applies at the end of the whole project and, in many cases, at the end of a phase or procurement relationship.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Closing is the formal completion work that turns finished effort into an officially concluded project or phase. It is not just stopping execution; it is confirming completion, documenting outcomes, and resolving remaining administrative and contractual matters.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects can appear done operationally while still remaining open in governance terms. Closing ensures acceptance, knowledge capture, financial completion, archival, and orderly handoff, which is important in predictive, agile, hybrid, internal, and vendor-supported work alike.

The most common misconception: that closing is a minor administrative step performed after the real work is finished. In practice, poor closing can leave open risks, disputed scope, missing records, unresolved contracts, and lost lessons that weaken future performance.

How it shows up in real projects: teams complete final acceptance, close contracts, issue final status reporting, archive project information, document lessons learned, transition ownership to operations, and release the project team and remaining budget lines.
Contrast: Close Project or Phase — Closing is the broader process group; Close Project or Phase is the specific process performed within it.
Example: After an ERP rollout, the project manager secures sponsor sign-off, transfers support ownership to operations, archives final documents, records lessons learned, and releases the implementation team.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / Process Groups: A Practice Guide — Closing Process Group, page citation varies by edition and format

Core Meaning: The process group that formally completes or closes a project, phase, or contract.
Expansion: The Closing Process Group ensures work is formally finished and that administrative, contractual, and procedural activities are completed. It includes confirming acceptance, archiving records, and capturing lessons learned. PMI treats it as the structured end of authorised work, not merely stopping effort.
Contrast: Close Project or Phase — the Closing Process Group is the broader process group category; Close Project or Phase is a specific process within PMI process-based guidance.
Example: After a system implementation is accepted, the team closes supplier agreements, archives project files, and records lessons learned before disbanding.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — Closing Focus Area

Core Meaning: The act of formally completing and finishing a project, phase, or contract.
Expansion: Closure confirms that work and obligations are complete, captures lessons learned, releases resources, and obtains formal acceptance. Skipping proper closure leaves open commitments, unrecorded learning, and unclear hand-offs.
Contrast: Cutover — closure formally ends the endeavour and its obligations; cutover is the operational switch to the new solution.
Example: At project end the manager secures sign-off, archives documents, and runs a closeout review.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; defined for glossary completeness

Core Meaning: The collaborative development of solutions, outcomes, or value with stakeholders rather than for them.
Expansion: In PMI-aligned practice, co-creation emphasises active stakeholder participation in shaping requirements, decisions, and deliverables. It is especially relevant where user insight, shared ownership, or innovation improves results. The concept supports stronger alignment between delivered outputs and stakeholder needs, even when it is not always presented as a formal standalone glossary term in PMI standards.
Contrast: Collaboration — co-creation goes beyond working together by jointly shaping the solution or outcome itself.
