Example: A product team runs workshops with customers, operations staff, and developers to jointly design service features before finalising the release scope.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A numbering system used to uniquely identify each component of the work breakdown structure.
Expansion: The code of accounts provides a structured identifier for WBS elements so scope, cost, schedule, and reporting data can be linked consistently. It supports traceability across planning, control, and accounting records.
Contrast: Work breakdown structure — the WBS is the hierarchical decomposition of project work; the code of accounts is the identifier scheme used to label its components.
Example: A construction project assigns 1.2.3 to the electrical rough-in work package so estimates, purchase orders, and progress reports all map to the same scope element.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 698

Term: Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct
Core Meaning: PMI's formal ethical standard that defines expected behaviour through the values of responsibility, respect, fairness, and honesty.
Expansion: The Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct sets the behavioural expectations for PMI members, certification holders, and others covered by the standard. It combines aspirational standards, which describe the conduct practitioners should strive toward, with mandatory standards, which describe conduct that is required. In practice, it serves as a foundation for professional judgment, stakeholder trust, and responsible decision-making across all forms of project work.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct is PMI's formal statement of ethical principles and professional obligations. It is organised around four core values: responsibility, respect, fairness, and honesty.

Why it matters and where it applies: project professionals regularly make decisions involving trade-offs, confidential information, authority, conflict, and stakeholder impact. The code provides a common ethical framework that supports trust, credibility, and consistency across projects, programs, portfolios, and organisational roles.

The most common misconception: that the code applies only to extreme misconduct or disciplinary cases. In reality, it also guides everyday professional choices such as reporting status accurately, disclosing conflicts of interest, treating stakeholders respectfully, and taking ownership of decisions and consequences.

How it shows up in real projects: it appears in procurement integrity, truthful reporting, escalation of issues, respectful stakeholder engagement, protection of confidential information, and decisions where legal compliance alone is not enough to determine the right course of action.
Contrast: Organisational policy — an organisational policy governs local rules and procedures, while the Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct provides PMI's profession-wide ethical expectations and values-based standards.
Example: A project manager reports a schedule delay transparently to sponsors rather than masking it in dashboard metrics, acting in line with responsibility and honesty even under pressure.
Source: PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct, p. 2-4

Core Meaning: People working together deliberately to achieve shared project outcomes through communication, coordination, and joint problem solving.
Expansion: In PMI context, collaboration refers to cooperative work across team members and stakeholders to integrate knowledge, make decisions, and deliver value. It is a behavioural and operating principle rather than a single process, and it is closely tied to team performance, stakeholder engagement, and adaptive ways of working. Effective collaboration improves alignment, speeds issue resolution, and reduces the loss that occurs when work is handed off in silos.
Contrast: Communication — communication is the exchange of information; collaboration adds shared work, co-creation, and coordinated decision making.
Example: A cross-functional project team holds short daily coordination sessions where design, procurement, and operations resolve dependencies together instead of escalating them through separate reporting lines.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The process of determining, documenting, and managing stakeholder needs and requirements to meet objectives.
Expansion: Collect Requirements turns stakeholder expectations into documented requirements that can guide scope definition and solution development. It uses techniques such as interviews, workshops, observation, prototypes, and surveys to clarify what must be delivered. The process helps reduce ambiguity early and creates a basis for later validation and control.
Contrast: Define Scope — Collect Requirements captures and documents stakeholder needs; Define Scope uses those requirements to develop the detailed description of the project and product scope.
Example: For a customer portal project, the team runs workshops with service agents and end users to document response-time, accessibility, and reporting requirements before finalising scope.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 698

Core Meaning: A source of variation that is inherent in a stable process rather than arising from a specific unusual event.
Expansion: In project management and quality management, common cause refers to the normal, expected variation built into the way a process currently operates. It indicates that performance differences are coming from the system itself, not from an isolated defect or one-off disturbance. The practical implication is that improving results usually requires changing the process, not reacting to individual outcomes as if each were a separate problem.
Contrast: Special cause — common cause variation comes from the system as designed, while special cause variation comes from a specific, identifiable circumstance.
Example: A testing team sees small day-to-day fluctuations in defect detection rates because workload, test complexity, and reviewer capacity naturally vary within a stable range.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The exchange of information intended to create shared understanding among participants.
Expansion: Communication in PMI usage is more than sending messages; it includes transmitting, receiving, and interpreting information so meaning is understood. It underpins stakeholder engagement, coordination, decision-making, and issue resolution across all delivery approaches. Weak communication degrades alignment even when plans and roles are otherwise clear.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Communication is the purposeful exchange of information between people or groups through verbal, nonverbal, written, visual, or digital means. In project work, it includes both the message itself and whether the receiver actually understands what was intended.

Why it matters and where it applies: every project depends on communication to align scope, schedule, risks, decisions, and stakeholder expectations. It shows up in governance reporting, team coordination, vendor interactions, change discussions, and day-to-day collaboration.

The most common misconception: that communication is complete once information is sent. In practice, project communication is only effective when the message is received, interpreted correctly, and useful for action.

How it shows up in real projects: status reports, daily coordination, issue escalation, workshops, demos, risk reviews, stakeholder briefings, and informal conversations that prevent misunderstanding before it becomes rework.
Contrast: Communications Management Plan — Communication is the actual exchange of information; the Communications Management Plan defines how project communications will be structured and governed.
Example: A project manager briefs sponsors on milestone risk, confirms the team understood the revised priorities, and follows up with written actions so everyone is aligned.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Communication; PMBOK Guide glossary page citation not confirmed in available search results

Core Meaning: The approaches or channels used to share information among project participants and stakeholders.
Expansion: Communication methods include interactive, push, and pull approaches, each suited to different audiences and purposes. Choosing the right method affects speed, clarity, traceability, and engagement. PMI uses the term to guide deliberate matching of message, audience, urgency, and medium.
Contrast: Communication skills — Communication methods are the channels or approaches used to convey information; communication skills are the personal abilities used to communicate effectively through those methods.
Example: A team uses workshops for issue resolution, email for formal approvals, and a dashboard portal for on-demand status access by executives.
Source: PMBOK Guide — communication methods; exact page citation not confirmed in available search results

Core Meaning: The interpersonal abilities used to exchange information clearly and build shared understanding among project participants.
Expansion: Communication skills enable project work by supporting clear sending, receiving, and interpretation of information. In PMI practice they include listening, speaking, writing, questioning, and tailoring messages to audience needs. They are central to leadership, stakeholder engagement, conflict management, and team coordination.
Contrast: Communication methods — communication skills are the human abilities used to communicate effectively; communication methods are the channels or approaches used to deliver information.
Example: A project manager adjusts a risk update for executives, then uses active listening with the delivery team to confirm that actions and owners are understood.
Source: PMI Lexicon, Communication skills; page citation not confirmed in available sources

Core Meaning: The project management function of planning, creating, distributing, storing, retrieving, monitoring, and disposing of project information so stakeholders get what they need when they need it.
Expansion: Communications management is the disciplined handling of project information across its full lifecycle, from planning communications needs through monitoring whether communication is effective. PMI treats it as more than sending messages; it is about enabling understanding among stakeholders with different interests, authority, and information needs. It matters because weak communications distort decisions, hide risk, and slow delivery even when technical work is strong. It applies across all delivery approaches because every project depends on timely, usable, and appropriately targeted information exchange.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Communications management is the structured management of who needs information, what information they need, when they need it, how it will be delivered, and how effectiveness will be assessed. It includes formal and informal communication, documentation practices, channels, escalation paths, and feedback loops.

Why it matters and where it applies: most project failure points involve misunderstanding, delayed awareness, or missing alignment rather than pure technical inability. Communications management supports stakeholder engagement, governance reporting, team coordination, issue escalation, and decision quality in predictive, agile, and hybrid settings.

The most common misconception: that communications management means producing status reports or running meetings. In practice, it is broader and focuses on whether the right people actually receive, understand, and can act on the information.

How it shows up in real projects: communication plans, stakeholder-specific reporting cadences, dashboards, escalation rules, collaboration norms, meeting structures, feedback mechanisms, and periodic adjustment when stakeholder needs or project conditions change.
Contrast: Stakeholder engagement — communications management focuses on information flow and understanding; stakeholder engagement focuses on building support, involvement, and relationships.
Example: On a construction project, the team sets weekly site briefings for subcontractors, monthly steering updates for executives, immediate safety escalation rules, and a document repository with version controls.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition — Project Communications Management chapter and glossary, page citation depends on format

Core Meaning: A component of the project management plan that describes how, when, and by whom information will be administered and disseminated.
Expansion: The Communications Management Plan defines the project's approach to creating, storing, distributing, and monitoring information. It helps align communication methods, timing, audiences, and responsibilities with stakeholder needs. It is used to make communication more deliberate and less dependent on ad hoc habits across the team.
Contrast: Stakeholder Engagement Plan — the Communications Management Plan defines how information is handled and shared; the Stakeholder Engagement Plan focuses more broadly on how stakeholders are engaged and supported.
Example: A transformation project sets a plan that weekly progress dashboards go to sponsors, daily coordination updates go to the core team, and monthly steering summaries go to executives.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, section 10.1.3.1, page 377

Core Meaning: A representation of how information is encoded, transmitted, received, and interpreted between participants.
Expansion: The communications model explains that effective communication depends on both sender and receiver, the medium used, and the possibility of noise or misunderstanding. It helps project professionals design messages that are more likely to be understood as intended. PMI uses it to emphasise that communication quality is shaped by the full exchange, not just message delivery.
Contrast: Communication — Communication is the actual exchange of information; a communications model is the conceptual framework used to understand how that exchange works.
Example: Before announcing a process change, a project manager uses multiple channels and checks for understanding because the message could be interpreted differently by field teams and executives.
Source: PMBOK Guide — communications model; exact page citation not confirmed in available search results

Core Meaning: A cluster of related knowledge, attitudes, skills, and personal characteristics that affects job performance, can be measured against standards, and can be improved through development.
Expansion: In PMI usage, competence is broader than technical skill alone and includes the capabilities and characteristics that enable effective performance. It matters in project work because outcomes depend not only on methods and tools but also on judgement, leadership, collaboration, and behavioural effectiveness. The concept is used in professional development, role expectations, and capability assessment for project professionals.
Contrast: Communication skills — competence is a broader capability cluster that can include many skills and behaviours; communication skills are only one component.
Example: A PMO assesses a project manager's competence by looking at planning knowledge, stakeholder handling, leadership behaviour, and decision quality rather than only schedule-tool proficiency.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Competence, page 188

Core Meaning: The demonstrated knowledge, skills, behaviours, and abilities that enable effective performance in a project role.
Expansion: Competency in PMI usage refers to the capability to perform successfully, not just to possess knowledge or certification. It commonly includes technical project management capability, leadership or interpersonal effectiveness, and business or strategic understanding. Competency is used in role development, team selection, career growth, and organisational capability building.
Contrast: Capability — competency focuses on demonstrated effectiveness in a role; capability is a broader capacity or ability at the individual, team, or organisational level.
Example: A PMO assesses project manager competency across stakeholder engagement, scheduling, risk management, and strategic alignment before assigning leaders to complex initiatives.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A characteristic of a program, project, or its environment that is difficult to manage because of human behavior, system behavior, and ambiguity.
Expansion: Complexity reflects the degree to which project outcomes are affected by dynamic interactions, uncertainty, and emergent behavior rather than by isolated tasks alone. PMI treats complexity as more than size or difficulty, since a small initiative can still be highly complex if stakeholder interests conflict or dependencies are unstable. Understanding complexity helps teams choose governance, planning, and delivery approaches that fit the real conditions of the work.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Complexity is the condition created when many interacting elements, unclear cause-and-effect relationships, and changing contexts make work hard to predict and manage. It arises from both technical systems and the people and institutions around them.

Why it matters and where it applies: Complexity shapes planning depth, governance style, risk exposure, and the suitability of predictive, adaptive, or hybrid approaches. It is especially relevant in transformation programs, cross-functional change, regulated environments, and initiatives with many interdependencies or powerful stakeholders.

The most common misconception: that complexity is the same as scale. A large project can be relatively straightforward, while a modest project can be highly complex if requirements are ambiguous, stakeholder agendas conflict, or external conditions keep shifting.

How it shows up in real projects: A regional system migration seems manageable in budget and duration, but becomes complex because multiple legacy systems interact, regulations differ by country, and each business unit wants different outcomes, forcing iterative decision-making and frequent replanning.
Contrast: Complicated — something complicated may have many parts but can still be analyzed with stable cause and effect; complexity involves interaction, emergence, and uncertainty that make outcomes harder to predict.
Example: A merger integration project becomes complex when technology dependencies, cultural differences, legal requirements, and executive politics interact in ways that constantly change priorities.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, complexity glossary entry, page 239

Core Meaning: Conformance to applicable laws, regulations, standards, policies, contracts, or other mandated requirements affecting project work.
Expansion: Compliance in project management means ensuring that project decisions, deliverables, and ways of working satisfy required external and internal obligations. These obligations may come from regulation, contractual commitments, organisational policies, industry standards, or governance bodies. PMI treats compliance as a practical constraint on planning and execution, not just a legal concern, because noncompliance can create risk, rework, delay, penalties, or loss of stakeholder trust.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Compliance is the disciplined adherence of project work and outputs to mandatory requirements. It covers both the product being created and the processes used to create, approve, document, and deliver it.

Why it matters and where it applies: Projects often operate inside regulatory, contractual, safety, privacy, financial, or quality boundaries. Compliance protects the organisation from legal exposure, failed audits, rejected deliverables, and reputational damage, and it is especially visible in healthcare, finance, construction, government, and regulated technology environments.

The most common misconception: that compliance is only a legal or audit activity handled outside the project team. In reality, compliance needs to be built into requirements, planning, decision gates, procurement, quality activities, and acceptance criteria throughout the project.

How it shows up in real projects: Teams include regulatory requirements in scope, define evidence and approval records, perform control checks, trace requirements to deliverables, and verify that vendors and internal practices meet mandated standards before release or handover.
Contrast: Quality — quality focuses on fitness for use and conformance to requirements broadly, while compliance focuses specifically on adherence to mandated rules, obligations, and prescribed standards.
Example: A healthcare software project includes privacy controls, audit logging, validation records, and formal approval steps to ensure the release complies with patient-data regulations.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A component is an identifiable part of a larger whole, such as a project, program, portfolio, subsidiary portfolio, or subprogram, managed within a broader structure.
Expansion: In PMI usage, component refers to a distinct element within a program or portfolio structure that can be selected, authorised, prioritised, and managed. A component may itself be temporary or ongoing depending on the context, but it is treated as a unit for governance and decision-making. The term matters because portfolio and program management depend on seeing how individual components contribute to strategic objectives and benefits.
Contrast: Deliverable — a component is a managed part of a larger organisational or governance structure; a deliverable is a result, output, or capability produced.
Example: In a digital-transformation portfolio, the CRM replacement project is one component, while the customer-data governance initiative is another.
Source: The Standard for Portfolio Management, Fourth Edition / The Standard for Program Management, Fifth Edition — term: Component

Core Meaning: A condition is a state or circumstance that exists and may affect how project work, decisions, or outcomes are understood or managed.
Expansion: In PMI usage, condition usually appears as part of a broader concept such as a contractual condition, environmental condition, or triggering condition for an action or response. It is not typically treated as a standalone foundational project-management concept, but as a qualifying state that influences interpretation or action.
Contrast: Assumption — a condition is an existing or stipulated state or circumstance; an assumption is something treated as true for planning without full proof.
Example: A project contract may specify a condition that final payment is released only after successful acceptance testing.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The process of drawing out, discovering, and capturing information from stakeholders and other sources to understand needs.
Expansion: In PMI business analysis practice, Conduct Elicitation is used to obtain information relevant to requirements, problems, opportunities, and solution options. It includes techniques such as interviews, workshops, observation, and prototypes to uncover explicit and implicit stakeholder needs. The purpose is to build a reliable basis for analysis and decision-making.
Contrast: Collect Requirements — Conduct Elicitation focuses on drawing out information through business analysis activities; Collect Requirements is the project management process that develops and manages requirements for the project and product.
Example: A business analyst facilitates workshops with finance and operations users, observes current workflows, and interviews supervisors to uncover reporting needs for a new system.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A configuration item is a product, component, or result whose characteristics are formally identified and controlled through configuration management.
Expansion: A configuration item is any item selected for formal identification, documentation, status accounting, and change control so its integrity can be maintained over time. In project management, configuration items may include specifications, design documents, code modules, hardware units, baselines, or other approved artifacts whose versions matter. The concept is foundational because complex projects need a reliable way to know exactly what version of a product or document is approved, changed, tested, and released.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A configuration item is a defined unit placed under configuration management so that its functional and physical characteristics remain visible and controlled. Once designated, it is tracked through identification, versioning, status accounting, and approved change processes.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects that produce technical products, systems, regulated outputs, or tightly integrated deliverables need traceability across versions and approvals. Configuration items help teams prevent uncontrolled change, support audits, and ensure that testing, release, and operations are all working from the correct version.

The most common misconception: that every project artifact is automatically a configuration item. In practice, only selected items whose integrity, traceability, or compliance matter are usually designated and controlled at that level.

How it shows up in real projects: a design drawing, approved requirements specification, software build, network appliance image, or validated procedure may each be assigned an identifier, version history, baseline status, and formal change record.
Contrast: Work performance data — a configuration item is a controlled product or artifact under formal change discipline; work performance data is raw execution information about project activities.
Example: On a medical-device project, the approved firmware build and the validated system requirements specification are each managed as configuration items with version numbers and formal change approval.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition — configuration management context; Practice Standard for Project Configuration Management — term: Configuration item

Core Meaning: A process for ensuring that the descriptions of project deliverables and product components are correct and complete and that changes are controlled.
Expansion: Configuration management establishes how project artifacts, product components, and related documentation are identified, versioned, evaluated, and controlled over time. It helps teams maintain integrity between what was approved, what is being built, and what is actually delivered. PMI treats it as a key discipline for managing complexity, especially where many technical elements and interdependencies are involved.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Configuration management is the structured discipline used to identify configuration items, record their current state, control approved changes, and verify that the resulting product or deliverable matches what was authorised. It combines version control, status accounting, and formal change evaluation so the project does not lose track of what is current.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects with technical complexity, regulated deliverables, multiple interfaces, or frequent changes can quickly drift into confusion if components are altered without traceability. Configuration management is especially important in engineering, software, infrastructure, pharmaceuticals, and any environment where approved baselines and auditability matter.

The most common misconception: that configuration management is just document version control or a filing exercise. In practice it is broader, because it links the approved configuration of deliverables and components to governance, impact analysis, and verification of what is actually implemented.

How it shows up in real projects: teams define configuration items, assign unique identifiers, maintain version histories, route proposed modifications through formal review, and verify before release that the built product matches approved specifications and interfaces.
Contrast: Change control — configuration management governs the identification and integrity of configuration items and their approved states, while change control focuses on evaluating and approving proposed changes to baselines.
Example: On a medical-device project, the team tracks approved hardware drawings, firmware builds, interface specifications, and test procedures as configuration items so every release can be traced to the exact approved versions.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 699

Core Meaning: A component of the project management plan that describes how configuration items will be identified, controlled, recorded, and verified.
Expansion: The configuration management plan establishes the procedures for managing the consistency of project deliverables and related documentation as they evolve. It typically defines configuration items, version control rules, change control interfaces, status accounting, and verification responsibilities. PMI treats it as a way to preserve integrity and traceability across approved baselines and product components.
Contrast: Change management plan — a configuration management plan governs identification and control of configuration items and versions; a change management plan defines how changes are proposed, reviewed, and approved.
Example: On an engineering project, the team defines how design drawings, specifications, and software builds are versioned, approved, and audited before release to construction.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Sixth Edition, page 96

Core Meaning: A cognitive bias in which people favour information that supports their existing beliefs, expectations, or hypotheses.
Expansion: PMI identifies confirmation bias as a cognitive bias that confirms preexisting beliefs or hypotheses. In projects, it can distort forecasting, risk assessment, stakeholder interpretation, and issue escalation because teams may notice supporting evidence while discounting warning signs. Recognising it improves decision quality by encouraging disconfirming evidence, broader review, and more balanced judgment.
Contrast: Anchoring bias — confirmation bias favours evidence that supports an existing belief; anchoring bias overweights the first information received.
Example: A project manager believes a supplier is still on track and pays attention to optimistic status updates while overlooking repeated missed intermediate dates.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, p. 14

Core Meaning: Conflict is disagreement or opposition among individuals, groups, or stakeholders arising from differences in goals, priorities, values, perceptions, or resource needs.
Expansion: In PMI project management, conflict is a normal condition of collaborative work rather than an automatic sign of dysfunction. It often emerges from schedule pressure, scarce resources, technical trade-offs, unclear roles, or competing stakeholder interests. Managing conflict constructively is foundational because unresolved conflict can damage decision quality, morale, trust, and delivery performance, while healthy conflict can improve problem solving.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Conflict is the friction that appears when people or groups differ in interests, assumptions, interpretations, or desired outcomes. In projects, it can be interpersonal, cross-functional, technical, procedural, or stakeholder-driven.

Why it matters and where it applies: nearly every project involves interdependence, limited resources, and competing priorities, so conflict appears in planning, execution, governance, procurement, and stakeholder engagement. Project leaders need to surface the issue, understand the source, and apply an appropriate response so the disagreement produces clarity rather than disruption.

The most common misconception: that all conflict is harmful and should be suppressed quickly. PMI-aligned practice distinguishes destructive conflict from productive disagreement, since thoughtful challenge can improve risk identification, solution quality, and commitment.

How it shows up in real projects: engineers dispute scope versus quality trade-offs, business stakeholders compete for release priority, or functional managers and project managers disagree over resource allocation. The response may involve collaboration, compromise, escalation, clear decision rights, or resetting expectations.
Contrast: Issue — conflict is disagreement among parties; an issue is a current problem or point requiring resolution, which may or may not involve interpersonal disagreement.
Example: Two department leads argue over which team gets the only available data architect, so the project manager facilitates a decision based on milestone criticality and agreed portfolio priorities.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition — Manage Team conflict management discussion

Core Meaning: The practice of addressing and resolving disagreements among stakeholders or team members so they do not damage project performance.
Expansion: Conflict management is a leadership and team-management capability used to surface, address, and resolve differences in goals, priorities, perceptions, or working styles. PMI treats conflict as a normal feature of project environments rather than something to avoid entirely. Effective conflict management protects relationships, supports decision quality, and helps the team maintain focus on project outcomes instead of interpersonal friction. It is particularly important in cross-functional, high-pressure, and resource-constrained projects where competing interests are common.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Conflict management is the structured handling of disagreement through techniques such as collaboration, compromise, smoothing, forcing, or withdrawal, depending on context. The goal is not simply to stop conflict, but to resolve it in a way that supports the work and preserves productive relationships.

Why it matters and where it applies: Projects bring together people with different incentives, expertise, authority, and constraints. If conflict is ignored, it can slow decisions, reduce morale, create rework, and undermine stakeholder alignment; if handled well, it can improve clarity and lead to better solutions.

The most common misconception: that all conflict is harmful or that good project managers eliminate it. In practice, healthy task-related conflict can surface risks and improve decisions, while unmanaged personal conflict is what usually harms performance.

How it shows up in real projects: A project manager mediates priority disputes between departments, clarifies roles, facilitates trade-off discussions, and uses agreed escalation paths when consensus cannot be reached at the team level.
Contrast: Issue management — conflict management addresses interpersonal or stakeholder disagreement, while issue management tracks and resolves project problems regardless of whether people are in conflict.
Example: During a product launch project, the marketing lead wants new features added while the testing lead argues they threaten release quality, so the project manager facilitates a trade-off discussion and reaches agreement on a phased release.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The degree to which a result meets established quality requirements, specifications, or standards.
Expansion: PMI defines conformance as the degree to which results meet the set quality requirements. The term is central to quality management because it focuses attention on whether deliverables actually satisfy agreed criteria rather than merely being completed. It is often discussed alongside nonconformance and the cost of quality when evaluating prevention, appraisal, and defect-related outcomes.
Contrast: Compliance — conformance is about meeting defined quality requirements; compliance is about meeting laws, regulations, or mandated rules.
Example: A medical-device project verifies that a prototype meets all specified tolerance levels and acceptance criteria before approving it for the next stage.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, p. 15

Core Meaning: A group decision outcome in which participants can support the selected option even if it is not each person's first choice.
Expansion: Consensus is reached when a group has discussed alternatives and arrived at a decision the members can accept and stand behind. In PMI practice it is associated with group decision-making and facilitation because it helps create commitment and reduces resistance during execution. It does not mean everyone prefers the same option, only that the group can move forward together.
Contrast: Unanimity — consensus means people can support the decision; unanimity means everyone fully agrees with it.
Example: A cross-functional team compares three rollout approaches and reaches consensus on a phased deployment that balances risk, speed, and user impact.
Source: Process Groups: A Practice Guide, p. 188

Core Meaning: Mathematical techniques that optimise an objective such as cost, schedule, or resource use while respecting defined limits or constraints.
Expansion: PMI refers to constrained optimisation methods as decision techniques used in project settings where choices must satisfy restrictions such as budget, capacity, or time. They are often applied in areas such as resource optimisation, scheduling, and portfolio selection.
Contrast: Data gathering and representation techniques — constrained optimisation methods use mathematical optimisation to select the best feasible solution, rather than primarily collecting or displaying information.
Example: A portfolio team uses linear programming to select the mix of projects that maximises strategic value without exceeding funding and staffing limits.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Sixth Edition, page 717

Core Meaning: A limiting factor that affects the execution of a project, program, portfolio, or process.
Expansion: A constraint restricts available choices and shapes how work can be planned and delivered. Common constraints involve time, cost, scope, quality, resources, risk tolerance, compliance, or technology. PMI treats constraints as conditions that must be worked within rather than simply obstacles to remove.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A constraint is any boundary or restriction that limits what the team can do, when it can do it, or how it can do it. Some constraints are fixed from the start, while others emerge as the project environment changes.

Why it matters and where it applies: constraints influence trade-offs, feasibility, sequencing, resource allocation, and stakeholder expectations. They apply in every domain, from construction and software to product development and transformation programs.

The most common misconception: that constraints are only the classic three of scope, schedule, and cost. In reality, quality standards, legal obligations, resource scarcity, architecture decisions, and organizational policies can constrain delivery just as strongly.

How it shows up in real projects: a fixed regulatory deadline limits schedule flexibility, a capped budget narrows design options, or a mandated platform constrains technical choices and vendor selection.
Contrast: Assumption — A constraint is a known limiting factor; an assumption is something treated as true for planning without full proof at the time.
Example: A hospital system upgrade must go live before a regulatory compliance date, forcing the team to reduce optional features to meet the immovable deadline.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Constraint; PMBOK Guide glossary page citation not confirmed in available search results

Core Meaning: Constraint management is the practice of identifying, analysing, monitoring, and responding to limiting factors that restrict project options or performance.
Expansion: Constraint management focuses on the boundaries that shape what a project can realistically do, such as limits on scope, schedule, cost, resources, quality, compliance, or technology. While PMI commonly defines individual constraints rather than always naming constraint management as a formal glossary term, the practice is real and central to planning and control. It helps teams make trade-offs explicitly, protect feasibility, and respond when one constraint changes and pressures the others.
Contrast: Risk management — constraint management handles known limiting conditions already bounding the project; risk management addresses uncertain events or conditions that may affect objectives.
Example: A public-sector project team tracks a fixed regulatory deadline, capped funding, and mandatory security standards as constraints and replans scope to remain feasible.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A project delivery context focused on building, altering, or repairing physical assets and infrastructure.
Expansion: Construction is a project environment in which the work produces tangible physical outputs such as buildings, roads, utilities, or industrial facilities. It typically involves sequencing, site conditions, safety, permits, subcontractors, and coordination across many trades. PMI treats it as an application context rather than a formal glossary concept, so core project management principles still apply within this domain.
Contrast: Manufacturing — construction creates fixed physical assets on a site; manufacturing produces items in a repeatable production environment.
Example: A project team manages the construction of a hospital wing, coordinating excavation, structural work, mechanical systems, inspections, and commissioning.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A stakeholder-participation pattern in which some parties provide input before action is taken and others are kept updated after decisions or progress occur.
Expansion: Consulted and informed describes two distinct involvement expectations often used in responsibility assignment and communication planning. Consulted stakeholders are asked for input and their views are considered before decisions or work proceed, while informed stakeholders are kept aware of status, decisions, or outcomes. In PMI practice this distinction helps clarify who contributes to decisions and who simply needs visibility.
Contrast: Responsible and accountable — consulted and informed describe participation in communication and decision support; responsible and accountable describe ownership for doing work and owning the result.
Example: During an enterprise system rollout, the security architect is consulted on access design, while department managers are informed once the deployment schedule is approved.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A planned allowance or predefined response used to address identified uncertainty or risk.
Expansion: Contingency is the planned flexibility set aside to deal with identified risks or expected uncertainty in a project. In PMI practice it commonly appears through contingency reserves and contingency plans rather than as a standalone formal glossary entry. Its purpose is to let the project respond to anticipated variation without treating every likely issue as an unmanaged exception.
Contrast: Management reserve — contingency addresses identified risks and expected uncertainty; management reserve is held for unforeseen work outside the baseline.
Example: A data-center migration schedule includes contingency time to absorb likely delays in network cutover approvals.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, section 7.3.3.1, page 254

Core Meaning: A predefined response plan to be executed if a risk event occurs.
Expansion: A contingency plan specifies the actions the team will take if an identified risk materializes. It is part of risk response planning and is intended to reduce the impact of a threat or capture an opportunity if a trigger occurs. PMI distinguishes it from contingency reserve, which is the time or money set aside to support the response.
Contrast: Contingency reserve — a contingency plan is the action response; contingency reserve is the time or money reserved to support it.
Example: A project team defines that if a sole-source vendor misses a key delivery milestone, it will activate an approved backup supplier and resequence installation work.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Contingency plan; exact page not available in provided results

Core Meaning: Time or money allocated in the schedule or cost baseline for known risks with active response strategies.
Expansion: Contingency reserve is a planned allowance for identified risks that may affect schedule or cost. It is set aside so the project can respond to expected uncertainty without immediately treating every impact as an exception. PMI positions it as part of disciplined risk and cost or schedule planning rather than as an arbitrary buffer.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Contingency reserve is budget or time held for identified risks, sometimes called known unknowns. It is linked to risks that have been analysed and for which response strategies have been defined.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects face uncertainty even when they are well planned, and contingency reserve provides controlled flexibility to absorb that uncertainty. It is especially relevant in cost estimating, schedule development, reserve analysis, and risk response planning.

The most common misconception: that contingency reserve is the same as management reserve or just hidden padding. It is neither; contingency reserve is tied to identified risks and planned responses, while management reserve is held for unforeseen work outside the baseline.

How it shows up in real projects: a project may include extra time for likely vendor delay or extra budget for expected rework on a complex integration. As risks retire or become clearer, the reserve can be adjusted, consumed, or released through normal control processes.
Contrast: Management reserve — contingency reserve covers identified risks within the baseline; management reserve covers unforeseen work and sits outside the performance baseline.
Example: A data-centre migration project includes extra weekend cutover time in case identified interface issues require rollback and retesting.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, section 7.3.3.1, page 254

Core Meaning: A predefined course of action that the project team will execute if a specified risk trigger occurs.
Expansion: A contingency response strategy defines what the team will do if a particular risk event happens. It is linked to a trigger condition and is intended to reduce negative impact or enable an opportunity response when the event materialises. In PMI practice this sits within risk response planning and is distinct from the reserve that may fund or schedule the action.
Contrast: Contingency reserve — the strategy is the planned action to take; the reserve is the time or cost allowance that may support that action.
Example: If a critical hardware supplier misses its committed ship date, the project will activate a backup vendor and resequence installation tasks.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The practice of making feature increments ready for immediate customer delivery through small batches of work and extensive automation.
Expansion: PMI defines continuous delivery as the practice of delivering feature increments immediately to customers, often through small batches of work and automation technology. It supports fast, reliable flow from completed work to usable value by reducing manual handoffs and release friction. The concept is especially important in adaptive and product-focused environments where rapid feedback improves outcomes and lowers delivery risk.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Continuous delivery is a way of working in which teams design, build, test, and prepare increments so they can be released at any time with minimal delay. It depends on small batch sizes, automated testing, stable integration practices, and release readiness built into everyday work.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters because long delays between development and release hide defects, increase coordination cost, and slow learning. It is especially useful in software, digital products, platform services, and other environments where frequent deployment and customer feedback improve value delivery.

The most common misconception: that continuous delivery means every change must be released instantly to production. In practice, it means the increment is kept in a releasable state at all times, while the business may still choose when to release.

How it shows up in real projects: Teams automate builds and tests, integrate code frequently, use deployment pipelines, and release approved increments in small, low-risk updates instead of bundling large releases.
Contrast: Continuous integration — continuous delivery keeps increments ready for immediate release; continuous integration focuses on frequently merging and validating work in a shared codebase.
Example: A digital-banking team automates build, test, security scan, and deployment steps so a completed account-alert feature can be released safely as soon as product approval is given.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, p. 16

Core Meaning: An ongoing effort to improve products, services, or processes through incremental learning and change.
Expansion: Continuous improvement is the practice of repeatedly making small, evidence-based enhancements instead of waiting for a major redesign. PMI connects it to retrospectives, lessons learned, quality management, and team learning across the life cycle. It matters because projects surface defects, delays, and coordination issues that can often be improved while work is still underway. The concept applies in predictive, agile, and hybrid environments.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Continuous improvement is a disciplined pattern of reviewing results, identifying causes, and adjusting how work is performed. It is ongoing and iterative, not a one-time workshop or end-of-project exercise.

Why it matters and where it applies: It helps teams improve quality, reduce waste, and increase delivery reliability while the project is still active. It applies in team practices, delivery workflows, governance routines, and handoffs to operations.

The most common misconception: that continuous improvement means changing processes constantly. In practice, it means making targeted changes that are tested, useful, and tied to better outcomes.

How it shows up in real projects: teams run retrospectives, analyze root causes, refine templates, adjust workflows, and track whether those changes reduce rework or cycle time.
Contrast: Lessons learned — continuous improvement is the ongoing practice of improving performance; lessons learned are the insights captured to inform that improvement.
Example: After repeated defects in requirements handoff, a project team adds a short peer review step and then tracks whether the change lowers rework over the next month.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A development practice in which team members frequently merge work into a shared codebase and validate it through automated checks.
Expansion: Continuous integration is a technical practice used in agile and product-development environments to integrate small changes into a common baseline on a frequent basis. Each integration is typically verified with automated builds and tests so defects surface early rather than accumulating until later. In project terms, it supports rapid feedback, lowers integration risk, and improves delivery flow in iterative work.
Contrast: Continuous delivery — continuous integration focuses on frequently merging and validating changes; continuous delivery extends that flow toward release readiness.
Example: A software team merges code changes several times a day and runs automated unit and integration tests after each commit to detect defects immediately.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A mutually binding agreement that obligates the seller to provide the specified products, services, or results and obligates the buyer to provide monetary or other valuable consideration.
Expansion: In project management, a contract defines the legal and commercial relationship between the parties and sets expectations for scope, responsibilities, performance, and remedies. PMI uses the term in procurement and supplier management contexts where external parties contribute deliverables or capabilities. The form may vary, but the essential feature is enforceable mutual obligation.
Contrast: Procurement statement of work — the contract is the binding agreement between parties; the procurement statement of work describes the deliverable or work to be acquired.
Example: A city signs a fixed-price contract with a construction firm to deliver a pedestrian bridge according to specified design, quality, and completion requirements.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, contract glossary entry, page 698

Core Meaning: The process of selecting a seller and formally establishing the contract for the procured goods or services.
Expansion: In PMI usage, contract award is the outcome of source selection and negotiation that results in a binding agreement with a selected seller. It occurs during procurement activities after proposals or bids have been evaluated against defined criteria. The term matters because it marks the point where procurement planning becomes an enforceable external commitment with cost, schedule, scope, and risk implications.
Contrast: Procurement negotiation — contract award is the formal selection and establishment of the agreement; negotiation is the activity used to reach acceptable terms before award.
Example: A public-sector project evaluates three vendor proposals for bridge inspection software, selects the highest-rated bidder, and issues the signed agreement as the contract award.
Source: PMI Lexicon / PMBOK Guide procurement terminology — exact page citation not retrievable from current source

Core Meaning: The process of completing and settling a contract, including verifying deliverables and resolving any open claims or obligations.
Expansion: Contract closure is the procurement-focused closeout work performed once contracted deliverables have been accepted or the agreement is otherwise ended. It includes confirming that work was completed as required, documenting final records, and ensuring administrative and legal obligations are settled. In project management it is important because incomplete closeout can leave disputes, unpaid balances, warranty confusion, or audit gaps after project work appears finished.
Contrast: Close Project or Phase — contract closure concerns the formal completion of a specific procurement agreement; project or phase closure addresses the broader project-level administrative and delivery closeout.
Example: After an equipment supplier completes factory acceptance testing and final installation, the project team confirms all deliverables, settles the final invoice, archives records, and closes the contract.
Source: PMBOK Guide Fourth Edition, Close Procurements process; PMI Lexicon procurement terminology — exact page citation not retrievable from current source

Core Meaning: A classification of contractual arrangement that defines how payment, cost risk, and performance obligations are structured between buyer and seller.
Expansion: Contract type describes the commercial form used to allocate financial risk and compensate the seller, such as fixed-price, cost-reimbursable, or time-and-materials arrangements. PMI treats it as a key procurement decision because the chosen type influences incentives, change handling, oversight needs, and the distribution of uncertainty between parties. Selecting the right contract type depends on how well scope is defined, how much cost variability is expected, and how much risk each party can reasonably manage.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Contract type is the basic commercial structure of an agreement and governs how the seller gets paid, what cost exposure each side accepts, and how performance is managed. Common PMI categories include fixed-price, cost-reimbursable, and time-and-materials contracts.

Why it matters and where it applies: the wrong contract type can distort behaviour, increase disputes, or shift risk to the party least able to control it. It is especially important in procurement-heavy projects such as construction, engineering, outsourcing, technology implementation, and regulated acquisitions.

The most common misconception: that one contract type is always best, or that fixed-price always means low risk for the buyer. In practice, fixed-price can increase total cost or create change conflict when scope is immature, while other types may better support uncertainty if governance is strong.

How it shows up in real projects: a buyer may use fixed-price for standard hardware, time-and-materials for specialist short-term expertise, and cost-reimbursable for research work where the solution cannot be fully specified upfront.
Contrast: Agreement — an agreement is the overall documented understanding between parties; contract type is the specific commercial structure within that agreement.
Example: A hospital project uses a fixed-price contract for standard imaging equipment installation but a time-and-materials contract for specialist cybersecurity support during system integration.

Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition procurement and agreement concepts; Procurement-related definitions in earlier PMBOK editions — exact page citation not retrievable from current source

Core Meaning: The act of comparing actual performance with planned performance, analyzing variances, and taking corrective or preventive action as needed.
Expansion: In PMI project management, control is the management activity used to keep project work aligned with approved plans, baselines, and objectives. It involves measuring performance, identifying deviations, assessing their significance, and responding through decisions or actions. Control is foundational because projects rarely proceed exactly as planned, and disciplined control is what turns monitoring information into managed outcomes.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Control is the decision-and-action side of performance management. Monitoring reveals what is happening; control determines whether intervention is needed and implements the response.

Why it matters and where it applies: every knowledge area uses control in some form, including scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, risks, procurements, and stakeholder engagement. Without control, a project can produce lots of status data yet still drift away from its intended outcomes.

The most common misconception: that control means rigid command, bureaucracy, or punishing variance. In PMI terms, control is not about restricting teams for its own sake; it is about maintaining alignment, making informed adjustments, and protecting value.

How it shows up in real projects: a team compares earned value results to the schedule baseline, sees repeated slippage on a critical path activity, approves corrective action, updates forecasts, and manages the associated change through governance.
Contrast: Monitor — monitoring observes and reports performance; control evaluates deviations and drives corrective or preventive action.
Example: During a system rollout, the project manager detects defect rates above the quality threshold, pauses deployment to fix root causes, and adjusts the release plan to restore control.
Source: PMBOK Guide process definitions for Monitor and Control Project Work and Control processes; PMI Lexicon control terminology — exact page citation not retrievable from current source

Core Meaning: A management control point where scope, budget, actual cost, and schedule are integrated and compared to earned value for performance measurement.
Expansion: A control account sits above one or more work packages in the WBS and provides a practical point for planning and monitoring performance. It is where authorised scope, budgets, schedules, and actuals come together so the team can track progress and variance in a manageable way. It is especially important in earned value management because it creates a consistent unit for integrated control.
Contrast: Work package — a work package is the lowest level of planned work in the WBS; a control account is a higher-level management point used to integrate and measure several work elements.
Example: In an aircraft upgrade project, Avionics Integration is set up as a control account containing several work packages for design, installation, and testing, each rolled up for performance reporting.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 699

Core Meaning: A graphic display of process data over time and against established control limits, with a centerline that assists in detecting trends of plotted values toward either control limit.
Expansion: A control chart is used to assess whether a process is stable and behaving within expected variation. By plotting results over time, it helps the team distinguish normal variation from signals that suggest a special cause or loss of control. In projects, it is most often associated with quality control, though it can also support monitoring of other recurring performance data.
Contrast: Histogram — a control chart shows process behavior over time against control limits; a histogram shows the frequency distribution of values.
Example: A testing team tracks weekly defect escape rates on a control chart to see whether product quality is remaining within expected limits.
