Figure.caption: The red point above the UCL is out of control.

Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 699

Core Meaning: The process of monitoring the status of the project to update the project costs and manage changes to the cost baseline.
Expansion: Control costs tracks actual spending against the approved cost baseline and identifies variances that may require corrective action or reforecasting. It uses performance information to determine whether the project remains within authorised funding levels. The process also supports forecasting and formal budget change control when adjustments are needed.
Contrast: Determine budget — Determine budget establishes the approved cost baseline; Control costs monitors actual cost performance against that baseline.
Example: A cloud migration project reviews actual vendor invoices against the budget and revises the forecast when infrastructure costs trend above plan.
Source: PMI Lexicon — control costs

Core Meaning: The upper and lower boundaries of acceptable variation in a process or performance result before investigation or action is indicated.
Expansion: Control limits are thresholds used in quality and performance analysis to distinguish expected variation from signals that a process may be out of control. In PMI usage they are commonly associated with control charts and statistical process control. They matter because they help project teams avoid overreacting to normal fluctuation while still identifying non-random variation that warrants investigation.
Contrast: Specification limits — control limits reflect the natural performance boundaries of a process; specification limits reflect required tolerance or acceptance criteria for the product or result.
Example: A manufacturing project tracks component thickness on a control chart and investigates the production line when measurements trend toward the upper control limit even though final units still meet specification.
Source: PMBOK Guide quality management tools and control chart terminology; PMI Lexicon — exact page citation not retrievable from current source

Core Meaning: The process of managing procurement relationships, monitoring contract performance, and making changes and corrections as needed.
Expansion: Control Procurements focuses on overseeing seller performance after a contract has been awarded. It includes tracking deliverables, reviewing invoices, managing claims, and ensuring that both buyer and seller meet contractual obligations. The process also supports change control when contract modifications are needed. It helps the project team detect and address procurement issues before they affect project objectives.
Contrast: Close Procurements — Control Procurements manages active contracts during performance; Close Procurements completes the contract after the work is finished.
Example: A project manager reviews a vendor's monthly deliverables, confirms milestone completion, and processes a contract change when the scope is adjusted.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Control Procurements

Core Meaning: The process of monitoring and recording results of executing the quality activities to assess performance and recommend necessary changes.
Expansion: Control Quality checks deliverables and work results against quality requirements and standards. It uses inspection, testing, and related verification methods to identify defects, measure compliance, and support corrective action. PMI treats it as a monitoring and controlling process focused on outputs and performance results rather than on designing or improving the quality management processes themselves.
Contrast: Manage Quality — Control Quality evaluates deliverables and results; Manage Quality focuses on improving and assuring the processes used to create them.
Example: A software project team executes test cases on a release candidate, records defects, and verifies that acceptance criteria are met before deployment approval.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Control Quality; exact page not available in provided results

Core Meaning: The process of monitoring the status of the project to update the project schedule and manage changes to the schedule baseline.
Expansion: Control schedule compares actual schedule performance with the approved schedule baseline and identifies variances that may require action. It supports forecasting, corrective action, and formal change control when the project is trending away from plan. The process continues throughout delivery and is central to schedule management.
Contrast: Develop schedule — Develop schedule creates the schedule model; Control schedule monitors performance and manages changes to the approved schedule baseline.
Example: A product launch team identifies a two-week testing delay, analyses the impact on downstream milestones, and updates the forecast through schedule control.
Source: PMI Lexicon — control schedule

Core Meaning: The process of monitoring the status of the project and product scope and managing changes to the scope baseline.
Expansion: Control scope keeps the project aligned with the approved scope baseline by tracking scope status and processing proposed scope changes through formal control. It helps prevent uncontrolled expansion of work and preserves baseline integrity. The process supports comparison between actual scope performance and what was authorised.
Contrast: Validate scope — Control scope manages scope changes and baseline integrity; Validate scope focuses on formal acceptance of completed deliverables.
Example: A facilities project receives a request for an additional conference room and routes it through integrated change control before changing the approved scope baseline.
Source: PMI Lexicon — control scope

Core Meaning: A specified value for an indicator such as schedule, cost, quality, or risk that, when reached or exceeded, triggers review or management action.
Expansion: A control threshold sets the point at which normal variation is no longer treated as acceptable and a response is required. It supports objective monitoring by defining in advance when a variance, condition, or trend must be escalated or corrected. In PMI practice, thresholds are used to make control decisions consistent across project work.
Contrast: Tolerance — a control threshold is the trigger point for action, while tolerance is the permitted range of variation within acceptable limits.
Example: A project sets a schedule control threshold of 10 percent slippage on any critical-path milestone, so crossing that limit requires escalation and recovery planning.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / Process groups practice context; exact page citation not confirmed

Core Meaning: The act of measuring actual performance, comparing it with plan, and taking action to keep project work aligned with objectives.
Expansion: Controlling is the management activity that checks whether project work is performing as intended and responds when it is not. It includes tracking results against baselines, thresholds, forecasts, and expected outcomes, then deciding on corrective or preventive action. In PMI practice, controlling is continuous and tightly linked with execution rather than being a separate end-of-phase review.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Controlling is the ongoing discipline of assessing performance against what was approved or expected. It uses measures such as variance, trends, forecasts, thresholds, and exception signals to determine whether intervention is needed.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects operate under uncertainty, so plans alone do not keep work on course. Controlling matters because it enables timely response across scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk, procurement, and stakeholder performance before deviations become larger failures.

The most common misconception: that controlling means rigid command-and-control behavior or micromanagement. In PMI terms, it is better understood as evidence-based oversight that supports adaptation, accountability, and informed decision-making.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager reviews milestone status, cost performance, defect trends, and risk triggers, then approves corrective actions, raises change requests, or escalates issues when results move outside agreed limits.
Contrast: Executing — executing performs the project work, while controlling evaluates that work against plan and drives adjustments when needed.
Example: During weekly governance reviews, a project manager compares actual burn rate and milestone completion against the plan and redirects resources when delivery drifts behind target.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 1.2.4 and Process Group context; exact page citation not confirmed

Core Meaning: A thinking approach that evaluates alternatives and narrows them toward the most suitable answer or decision.
Expansion: Convergent thinking is used after options have been generated and a team needs to assess, compare, and select among them. It applies criteria, constraints, and judgment to reduce many possibilities to one preferred solution or a short list of viable choices. In project work, it commonly supports decision making, problem solving, and prioritisation.
Contrast: Divergent thinking — convergent thinking narrows options toward selection, while divergent thinking expands the range of possible ideas.
Example: After a workshop produces several solution concepts for reducing cycle time, the team scores them against cost, feasibility, and impact and selects one for implementation.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms / exact page citation not confirmed

Core Meaning: The alignment of people, activities, resources, and information so interdependent project work proceeds coherently.
Expansion: Coordination ensures that related tasks, handoffs, and decisions occur in the right sequence across roles or teams. It is especially important where dependencies, shared resources, or multiple stakeholders create the risk of delay or conflict. In PMI practice, coordination is a practical aspect of integration and delivery management rather than a standalone method.
Contrast: Communication — coordination aligns actions and dependencies, while communication is the exchange of information that helps enable that alignment.
Example: A project manager coordinates design, procurement, installation, and testing teams so site access, materials delivery, and readiness reviews happen in the required order.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms / exact page citation not confirmed

Core Meaning: An intentional activity that realigns the performance of the project work with the project management plan.
Expansion: Corrective action is taken when project work has deviated from the approved plan and needs to be brought back into alignment. It addresses an existing gap in performance rather than a hypothetical future problem. Typical actions include reallocating resources, changing sequencing, revising methods, or taking other steps to recover expected results.
Contrast: Preventive action — corrective action addresses an existing variance or misalignment; preventive action reduces the likelihood of a future variance.
Example: After integration testing falls behind schedule, the project manager assigns additional testers and adjusts task sequencing to recover the planned finish date.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 699

Core Meaning: A statistical relationship in which two variables tend to change together.
Expansion: Correlation measures the strength and direction of association between variables, helping analysts spot relationships in performance or risk data. A key caution is that correlation does not establish causation; a third factor may drive both.
Contrast: Causation — correlation shows two things move together; causation shows one actually produces the other.
Example: Analysis shows schedule slippage correlates with the number of open change requests.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; defined for glossary completeness

Core Meaning: A statistical technique used to evaluate the degree to which two variables change together in a project context.
Expansion: In project management, correlation analysis helps examine whether changes in one variable are associated with changes in another, such as scope change frequency and schedule variance. It is used within quantitative analysis to support forecasting, risk analysis, and performance interpretation. A correlation indicates association, not proof that one factor causes the other.
Contrast: Regression analysis — correlation analysis measures the strength and direction of association, while regression analysis models how one variable predicts another.
Example: A project controls analyst tests whether rising defect counts are associated with increased rework effort across monthly reporting periods.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The approved version of the time-phased project budget, excluding any management reserves, that is used as a basis for comparison to actual results.
Expansion: The cost baseline is the authorised budget framework against which project cost performance is measured. It distributes planned costs over time so the team can compare what was supposed to be spent with what was actually spent. PMI treats it as a controlled baseline that may be changed only through formal change control.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The cost baseline is the approved, time-phased version of the project budget used for performance measurement. It provides the financial reference point for tracking actual costs, variances, and trends over the life of the project.

Why it matters and where it applies: without a stable cost baseline, the team cannot meaningfully control spending or evaluate cost performance. It is central to cost control, earned value analysis, forecasting, and financial reporting.

The most common misconception: that the cost baseline is identical to the total project budget. The distinction matters because the total funding picture can include management reserve, while the cost baseline used for performance measurement excludes it.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager compares monthly actual expenditures and earned value results against the approved phased baseline, then raises formal changes if the authorised spending plan itself must be revised.
Contrast: Project budget — the cost baseline is the approved time-phased basis for measuring cost performance; the broader project budget can include additional reserve amounts beyond the baseline.
Example: An ERP implementation approves a phased cost baseline for design, build, testing, and deployment, then tracks actual spend against each phase over time.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 699

Core Meaning: An analysis technique that compares expected project costs with anticipated benefits to support selection and justification decisions.
Expansion: Cost benefit analysis is used to determine whether the expected value of a proposed project or change justifies the resources required to deliver it. In PMI practice, it commonly appears in business-case thinking, project selection, and evaluation of response options by comparing financial and nonfinancial benefits against implementation and operating costs. It supports decision making but does not replace strategic judgment, risk consideration, or benefit realisation planning.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Cost benefit analysis is a structured comparison of what an initiative is expected to cost and what value it is expected to produce. The value side may include revenue, savings, risk reduction, compliance, service improvement, or strategic benefit.

Why it matters and where it applies: organisations use it when selecting projects, evaluating alternatives, approving changes, or assessing response strategies. It helps decision makers allocate limited resources to work that is expected to produce the greatest overall benefit.

The most common misconception: that it is only a finance exercise based on immediate cash return. In practice, project teams often include qualitative and longer-term benefits as well, while still recognising uncertainty and assumptions.

How it shows up in real projects: a sponsor compares several automation options, estimates implementation cost and expected operational savings, and uses the analysis to justify which option moves forward.
Contrast: Benefit-cost ratio — cost benefit analysis is the broader evaluation process; a benefit-cost ratio is one metric that may be produced from that analysis.
Example: A PMO evaluates whether replacing a manual reporting process with a dashboard platform is justified by reduced labour time, fewer reporting errors, and faster decision making.
Source: PMBOK Guide / not page-confirmed from available source text

Core Meaning: The process of aggregating the estimated costs of individual activities or work packages to establish an authorized cost baseline.
Expansion: Cost budgeting turns detailed estimates into an approved project budget structure. It aligns planned spending with scope, schedule, reserves, and control accounts so performance can be measured over time. In practice, it connects estimating with baseline approval and funding visibility.
Contrast: Cost estimate — a cost estimate predicts the likely cost of work; cost budgeting aggregates approved estimates into the authorized baseline for control.
Example: After estimating site preparation, equipment installation, testing, and training, a construction team rolls the figures up into a phased budget for executive approval.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Version 4.0, page 7

Core Meaning: The process of monitoring project cost performance and managing changes to the cost baseline.
Expansion: Cost control is the project management work of tracking actual spending, comparing results against the approved cost baseline, and taking action when variances or trends emerge. It includes influencing factors that create cost changes, ensuring approved changes are reflected in plans, and keeping stakeholders informed about performance. In PMI practice, it is central to maintaining financial discipline throughout execution and closing.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Cost control is the ongoing management process used to measure, understand, and respond to project cost performance. It combines performance data, forecasting, change control, and corrective action to keep spending aligned with approved expectations.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects rarely unfold exactly as planned, so costs shift as scope, risks, resource rates, and productivity change. Effective cost control helps managers detect issues early, protect funding, and support informed trade-off decisions across schedule, scope, quality, and risk.

The most common misconception: that cost control means simply cutting spending. In practice, it is about disciplined visibility and decision making, which may lead to reducing costs, increasing investment in justified areas, or formally updating baselines.

How it shows up in real projects: teams compare earned value data to the cost baseline, investigate cost variances, approve or reject cost-related change requests, and revise forecasts such as estimate at completion.
Contrast: Cost estimating — cost estimating predicts likely costs before or during planning; cost control monitors and manages cost performance against the approved baseline.
Example: On a construction project, the project manager detects a negative cost variance in structural steel work, investigates supplier rate changes, and raises a change request to address the forecast overrun.
Source: PMBOK Guide / not page-confirmed from available source text

Core Meaning: An approximation of the monetary resources needed to complete project work.
Expansion: A cost estimate predicts how much money will be needed for activities, work packages, or the full project. It is developed using available information, assumptions, and estimating methods appropriate to the level of detail known at the time. Cost estimates support budgeting, funding decisions, and cost control. They may be refined as the project progresses and more information becomes available.
Contrast: Cost baseline — A cost estimate predicts likely cost; a cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget used for control.
Example: Before procurement begins, a project team estimates the cost of hardware, installation, and testing for a new data platform.
Source: Practice Standard for Project Estimating — Cost estimate

Core Meaning: A knowledge area or discipline concerned with planning, estimating, budgeting, financing, funding, managing, and controlling costs so the project can be completed within the approved budget.
Expansion: Cost management covers the full set of activities used to define, organize, authorize, and control project spending. It begins with how costs will be managed, continues through estimating and budgeting, and extends into performance monitoring during delivery. PMI frames it as essential to financial predictability and informed decision-making across the project life cycle.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Cost management is the discipline that governs how a project plans and controls money. It includes the methods, baselines, thresholds, and reporting practices that keep financial performance visible and actionable.

Why it matters and where it applies: every project operates under resource constraints, and weak cost management can undermine scope, schedule, quality, and stakeholder confidence. It applies in predictive, adaptive, and hybrid settings, though the techniques and level of detail may differ.

The most common misconception: that cost management is only accounting or budget tracking after work starts. In practice it begins early with estimation logic, reserve decisions, and the design of a realistic baseline against which performance can be measured.

How it shows up in real projects: teams prepare estimates, establish a phased budget, define variance thresholds, review earned value or other performance signals, and take corrective action when spending trends threaten approved limits.
Contrast: Financial management — cost management focuses on the project-level planning and control of spending; financial management is broader and covers organizational funding, accounting, and financial governance.
Example: A product-development project defines estimating methods, sets a cost baseline by release, tracks monthly variances, and uses reserve analysis when supplier prices rise.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition, page 235

Core Meaning: A component of the project or program management plan that describes how costs will be planned, structured, and controlled.
Expansion: The cost management plan sets the rules for estimating, budgeting, managing, and controlling project costs. It defines matters such as units of measure, levels of precision, control thresholds, and reporting formats. It gives the team a consistent basis for cost decisions and performance monitoring.
Contrast: Cost baseline — the cost management plan defines how cost work will be performed and governed; the cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget used for measurement.
Example: On a factory-upgrade project, the team documents that estimates will be produced in labor hours and local currency, tracked monthly, and escalated if variance exceeds the defined threshold.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Seventh Edition, page 241

Core Meaning: All costs incurred over the life of the product by investment in preventing nonconformance to requirements, appraising the product or service for conformance, and failing to meet requirements.
Expansion: Cost of quality is the total cost view of achieving and not achieving quality. It includes the cost of prevention and appraisal as well as the cost of internal and external failure. PMI uses the concept to show that spending appropriately on quality upfront can reduce much larger downstream costs caused by defects, rework, and dissatisfied customers.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Cost of quality is a framework for understanding the full economic impact of quality-related decisions. It includes money spent to prevent defects, evaluate conformance, and deal with failures when requirements are not met.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects often underestimate the hidden cost of poor quality, especially when rework, delays, warranty exposure, or operational disruption occur later. This concept matters in planning, quality management, procurement, and decision-making about how much to invest in prevention versus correction.

The most common misconception: that quality always costs more. In practice, poor quality is often far more expensive, and thoughtful prevention and appraisal frequently lower total cost over the life of the product or deliverable.

How it shows up in real projects: teams invest in reviews, training, testing, standards, and process discipline to reduce defects before release. When they do not, the result is often rework, schedule slippage, post-delivery fixes, and stakeholder dissatisfaction that cost more than the original preventive effort.
Contrast: Quality control — cost of quality is the full cost framework around achieving and missing quality; quality control is the activity of checking outputs for conformance.
Example: A software programme funds automated regression testing and code review because fixing defects before release is far cheaper than patching them in production.

Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, section 8.1.2.3, page 282

Core Meaning: A measure of the cost efficiency of budgeted resources, expressed as the ratio of earned value to actual cost.
Expansion: Cost Performance Index, or CPI, shows how efficiently the project is using cost relative to the value of work completed. It is one of the central earned value metrics for monitoring cost performance and forecasting future financial outcomes. A CPI greater than 1.0 indicates better cost efficiency than planned, while a value below 1.0 indicates poorer cost efficiency.
Contrast: Cost variance — Cost Performance Index is a ratio of efficiency; cost variance is the numerical difference between earned value and actual cost.
Example: A project that has earned 90 units of value while spending 100 units of cost has a CPI of 0.9, signaling cost inefficiency.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 700

Core Meaning: A cost-reimbursable contract in which the buyer pays the seller for allowable actual costs plus an award fee based on the buyer's evaluation of performance.
Expansion: A CPAF contract reimburses the seller for legitimate actual costs incurred and adds an award fee that represents profit. Unlike a purely formula-based incentive, the award depends on how the buyer assesses the seller's performance against stated criteria. It is used when the buyer wants flexibility in the work and also wants to motivate strong overall performance.
Contrast: Cost Plus Incentive Fee Contract (CPIF) — CPAF uses a buyer-evaluated award fee, while CPIF ties fee changes to predefined performance formulas.
Example: A public-sector engineering project reimburses a contractor's allowable design costs and then grants an additional award fee based on quality, collaboration, and schedule responsiveness.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Version 4.0, page 6

Core Meaning: A contract type in which the buyer reimburses the seller for allowable costs and pays an agreed fee.
Expansion: A cost plus contract is used when the work cannot be defined precisely enough to support a fixed-price arrangement. The buyer pays allowable actual costs and a fee that may be fixed, incentive-based, or otherwise structured under the contract terms. In PMI usage, it sits within the cost-reimbursable family and is chosen when scope uncertainty is high and flexibility is needed.
Contrast: Fixed-price contract — a cost plus contract reimburses allowable costs plus fee, while a fixed-price contract sets one agreed total price for the work.
Example: A pharmaceutical development project hires a specialist research firm under a cost plus contract because the number of test cycles and analysis hours cannot be estimated reliably at the start.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms / A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition — contract types under procurement, exact page citation not confirmed

Core Meaning: A cost-reimbursable contract in which the buyer reimburses allowable costs and pays a fixed fee as profit.
Expansion: A CPFF contract reimburses the seller for allowable actual costs of the work and adds a fixed fee. That fee does not change because of the seller's actual cost performance unless the contract scope itself changes. It is useful when the work cannot be fully defined at the start but the parties want the profit arrangement to remain stable.
Contrast: Cost Plus Incentive Fee Contract (CPIF) — CPFF pays a fixed fee, while CPIF allows the fee to vary based on defined performance outcomes.
Example: A research project hires a specialist contractor under a CPFF contract to explore a new material, reimbursing approved costs and paying a fixed fee for the effort.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Version 4.0, page 6

Core Meaning: A technique used to assess how uncertainty may affect project cost outcomes and overall budget exposure.
Expansion: Cost risk analysis examines the likelihood and impact of cost-related uncertainties on a project or programme. It is commonly used to estimate contingency needs, test the range of possible cost outcomes, and improve confidence in funding decisions. In PMI usage, it is treated as a risk-analysis practice rather than a formal core glossary term.
Contrast: Quantitative risk analysis — cost risk analysis focuses specifically on cost uncertainty, while quantitative risk analysis may address broader project objectives such as schedule, scope, or overall performance.
Example: Before approving a construction budget, the team models the possible effect of material-price volatility and productivity variation to estimate the probability of exceeding the approved cost baseline.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The amount of budget deficit or surplus at a given point in time, expressed as the difference between earned value and actual cost.
Expansion: Cost variance shows whether the project is spending more or less than the value of work completed. A positive variance indicates favorable cost performance, while a negative variance indicates an unfavorable cost condition. It is one of the primary earned value metrics used to monitor cost control. Because it is a point-in-time measure, it should be interpreted alongside trends and forecasts.
Contrast: Cost Performance Index — Cost variance is the numerical difference between earned value and actual cost; Cost Performance Index is the ratio of earned value to actual cost.
Example: If a project has earned value of 120 and actual cost of 100, the cost variance is 20, showing favorable cost performance.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Cost variance

Core Meaning: A reimbursable contract in which the buyer pays the seller allowable costs and an additional fee tied to agreed performance targets.
Expansion: A cost-plus-incentive-fee contract is a cost-reimbursable agreement that pays the seller for legitimate incurred costs plus an incentive fee based on performance against specified objectives. PMI presents it as one of the main contract types used when the work cannot be estimated precisely enough for a fixed-price arrangement. It is intended to align seller behaviour with buyer priorities such as cost control, schedule achievement, or technical performance while preserving flexibility for uncertain work.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A cost-plus-incentive-fee contract reimburses allowable seller costs and adds a fee that varies according to an agreed incentive formula. The formula usually shares savings or overruns between buyer and seller and defines how performance affects final compensation.

Why it matters and where it applies: it is useful when scope or technical uncertainty makes precise pricing difficult, but the buyer still wants to motivate efficient performance. It commonly appears in research, engineering, defence, and complex development efforts where uncertainty is high and collaboration is necessary.

The most common misconception: that this contract simply pays whatever the seller spends. In practice, the incentive arrangement is designed to influence behaviour, and the contract sets rules for allowable costs, target cost, target fee, and sharing ratios.

How it shows up in real projects: a buyer funds prototype development for a complex system and agrees that if the seller finishes below target cost, both parties share the savings, while overruns reduce the seller's earned fee according to the contract formula.
Contrast: Cost-plus-fixed-fee contract — a cost-plus-incentive-fee contract varies the seller's fee based on performance, while a cost-plus-fixed-fee contract pays a set fee regardless of actual performance against incentive targets.
Example: A public-sector agency hires a contractor to develop a new radar prototype under a target-cost arrangement where underruns increase the contractor's fee and overruns reduce it based on a negotiated sharing ratio.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition Glossary — cost-plus-incentive-fee contract

Core Meaning: A contract type that involves payment to the seller for all legitimate actual costs incurred for completed work, plus a fee representing seller profit.
Expansion: A cost-reimbursable contract is used when work scope is uncertain or cannot be estimated precisely enough for a fixed-price arrangement. The buyer reimburses allowable costs and pays an additional fee according to the contract terms. This form increases flexibility but requires closer oversight of cost control and contract administration.
Contrast: Fixed-price contract — a cost-reimbursable contract repays allowable actual costs plus fee, while a fixed-price contract sets a defined price for the specified work.
Example: A research project hires a specialist laboratory under a cost-reimbursable contract because the exact testing effort cannot be known at the start.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Version 4.0, page 8

Core Meaning: A schedule compression technique used to shorten the schedule duration for the least incremental cost by adding resources.
Expansion: Crashing reduces planned duration by applying additional resources to activities, but only where that action can realistically shorten the work. It is typically considered when the project must meet a required finish date or recover from delay on critical-path work. Because it usually increases cost and can raise coordination risk, it must be evaluated carefully rather than applied broadly.
Contrast: Fast tracking — crashing shortens duration by adding resources or cost to activities; fast tracking shortens duration by overlapping activities that were originally planned in sequence.
Example: A software release nearing a fixed compliance deadline adds experienced testers to critical-path system testing to shorten the test window and protect the launch date.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 700

Core Meaning: The process of subdividing project deliverables and project work into smaller, more manageable components.
Expansion: Create WBS decomposes the approved project scope into a hierarchical structure of deliverables and work packages. It is foundational because it turns scope into something the team can estimate, assign, monitor, and control with precision. PMI treats it as a core scope management process that helps define the full work of the project and supports the scope baseline.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Create WBS is the scope decomposition process that breaks large deliverables and project work into smaller components until the team reaches manageable work packages. The result is not just a list of tasks but a structured representation of the total project scope.

Why it matters and where it applies: it gives the team a common frame for planning schedule, cost, resources, accountability, and control. It applies across predictive work especially strongly, but the core idea of decomposition also appears in adaptive planning when teams break work into manageable pieces.

The most common misconception: that a WBS is simply a task list or a schedule. In PMI usage, the WBS is scope-focused and deliverable-oriented, while the schedule sequences activities over time.

How it shows up in real projects: teams decompose major deliverables into subdeliverables and work packages, assign ownership, estimate effort and cost at the work-package level, and use the resulting structure as a basis for scope baseline, reporting, and change control.
Contrast: Define Scope — Create WBS decomposes approved scope into hierarchical components; Define Scope develops the detailed description of what the project and product include.
Example: In a hospital construction project, the team decomposes the approved scope into foundations, structure, mechanical systems, interior fit-out, medical equipment installation, and commissioning work packages.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Create WBS; exact page not available in provided results

Core Meaning: Measures or standards used as a basis for decision making or evaluation in project work.
Expansion: Criteria are the specific standards, rules, or measures used to judge options, performance, or results. In project management they appear in areas such as selection, acceptance, quality, and procurement decisions.
Contrast: Requirements — criteria are the standards used to evaluate or decide, while requirements describe conditions or capabilities that must be met.
Example: A project team uses vendor-selection criteria such as experience, delivery capability, and technical fit to evaluate procurement proposals.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Version 4.0, page 5

Core Meaning: An activity with zero float that, if delayed, delays the project finish date.
Expansion: A critical activity is an activity on the critical path and therefore has no scheduling flexibility without affecting the planned completion date. PMI uses the concept to identify the work that directly drives schedule duration and requires close monitoring. As logic, durations, or constraints change, the set of critical activities can also change.
Contrast: Critical path — a critical activity is a single activity on the path; the critical path is the full sequence of activities that determines project duration.
Example: In an office relocation project, final network cutover is a critical activity because any delay to it pushes the go-live and occupancy dates.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Critical activity; exact page not available in provided results

Core Meaning: A scheduling method that plans work around resource constraints and protects delivery dates with strategically placed buffers.
Expansion: Critical Chain Method is a schedule-development and control approach derived from the theory of constraints. Instead of focusing only on logical task dependencies, it also accounts for limited resource availability and uses project and feeding buffers to protect the completion date. It is most useful where shared resources and multitasking significantly affect schedule performance.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Critical Chain Method identifies the longest chain of dependent work after considering both task logic and resource constraints. It then protects that chain with buffers rather than padding every activity.

Why it matters and where it applies: many project schedules look feasible on paper until scarce resources are assigned across multiple activities. Critical Chain helps in engineering, product development, and other resource-constrained environments by making schedule risk visible and manageable.

The most common misconception: that it is just the critical path method with extra contingency. In practice, it changes both how the schedule is built and how protection is managed by centralising uncertainty into explicit buffers.

How it shows up in real projects: teams sequence work around key specialists, limit multitasking, monitor buffer consumption, and escalate recovery actions when buffer burn suggests the delivery date is under threat.
Contrast: Critical Path Method — Critical Chain Method accounts explicitly for resource constraints and uses buffers, while Critical Path Method focuses primarily on logical dependencies and float.
Example: A hardware development project reorganises its schedule around a small number of specialist test engineers, adds feeding buffers before the critical chain, and tracks buffer consumption each week to protect the launch date.

Core Meaning: A scheduling method that focuses on resource constraints and protects delivery dates by placing buffers around the work that governs project completion.
Expansion: Critical chain project management modifies schedule planning by considering both task dependencies and resource availability, not just logical sequence. It typically uses feeding buffers and a project buffer to absorb uncertainty and protect the committed finish date. The method is especially relevant where shared resources, multitasking, and optimistic task estimates create schedule instability.
Contrast: Critical path method — critical path focuses on the longest path of dependent activities based mainly on logic and duration, while critical chain also incorporates resource constraints and buffer management.
Example: An engineering project with a small pool of specialist designers sequences work around resource availability and uses a project buffer to protect the final milestone.

Core Meaning: The sequence of activities that represents the longest path through a project and determines the shortest possible project duration.
Expansion: Critical path identifies the activities that directly drive the planned finish date of the project. When one of those activities slips and nothing else changes, the overall project completion date is likely to slip as well. PMI uses it as a core concept in schedule analysis, schedule control, and schedule compression.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The critical path is the longest-duration path through the schedule network, based on logic and activity durations. It indicates the earliest possible completion date under the current plan.

Why it matters and where it applies: it helps the team focus attention on the work that most affects delivery timing. It is widely used in predictive scheduling, schedule risk analysis, and decisions about crashing or fast tracking.

The most common misconception: that the critical path is permanent or that only one critical path can exist. In practice it can shift as progress, dependencies, estimates, or constraints change, and some schedules can contain multiple near-critical or critical paths.

How it shows up in real projects: if system testing sits on the critical path and begins to slip, the project manager may add test resources, overlap successor work where acceptable, or revise sequencing to protect the go-live date.
Contrast: Float — critical path identifies the activity sequence that drives project duration; float is the amount of scheduling flexibility available before delay affects a successor or finish date.
Example: In a facility upgrade, procurement of long-lead equipment, installation, commissioning, and final inspection form the critical path because delay in any of them delays handover.

Core Meaning: A method used to estimate the minimum project duration and determine the amount of scheduling flexibility on the logical network paths within the schedule model.
Expansion: Critical path method is a schedule analysis method that calculates the longest path of dependent activities through a project network and identifies the shortest possible duration for the project. PMI uses it to determine which activities have zero or very little total float, making them the primary drivers of finish-date performance. It is central to predictive scheduling and remains useful even when projects use rolling wave planning or mixed delivery approaches. The method supports schedule compression, forecasting, and impact analysis when delays or changes occur.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Critical path method, often shortened to CPM, analyses activity durations, dependencies, and network logic to find the path that governs the project's earliest possible completion. Activities on the critical path cannot slip without affecting the planned finish date unless corrective action is taken elsewhere.

Why it matters and where it applies: it helps project managers focus attention on the work that most directly controls delivery timing. It is most visible in predictive schedules for construction, engineering, infrastructure, product launches, and other projects where sequence logic and finish-date commitments matter.

The most common misconception: that the critical path is simply the list of longest-duration activities or that it never changes. In practice it is determined by network logic, constraints, leads and lags, and actual progress, so the critical path can shift during execution.

How it shows up in real projects: teams use CPM to build baseline schedules, identify near-critical work, test what-if scenarios, and decide whether crashing or fast tracking is needed after a delay to preserve milestone dates.
Contrast: Critical chain method — CPM focuses on the longest logical path and float in the schedule network; critical chain method also accounts for resource constraints and uses buffers.
Example: On a hospital expansion project, excavation, foundation, steel erection, and inspection activities form the critical path, so a delay in steel delivery pushes the planned opening date unless the team compresses later work.
