Source: PMBOK Guide — decision tree analysis term; exact page citation requires source verification

Core Meaning: To subdivide project scope or deliverables into smaller and more manageable components.
Expansion: Decompose is the action used to break larger work into parts that can be understood, planned, estimated, and assigned more reliably. It is most commonly used when building the work breakdown structure.
Contrast: Decomposition — Decompose is the verb or action; decomposition is the technique or resulting breakdown.
Example: A team decomposes a training rollout deliverable into course design, content review, pilot delivery, and final deployment work packages.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Glossary, decompose, p. 698

Core Meaning: A technique used to divide and subdivide the project scope and project deliverables into smaller, more manageable parts.
Expansion: Decomposition is a core planning technique that turns broad scope into work that can be estimated, scheduled, owned, and controlled. In PMI usage, it is central to creating a work breakdown structure and can also support activity definition, risk analysis, and other planning work. The goal is not to create unnecessary detail, but to reach a level where planning and accountability become practical.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Decomposition is the disciplined breakdown of higher-level scope into progressively smaller components. It converts a complex result into manageable pieces such as deliverables, subdeliverables, and work packages.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects fail when scope is too vague to estimate, assign, or track. Decomposition improves clarity, supports resource planning, and creates the structure needed for scheduling, costing, and control across predictive, adaptive, and hybrid work.

The most common misconception: that more detail is always better. In practice, over-decomposition creates administrative burden, while under-decomposition leaves work too ambiguous to manage well.

How it shows up in real projects: a project team starts with a major deliverable, breaks it into component outputs, then defines work packages that can be owned, estimated, and monitored against the scope baseline.
Contrast: Rolling wave planning — Decomposition breaks scope into smaller components; rolling wave planning decides how much detail to plan now versus later.
Example: In an ERP implementation, the team decomposes the finance module into requirements, configuration, integration, testing, training, and deployment work packages.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Glossary, decomposition, p. 699

Core Meaning: An imperfection or deficiency in a project component or deliverable such that it does not meet its requirements or specifications.
Expansion: A defect is a specific form of nonconformance in which the delivered result fails to satisfy stated or expected quality criteria. PMI uses the term in quality management to distinguish an actual flaw in the product, component, or output from broader issues in process performance. Defects are typically identified through inspection, testing, review, or use and may lead to rework or defect repair.
Contrast: Nonconformance — a defect is a particular flaw in a deliverable or component; nonconformance is the broader condition of not meeting a requirement.
Example: During user acceptance testing, the team finds that a reporting screen displays incorrect totals when filters are applied, so the issue is logged as a defect.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Defect

Core Meaning: The examination of defects to identify patterns, causes, and corrective actions.
Expansion: Defect analysis looks at defect data to understand what is failing, why it is failing, and how to reduce recurrence. In PMI practice, it supports quality control, root cause analysis, and process improvement. It is commonly used when teams need to move from isolated defect fixes to systemic prevention.
Contrast: Defect repair — defect analysis investigates the defect and its cause; defect repair corrects the defect itself.
Example: After repeated test failures, the team analyzes defect logs and finds that most issues come from unclear interface requirements.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Activities that keep errors from being introduced into deliverables or processes rather than finding them after they occur.
Expansion: In PMI-aligned practice, defect prevention focuses on building quality into the work through sound processes, standards, training, and early verification. It is part of a prevention-oriented quality mindset in which the team reduces the likelihood of rework, waste, and downstream failure. It is especially relevant in planning and managing quality where the cost of preventing errors is often lower than the cost of correcting them later.
Contrast: Inspection — defect prevention aims to stop defects from occurring; inspection detects defects after work has already been performed.
Example: A project team introduces peer reviews, coding standards, and automated test checks before integration so common errors are avoided instead of discovered during user acceptance testing.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The action taken to correct a defect in a component or deliverable.
Expansion: Defect repair is the work performed to fix a discovered nonconformance so the product can meet requirements or quality expectations. It may involve rework, replacement, correction, or another approved remedy depending on the nature of the defect. In PMI usage, it is a specific response to a quality problem rather than a general performance adjustment.
Contrast: Corrective action — defect repair fixes a specific product defect; corrective action more broadly addresses the cause of a variance or nonconformance.
Example: During system testing, the team finds an error in tax calculations and updates the software so invoices are computed correctly before release.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Defect repair

Core Meaning: The process of identifying and documenting the specific actions to be performed to produce the project deliverables.
Expansion: Define Activities breaks work packages and planning components into the discrete activities needed to create the planned outputs. It makes the schedule actionable by identifying what work must actually be performed rather than stopping at high-level scope statements. This process creates the activity list and related attributes used in sequencing, estimating, and scheduling.
Contrast: Create WBS — Create WBS decomposes scope into deliverables and work packages; Define Activities identifies the actions required to produce those deliverables.
Example: For an office relocation project, the team defines activities such as survey the new site, procure furniture, install network cabling, move equipment, and complete occupancy testing.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition — Define Activities, page 183

Core Meaning: The process of developing a detailed description of the project and product.
Expansion: Define Scope turns approved requirements and high-level intent into a clear statement of what the project includes and excludes. PMI positions it as the process that establishes the boundaries of the project, the major deliverables, assumptions, and constraints that will guide downstream planning and control. It matters because teams cannot manage change, verify completion, or control expectations without a shared scope baseline. In practice, it is where ambiguity is reduced enough for reliable planning to begin.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Define Scope develops the project scope statement from charter information, requirements documentation, and other early planning inputs. It describes the work to be done at a level detailed enough to support later decomposition, validation, and control.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because scope clarity is the basis for schedule, cost, quality, resource, and stakeholder planning. It applies across predictive, adaptive, and hybrid work, although the level of detail and timing of elaboration can vary.

The most common misconception: that scope is just a list of requested features or tasks. In PMI terms, scope also includes boundaries, exclusions, assumptions, acceptance thinking, and the shared understanding of what the project is not intended to deliver.

How it shows up in real projects: a team defining scope for a customer portal states that the release includes registration, profile updates, and billing history, excludes payment processing integration in phase one, and records the constraint that the solution must use the existing identity platform.
Contrast: Collect Requirements — Collect Requirements captures stakeholder needs and expectations; Define Scope synthesizes those inputs into the formal project and product scope description.
Example: In a warehouse automation project, the team defines scope to include barcode scanning, conveyor controls, and reporting dashboards while explicitly excluding redesign of the enterprise ERP.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition — Define Scope, page 150

Core Meaning: A formal shared description of the state a product increment must meet to be considered complete.
Expansion: Definition of Done sets the quality and completion conditions that an increment, feature, or work item must satisfy before the team treats it as finished. It creates a common understanding across the team and stakeholders about what complete means, reducing ambiguity and hidden rework. In agile delivery, it often includes coding, testing, integration, documentation, compliance, and approval expectations.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Definition of Done is a working agreement that establishes the minimum conditions for completion. It gives teams a consistent completion standard so that items counted as done are genuinely usable and not partially finished.

Why it matters and where it applies: it improves transparency, predictability, and quality in iterative delivery. It is especially important in agile and hybrid environments where teams produce frequent increments and need a reliable basis for review, release, and progress measurement.

The most common misconception: that done simply means development work is finished. In practice, done usually includes broader expectations such as testing, integration, documentation, defect thresholds, and any other conditions needed for the increment to be considered complete.

How it shows up in real projects: teams post the Definition of Done visibly, use it during iteration planning and reviews, and check every backlog item against it before accepting it into an increment or release.
Contrast: Acceptance criteria — Definition of Done sets the common completion standard for all items or increments; acceptance criteria define the specific conditions for an individual requirement or backlog item.
Example: A Scrum team defines done as coded, peer reviewed, unit tested, integrated, security checked, documented, and accepted by the product owner before a story can count toward the sprint increment.
Source: Agile Practice Guide — Definition of Done

Core Meaning: A team's checklist for a user-centric requirement that has all the information the team needs to begin working on it.
Expansion: Definition of Ready is a working agreement that helps a team decide whether a backlog item is sufficiently understood and prepared for implementation. It improves flow by reducing ambiguity at the point work starts. Teams often use it in adaptive delivery to confirm that acceptance criteria, dependencies, and needed information are in place.
Contrast: Definition of Done — Definition of Ready sets the entry criteria for starting work; Definition of Done sets the completion criteria for finishing it.
Example: Before pulling a user story into an iteration, a product team checks that the story has acceptance criteria, dependencies identified, and stakeholder clarifications resolved.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Definition of Ready, p. 7

Core Meaning: The most accurate type of estimate, prepared from detailed information and used when project scope and activities are well understood.
Expansion: A definitive estimate is developed after substantial planning detail is available and is therefore expected to be more reliable than rough order of magnitude or budgetary estimates. PMI associates it with later planning stages when work packages, resource needs, and assumptions are defined with greater precision. It is commonly used to establish the performance baseline and support final approval decisions.
Contrast: Budgetary estimate — a definitive estimate is based on much more detailed and mature information and is therefore intended to be more accurate.
Example: After finalising the engineering design and procurement packages, the project team prepares a definitive cost estimate to establish the approved cost baseline.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Definitive estimate

Core Meaning: A condition in which planned work or progress occurs later than scheduled, affecting activity timing, milestones, or overall project completion.
Expansion: In project management, delay refers to slippage against the planned schedule at the activity, milestone, or project level. It may result from late decisions, resource shortages, rework, external dependencies, or unforeseen events.
Contrast: Lag — delay is an outcome or condition of lateness in the schedule; lag is an intentionally modeled waiting period between activities.
Example: A permitting approval arrives three weeks late, delaying site mobilisation and pushing the construction start milestone beyond the baseline date.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Assigning responsibility and the authority to carry out specific tasks to another person.
Expansion: Delegation lets managers distribute work to the most suitable people while retaining overall accountability. Effective delegation pairs the task with the matching authority and clear expectations, building capability across the team.
Contrast: Empowerment — delegation hands over specific tasks/authority; empowerment is broad standing autonomy to decide and act.
Example: A manager delegates supplier negotiations to a senior team member while remaining accountable for the contract.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; defined for glossary completeness

Core Meaning: Any unique and verifiable product, result, or capability to perform a service that is produced to complete a process, phase, or project.
Expansion: A deliverable is a tangible or intangible output that a project, phase, or process produces and that can be reviewed and accepted against defined criteria. It may be final, such as a completed facility or deployed system, or intermediate, such as a design package or test report. PMI uses the term broadly because deliverables are the basis for scope definition, progress measurement, quality verification, and formal acceptance.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A deliverable is the output work is meant to create. It is not merely effort performed; it is a specific result that can be identified, checked, and handed over or approved.

Why it matters and where it applies: deliverables connect scope to execution. They are central in planning, scheduling, quality management, procurement, reporting, and stakeholder acceptance because they define what completion actually looks like.

The most common misconception: that any activity or milestone is itself a deliverable. An activity is work performed and a milestone is a zero-duration marker, while a deliverable is the actual output created by that work.

How it shows up in real projects: project teams decompose scope into deliverables in the WBS, assign acceptance criteria, track completion by deliverable status, and obtain sign-off when each required output is produced and verified.
Contrast: Milestone — a deliverable is an output or result; a milestone is a scheduling marker that indicates a significant point or event.
Example: In an ERP implementation, approved process maps, configured modules, training materials, and the live production deployment are all project deliverables.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / PMI Lexicon — Deliverable

Core Meaning: The production and handoff of project outputs so intended value can be realised by customers or other stakeholders.
Expansion: In PMI usage, delivery refers to the work of creating and providing outputs, outcomes, or benefits through a project, program, or product-related effort. It is the practical expression of planned work becoming usable results. The term often appears in phrases such as delivery approach, delivery performance, and value delivery rather than as a tightly bounded standalone glossary term.
Contrast: Product development life cycle — delivery is the provision of outputs or value; the product development life cycle is the broader sequence of phases a product passes through.
Example: A project team completes testing, deploys a new payroll module, and releases it to the business for operational use.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The development, cadence, and management method used to produce and deliver a project's outputs or outcomes.
Expansion: In PMI usage, delivery approach describes how work is structured and delivered, commonly along a predictive, adaptive, or hybrid continuum. It shapes planning detail, stakeholder engagement, risk response, governance rhythm, and how value is reviewed during execution. Choosing the right delivery approach is a central project design decision because it affects how the team responds to uncertainty, change, and learning.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Delivery approach is the overall way a project organises development and delivery work. It defines whether work is planned largely upfront, evolved iteratively, or managed through a combination of both.

Why it matters and where it applies: the delivery approach influences schedules, roles, controls, funding assumptions, and how often stakeholders see usable results. It applies across projects of all kinds, from construction and infrastructure to software, product, and transformation initiatives.

The most common misconception: that delivery approach is simply a synonym for methodology or that one approach is inherently superior. In practice, PMI treats the choice as context-driven, based on uncertainty, change frequency, compliance needs, and stakeholder expectations.

How it shows up in real projects: teams may use a predictive approach for fixed-scope facility construction, an adaptive approach for product development with evolving requirements, or a hybrid approach when hardware follows stage gates while software is iteratively refined.
Contrast: Development approach — delivery approach is the broader way the project delivers work and value; development approach refers more specifically to how the product, service, or result is developed.
Example: A company rolling out a new customer platform uses a hybrid delivery approach: infrastructure deployment follows a predictive sequence while customer-facing features are released iteratively based on user feedback.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Eighth Edition, Glossary, term Delivery approach

Core Meaning: The delivery performance domain addresses the activities and functions associated with delivering the scope and quality that the project was undertaken to produce.
Expansion: The delivery performance domain in PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition focuses on creating project outcomes efficiently and effectively while maintaining intended quality. It covers the flow from work execution to value delivery, including managing requirements, quality, team capability, and the fit between deliverables and intended outcomes. PMI presents it as one of the eight project performance domains that require ongoing attention throughout the project, not as a one-time phase.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The delivery performance domain is the part of project performance concerned with producing the project's outputs and enabling the desired outcomes. It links the actual work of the project to the value the project is expected to create.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects succeed only if they deliver usable results, not merely completed activities. This domain applies across predictive, adaptive, and hybrid environments because every project must turn effort into verified deliverables that meet stakeholder expectations.

The most common misconception: that delivery is just task execution or handoff at the end. In PMI's framing, delivery includes maintaining quality, aligning with requirements, and ensuring the work being produced remains relevant to the intended outcome.

How it shows up in real projects: teams validate features against acceptance criteria, monitor defects and rework, coordinate reviews with users, and adjust the way work is developed so that increments or final outputs are fit for purpose.
Contrast: Project work performance domain — the delivery performance domain focuses on producing validated deliverables and outcomes; the project work performance domain focuses more broadly on how project work is structured, managed, and improved.
Example: A hospital system implementation team releases modules in stages, tests each against clinical workflow requirements, fixes usability defects, and confirms each module supports the intended care-delivery outcome before wider rollout.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) – Seventh Edition, Section 2.4.4, pp. 33-36

Core Meaning: An information-gathering technique in which subject matter experts provide input anonymously and iteratively to reach a more reliable consensus.
Expansion: The Delphi Technique gathers judgments from experts without requiring them to debate face to face, which reduces the influence of hierarchy, reputation, and group pressure. Responses are collected, summarised, and recirculated through multiple rounds until views converge or become clearer. In projects it is commonly used for estimating, risk identification, prioritisation, and forecasting where uncertainty is high.
Contrast: Brainstorming — Delphi uses anonymous, iterative expert input to reduce bias; brainstorming is an open group idea-generation activity.
Example: For a novel product launch, the project manager uses the Delphi Technique with legal, engineering, and operations experts to estimate regulatory and implementation risks.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Glossary, Delphi Technique, p. 699

Core Meaning: A relationship in which one activity, task, or outcome relies on another for sequencing, input, or completion.
Expansion: A dependency expresses how work is connected so that one element cannot start, finish, or proceed as intended without another element occurring first or in a defined order. In project management, dependencies are critical for schedule logic, coordination across teams, and identifying constraints that affect delivery. They may be mandatory, discretionary, internal, or external depending on the nature of the relationship.
Contrast: Predecessor — a dependency is the relationship itself; a predecessor is the specific activity that comes before another in that relationship.
Example: A testing activity depends on completion of software configuration because the test team needs a stable build before execution can begin.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / PMI Lexicon — Dependency

Core Meaning: The act of identifying and defining logical relationships among project activities.
Expansion: Dependency determination establishes how activities relate to one another so that sequencing reflects real technical, external, and discretionary constraints. In PMI practice, these relationships may include mandatory, discretionary, internal, and external dependencies and are essential for building a valid schedule model. Clear dependency determination improves the realism of critical path analysis, float calculations, and schedule forecasts.
Contrast: Sequence Activities — dependency determination identifies the logical relationships themselves; Sequence Activities is the broader process that uses those relationships to order the work.
Example: When planning a data-center migration, the team determines that server configuration must finish before cutover testing can begin, while user training can run partly in parallel.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition — Sequence Activities, page 185

Core Meaning: The identification, analysis, sequencing, monitoring, and coordination of relationships in which one activity, deliverable, team, or outcome relies on another.
Expansion: Dependency management helps a project understand how work items influence one another so that plans, commitments, and handoffs remain realistic. In PMI-aligned practice, it supports scheduling, risk management, and cross-team coordination by making constraints and interfaces visible. It is particularly important in complex projects, programs, and product environments where delays or changes in one area can cascade into others.
Contrast: Constraint — a dependency is a reliance relationship between work elements; a constraint is a limiting factor such as time, budget, or policy.
Example: A system-testing phase cannot begin until the data-migration team completes environment preparation, so the project manager tracks that dependency and escalates slippage early.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The act of placing a product, service, or result into the target environment so it can be used or operated.
Expansion: Deployment moves the project output from preparation or testing into the environment where it will actually be used. It often includes installation, configuration, cutover, migration, and readiness checks that support operational use. In practice it is closely connected to transition planning, go-live control, and realization of project value.
Contrast: Release — deployment is the technical or operational placement of the solution; release is the event or decision that makes it available to users.
Example: After final testing, the team deploys the new payroll application to production over a weekend and verifies that employees can submit timesheets on Monday.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A product design approach that optimises a design for a specific characteristic such as manufacturability, reliability, cost, serviceability, or safety.
Expansion: Design for X is a family of design approaches that deliberately emphasises one or more critical quality or lifecycle characteristics during product development. PMI uses it in the quality context as a way to build desired attributes into the design rather than relying only on inspection later. It helps teams align technical choices with operational needs, customer expectations, and downstream constraints such as production or maintenance.
Contrast: Design of experiments — Design for X optimises the product design for desired characteristics; Design of experiments tests how factors affect outcomes.
Example: A medical-device project applies design for manufacturability so the product can be assembled consistently at scale without driving up defect rates.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Glossary, Design for X, p. 706

Core Meaning: A statistical method for identifying which factors may influence specific variables of a product or process under development or in production.
Expansion: Design of experiments is a structured analytical technique used to test how different variables affect a desired outcome. PMI places it within quality management as a way to understand cause-and-effect relationships and optimise product or process performance. It is especially valuable when multiple inputs may interact and teams need evidence to choose the best configuration.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Design of experiments is a planned method for varying selected factors systematically and measuring their effects on an output. Instead of changing one thing at a time, it can reveal which variables matter most and whether combinations of variables interact.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects that involve product development, process improvement, testing, or quality optimisation often need more than trial and error. This method helps teams improve quality, reduce waste, and make defensible technical decisions in engineering, manufacturing, healthcare, and other data-rich settings.

The most common misconception: that design of experiments is only for specialists in advanced laboratory settings. In practice, the core idea is broadly applicable wherever teams need structured evidence about what drives performance.

How it shows up in real projects: a team tests different material types, temperatures, and assembly settings to determine which combination produces the best reliability with acceptable cost and cycle time.
Contrast: Design for X — Design of experiments analyses how variables influence outcomes; Design for X focuses the design on achieving a target characteristic such as manufacturability or safety.
Example: A manufacturing project tests several machine-speed and temperature combinations to find the setup that minimises defects while maintaining throughput.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Glossary, design of experiments, p. 706

Core Meaning: An estimate developed with a high level of detail, usually by estimating individual components of work and rolling them up.
Expansion: A detailed estimate is built from the bottom up using specific work elements rather than broad analogies or rough judgment. It is typically more accurate than high-level estimating because it considers the individual parts of the work, their quantities, and their resource needs. PMI uses this approach when enough information exists to support component-level estimating and validation.
Contrast: Analogous estimating — a detailed estimate is built from specific components; analogous estimating relies on comparison with similar past work.
Example: For a data center move, the team estimates cabling, rack installation, testing, and cutover separately, then rolls the amounts into one project estimate.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Detailed estimate, page 716

Core Meaning: The process of aggregating the estimated costs of individual activities or work packages to establish an authorized cost baseline.
Expansion: Determine Budget combines approved cost estimates into a time-phased funding baseline for the project. It establishes the cost baseline used for monitoring and controlling expenditures and performance. The process also considers reserves so uncertainty is reflected in a disciplined way rather than handled informally.
Contrast: Estimate Costs — Determine Budget aggregates approved estimates into the project cost baseline; Estimate Costs develops the individual cost estimates first.
Example: A product launch team rolls up design, testing, training, deployment, and contingency amounts into the approved project budget.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Determine Budget, page 715

Core Meaning: A business analysis process used to define the desired condition that the organisation or initiative intends to achieve.
Expansion: Within PMI business analysis practice, Determine Future State establishes the target environment, capabilities, and outcomes needed to realise value. It translates needs and strategic intent into a clearer picture of what the solution and organisation should look like after change. This process creates a basis for evaluating options, defining scope, and aligning stakeholders around the intended direction.
Contrast: Current state analysis — Determine Future State defines the desired target condition; current state analysis examines how things work today.
Example: Before selecting a new service platform, the business analyst defines the future state as a process with self-service case creation, automated routing, and visibility of service metrics across regions.
Source: The PMI Guide to Business Analysis, term Determine Future State

Core Meaning: A business analysis process used to define when and how a solution's performance and value will be assessed.
Expansion: In PMI business analysis, this process establishes the evaluation method, measures, data needs, timing, and responsibilities for assessing a solution after or during implementation. It ensures the initiative does not stop at delivery but also checks whether the solution is producing intended outcomes and sufficient value. The resulting evaluation approach supports evidence-based decisions about acceptance, improvement, or further change.
Contrast: Validate requirements — Determine solution evaluation approach plans how solution performance will be assessed; validate requirements confirms that requirements are defined correctly and support business goals.
Example: For a new procurement system, the team defines an evaluation approach that measures cycle-time reduction, adoption rates, error frequency, reporting quality, and ownership of post-launch data collection.
Source: The PMI Guide to Business Analysis, p. 289

Core Meaning: The process of developing a document that formally authorizes a project or phase and documents initial requirements that satisfy stakeholder needs and expectations.
Expansion: Develop Project Charter creates the formal basis for the project to exist and for resources to be committed to it. PMI describes it as the point at which the project is authorized and the project manager is given authority to apply organizational resources to project activities. It connects the business need, stakeholder expectations, and initial scope at a level high enough to guide the work before detailed planning begins. Without it, projects often start with activity but without clear authority, purpose, or boundaries.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Develop Project Charter is the initiating process that turns an idea, need, or approved business case into an authorized project. The resulting charter states why the project exists, what success broadly looks like, and who has authority to lead it.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because it aligns sponsor intent, strategic rationale, and initial expectations before significant planning effort is spent. It applies in predictive, adaptive, and hybrid environments even though the level of upfront detail may differ.

The most common misconception: that the charter is just an administrative form or a shortened project plan. In practice, it is not the full management plan; it is the authorization and framing document that establishes legitimacy and decision rights.

How it shows up in real projects: a sponsor approves a charter for a warehouse automation initiative, identifies the project manager, states high-level objectives and constraints, and gives the team the authority to begin detailed planning and stakeholder engagement.
Contrast: Develop Project Management Plan — Develop Project Charter authorizes and frames the project; Develop Project Management Plan defines how the project will be executed, monitored, and controlled.
Example: A hospital sponsor signs a charter for an electronic records project that states the business need, expected benefits, major stakeholders, budget boundary, and the project manager's authority before planning workshops begin.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition — Develop Project Charter, page 75

Core Meaning: The process of defining, preparing, and coordinating all plan components and consolidating them into an integrated project management plan.
Expansion: Develop Project Management Plan integrates subsidiary plans and baselines into the document set that guides how the project will be executed, monitored, and controlled. PMI treats it as the central planning process that aligns scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, communications, risk, procurement, and stakeholder approaches into one coherent management system. It matters because separate plans can be individually sound yet collectively inconsistent unless they are integrated. The result is not just documentation but an agreed operating model for the project.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Develop Project Management Plan creates the integrated roadmap for managing the project. It consolidates baselines, subsidiary plans, and key management decisions into a coordinated whole.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because projects fail when component plans conflict, assumptions differ, or control methods are unclear. It applies to all project environments, though adaptive teams may maintain lighter documentation and update planning artifacts more frequently.

The most common misconception: that the project management plan is a static binder produced once and then ignored. In practice, it is a controlled, living framework that guides execution and is updated through approved change as the project evolves.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager integrates scope, milestone schedule, budget baseline, change control approach, risk responses, communication cadence, and vendor management guidance so all teams operate against the same rules and assumptions.
Contrast: Project documents — the project management plan defines how the project will be managed; project documents capture working details, records, analyses, and evolving information used by the team.
Example: For a data center migration, the project manager consolidates the scope baseline, cutover schedule, testing approach, risk responses, governance structure, and communications plan into the integrated project management plan.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition — Develop Project Management Plan, page 82

Core Meaning: The process of analyzing activity sequences, durations, resource requirements, and schedule constraints to create the project schedule model for execution and control.
Expansion: Develop Schedule turns activity and resource planning data into a workable schedule model. It evaluates sequencing logic, durations, calendars, constraints, and resource availability to forecast start and finish dates. The result supports execution, progress tracking, forecasting, and schedule control throughout the project.
Contrast: Define Activities — Define Activities identifies the work to be scheduled; Develop Schedule analyzes that work to produce the schedule model.
Example: A construction manager sequences excavation, concrete, framing, and inspection activities to produce the approved project schedule.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Develop Schedule, page 715

Core Meaning: The process of improving competencies, team member interaction, and the overall team environment to enhance project performance.
Expansion: Develop Team focuses on helping the project team work more effectively together. It includes actions that build skills, strengthen trust, improve collaboration, and support productive working relationships. PMI treats it as an ongoing process because team capability and dynamics evolve across the life of the project.
Contrast: Acquire Resources — Acquire Resources gets people assigned to the project; Develop Team improves how the assigned team performs together.
Example: A project manager runs role-clarification workshops and peer-learning sessions to improve coordination between analysts, developers, and testers.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Develop Team, page 715

Core Meaning: A development approach is the method used to create and evolve the product, service, or result during the project life cycle.
Expansion: In PMI usage, a development approach describes how work is progressively shaped into deliverables, typically through predictive, iterative, incremental, adaptive, or hybrid patterns. It influences planning detail, stakeholder engagement cadence, feedback loops, and the way scope is refined over time. The chosen approach should fit uncertainty, complexity, risk, and the nature of the deliverable rather than reflect a default preference.
Contrast: Project life cycle — a development approach describes how the solution is developed; the project life cycle describes the broader phases a project passes through from start to finish.
Example: A product team uses an incremental development approach to release core reporting first, then adds analytics dashboards and automated alerts in later increments.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) – Seventh Edition, Section 2.3, pp. 18-24

Term: Development Approach and Life Cycle Performance Domain
Core Meaning: The performance domain concerned with selecting, tailoring, and using the development approach and life cycle that best fit the project's work and context.
Expansion: In PMI's performance domain model, this domain addresses how work will progress from concept through delivery, including predictive, iterative, incremental, adaptive, and hybrid approaches. It focuses on aligning the delivery approach with uncertainty, stakeholder needs, product characteristics, governance expectations, and value delivery. The domain matters because the chosen life cycle shapes planning detail, feedback timing, risk exposure, team interactions, and how progress is measured.
Deep Dive:
What it is: This performance domain from the PMBOK Guide explains how a project's development approach and life cycle are selected and applied. It frames delivery approach as a strategic choice that influences how work is organised, learned from, and governed.

Why it matters and where it applies: the wrong life cycle can create unnecessary rework, delay feedback, or overcomplicate governance. The domain is relevant on nearly every project because all projects need a way to sequence work, manage uncertainty, and connect delivery decisions to value.

The most common misconception: that a life cycle choice is a one-time label such as predictive or agile. PMI's treatment is broader: teams often tailor or blend approaches, and the fit should reflect the nature of the product, degree of change, and stakeholder environment.

How it shows up in real projects: a construction project may use a predictive life cycle for permitting and procurement, while a digital component inside the same initiative uses iterative releases and frequent user feedback, creating a hybrid model governed as one project.
Contrast: Project life cycle — the project life cycle is the sequence of phases a project goes through, while this performance domain is the broader PMI lens for choosing and tailoring the approach and life cycle.
Example: A healthcare transformation project uses predictive planning for regulatory milestones and adaptive delivery for a patient portal, selecting a hybrid approach to balance compliance and learning.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, 7th Edition, Performance Domain: Development Approach and Life Cycle

Core Meaning: A collaborative way of working that integrates software development and operations to enable faster, more reliable delivery and support of products and services.
Expansion: DevOps brings development and operations practices together so teams can build, test, release, and run digital products with tighter feedback loops. In PMI-aligned practice, it is commonly associated with automation, continuous integration, continuous delivery, and shared accountability for outcomes. It is especially relevant in digital delivery environments where speed, quality, and operational stability must be balanced.
Contrast: Agile — Agile is a broader mindset for adaptive value delivery; DevOps focuses specifically on integrating build-and-run practices across development and operations.
Example: A software team automates code integration, testing, deployment, and production monitoring so new features can be released weekly with fewer operational incidents.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A technique used to represent project information visually so relationships, flows, or structures can be understood and managed.
Expansion: A diagramming method is a visual modelling technique used in project management to show relationships among activities, information, causes, stakeholders, or system elements. PMI references diagramming methods in multiple contexts, including scheduling, quality analysis, and root cause investigation, because visual structure often reveals patterns that are harder to detect in narrative form. These methods matter because they support shared understanding, analysis, and communication across diverse stakeholders. Common examples include precedence diagrams, network diagrams, and cause-and-effect diagrams.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A diagramming method is a way of turning project information into a visual model that makes structure, logic, and relationships easier to interpret. Depending on the context, it may depict workflow, causal relationships, activity sequencing, interfaces, or sources of variation.

Why it matters and where it applies: project work often becomes difficult not because information is missing, but because relationships are hard to see. Diagramming methods help teams plan schedules, analyse quality issues, clarify dependencies, and communicate complex situations across sponsors, team members, and subject matter experts.

The most common misconception: that diagrams are just presentation aids added after analysis is complete. In practice, they are often part of the analysis itself because drawing the relationships exposes gaps, contradictions, missing assumptions, and coordination risks.

How it shows up in real projects: a team may build a precedence diagram to create the schedule network, or use a fishbone diagram to investigate recurring defects in a product release. In both cases, the visual model improves decision quality by making hidden relationships explicit.
Contrast: Data representation — a diagramming method is a specific visual technique for modelling relationships or structure; data representation is the broader act of presenting information in any form.
Example: While planning a facility relocation, the project team uses a network diagram to map activity dependencies and a cause-and-effect diagram to analyse repeated permit delays.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition — various process tool sections using diagramming methods, including Sequence Activities and Control Quality; confirm exact page by term in local edition

Core Meaning: A product delivered and experienced through digital technology, such as software, platforms, or online services.
Expansion: A digital product is created, delivered, and evolved primarily through digital channels rather than as a physical good. In project and product work, it usually implies ongoing enhancement, rapid feedback, and measurable user interaction after release. PMI sources retrieved do not show it as a formal glossary entry, but it is a distinct practitioner concept in modern delivery environments.
Contrast: Product — a digital product is a specific kind of product delivered through software or digital platforms; product is the broader category that also includes physical and service outputs.
Example: A bank launches a mobile app for account management and updates it monthly based on usage analytics and customer feedback.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The process of leading and performing the work defined in the project management plan and implementing approved changes to achieve the project's objectives.
Expansion: Direct and Manage Project Work is the core execution process where plans are turned into actual deliverables and work performance data. PMI positions it as the point at which the team carries out the approved approach, coordinates resources, manages interfaces, and implements approved changes. It is broader than task supervision because it includes integrating technical work, managing operational conditions, and ensuring that project outputs are produced as intended. In practice, it is where strategy becomes execution.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Direct and Manage Project Work is the execution process that mobilizes people, resources, tools, and decisions to produce deliverables. It includes coordinating work, using the approved project management plan, and incorporating approved change requests into ongoing execution.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because even a strong plan has no value unless the work is effectively directed, integrated, and completed. It applies across all project types, whether the team is constructing facilities, deploying technology, launching products, or delivering internal transformation.

The most common misconception: that this process just means assigning tasks and tracking whether people are busy. In PMI terms, it is much broader: it includes executing the integrated plan, managing interfaces among teams, producing deliverables, collecting work performance data, and maintaining alignment with approved changes.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager coordinates developers, vendors, testers, and business users during a customer portal rollout, resolves day-to-day execution issues, ensures approved changes are implemented, and keeps work moving toward planned deliverables and milestone outcomes.
Contrast: Monitor and Control Project Work — Direct and Manage Project Work performs and coordinates the work; Monitor and Control Project Work evaluates performance and recommends adjustments.
Example: During a manufacturing line upgrade, the project manager coordinates equipment delivery, installation crews, safety checks, software configuration, and approved scope changes so the line can be commissioned on schedule.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition — Direct and Manage Project Work, page 89

Core Meaning: A process decision toolkit that helps teams choose ways of working fit for their context.
Expansion: Disciplined Agile is PMI's toolkit for tailoring practices, lifecycle choices, and operating models to the needs of a team or organisation. Rather than prescribing one method, it guides people to select context-appropriate ways of working across agile, predictive, and hybrid environments. It is especially useful where teams need flexibility with governance, architecture, value delivery, and enterprise alignment.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Disciplined Agile is a toolkit and mindset for choosing how work gets done based on the situation rather than by following a single predefined method. It offers guidance across team, value-stream, and enterprise levels.

Why it matters and where it applies: many organisations operate in mixed environments where one method does not fit every initiative. Disciplined Agile helps teams tailor practices while still supporting coordination, governance, and business outcomes.

The most common misconception: that Disciplined Agile is just another agile framework competing directly with Scrum. In practice it is broader and helps teams decide when Scrum, Kanban, hybrid planning, lean governance, or other options make sense.

How it shows up in real projects: a team uses Disciplined Agile guidance to choose an iteration-based lifecycle, lightweight architecture reviews, and context-specific governance checkpoints instead of adopting a standard method unchanged.
Contrast: Scrum — Disciplined Agile is a decision toolkit for choosing fit-for-purpose ways of working; Scrum is a specific framework with defined roles and events.
Example: A PMO supports software, infrastructure, and compliance projects by using Disciplined Agile guidance so each team adopts a different lifecycle and governance approach suited to its delivery context.
Source: Disciplined Agile, Glossary, Disciplined Agile, p. 486

Core Meaning: The discount rate is the rate used to convert future cash flows into present value for financial evaluation and project selection.
Expansion: Within PMI project management practice, the discount rate is used in investment appraisal techniques such as net present value to account for the time value of money and risk. It helps organizations compare initiatives with different timing of costs and benefits on a common present-value basis. A higher discount rate reduces the present value of future returns and can materially affect whether a project appears financially attractive.
Contrast: Interest rate — an interest rate is the cost of borrowing or return on lending, while a discount rate in project evaluation is the factor used to translate future value into present value for decision making.
Example: A company compares two automation projects by discounting their expected five-year benefit streams to present value and selects the one with the stronger net present value.
Source: The Standard for Portfolio Management – Fourth Edition, section on financial value measures including present value and discounting, pp. 34-35

Core Meaning: A measurable work effort that is directly associated with completing specific project activities or deliverables.
Expansion: Discrete effort is work that can be planned, scheduled, and objectively tracked against tangible progress. In project management and earned value contexts, it contrasts with support-type effort that is not easily tied to a single definable output. It is important because performance measurement is strongest when progress can be linked to clearly scoped deliverables or tasks.
Contrast: Level of effort — discrete effort produces specific measurable outputs tied to defined activities, while level of effort supports the work without discrete end products for progress measurement.
Example: Configuring a server environment for a planned release is treated as discrete effort because completion can be measured against defined setup tasks and acceptance criteria.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A relationship that is based on preferred sequencing of activities and is not required by the nature of the work.
Expansion: A discretionary dependency reflects a chosen order of work, often based on best practice, experience, or a preferred approach. Unlike mandatory dependencies, it can be changed if the team decides a different sequence is better. PMI treats it as a scheduling choice that should be documented because it affects logic, float, and schedule flexibility.
Contrast: Mandatory dependency — a discretionary dependency is chosen by the team; a mandatory dependency is required by the work, contract, or physical reality.
Example: A team chooses to complete user interface design before backend integration even though the two could be developed in parallel.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Discretionary dependency, page 716

Core Meaning: The final decision on how an item — such as a defect, change request, or record — is to be handled or resolved.
Expansion: Disposition records the agreed outcome for an item: accept, reject, defer, repair, or archive. Capturing dispositions creates a clear audit trail of decisions and prevents items lingering unresolved.
Contrast: Resolution — disposition is the decision about what to do with an item; resolution is the act of actually carrying it out.
Example: The change control board reviews a request and records its disposition as 'deferred to next release'.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; defined for glossary completeness

Core Meaning: A disagreement between parties that has escalated into a formal disagreement requiring resolution, often in a contractual context.
Expansion: In PMI usage, a dispute is more serious than an ordinary disagreement because it has moved into a formal resolution path. It commonly arises in procurement when parties differ on contract interpretation, performance, payment, delay, or entitlement. Disputes are managed through agreed resolution mechanisms such as negotiation, escalation, mediation, arbitration, or dispute review structures.
Contrast: Claim — a claim is a request or assertion for relief or adjustment; a dispute is the unresolved disagreement that exists when the parties do not settle the claim.
Example: A supplier requests additional payment for scope it says was implied by the contract, the buyer rejects the request, and the matter becomes a formal dispute under the contract's resolution process.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A panel of neutral experts appointed at the start of a project to help avoid disputes and resolve them during project execution.
Expansion: A dispute review board is an alternative dispute resolution mechanism used on projects where disagreements may arise among contracting parties. PMI defines it as a panel of neutral experts established at the beginning of the project to help prevent disputes and address them as they emerge. It is especially relevant on large, complex, or claim-prone projects where early neutral input can reduce escalation.
Contrast: Mediation — a dispute review board is established early and remains involved through the project; mediation is usually introduced after a dispute has already formed.
Example: On a major rail-construction project, the owner and contractor use a dispute review board to review delay claims before they develop into formal legal proceedings.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge — page 252

Core Meaning: A team whose members work from different geographic locations and collaborate across distance.
Expansion: A distributed team is made up of people who do not share the same physical workspace and often operate across different sites, time zones, or countries. PMI treats this as an important organisational and delivery context because it affects communication, coordination, trust building, and knowledge sharing. Managing distributed teams well requires deliberate practices for collaboration, visibility, decision making, and stakeholder alignment.
Contrast: Virtual team — Distributed team emphasises geographic separation of members; virtual team emphasises collaboration primarily through electronic communication and may or may not involve broad geographic spread.
Example: A product project uses analysts in Canada, developers in India, and testers in Poland, with shared planning boards, overlapping meeting windows, and clear handoff protocols.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Glossary, distributed team, p. 706

Core Meaning: A thinking approach used to generate many possible ideas, options, or solutions.
Expansion: Divergent thinking expands the range of possibilities before narrowing choices, helping teams explore alternatives rather than locking too quickly onto one answer. PMI uses the concept in problem solving, creativity, ideation, and collaborative planning where multiple options improve decision quality. It is especially useful early in framing scope options, risk responses, requirements, and solution concepts.
Contrast: Convergent thinking — Divergent thinking generates multiple possibilities; convergent thinking evaluates and narrows them toward a decision.
Example: During a workshop for a customer portal project, the team first uses divergent thinking to generate many feature ideas before prioritising the few that fit budget and timeline constraints.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Glossary, divergent thinking, p. 370

Core Meaning: A technique used to elicit requirements by reviewing existing documents and identifying relevant information.
Expansion: Document analysis examines available records, reports, contracts, policies, lessons learned, and other written sources to extract useful project information. PMI uses it as a requirements elicitation and analysis technique when existing documentation can reveal needs, constraints, assumptions, or gaps. It is especially useful early in planning or when stakeholder access is limited.
Contrast: Interviews — document analysis relies on existing written sources; interviews rely on direct conversation with stakeholders.
Example: A project team reviews regulatory guidance, prior audit reports, and internal procedures to identify compliance requirements for a new reporting system.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Document analysis, page 717

Core Meaning: Recorded information used to support, communicate, govern, and control project work and decisions.
Expansion: Within PMI practice, documentation includes the recorded material that supports planning, execution, monitoring, control, compliance, and learning. It can include requirements, decisions, contracts, test evidence, logs, reports, and other project information. Its value is not in producing paperwork for its own sake, but in preserving clarity, traceability, and shared understanding.
Contrast: Project records — documentation is the broader body of recorded information used in the project, while records are retained evidence of actions, decisions, and outcomes.
Example: A project team maintains requirements documentation, change records, test results, and procurement files so that delivery decisions can be traced and audited.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A technique used to gather and assess information by examining existing project and business documents.
Expansion: Documentation reviews involve examining available records, reports, contracts, plans, and related materials to understand context, validate facts, or identify issues. PMI uses the technique in several areas because existing documentation can reveal assumptions, constraints, requirements, and historical performance.
Contrast: Document analysis — Documentation reviews refers to reviewing existing documents as a technique; Document analysis is the broader analytical examination of document content for insight or evidence.
Example: Before planning a migration project, the team reviews prior audit reports, support tickets, and architecture documents to identify recurring issues and constraints.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Glossary, documentation reviews, p. 707

Core Meaning: A directional concept referring to later activities, effects, or parts of a value stream that are influenced by earlier work.
Expansion: In project work, downstream usually describes impacts that appear later in delivery after an earlier decision, handoff, or output. It helps teams think about consequences beyond the immediate task, especially across dependencies and lifecycle stages.
Contrast: Upstream — downstream refers to later effects or later work, while upstream refers to earlier causes, inputs, or predecessor activities.
Example: A requirements decision made during planning creates downstream impacts on design, testing, training, and deployment.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A factor, condition, or force that shapes project direction, decisions, or the need for change.
Expansion: A driver is an underlying cause or influence that pushes a project, program, or organization toward a particular objective, response, or course of action. In PMI usage, drivers often explain why work is being initiated, changed, reprioritized, or governed in a certain way. They matter because they connect project choices to business context rather than treating plans as self-contained.
Contrast: Objective — a driver is the reason or force behind action; an objective is the specific result being pursued.
Example: A new regulatory requirement acts as a driver for a bank to launch a compliance remediation project on an accelerated schedule.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Driver

Core Meaning: A version of a product increment that is made available for review, testing, or use.
Expansion: Drop refers to a released build or version of a solution component, often used in iterative and incremental delivery contexts. It helps teams expose work for feedback, validation, integration, or deployment in controlled steps.
Contrast: Increment — a drop is a specific released version made available at a point in time; an increment is the usable addition of functionality or capability itself.
Example: A software team provides a monthly test drop of the application so business users can validate new features before the production release.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A schedule activity with zero duration used to show a logical relationship in an activity-on-arrow network diagram.
Expansion: A dummy activity does not represent real work. It is inserted only to preserve correct network logic when two activities need to be distinguished in the diagram. PMI uses it in activity-on-arrow scheduling to maintain accurate precedence relationships without adding duration or resources.
Contrast: Real activity — a dummy activity is only a logic placeholder; a real activity represents actual work that consumes time and resources.
Example: In a network diagram, a dummy activity is added so two successor tasks can share a predecessor relationship without implying extra work.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Dummy activity, page 716

Core Meaning: The total number of work periods needed to complete an activity, phase, or project.
Expansion: Duration is the elapsed working time required for a scheduled activity or component, taking into account the planned resource availability and working calendar. It differs from effort because it reflects how long the work takes on the schedule, not simply how much labor is expended. Duration is a basic input to schedule development, forecasting, and critical path analysis.
Contrast: Effort — duration is elapsed time on the schedule; effort is the amount of labor or work content required.
Example: A task estimated at 40 hours of effort may have a duration of 5 working days if one full-time analyst is assigned, or 3 days if two analysts split the work with some overlap.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / PMI Lexicon — Duration

Core Meaning: A schedule-shortening approach used to reduce the project or activity timeline without changing scope.
Expansion: Duration compression is the deliberate use of techniques such as crashing and fast tracking to shorten the schedule while keeping the agreed scope intact. PMI treats it as a schedule optimisation response when deadlines tighten or delays threaten key milestones. It is useful when time is the primary constraint, but it often increases cost, coordination complexity, or risk.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Duration compression is a way to reduce planned completion time by reworking how and when activities are performed. The two most common techniques are crashing, which adds resources to critical-path work, and fast tracking, which overlaps activities that were originally planned in sequence.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects often face immovable launch dates, regulatory deadlines, contract commitments, or recovery needs after slippage. Duration compression gives the project manager structured options to regain time while preserving scope, especially in schedule-driven projects such as product launches, construction phases, and system deployments.

The most common misconception: that schedule compression is simply working harder or asking everyone to move faster. In practice, effective compression is a selective analysis of critical-path options, trade-offs, and added risk, not a blanket demand for urgency.

How it shows up in real projects: a project team may assign additional skilled resources to a critical integration task, or begin user training before all configuration work is fully complete where the overlap is manageable. Each move is evaluated for downstream risk, cost impact, and whether the time saved is real rather than apparent.
Contrast: Schedule compression — duration compression is the specific reduction of elapsed time; schedule compression is the broader family of techniques used to shorten the schedule.
Example: A software rollout is behind plan, so the team adds an extra integration specialist to the critical path and overlaps training-material development with final configuration review to recover two weeks.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, Develop Schedule process, schedule compression techniques; confirm exact page in local edition

Core Meaning: An assessment of the likely amount of time needed to complete an activity or work package.
Expansion: A duration estimate expresses how long work is expected to take, considering resources, productivity, risks, and relevant constraints. It is used in schedule development and may be stated as a point estimate or a range. Good duration estimates support realistic sequencing, forecasting, and schedule commitments.
Contrast: Effort estimate — duration is elapsed working time to complete the work; effort is the amount of labor required, which may differ depending on resource allocation.
Example: A testing activity is estimated at five working days because two testers can execute the planned test set within that time under current environment constraints.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, Estimate Activity Durations process; confirm exact page in local edition

Core Meaning: The earliest possible point in time at which a schedule activity can finish based on the network logic and durations.
Expansion: Early Finish is calculated during schedule network analysis and shows the soonest an activity could complete if predecessor work occurs as planned. It is typically derived from the Early Start plus the activity duration. PMI uses it to support forward-pass calculations, float analysis, and schedule feasibility checks.
Contrast: Late Finish — Early Finish is the earliest feasible completion point, while Late Finish is the latest completion point that avoids delaying the project or successor work.
Example: If design is scheduled to start on Monday and takes five working days, the design activity may have an Early Finish of Friday.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) glossary, Early Finish, p. 243

Core Meaning: The earliest possible point in time at which a schedule activity can finish based on the network logic and durations.
Expansion: The early finish date is calculated during schedule network analysis to show when an activity could complete if preceding work occurs as planned. It is typically derived from the early start date plus the activity duration, adjusted for the scheduling method in use. It matters because it helps identify the earliest project timeline and supports float analysis. Project teams use it to understand schedule feasibility before resource or calendar constraints are fully applied.
Contrast: Late finish date — early finish shows the earliest feasible completion point, while late finish shows the latest completion point without delaying the project or successor work.
Example: In a construction schedule, foundation curing has an early finish date of 14 May if excavation and pouring both complete on time.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition Glossary, early finish date, page 243

Core Meaning: The earliest possible point in time at which a schedule activity can begin based on the network logic and durations.
Expansion: Early Start is the earliest time an activity can begin when predecessor relationships and durations are applied in schedule analysis. It is produced through the forward pass and is a key input to Early Finish calculations. PMI uses it to understand sequencing effects and the earliest achievable schedule.
Contrast: Late Start — Early Start is the earliest feasible beginning point, while Late Start is the latest start that avoids delaying the project or successor work.
Example: After approval is planned to finish on 3 March, procurement can have an Early Start of 4 March.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) glossary, Early Start, p. 243

Core Meaning: The earliest possible point in time at which a schedule activity can begin based on the network logic and durations.
Expansion: The early start date is the earliest time an activity can begin when predecessor relationships and assumed durations are applied in schedule analysis. It is produced through a forward pass in network calculations and forms the basis for the earliest achievable schedule. Teams use it to understand sequencing effects and potential schedule flexibility. It is a core input to determining early finish dates and float.
Contrast: Late start date — early start shows the earliest feasible beginning point, while late start shows the latest start that avoids delaying the project or successor work.
Example: After design approval is planned to finish on 3 March, procurement receives an early start date of 4 March in the project schedule.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition Glossary, early start date, page 243

Core Meaning: The measure of work performed expressed in terms of the budget authorized for that work.
Expansion: Earned value converts completed scope into a budget-based performance measure so progress can be compared objectively with plan and spend. It is a core concept in earned value management because it links scope, schedule, and cost rather than tracking each in isolation. Teams use it to understand whether the project is truly progressing as expected, not merely spending money or reporting activity. It is especially useful where formal baselines and performance measurement are needed.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Earned value is the budgeted value of completed work at a point in time. It answers the question of how much planned value the project has actually achieved through completed scope.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters because schedule status and cost status can look acceptable when viewed separately yet still hide performance problems. Earned value is widely applied in projects that use baselines, control accounts, and formal progress measurement, especially in complex delivery environments where objective control is important.

The most common misconception: that earned value is the same as money spent or hours used. It is not a spending measure and not an effort measure; it represents the planned budget value of work that is actually complete.

How it shows up in real projects: A project control team measures completed deliverables against the performance measurement baseline, calculates EV each reporting period, and uses it with planned value and actual cost to identify variance and forecast outcomes.
Contrast: Actual cost — earned value reflects the budgeted value of completed work, while actual cost reflects what has actually been spent.
Example: A construction project budgets 100,000 of planned work for foundation completion, and when the foundation is fully complete the project records that amount as earned value even if the actual spend was higher or lower.

Core Meaning: A method of performance measurement that integrates scope, schedule, and cost data to assess project progress and forecast outcomes.
Expansion: Earned value analysis evaluates project performance by comparing planned value, earned value, and actual cost. It shows not only how much has been spent, but how much value has actually been delivered relative to the plan. PMI uses it as a structured way to monitor cost and schedule performance and support forecasting.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Earned value analysis is the analytical use of earned value management data to determine whether project work is ahead, behind, under budget, or over budget. It relies on integrated performance measures such as planned value, earned value, actual cost, schedule variance, cost variance, and performance indices.

Why it matters and where it applies: simple spend tracking can hide the true status of a project because spending less than planned may also mean delivering less than planned. Earned value analysis is especially useful on projects with a defined baseline, measurable progress, and a need for objective forecasting, such as engineering, construction, infrastructure, defense, or large technology deployments.

The most common misconception: that earned value analysis is just a finance exercise or only useful for large projects. In reality, it is a project-control method for integrated performance insight, though it works best when scope is clearly defined and progress can be measured credibly.

How it shows up in real projects: a project controls lead compares earned value against planned value and actual cost each reporting cycle, identifies deteriorating cost performance early, and updates the forecasted estimate at completion for sponsor review.
Contrast: Earned value management — Earned value analysis is the assessment of performance using earned value data; earned value management is the broader system, processes, and governance that produce and use that data.
Example: On a construction project, the team finds that only 60 percent of planned work value has been earned while 75 percent of the budget for that period has already been spent, indicating both schedule and cost pressure.
