Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, earned value analysis discussion in Control Costs, pp. 263-267

Core Meaning: A methodology that integrates scope, schedule, and cost data to assess project performance and forecast outcomes.
Expansion: Earned value management, or EVM, compares planned value, earned value, and actual cost to show how much value has been delivered for the money spent and time elapsed. PMI uses it as a disciplined performance measurement approach that supports trend analysis, variance analysis, and forecasting rather than simple budget tracking. It is especially useful on projects that need objective progress measurement against a defined baseline.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Earned value management is a performance management method that quantifies completed work against an approved scope, schedule, and cost baseline. It converts progress into comparable measures so teams can calculate variances and performance indices.

Why it matters and where it applies: EVM gives early warning when work is slipping or costing more than planned and supports more credible forecasting at completion. It is common in large, complex, or highly governed projects such as construction, engineering, defense, and capital programs, but its logic can be scaled for smaller efforts.

The most common misconception: that EVM is just cost reporting or a finance exercise. In practice, it depends on credible scope definition, measurable progress rules, and an integrated baseline, so it is as much a planning and control discipline as a reporting method.

How it shows up in real projects: teams baseline the work, assign budgets to work packages, measure earned value as work is completed, review cost and schedule variances, and forecast estimate at completion to support corrective action.
Contrast: Variance analysis — variance analysis examines differences in performance; earned value management is the broader integrated system that generates those measures from scope, schedule, and cost baselines.
Example: On a rail-station upgrade, the team measures earned value monthly against budgeted work packages and identifies that civil works are behind schedule even though spending appears close to plan.

Source: Practice Standard for Earned Value Management, Second Edition — Earned Value Management, p. 13

Core Meaning: A contractual mechanism that permits defined changes to price when specified economic conditions change.
Expansion: In PMI procurement practice, economic price adjustment is associated with fixed-price-with-economic-price-adjustment contracts. It is used when long-duration work could be materially affected by inflation, commodity shifts, or other external economic changes identified in the contract. The adjustment is not discretionary; it follows conditions and formulas agreed in advance.
Contrast: Firm-fixed-price contract — economic price adjustment allows limited price changes under defined economic conditions, while a firm-fixed-price contract keeps the agreed price unchanged.
Example: A three-year construction materials agreement includes an index-based clause that adjusts the contract price if published steel prices move beyond an agreed threshold.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, glossary entry for fixed-price with economic price adjustment contract, page 243

Core Meaning: The number of labor units required to complete a schedule activity or work breakdown structure component, often expressed in hours, days, or weeks.
Expansion: Effort expresses how much human work is needed to complete a defined piece of work. It supports estimating, staffing, scheduling, and capacity decisions, but it is not the same as elapsed duration because resource availability and sequencing affect calendar time. In practice, teams use effort estimates to size work packages and assign people with the right skills. Clear effort estimates improve forecast quality and workload balancing.
Contrast: Duration — effort is the labor required to do the work, while duration is the total elapsed calendar time needed to finish it.
Example: A business analyst estimates that documenting requirements will take 40 hours of effort, which may span two calendar weeks if the analyst is only assigned part time.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Effort; page citation not confirmed in available source

Core Meaning: The process of drawing out, discovering, and obtaining information from stakeholders and other sources.
Expansion: Elicitation is used to uncover needs, expectations, constraints, assumptions, and other information that may not already be documented. In PMI business analysis, it is a defined process area as well as a glossary concept. It supports understanding the problem, opportunity, and stakeholder needs before analysis and solution definition proceed.
Contrast: Requirements gathering — elicitation is broader because it includes discovering and clarifying information, not just collecting stated requirements.
Example: A project team runs interviews and workshops with users to uncover reporting needs before defining solution scope.
Source: The PMI Guide to Business Analysis — Conduct Elicitation, page 167; glossary/index reference for Elicitation, page 395

Core Meaning: The ability to identify, assess, and manage one's own emotions and the emotions of others and groups.
Expansion: PMI treats emotional intelligence as a leadership capability that helps project professionals understand emotional signals in themselves and others and respond effectively. It supports collaboration, stakeholder engagement, conflict navigation, and team resilience under pressure. Rather than being soft or optional, it is a practical capability that improves judgment, trust, and influence across the project environment.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Emotional intelligence is the capability to recognise, interpret, and manage emotions in oneself and in other people. In PMI terms, it supports self-awareness, self-management, and relationship management in project settings.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects are carried out by people under conditions of uncertainty, time pressure, trade-offs, and change. Emotional intelligence helps project leaders reduce tension, improve communication, anticipate reactions, and maintain productive working relationships across teams and stakeholders.

The most common misconception: that emotional intelligence means being agreeable, avoiding conflict, or relying on intuition alone. In practice it means noticing emotional dynamics accurately and using that awareness to handle difficult conversations, decisions, and resistance more effectively.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager senses frustration building during a planning workshop, reframes the discussion, addresses concerns directly, and prevents a technical disagreement from becoming a relationship breakdown.
Contrast: Technical skill — emotional intelligence concerns understanding and managing emotions and relationships, while technical skill concerns domain knowledge and delivery mechanics.
Example: During a schedule recovery effort, the project manager notices stakeholder anxiety, acknowledges the pressure openly, and adapts communication so the team can commit to a realistic revised plan.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, page 34

Core Meaning: Giving team members the authority, autonomy, and resources to make decisions and act within their area of work.
Expansion: Empowerment shifts appropriate decision-making down to the people doing the work so they can respond quickly without waiting for approval. It depends on clear boundaries, trust, and competence, and is a central enabler of self-managing teams. Used well, it improves engagement, speed, and ownership of outcomes.
Contrast: Delegation — delegation assigns specific tasks/authority case by case; empowerment is the broader standing autonomy to decide and act.
Example: A team lead lets the team choose its own technical approach within agreed quality and budget limits.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; defined for glossary completeness

Core Meaning: A final product, service, or result delivered for customer, sponsor, or operational use.
Expansion: An end item is the completed output of a project or of a defined part of the work. It refers to what is ultimately handed over for use, acceptance, or deployment rather than to the work performed to create it. PMI usage distinguishes it from interim outputs and supporting work products that are not themselves the final handoff.
Contrast: Deliverable — an end item is the final delivered output, while a deliverable can be any verifiable output produced during the project.
Example: In an infrastructure project, the commissioned bridge is the end item, while drawings, permits, and test reports are supporting deliverables.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, End item

Core Meaning: The person or group that ultimately uses the product, service, or result created by the project.
Expansion: An end user is the intended user of the project outcome. PMI distinguishes end users from sponsors, customers, and other stakeholders because those who approve, fund, or receive the work may not be the same people who use it in practice. Identifying end users helps shape requirements, usability, training, adoption, and acceptance expectations.
Contrast: Customer — an end user uses the result, while a customer may buy, fund, or receive it without personally using it.
Example: In a hospital scheduling-system project, nurses and reception staff are the end users even though hospital leadership funds the work.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, End user

Core Meaning: An opportunity (positive risk) response strategy intended to increase the probability or the impact of a beneficial event.
Expansion: Enhance is an opportunity risk response strategy used when the team wants to raise the likelihood that a beneficial event will occur or increase the value if it does occur. Rather than merely accepting uncertainty, the project actively takes steps to improve the chance of a positive outcome. PMI places it among the standard strategies for responding to opportunities in project risk management. It is most useful when an upside event is plausible and the added effort to strengthen it is justified by the expected benefit.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Enhance is a proactive response to an opportunity. The team changes conditions so the positive event becomes more likely, more valuable, or both.

Why it matters and where it applies: many projects focus heavily on threats and overlook upside risk. Enhance matters because projects can improve schedule, cost, quality, or business value by deliberately strengthening favorable conditions, especially in innovation, procurement, delivery acceleration, and stakeholder engagement situations.

The most common misconception: that enhance means guaranteeing the benefit. It does not remove uncertainty; it improves the odds or the magnitude of the positive effect without making the outcome certain.

How it shows up in real projects: a team may assign its strongest engineers to a prototype to improve the chance of an early breakthrough, or add early stakeholder workshops to increase the likelihood of faster approvals and stronger adoption.
Contrast: Exploit — enhance increases the probability or impact of an opportunity, while exploit seeks to make the opportunity certain.
Example: A product team adds extra user testing early in development to increase the chance that the release will achieve unusually strong customer adoption.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition Glossary, enhance, page 244

Core Meaning: The structure and relationship of an enterprise's business, information, applications, and technology used to guide organizational change and solution design.
Expansion: Enterprise architecture gives a high-level view of how an organization works across capabilities, processes, systems, and technology. In project work, it helps ensure that initiatives fit the broader organizational environment rather than creating isolated solutions. It is commonly used to support strategic alignment, integration decisions, and consistency across multiple initiatives.
Contrast: Solution architecture — enterprise architecture defines the broader organizational structure and standards; solution architecture focuses on the design of a specific solution.
Example: A transformation program uses enterprise architecture principles so a new customer portal fits existing identity, data, and integration standards across the enterprise.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Conditions, not under the immediate control of the team, that influence, constrain, or direct the project, program, or portfolio.
Expansion: Enterprise environmental factors are the internal and external conditions that shape how a project can be planned and performed. They include influences outside the team’s direct control, so they must be considered rather than managed as project work itself. PMI treats them as important inputs because they can constrain options, affect estimates, and influence governance and delivery approaches. They matter across the project life cycle because they can change the feasibility or timing of decisions.
Deep Dive:
What it is: enterprise environmental factors are the surrounding conditions that affect the project but are not controlled by the project team. They form part of the context in which the project operates.

Why it matters and where it applies: EEFs matter because they can shape strategy, estimates, risk exposure, and delivery choices. They apply in every project environment, including organizational culture, market conditions, regulations, infrastructure, and resource availability.

The most common misconception: that EEFs are the same as organizational process assets. They are not; EEFs are conditions and constraints, while organizational process assets are internal assets such as policies, templates, and lessons learned.

How it shows up in real projects: a project may need to adapt to regulatory requirements, labor market shortages, or enterprise governance rules. These factors influence planning and execution even though the team cannot directly change them.
Contrast: Organizational process assets — EEFs are external or contextual conditions; OPAs are internal organizational assets and knowledge.
Example: A global rollout project adjusts its schedule because local labor laws and regional holiday calendars affect staffing availability.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — Enterprise environmental factors

Core Meaning: A large, related body of work intended to hierarchically organize a set of requirements and deliver specific business outcomes.
Expansion: An epic groups related requirements into a larger item that can be broken down into smaller pieces for planning and delivery. It is commonly used in adaptive and hybrid environments to organize work at a higher level before decomposition. PMI describes it as a way to structure requirements around business outcomes.
Contrast: Feature — an epic is a larger body of related work that can be decomposed into features or smaller requirements; a feature is a smaller, more specific capability.
Example: A digital banking program creates an epic for mobile account opening, then breaks it into smaller features such as identity verification, document upload, and approval workflow.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase / Agile Practice Guide — epic

Core Meaning: To raise an issue or decision to a higher level of authority for attention or resolution.
Expansion: Escalation is used when something exceeds a team's authority, tolerance, or ability to resolve, ensuring it reaches someone who can act. Clear escalation paths and thresholds keep problems from stalling while avoiding raising every minor matter upward.
Contrast: Delegate — escalation moves a decision up to higher authority; delegation moves a task down to others.
Example: When a risk breaches its threshold, the manager escalates it to the steering committee.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; defined for glossary completeness

Core Meaning: To raise an issue to a higher level of authority when it cannot be resolved effectively at the current level.
Expansion: Escalating an issue means moving it to someone with greater authority, decision rights, or resources. In project practice, this happens when an issue threatens objectives or exceeds the team's ability to resolve it directly.
Contrast: Issue resolution — resolution attempts to solve the issue at the current level, while escalation moves it upward for decision or intervention.
Example: A project manager escalates a vendor contract dispute to the sponsor after the team cannot resolve the delivery conflict within its authority.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The act of raising an issue, risk, or decision to a higher level of authority for resolution.
Expansion: Escalation is used when the project team cannot resolve a matter within its own authority, resources, or time. It moves the matter to a sponsor, manager, governance body, or other decision maker who can act. PMI uses escalation to keep unresolved matters from stalling delivery or exceeding the team's mandate.
Contrast: Issue escalation — escalation is the general act of raising a matter; issue escalation applies specifically to a problem that has already occurred.
Example: A project manager escalates a vendor delay to the steering committee because the delay threatens a contractual milestone the team cannot renegotiate alone.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Escalation

Core Meaning: A quantitative assessment of the likely amount or outcome of a variable such as cost, duration, resources, or effort.
Expansion: An estimate is a prediction based on available information, assumptions, data, and judgment at a given point in time. In project work, estimates support planning, budgeting, scheduling, forecasting, and decision making before results are known. They are not exact commitments and should be refined as uncertainty is reduced. Their usefulness depends on the quality of inputs, methods, and assumptions behind them.
Contrast: Actuals — an estimate forecasts a likely future result, while actuals record what really happened after the work was performed.
Example: During planning, a project team estimates that system testing will require three weeks and two full-time testers.
Source: PMI Lexicon / PMI Knowledge Base, estimate

Core Meaning: Estimate Activity Durations is the process of approximating the number of work periods needed to complete individual activities with estimated resources.
Expansion: This schedule management process develops duration estimates for the activities identified in the project schedule model. PMI positions it after activities are defined and sequenced because duration estimates depend on activity scope, resource capability, calendars, and constraints. Its output supports schedule development, forecasting, and analysis of delivery feasibility.
Contrast: Estimate Costs — Estimate Activity Durations approximates the time needed to complete activities, while Estimate Costs approximates the monetary resources required for project work.
Example: On a software project, the team estimates that configuring identity-management integration will take eight working days using one architect and two developers.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Sixth Edition, 6.4 Estimate Activity Durations, p. 200

Core Meaning: The process of estimating the type and quantities of resources needed to perform each activity.
Expansion: Estimate Activity Resources determines what people, equipment, materials, and supplies are required for each scheduled activity and in what amounts. PMI treats it as a core planning process because resource needs directly affect cost, duration, procurement, and schedule feasibility. The process considers availability, calendars, capability, and constraints, and it informs later scheduling and estimating decisions.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Estimate Activity Resources is a planning process used to determine the resource profile for each activity. It identifies both the kind of resource needed and the quantity required to complete the work.

Why it matters and where it applies: resource estimates influence duration estimates, cost estimates, procurement decisions, and workload balancing across the project. It matters especially in environments with scarce specialists, shared equipment, or supply constraints.

The most common misconception: that this process is the same as estimating duration. Duration is the time needed for work, while activity resource estimating identifies what and how much is needed to perform that work.

How it shows up in real projects: a testing activity may require two testers, one configured environment, and a defined set of devices, while a construction activity may require a crane, a certified crew, and specific materials.
Contrast: Estimate Activity Durations — resource estimating identifies what is needed to do the work, while duration estimating predicts how long the work will take.
Example: For a data-center installation, the team estimates that rack deployment needs three technicians, one lift, structured cabling, and network hardware kits.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Estimate Activity Resources

Core Meaning: The expected total cost of completing all work expressed as the sum of actual cost to date and the forecast cost for the remaining work.
Expansion: Estimate at completion is a forecasting measure used to predict the final project cost based on current performance and assumptions about the remaining work. It helps decision makers understand whether the approved budget is still realistic or whether corrective action is needed. In earned value management, different calculation methods are used depending on whether current variances are expected to continue or are considered atypical. It is central to cost control because it turns current performance into a forward-looking view.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Estimate at completion is the projected final cost of the project or control account when all authorized work is complete. It combines what has already been spent with the best current estimate of what remains.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters because sponsors and project managers need an early warning of likely overruns or underruns before the work is finished. It is especially relevant in projects using earned value management, periodic forecasting, and governance reviews tied to budget performance.

The most common misconception: that estimate at completion is just the original budget repeated later in the project. In practice it is a live forecast that changes when performance trends, risks, productivity, or scope conditions change.

How it shows up in real projects: During monthly performance reviews, the team recalculates EAC using actual cost, earned value indicators, and revised assumptions, then reports whether management reserve, replanning, or corrective action is required.
Contrast: Budget at completion — estimate at completion is the current forecast of final cost, while budget at completion is the original approved total budget baseline.
Example: An infrastructure project with rising labor inefficiency revises its forecast upward after three reporting periods and reports a new estimate at completion to the steering committee.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Estimate at completion; page citation not confirmed in available source

Core Meaning: The documented rationale, assumptions, methods, and source information used to develop an estimate.
Expansion: Estimate basis explains how an estimate was produced and what conditions it depends on. It commonly records the estimating approach, assumptions, exclusions, constraints, source data, and confidence considerations. PMI practice relies on it to make estimates reviewable, defensible, and easier to update when scope or conditions change.
Contrast: Estimate — the estimate is the resulting value or range, while the estimate basis explains how it was derived.
Example: A project manager submits a cost estimate together with labour-rate assumptions, vendor quotes, exclusions, and contingency reasoning.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Estimate basis

Core Meaning: The process of developing an approximation of the monetary resources needed to complete project work.
Expansion: Estimate Costs is the process used to approximate the funds required for activities, work packages, or the project as a whole. It draws on scope, schedule, resource, and risk information to produce a cost estimate. PMI treats it as a core planning activity that feeds budgeting and cost control.
Contrast: Determine Budget — Estimate Costs produces the cost estimates, while Determine Budget aggregates those estimates into an approved cost baseline.
Example: A team estimates the cost of software testing by combining labor hours, tool licences, and contingency for expected rework.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Estimate Costs, p. 241

Core Meaning: The span of possible values around an estimate that reflects uncertainty in the projected result.
Expansion: Estimate range expresses the likely lower and upper bounds for cost, duration, or another forecasted measure rather than implying false precision in a single point estimate. It helps stakeholders understand the uncertainty inherent in early planning and complex work. PMI uses range-based estimating concepts to communicate confidence, support reserves, and improve decision making under uncertainty.
Contrast: Point estimate — an estimate range expresses a band of plausible outcomes, while a point estimate gives one expected value.
Example: During concept planning, a team forecasts that a data migration will take between eight and twelve weeks because source data quality is still uncertain.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition, section 6.4 Estimate Activity Durations and section 7.2 Estimate Costs, pages 191 and 246

Core Meaning: The expected cost to finish all the remaining project work.
Expansion: Estimate to complete is a forward-looking cost forecast for the work that has not yet been done. It is used with actual cost and estimate at completion to understand current cost performance and predict the final outcome. In PMI earned value and estimating contexts, it helps teams decide whether the remaining work is still likely to fit within the approved budget. It is especially useful when performance trends or scope changes suggest the original forecast may no longer be valid.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Estimate to complete is the projected cost of the unfinished portion of the project. It answers the question of how much more money will be needed to complete the remaining authorized work.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because project leaders need a current forecast, not just a record of what has already been spent. It is used in cost control, earned value management, and periodic forecasting reviews.

The most common misconception: that ETC is the same as the original budget for the remaining work. In practice it is a live estimate that can change as productivity, risks, and scope conditions change.

How it shows up in real projects: a project team updates ETC each reporting cycle using actual performance, remaining scope, and revised assumptions, then uses it to support the estimate at completion.
Contrast: Estimate at completion — ETC is the forecast for the remaining work only, while EAC is the forecast for the total final cost.
Example: A software project has completed most of its testing but still needs defect fixes and final regression testing, so the team revises the estimate to complete for the remaining effort.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — Estimate to complete (ETC)

Core Meaning: The process of developing an approximation of the cost, duration, resources, or other quantitative outcome of project work.
Expansion: Estimating is the project-management activity of producing approximate values for uncertain work outcomes. It supports planning, budgeting, scheduling, and resource decisions by turning incomplete information into usable forecasts. PMI treats estimating as a practical management activity rather than a single technique, with methods chosen based on the level of detail and uncertainty available.
Contrast: Estimate — estimating is the process of producing an approximation; an estimate is the resulting quantified assessment.
Example: A project team estimates the effort and duration needed to configure a new reporting dashboard before committing to the release plan.
Source: PMI Lexicon / PMI Knowledgebase — Estimate; exact standard page citation not available in retrieved PMI sources

Core Meaning: Techniques used to develop approximate values for project work such as cost, duration, or resources.
Expansion: Estimating methods are the approaches a project team uses to forecast values when exact figures are not yet known. PMI commonly recognises methods such as analogous, parametric, bottom-up, and three-point estimating, selected based on available data, uncertainty, and required precision. The method chosen influences the effort needed, the confidence level of the result, and how estimates are refined over time.
Contrast: Estimate — estimating methods are the techniques used to create an estimate; an estimate is the resulting approximation.
Example: A project manager starts with analogous estimating from a prior office move, then refines key work packages with bottom-up estimates once requirements are clearer.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The process of developing an approximate value for cost, duration, resources, or other project variables.
Expansion: Estimation is the broader practice of approximating project values when exact figures are not yet known. It can be applied to cost, schedule, resources, and other planning variables. PMI uses estimation to support planning, forecasting, and decision-making under uncertainty.
Contrast: Estimate — estimation is the process of creating an approximate value, while an estimate is the resulting value or output.
Example: During planning, the team performs estimation for development effort, testing duration, and procurement lead time.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Acting with integrity and displaying behavior that is honest.
Expansion: Ethics in PMI is framed as integrity and honest behavior in project work. It underpins trustworthy decision-making, stakeholder relationships, and responsible use of authority. Within PMI practice, ethics informs how project professionals handle conflicts of interest, reporting, procurement, and accountability.
Contrast: Compliance — ethics is about principled conduct and integrity; compliance is about following required rules and procedures.
Example: A project manager discloses a conflict of interest when evaluating a vendor proposal instead of influencing the award decision privately.
Related Terms: Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct
Source: PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct / PMI Knowledgebase — Ethics; exact standard page citation not available in retrieved PMI sources

Core Meaning: The process of determining whether a deployed solution is delivering the intended business value and outcomes.
Expansion: Evaluate solution performance is the business analysis process of assessing whether a solution is achieving the goals or objectives it was intended to support. PMI positions it as a post-release evaluation activity that may be performed in project, program, or portfolio contexts when decision makers need evidence of actual value. It examines both the results being achieved and the reasons those results are or are not being achieved.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Evaluate solution performance is the disciplined assessment of whether a solution, once in operation, is producing the expected business outcomes. It compares actual performance data against defined evaluation criteria and intended value.

Why it matters and where it applies: delivery success does not automatically mean value realization. This process matters because a solution can meet requirements and still fail to improve business performance, customer outcomes, or operational efficiency, so it is used after release and in ongoing value reviews.

The most common misconception: that this is just testing or a closeout check. Testing verifies whether the solution works as specified, while evaluating solution performance examines whether the solution is worthwhile and effective in the real business environment.

How it shows up in real projects: after launch, teams review adoption, cycle time, error rates, customer satisfaction, or financial outcomes, then recommend corrective action, enhancement, replacement, or a revised evaluation approach.
Contrast: Verify requirements — verify requirements checks whether the solution meets specified requirements; evaluate solution performance checks whether it delivers the intended business value.
Example: After a claims-processing platform goes live, the team measures handling time, rework rates, and user adoption to determine whether the solution is producing the expected improvement.
Source: The PMI Guide to Business Analysis — Evaluate Solution Performance, pp. 280-282

Core Meaning: The standards or measures used to assess options, deliverables, or results against desired requirements.
Expansion: Evaluation criteria define how something will be judged during review, selection, or acceptance. They help make assessments consistent and transparent by tying judgment to agreed measures rather than opinion alone. In project practice, they are used in areas such as procurement, quality review, and acceptance-related evaluation.
Contrast: Acceptance criteria — evaluation criteria are used to assess and compare; acceptance criteria define the conditions that must be met for acceptance.
Example: A procurement team scores vendor proposals using evaluation criteria for technical capability, delivery approach, and risk management.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A significant occurrence or happening that may affect the project.
Expansion: An event is a point-in-time occurrence that can influence project work, decisions, or outcomes. In project contexts, events may be internal or external and can create opportunities, risks, or triggers for action. They are important because they often prompt responses, updates, or governance decisions. PMI uses event-related concepts in risk and control discussions, where an occurrence can change the project environment.
Contrast: Issue — an event is an occurrence that may affect the project, while an issue is a current condition or problem requiring attention.
Example: A regulatory announcement during a project becomes an event that triggers a review of compliance requirements and schedule impacts.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — Event

Core Meaning: A specific item, scope element, or condition that is explicitly stated as outside the boundaries of the project or deliverable.
Expansion: Exclusions identify what the project will not deliver, support, or include, making the scope boundary clearer for stakeholders. PMI uses exclusions in scope definition because clarity about what is out of scope is as important as clarity about what is in scope. They reduce misunderstanding, protect against uncontrolled expansion, and support acceptance discussions. Well-written exclusions are especially important where adjacent teams or vendors may assume responsibility sits with the project when it does not.
Deep Dive:
What it is: an exclusion is an explicit statement of non-inclusion. It defines work, features, services, or responsibilities that the project is not intended to provide.

Why it matters and where it applies: exclusions matter because many scope disputes come from assumptions about omitted work rather than disagreement about included work. They are especially useful in charters, scope statements, contracts, and vendor-managed initiatives where boundary clarity affects cost, schedule, and accountability.

The most common misconception: that exclusions are optional fine print or a defensive legal note. In practice they are a practical scope-management tool that helps align expectations and prevent hidden commitments.

How it shows up in real projects: a system implementation may include software configuration and training but exclude legacy data cleansing, custom integrations beyond a defined list, or post-go-live support after a stated handoff date.
Contrast: Constraint — an exclusion states what is outside scope, while a constraint limits how in-scope work can be performed.
Example: A CRM implementation project states that migrating historical email archives is excluded, so stakeholders cannot assume those records will be loaded as part of go-live.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition, section 5.3.3.1 Project Scope Statement, page 157

Core Meaning: The process group in which project work is performed to complete the work defined in the project management plan.
Expansion: Executing is where the project team carries out the planned work to produce deliverables and manage stakeholder engagement, communications, quality, and resources. It turns plans into actual outputs and is often the longest and most visible part of the project. PMI treats it as the process group where most project effort is spent and where coordination across people, work, and change becomes real.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Executing is the process group focused on performing the work authorized in the project management plan. It includes directing and managing project work, acquiring and developing the team, managing communications, and producing deliverables.

Why it matters and where it applies: this is where project intent becomes tangible output. It applies in every delivery approach because even adaptive and hybrid projects still require coordinated work, stakeholder interaction, and control of quality and change.

The most common misconception: that executing is just doing the tasks. In PMI terms it also includes coordination, leadership, communication, quality management, and integration of work across the project.

How it shows up in real projects: teams build, test, review, approve, and hand over deliverables while the project manager resolves issues, manages stakeholder expectations, and keeps work aligned with the plan.
Contrast: Planning — executing performs the authorised work; planning defines how the work will be done.
Example: During a system rollout, the team configures the software, migrates data, trains users, and manages defects while the project manager coordinates approvals and communications.
Source: PMBOK Guide / PMI Knowledgebase — Executing; exact standard page citation not available in retrieved PMI sources

Core Meaning: The process group in which project work is performed to complete the work defined in the project management plan.
Expansion: The Executing Process Group is where the team carries out the planned work to produce deliverables and implement the approved approach for the project. It includes directing work, managing team performance, engaging stakeholders, managing communications, and applying quality and resource practices during delivery. In PMI's process-based view, this is where planned intent becomes actual outputs and where much of the project's time and budget are consumed.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The Executing Process Group is the collection of processes used to perform the work authorised in the project management plan. It covers the coordinated action needed to create deliverables and keep people, resources, and communications moving.

Why it matters and where it applies: this is the point where the project produces tangible results rather than plans alone. It applies across predictive, agile, and hybrid environments because every project still needs work to be carried out, stakeholders engaged, and outputs produced.

The most common misconception: that executing only means team members doing assigned tasks. In PMI terms it also includes leadership, communication, quality management, knowledge sharing, and integrating multiple streams of work while the project unfolds.

How it shows up in real projects: teams build products, configure systems, perform testing, hold working sessions, manage suppliers, communicate status, and address issues while the project manager aligns execution with the plan and approved changes.
Contrast: Planning Process Group — planning defines how the work will be done; executing performs the authorised work and creates the deliverables.
Example: In an enterprise system rollout, the team configures the platform, migrates data, trains users, coordinates vendors, and manages stakeholder communications while producing the approved deliverables.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 3.5, page 25

Core Meaning: The performance of the work defined in the project management plan to produce the project deliverables.
Expansion: Execution is the part of project work where resources are coordinated and activities are carried out to create approved outputs and deliverables. It includes directing people, managing quality, communications, procurements, stakeholder engagement, and implementing planned responses as the work proceeds. Although it is often described as doing the work, effective execution also requires continual coordination with monitoring and controlling. Strong execution turns plans into measurable outcomes.
Contrast: Monitoring and controlling — execution performs the planned work, while monitoring and controlling measures performance and adjusts the work to keep it aligned with objectives.
Example: After planning a system upgrade, the project team configures environments, develops integrations, trains users, and deploys components during execution.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Direct and Manage Project Work; page citation not confirmed in available source

Core Meaning: The conditions that must be met before a project, phase, stage, or process can be formally closed or allowed to proceed.
Expansion: Exit criteria define the measurable conditions used to determine whether work is complete enough to close a phase or move through a review point. They support governance by making continuation and closure decisions more objective and consistent. PMI uses the term in lifecycle, phase gate, and control contexts where clear completion conditions reduce ambiguity and rework.
Contrast: Definition of done — exit criteria govern readiness to complete or transition a broader body of work, while definition of done usually confirms completion of a specific increment or backlog item.
Example: At the end of the design phase, the project cannot move into build until the approved design package, security review, and sponsor signoff satisfy the phase exit criteria.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Seventh Edition, section on phase gates and phase exit criteria, page 40

Core Meaning: A formal review point where defined criteria are checked before authorising continuation, transition, or closure.
Expansion: An exit gate is a governance checkpoint used to decide whether work may proceed beyond a phase, stage, or major milestone. It relies on predefined criteria, decision authority, and evidence of readiness rather than informal judgment alone. PMI presents this idea through phase gates and stage reviews that support disciplined oversight and investment decisions. Exit gates are most useful when organisations need structured control over risk, funding, compliance, or strategic alignment.
Deep Dive:
What it is: an exit gate is a decision point between bodies of work. At that point, leaders review performance, readiness, and business justification before allowing the next stage to begin.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because projects often commit significant time and money after early stages, and a gate reduces the chance of continuing weak or unready work. It is common in product development, construction, regulated environments, and portfolio-funded initiatives.

The most common misconception: that a gate is just a status meeting or administrative approval. In practice a real gate is tied to defined evidence, decision rights, and the possibility of delaying, redirecting, or stopping the effort.

How it shows up in real projects: after feasibility and design, a steering committee may review cost forecasts, major risks, compliance evidence, and scope readiness before approving build and release of the next tranche of funding.
Contrast: Milestone — a milestone marks a significant point in time, while an exit gate is a governance decision point with approval criteria.
Example: A medical device project cannot enter validation testing until the exit gate confirms design controls, traceability, and regulatory documentation are complete.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The ongoing practice of aligning stakeholder expectations with what the project can realistically deliver and when.
Expansion: Expectation management is the practical work of ensuring stakeholders understand likely outcomes, constraints, tradeoffs, and changes throughout the project. It helps reduce misunderstanding, dissatisfaction, and conflict by keeping expectations realistic as scope, risk, schedule, or priorities evolve. In PMI usage it is better understood as a stakeholder engagement and communication practice than as a named formal process.
Contrast: Stakeholder engagement — expectation management focuses specifically on aligning what stakeholders expect; stakeholder engagement is the broader practice of involving and working with stakeholders throughout the project.
Example: When a vendor delay threatens a release date, the project manager resets stakeholder expectations early by explaining schedule impacts and the resulting feature tradeoffs.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A quantitative risk analysis technique that calculates the average outcome of uncertain events in monetary terms.
Expansion: Expected monetary value analysis evaluates uncertain future events by multiplying each possible outcome by its probability and summing the results. PMI uses it in risk analysis to support decisions where threats and opportunities have financial impact. It helps compare alternatives, estimate contingency exposure, and make risk-informed choices using probability-weighted values.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Expected monetary value analysis is a statistical technique used to quantify uncertainty in financial terms. It converts possible risk outcomes into a single probability-weighted value that supports comparison and decision making.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects often face decisions involving uncertain costs, savings, claims, or revenue effects. This technique is used in quantitative risk analysis, decision tree analysis, and contingency planning when leaders need a rational estimate of likely monetary exposure or upside.

The most common misconception: that expected monetary value predicts the exact amount the project will gain or lose. It does not forecast one certain result; it expresses the average expected value across many possible outcomes and is therefore most useful for planning and comparing options.

How it shows up in real projects: a team estimates the probability and cost impact of supplier delay, redesign, or expedited shipping choices, then uses expected monetary value to choose the option with the best risk-adjusted financial outcome.
Contrast: Decision tree analysis — expected monetary value analysis is the probability-weighted calculation itself; decision tree analysis is the broader decision model that often uses expected monetary value at decision branches.
Example: A project team estimates a 30 percent chance of a regulatory delay causing a cost impact of 100000 and a 70 percent chance of no impact, producing an expected monetary value of 30000 for that risk.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge — not fully verified exact page in available PMI search results

Core Meaning: Judgment provided based upon subject matter expertise.
Expansion: Expert judgment is the use of knowledge and experience from qualified individuals to support project decisions, estimates, and analyses. PMI identifies it as a common technique across many processes because projects often require informed interpretation where data alone is insufficient. It is especially useful for tailoring, estimating, risk assessment, and evaluating complex tradeoffs. Its quality depends on the relevance and credibility of the expert and the context in which the judgment is applied.
Contrast: Data analysis — expert judgment relies on informed human expertise, while data analysis relies primarily on quantitative or structured evidence.
Example: A project manager consults a senior architect to judge whether a proposed integration approach is feasible within the current schedule.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — Expert judgment

Core Meaning: Knowledge that can be articulated, documented, and shared in a formal way.
Expansion: Explicit knowledge is information that can be written down, stored, and transferred through documents, procedures, models, or databases. In project environments, it supports repeatability, training, and governance because it can be communicated directly. PMI contrasts it with tacit knowledge, which is harder to codify.
Contrast: Tacit knowledge — explicit knowledge can be documented and shared directly, while tacit knowledge resides in experience and is harder to formalise.
Example: A lessons learned register captures explicit knowledge about a vendor approval process that worked well on a previous project.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) glossary, Explicit knowledge, p. 181

Core Meaning: An opportunity risk response strategy used to ensure that a beneficial event happens.
Expansion: Exploit is an opportunity response strategy chosen when the team wants to eliminate uncertainty around a positive risk by taking action so the benefit is realized. It is the strongest proactive response to an opportunity because the intent is not just to improve the odds but to secure the upside outcome. PMI includes it as a standard strategy for responding to positive risks. It is appropriate when the opportunity is important enough that the project should commit resources to capture it directly.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Exploit is a deliberate effort to make an opportunity occur. The team changes plans, assigns resources, or restructures work so the positive event becomes effectively certain or as close to certain as practicable.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects often leave upside value unrealized because they treat opportunities passively. Exploit matters where a favorable option can materially improve business value, delivery speed, market position, or customer benefit and the project can take direct action to lock that result in.

The most common misconception: that exploit is just the positive version of taking a chance. In fact it is not passive optimism; it is intentional intervention to secure the opportunity.

How it shows up in real projects: a project may bring in top specialists, switch to a faster technical approach, or reserve scarce equipment early so a schedule acceleration opportunity is not left to chance.
Contrast: Enhance — exploit aims to ensure the opportunity occurs, while enhance only increases its probability or impact.
Example: A manufacturer prepurchases a limited-availability machine to guarantee an earlier production start and capture a market window.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition Glossary, exploit, page 244

Core Meaning: A relationship between a project activity and a non-project activity that affects timing or execution.
Expansion: An external dependency exists when project work depends on something outside the direct control of the project team. This may involve regulators, vendors, customers, operational groups, or other external activities. PMI treats it as a scheduling and coordination consideration because it can constrain sequencing, introduce risk, and affect delivery commitments.
Contrast: Internal dependency — an external dependency relies on activities outside the project boundary or team control; an internal dependency exists between activities within the project.
Example: A construction project cannot begin site excavation until a city agency issues the required permit, creating an external dependency on the regulator.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — External dependencies

Core Meaning: A risk that arises from outside the project and is generally beyond the direct control of the project team.
Expansion: External risk comes from sources such as regulation, market conditions, weather, suppliers, or geopolitical events outside the project boundary. PMI uses the term to distinguish risks whose causes are external to the project environment, even though their effects can still be managed. The team therefore emphasises scanning, response planning, reserves, and contingency actions rather than assuming direct control over the source.
Contrast: Internal risk — external risk originates outside the project boundary; internal risk originates within the project, team, or organisation.
Example: A highway project faces external risk when a new environmental permitting rule delays approvals from a government agency.
Source: PMI Lexicon — External risk; specific page not confirmed in available PMI source

Core Meaning: A technique used to guide a group toward a decision, solution, or shared understanding.
Expansion: Facilitation helps a group work productively by structuring discussion, encouraging balanced participation, and keeping attention on the objective. In PMI usage, it is a practical technique for workshops, planning sessions, problem solving, and conflict resolution. It supports better decisions by improving collaboration rather than supplying the content of the decision itself.
Contrast: Expert judgment — facilitation manages the group process for reaching an outcome, while expert judgment contributes specialised knowledge to inform the outcome.
Example: A project manager facilitates a requirements workshop so operations, finance, and IT can agree on the minimum viable scope for a system rollout.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase, term Facilitation

Core Meaning: The cost incurred because a product, service, or result does not conform to requirements or expectations.
Expansion: Failure cost is part of cost of quality and covers the consequences of defects, errors, and nonconforming work. PMI distinguishes internal failure costs, which occur before release or handoff, from external failure costs, which occur after the customer receives the output. It helps teams quantify the financial effect of poor quality and balance prevention, appraisal, and rework decisions.
Contrast: Appraisal cost — failure cost is spent after nonconformance occurs; appraisal cost is spent to assess conformance before failure is discovered.
Example: A software team incurs failure cost when defects found after release require emergency fixes, customer support, and service credits.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — cost of quality discussion; specific page not confirmed in available PMI source

Core Meaning: A backup course of action prepared for use if the primary response proves ineffective or a specific risk event occurs.
Expansion: A fallback plan is activated when the original plan or risk response does not deliver the intended result. In project risk practice, it provides a predefined alternative so the team can respond quickly under adverse conditions.
Contrast: Contingency plan — a contingency plan is the planned response to a defined risk event, while a fallback plan is the backup if that response fails or is insufficient.
Example: A project plans to migrate systems over a weekend, but if the new environment fails validation after cutover, the team executes a fallback plan to restore the legacy platform.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — 6th Edition, p. 443

Core Meaning: A schedule compression technique in which activities or phases normally done in sequence are performed in parallel for at least part of their duration.
Expansion: Fast tracking shortens the schedule by overlapping work that was originally planned sequentially. It can reduce overall project duration without immediately adding resources, but it often increases coordination complexity and the risk of rework. PMI treats it as a schedule compression technique used when time pressure outweighs the added delivery risk.
Contrast: Crashing — fast tracking shortens duration by overlapping activities, while crashing shortens duration by adding resources.
Example: A construction project starts interior fit-out work before all exterior work is fully complete so the overall completion date can be pulled forward.
Source: PMI Lexicon / PMBOK Guide glossary, Fast tracking; page citation not available in retrieved source

Core Meaning: The degree to which a project, option, or solution is practically achievable.
Expansion: Feasibility assesses whether something can realistically be done given technical, financial, schedule, legal, and operational constraints. It is usually examined early to avoid investing in options that cannot succeed.
Contrast: Viability — feasibility asks whether it can be done; viability asks whether it is worth doing and sustainable.
Example: A feasibility study concludes the timeline is achievable only if a second supplier is added.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; defined for glossary completeness

Core Meaning: An assessment of whether a proposed solution, initiative, or project is practical and viable to pursue.
Expansion: Feasibility analysis examines whether the proposed work can realistically be delivered and sustained within relevant constraints. PMI usage commonly includes technical, operational, schedule, cost, and sometimes legal or market considerations as part of that assessment. It is typically used early to support go or no-go decisions before substantial project investment is committed.
Contrast: Business case — feasibility analysis tests whether the proposed solution can work; a business case explains why the investment should be made.
Example: Before launching a new customer portal project, the team assesses integration complexity, support readiness, implementation timing, and expected operating viability.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Feasibility analysis; specific page not confirmed in available PMI source

Core Meaning: A study that produces a potential recommendation to address business needs by examining whether a proposed solution is practical and viable.
Expansion: A feasibility study evaluates whether a proposed initiative is worth pursuing before major investment is committed. PMI describes it as examining factors such as operational feasibility, technology or system feasibility, cost-effectiveness, and timeliness. It supports early decision making by testing whether the proposed path is realistic, supportable, and aligned with the need being addressed.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A feasibility study is an early assessment used to determine whether a proposed project or solution is viable. It explores whether the idea can work in practical, technical, operational, and economic terms before full planning and delivery begin.

Why it matters and where it applies: it helps decision makers avoid committing resources to initiatives that are unrealistic, poorly timed, or unlikely to deliver sufficient value. It is especially important in business cases, pre-project analysis, capital projects, technology change, and transformation efforts where the cost of a wrong start is high.

The most common misconception: that a feasibility study is just a rough estimate or a quick approval note. In practice, it is a structured evaluation of viability, and its purpose is not to justify a preferred answer but to test whether the proposal should proceed, change, or stop.

How it shows up in real projects: teams assess options, assumptions, constraints, expected benefits, implementation practicality, and major risks, then recommend whether to proceed and under what conditions.
Contrast: Business case — a feasibility study tests whether a solution is viable, while a business case justifies why the investment should be made.
Example: Before funding a new enterprise system, an organisation studies technical integration limits, expected operating impact, timeline realism, and cost-effectiveness to decide whether implementation should proceed.
Source: PMI Lexicon / PMBOK-related glossary, Feasibility study; page citation not available in retrieved source

Core Meaning: A distinct capability or characteristic of a product, service, or result that provides value to a user or stakeholder.
Expansion: A feature describes what the product does or enables from a user or stakeholder perspective. In PMI and agile-related usage, features often sit above detailed requirements or backlog items and can be decomposed for planning and delivery. The term focuses on delivered capability rather than the project work performed to create it.
Contrast: Requirement — a feature is a capability the solution provides; a requirement is a condition or capability that the solution or its components must satisfy.
Example: In a banking app project, mobile check deposit is a feature that lets customers submit checks by photographing them.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Feature; specific page not confirmed in available PMI source

Core Meaning: Information about reactions, results, or performance that is used to confirm, adjust, or improve project work.
Expansion: Feedback helps project teams learn whether their actions are producing the intended results. It can come from stakeholders, customers, team members, or performance data, and it is used to refine decisions, communication, and deliverables. PMI links feedback to communication, governance, and checking outcomes, making it a key input for adaptation and control.
Contrast: Status report — feedback is used to improve or adjust work, while a status report primarily communicates progress and current condition.
Example: After a sprint review, users say a screen is confusing, so the team revises the design before the next release.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase, Feedback, page 170

Core Meaning: Formal confirmation that the completed project deliverables meet agreed requirements and are accepted by the customer, sponsor, or authorised stakeholder.
Expansion: Final acceptance is the formal approval that the overall project output satisfies the agreed acceptance criteria and can be handed over or closed. It typically follows verification that required deliverables are complete and conform to scope and quality expectations. In PMI practice it is a key closure outcome because it evidences completion of the work that was authorised.
Contrast: Accepted deliverables — final acceptance is the overall formal approval of the completed project or final product; accepted deliverables are specific outputs approved during execution or closure.
Example: At the end of an ERP rollout, the sponsor signs the final acceptance document after confirming that all agreed functions, training, and transition activities are complete.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Final acceptance; specific page not confirmed in available PMI source

Core Meaning: A report that summarises project performance, results, and lessons at the end of the project.
Expansion: The final report records what the project delivered, how it performed against objectives, and what was learned during execution. It supports formal closure by giving stakeholders a concise end-of-project record. In practice, it also helps organisational learning by preserving outcomes, variances, and recommendations for future work.
Contrast: Lessons learned register — a final report summarises overall project outcomes at closure, while a lessons learned register captures learning in more detailed form across the life of the project.
Example: At the end of an office relocation project, the project manager issues a final report covering scope delivered, budget performance, schedule variance, stakeholder feedback, and key lessons.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase, term Final report

Core Meaning: The obtaining and management of funds needed to support project work.
Expansion: Financing concerns how money is secured and made available so project activities can proceed. In project settings, it connects the business case and investment decision to the practical ability to execute the work. It is distinct from budgeting because budgeting allocates planned spending, while financing provides the money that makes that spending possible.
Contrast: Budget — financing is about obtaining and structuring the money; a budget is the planned allocation and control of that money.
Example: An infrastructure project secures bridge financing to cover design and early procurement costs before long-term capital funds are released.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Financing, p. 170

Core Meaning: The point in time associated with the completion of a schedule activity.
Expansion: Finish Date identifies when a scheduled activity is planned or calculated to be complete. It is used in schedule development and control alongside related terms such as early finish and late finish. Finish dates help teams understand sequencing, monitor progress, and evaluate the effect of delays on downstream work.
Contrast: Early Finish — Finish Date is the general completion point for an activity, while Early Finish is the earliest possible completion date based on schedule logic.
Example: A testing activity is planned to finish on 18 July, and that date is recorded as its finish date in the schedule.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Finish date, page 181

Core Meaning: A schedule constraint that sets the earliest allowable finish date for an activity.
Expansion: Finish not earlier than prevents an activity from finishing before a specified date. It is used when external commitments, approvals, or timing windows require work to end no sooner than a given point in time.
Contrast: Finish not later than — Finish not earlier than sets the earliest allowed finish, while Finish not later than sets the latest allowed finish.
Example: A regulatory review must not finish before the public comment period closes, so the activity is constrained with finish not earlier than.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Finish not earlier than, page 181

Core Meaning: A schedule constraint that sets the latest allowable finish date for an activity.
Expansion: Finish not later than prevents an activity from finishing after a specified date. It is used when a deadline, milestone, or external dependency requires completion by a fixed point in time.
Contrast: Finish not earlier than — Finish not later than sets the latest allowed finish, while Finish not earlier than sets the earliest allowed finish.
Example: A contract deliverable must be completed by the end of the quarter, so the schedule applies a finish not later than constraint.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Finish not later than, page 181

Core Meaning: A schedule constraint that requires an activity to finish on a specified date.
Expansion: Finish on fixes the completion date of an activity to a specific point in time. It is used when the finish date is externally imposed and cannot move without violating the schedule requirement.
Contrast: Finish not later than — Finish on requires completion on one exact date, while Finish not later than allows completion on or before a date.
Example: A project milestone tied to a product launch is constrained to finish on the launch date.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Finish on, page 181

Core Meaning: A logical relationship in which a successor activity cannot finish until a predecessor activity has finished.
Expansion: Finish-to-Finish is a schedule dependency that links the completion of one activity to the completion of another. It does not require the two activities to start together, only that the successor cannot finish before the predecessor finishes. PMI includes it as one of the four core precedence relationships used in schedule network logic.
Contrast: Finish-to-Start relationship — Finish-to-Finish controls when the successor can finish, while Finish-to-Start controls when the successor can start.
Example: Final proofreading cannot finish until final layout updates are complete, so the two activities are linked with a Finish-to-Finish relationship.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Finish-to-Finish, page 181

Core Meaning: A logical relationship in which a successor activity cannot finish until a predecessor activity has finished.
Expansion: Finish-to-Finish is one of the precedence relationships used in schedule network analysis. It links the completion of one activity to the completion of another rather than linking their start dates. Project managers use it to model work that may proceed in parallel but must conclude in a defined sequence.
Contrast: Finish-to-Start relationship — Finish-to-Finish controls when the successor can finish; Finish-to-Start controls when the successor can start.
Example: Quality review can run while document drafting is underway, but the review activity cannot finish until drafting is complete, so the tasks are linked Finish-to-Finish.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Finish-to-Finish relationship, p. 127

Core Meaning: A precedence relationship in which a successor activity cannot start until its predecessor activity has finished.
Expansion: Finish-to-start is the most common logical relationship used in project schedule network diagrams. It defines sequence by making the start of one activity dependent on the completion of another. Project teams use it to build realistic schedules, analyse dependencies, and evaluate the effects of delays across the network.
Contrast: Start-to-start — finish-to-start requires the predecessor to finish before the successor starts, while start-to-start only requires the predecessor to start before the successor can start.
Example: In a building project, final electrical inspection must finish before the occupancy permit application can start.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Seventh Edition / Process Groups: A Practice Guide, schedule network relationship definitions, exact page citation to confirm in PMI source

Core Meaning: A logical relationship in which a successor activity cannot start until a predecessor activity has finished.
Expansion: Finish-to-Start is the most common dependency used in project schedules. It defines a handoff in which one piece of work must be completed before the next can begin. PMI treats it as one of the core logical relationship types used to build schedule networks and analyse project timing.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Finish-to-Start is a dependency between two activities where the predecessor must finish before the successor can start. It is the default sequencing logic used in many schedules because it reflects straightforward task handoffs.

Why it matters and where it applies: it allows teams to model real execution order, calculate the critical path, and forecast how delays will affect later work. It is widely used in construction, systems implementation, product delivery, and any project where one output enables the next step.

The most common misconception: that every dependency should be Finish-to-Start. Some work is better represented with Start-to-Start or Finish-to-Finish relationships, and forcing everything into Finish-to-Start logic can distort the schedule.

How it shows up in real projects: approval must finish before procurement can start, code development must finish before formal system testing begins, or excavation must finish before foundation pouring starts.
Contrast: Start-to-Start relationship — Finish-to-Start requires the predecessor to finish before the successor starts, while Start-to-Start requires only that the predecessor start before the successor starts.
Example: A data migration activity cannot start until the data cleansing activity has finished.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Finish-to-Start, page 117; glossary index page 181

Core Meaning: A fixed-price contract in which the buyer pays an agreed total amount for the specified work regardless of the seller's actual cost.
Expansion: A Firm Fixed Price contract establishes a total price in advance for a defined scope of work. Under this arrangement, the seller generally bears most of the cost risk because payment does not increase simply because actual effort or cost is higher than expected. It is most suitable when requirements are stable, specifications are clear, and changes are expected to be limited.
Contrast: Cost-reimbursable contract — an FFP contract pays a set total amount for defined work, while a cost-reimbursable contract pays allowable actual costs subject to the contract terms.
Example: A buyer contracts a supplier to deliver a standard payroll interface for a single agreed amount based on a detailed statement of work and acceptance criteria.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 725

Core Meaning: A scheduling constraint or condition in which the activity duration remains set and changes are managed by adjusting resources or scope rather than elapsed time.
Expansion: Fixed duration means the planned time span for the work is held constant. If performance issues arise, the team usually changes staffing, productivity assumptions, or work content instead of extending the duration.
Contrast: Fixed units — fixed duration holds time constant, while fixed units holds the assigned resource quantity constant.
Example: A five-day user training event must occur within one workweek, so the team adds facilitators rather than extending the session length.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A fee component in a contract that remains constant and does not vary with the seller's actual cost experience.
Expansion: Fixed fee usually appears in cost-reimbursable contracting, especially cost-plus-fixed-fee arrangements. It represents a stable amount of seller profit unless the contract itself is formally changed.
Contrast: Incentive fee — a fixed fee remains constant, while an incentive fee changes based on defined performance results.
Example: A specialist engineering firm is reimbursed for allowable project costs and receives a fixed fee for managing the work.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 12.1.1.6

Term: Fixed Price with Economic Price Adjustment Contract (FPEPA)
Core Meaning: A fixed-price contract that permits predefined price adjustments when specified economic conditions change.
Expansion: An FPEPA contract starts with a fixed-price arrangement but includes clauses that adjust the price if external economic conditions, such as inflation or commodity-index shifts, move beyond agreed thresholds. It is useful for long-duration procurements where market volatility could otherwise make a pure fixed-price agreement impractical. The adjustment rules are defined in advance so the buyer and seller share certain external risks in a controlled way.
Contrast: Firm-Fixed-Price Contract — FPEPA allows limited price changes for specified economic triggers, while firm-fixed-price keeps the price unchanged throughout the contract.
Example: A multi-year construction materials contract allows the contract price to be adjusted if a published steel-price index rises or falls beyond the threshold stated in the agreement.
Source: PMBOK Guide Glossary, Fixed-Price with Economic Price Adjustment Contract, p. 181

Core Meaning: A type of activity in which the number of resource units assigned remains constant regardless of the amount of work or duration.
Expansion: Fixed units means the resource allocation stays constant even if the activity duration or work changes. This is useful when a specific staffing level or equipment count must be maintained for the activity. PMI uses it as one of the activity type settings that influence how schedule calculations respond to changes.
Contrast: Fixed work — Fixed units keeps the resource units constant, while fixed work keeps the total effort constant.
Example: A concrete pour is planned with two crane operators throughout the activity, so the resource units remain fixed even if the duration shifts.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A type of activity in which the amount of work remains constant regardless of the number of resources assigned.
Expansion: Fixed work means the total effort required for the activity does not change when resources are added or removed. If more people are assigned, the duration may decrease, but the work itself stays the same. PMI uses this concept when discussing activity types and how resource assignment affects schedule calculations.
Contrast: Fixed duration — Fixed work keeps the effort constant, while fixed duration keeps the time constant regardless of resource assignment.
Example: A 40-hour requirements review remains a 40-hour effort whether one analyst or two analysts are assigned, although the elapsed duration may change.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A contract type that sets a total price for a defined product, service, or result, with most cost risk borne by the seller.
Expansion: A fixed-price contract establishes a predetermined amount to be paid for agreed scope, deliverables, or outcomes. It is most effective when requirements are stable enough to define the work clearly and evaluate performance against specific acceptance criteria. PMI treats it as a major procurement contract family, with variants such as firm-fixed-price and fixed-price incentive arrangements.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A fixed-price contract is a procurement agreement in which the seller commits to deliver specified work for an agreed total price. The buyer gains greater cost predictability, while the seller accepts more responsibility for controlling execution costs.

Why it matters and where it applies: it is widely used when scope can be defined with reasonable precision, such as construction packages, equipment purchases, and standardised services. It helps organisations manage budgets and compare bids, but it also makes scope definition, change control, and acceptance criteria especially important.

The most common misconception: that fixed-price means no risk remains for the buyer. In practice, buyer risk still exists through poor scope definition, aggressive seller assumptions, quality disputes, change requests, and claims when actual conditions differ from what was specified.
