Core Meaning: A performance domain is a group of related activities that are critical for the effective delivery of project outcomes.
Expansion: In PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, performance domains replace the idea that project management is best understood only through process groups and knowledge areas. They describe broad areas of attention, such as stakeholders, planning, delivery, and uncertainty, that interact throughout the life of the project. PMI uses them to emphasise outcomes, systems thinking, and tailoring rather than a single prescriptive sequence of processes.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A performance domain is a lens for managing project work in a holistic way. Rather than treating project management as a checklist of isolated processes, PMI groups related capabilities and activities into domains that together influence project performance.

Why it matters and where it applies: performance domains help practitioners think across the whole project system, especially in environments that are adaptive, hybrid, or highly tailored. They support better judgment by focusing attention on what must function well for the project to deliver value, not just on whether a process was completed.

The most common misconception: that a performance domain is just a renamed knowledge area or a sequential project phase. It is neither; domains are interdependent areas of ongoing attention that can operate simultaneously and be tailored to context.

How it shows up in real projects: a project team may strengthen stakeholder engagement, adapt planning detail, refine delivery practices, and manage uncertainty at the same time, using the performance domains as an integrated frame for decision-making.
Contrast: Knowledge area — a performance domain is an outcome-oriented area of ongoing attention in PMBOK Seventh Edition, while a knowledge area is a structured subject area used prominently in earlier PMI frameworks.
Example: A digital-transformation project uses the stakeholder, planning, delivery, and uncertainty performance domains together to balance executive alignment, rolling-wave plans, iterative releases, and emerging risks.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Seventh Edition, section on Project Performance Domains, p. 21

Core Meaning: Performance data that has been analyzed, integrated, and placed in context so it can support interpretation and decisions.
Expansion: Performance information is produced when raw project data is compared, aggregated, or evaluated against plans, baselines, thresholds, or expectations. It helps reveal trends, variances, root issues, and likely outcomes rather than just reporting isolated facts. PMI uses the distinction to show the progression from measurement to analysis to management action.
Contrast: Performance data — performance information is the analyzed and contextualized form of raw performance data.
Example: After comparing actual progress and cost data to the baseline, the project manager determines that the project is behind schedule and likely to exceed the next milestone budget.
Source: PMBOK Guide — work performance information; page citation not available in retrieved source

Core Meaning: The process of collecting and comparing project results against approved baselines to assess how the project is performing.
Expansion: Performance measurement compares actual results with planned values so the team can see cost, schedule, and other performance variances. It supports monitoring and controlling by turning raw status data into insight about progress and trend. In PMI usage, it is closely associated with earned value, baselines, and work performance information. It helps determine whether corrective or preventive action is needed.
Contrast: Performance measurement baseline — performance measurement is the act of assessing results; the baseline is the approved reference used for that assessment.
Example: A project manager reviews earned value results each month to compare actual schedule and cost performance against the approved baseline and decide whether recovery action is needed.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition / Process Groups: A Practice Guide, glossary entry for performance measurement; PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 7.4 and glossary, page citation to be confirmed against edition in use

Core Meaning: An approved integrated scope, schedule, and cost baseline used to compare actual results against planned performance.
Expansion: The performance measurement baseline combines the approved scope, schedule, and cost baselines into one reference point for measuring project performance. It is used to compare planned and actual results and to support earned value analysis and performance reporting. Because it is an approved baseline, changes to it should go through formal change control. It helps the team and stakeholders see whether the project is on track and where corrective action may be needed.
Contrast: Cost baseline — the cost baseline covers planned spending only; the performance measurement baseline integrates scope, schedule, and cost for performance comparison.
Example: A construction project uses the approved performance measurement baseline to compare earned value against planned work and actual costs each month.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — Performance measurement baseline, page 95-96

Core Meaning: Quantitative or qualitative indicators used to assess how well project work, delivery, or outcomes are performing against planned expectations.
Expansion: Performance measures are the metrics and indicators used to evaluate progress, efficiency, quality, schedule status, cost status, and achievement of intended results. PMI uses measurement as a core management practice because decisions depend on evidence rather than impressions. The specific measures vary by context, but they are valuable only when they are clearly defined, relevant to objectives, and tied to meaningful thresholds or targets.
Contrast: Measurement baseline — performance measures are the indicators used to assess status; the baseline is the approved reference point they are often compared against.
Example: A project manager tracks schedule performance index, defect escape rate, and stakeholder satisfaction trend to determine whether a software release remains healthy.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition — performance measures entry/page citation to be confirmed in PMI Lexicon

Core Meaning: A project document or communication that presents performance information such as status, progress, forecasts, and variances to stakeholders.
Expansion: A performance report communicates how the project is performing against approved plans and expected outcomes. In PMI practice it may include measures such as schedule and cost variance, milestone status, risk information, forecasts, and narrative interpretation for decision-making. Its purpose is not just to display data but to support governance, corrective action, and stakeholder alignment.
Contrast: Work performance data — performance reports interpret and communicate analysed performance information; work performance data are the raw observations and measurements.
Example: A monthly steering-committee report shows SPI and CPI trends, major milestone slippage, key risks, and a forecasted completion date.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Glossary — exact page citation requires source confirmation

Core Meaning: The process of collecting, organizing, and communicating analyzed performance information to stakeholders.
Expansion: Performance reporting converts project analysis into forms stakeholders can understand and act on, such as dashboards, status reports, and trend summaries. It communicates progress, variances, forecasts, risks, and issues in ways matched to governance and stakeholder needs. In PMI practice, it is essential because insight has limited value unless it is distributed clearly and at the right decision points.
Contrast: Performance information — performance reporting is the communication of analyzed information to stakeholders, not the information itself.
Example: A project manager issues a monthly sponsor report that summarizes earned value results, milestone slippage, top risks, and corrective actions.
Source: PMBOK Guide — work performance reports; page citation not available in retrieved source

Core Meaning: A formal authorization from an external authority that allows specific project work to proceed under defined legal, regulatory, safety, or environmental conditions.
Expansion: In project management, a permit is an external approval the project may need before performing certain work or operating an asset. It matters because scope, schedule, cost, and compliance risk can all be affected if permitting requirements are missed or delayed.
Contrast: Approval — a permit is typically issued by a regulator or other authority outside the project governance structure; an approval may come from internal project stakeholders.
Example: A construction project cannot begin excavation until the municipality issues the required excavation and road-occupancy permits.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A realistic archetype representing a user or stakeholder group so the team can better understand needs, behaviours, and priorities when defining and delivering value.
Expansion: A persona is a synthesized representation of a target user or stakeholder segment built from research, observation, or analysis. In PMI-related practice, especially business analysis, product, and adaptive environments, personas help teams frame requirements, features, and acceptance decisions around real user needs rather than abstract assumptions. They are useful when outcomes depend on user adoption, experience, or differentiated needs across stakeholder groups.
Contrast: Stakeholder — a persona is a representative archetype of a user or group; a stakeholder is an actual person, group, or organization affected by or able to influence the work.
Example: For an internal HR system project, the team creates personas for a hiring manager, a recruiter, and a candidate to shape workflows and prioritise features.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Program Evaluation and Review Technique is a schedule analysis method that uses uncertain activity-duration estimates to model likely completion timing.
Expansion: PERT applies three-point duration estimates such as optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic values to represent uncertainty in schedule planning. In PMI usage, it is a recognised scheduling technique within network-based analysis rather than a complete delivery approach. It is most useful when activity durations are difficult to predict with confidence.
Contrast: Critical path method — PERT emphasises uncertainty in duration estimates; critical path method identifies the longest path that determines planned project duration.
Example: A research project estimates prototype testing at 5, 10, and 18 days and uses PERT-based analysis to understand likely schedule impact.

Figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='30' y1='150' x2='300' y2='150' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><path d='M50,150 C95,150 110,46 138,46 C176,46 235,150 290,150' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='2' fill='none'/><line x1='60' y1='150' x2='60' y2='138' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='138' y1='150' x2='138' y2='48' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><line x1='180' y1='150' x2='180' y2='96' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.4' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><line x1='280' y1='150' x2='280' y2='138' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><text x='60' y='166' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>O</text><text x='138' y='166' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>M</text><text x='180' y='166' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>E</text><text x='280' y='166' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>P</text><text x='160' y='28' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>E = (O + 4M + P) / 6</text></svg>
Figure.caption: Optimistic, Most likely, Pessimistic - E is the weighted expected value.

Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — index entry for Program Evaluation and Review Technique, pp. 45, 97, 99-100, 107, 115

Core Meaning: A collection of logically related project activities that culminates in the completion of one or more deliverables.
Expansion: A phase divides a project into manageable segments with a clear purpose and set of outcomes. Phases often end with a review, approval, or decision point before the project proceeds. They help organize planning, control, and governance across the life cycle.
Contrast: Stage — a phase is a formal segment of the project life cycle; stage is often used informally for a similar concept.
Example: An infrastructure project uses design, procurement, construction, and commissioning phases to manage work and approvals.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — phase

Core Meaning: A review at the end of a phase in which a decision is made to continue, revise, or end the project or program.
Expansion: A phase gate is a formal decision point used to assess whether the work is ready to move forward. It supports governance by checking deliverables, risks, business justification, and readiness before the next phase begins. It is commonly used in stage-based and predictive environments where control at transition points matters.
Contrast: Milestone — a milestone marks a significant point in time; a phase gate is a formal review and decision point at a phase boundary.
Example: At the end of design, a steering committee reviews scope maturity, budget status, and risks before approving the project to enter build.

Figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='10' y='52' width='56' height='34' rx='4' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='38' y='73' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>Phase 1</text><rect x='106' y='52' width='56' height='34' rx='4' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='134' y='73' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>Phase 2</text><rect x='202' y='52' width='56' height='34' rx='4' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='230' y='73' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>Phase 3</text><line x1='66' y1='69' x2='74' y2='69' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='98' y1='69' x2='106' y2='69' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='162' y1='69' x2='170' y2='69' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='194' y1='69' x2='202' y2='69' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><polygon points='86,56 98,69 86,82 74,69' fill='none' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.4'/><polygon points='182,56 194,69 182,82 170,69' fill='none' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.4'/><polygon points='278,56 290,69 278,82 266,69' fill='none' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='86' y='102' fill='#B5462F' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>gate</text><text x='182' y='102' fill='#B5462F' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>gate</text><text x='278' y='102' fill='#B5462F' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>gate</text><text x='150' y='126' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>each gate: go / hold / recycle / kill</text></svg>
Figure.caption: Phases (boxes) are separated by gates (diamonds) where the project is continued or stopped.

Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition — phase gate, page 556

Core Meaning: A phase review is a formal assessment at the end of a project phase used to evaluate results and support a continuation decision.
Expansion: A phase review examines whether phase deliverables, performance, risks, and readiness satisfy the criteria for moving forward. It is used to support decisions such as continue, rework, hold, or terminate. In PMI-aligned practice, it serves as a governance checkpoint between major portions of work.
Contrast: Phase gate — a phase review is the evaluation activity; a phase gate is the control point or decision mechanism associated with moving between phases.
Example: After completing design, a product team holds a phase review to confirm scope maturity, budget status, and technical readiness before authorising build work.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An earned value measure of progress based on the actual physical completion of work rather than time elapsed or cost spent.
Expansion: Physical percent complete estimates how much of a deliverable or work package is truly finished in observable terms. PMI uses it in earned value and progress measurement because it ties completion to tangible accomplishment rather than effort consumed. It is most useful where work can be objectively inspected, such as construction, manufacturing, installation, or equipment deployment.
Contrast: Percent complete — physical percent complete is based on observable completed work; generic percent complete is often a broader and sometimes more subjective progress estimate.
Example: If 30 of 100 network devices have been fully installed and accepted, the physical percent complete for that installation package is 30 percent.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Physical resources are the equipment, materials, supplies, facilities, and other tangible nonhuman assets required to perform project work.
Expansion: PMI distinguishes physical resources from team resources because they are tangible assets rather than people. They must be planned, acquired, assigned, used, monitored, and released in a controlled way to support project execution. Their availability often affects schedule feasibility, cost performance, and sequencing decisions.
Contrast: Team resources — physical resources are tangible nonhuman assets, while team resources are the people assigned to the project.
Example: A construction project plans cranes, concrete, scaffolding, and temporary site offices as physical resources needed for successive work packages.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — index references for physical resources, pp. 79-80, 83, 92

Core Meaning: A proposed means of accomplishing something.
Expansion: A plan describes how work is intended to be carried out to achieve an objective. In PMI usage, plans can exist at many levels, from a high-level approach to detailed subsidiary plans. They provide direction, coordination, and a basis for control as the project progresses.
Contrast: Baseline — a plan is the proposed approach; a baseline is the approved reference version used for comparison and control.
Example: A project team creates a plan for how requirements will be gathered, reviewed, and approved before design begins.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — plan, page 153

Core Meaning: Plan Communications Management is the process of developing an appropriate approach and plan for project communications based on stakeholder information needs.
Expansion: This process determines what information will be communicated, to whom, when, in what format, and through which channels. It aligns project communication methods with stakeholder expectations, governance requirements, and the operating environment. PMI treats it as a planning process that creates the communications management plan and improves the likelihood that the right people receive the right information at the right time.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Plan Communications Management is the planning process that defines the project's communication strategy, communication requirements, methods, frequency, ownership, and escalation paths. It turns stakeholder information needs into a practical communication model for the project.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects fail when information is late, unclear, excessive, or sent to the wrong audience. This process matters in all delivery environments because sponsors, customers, team members, and governance bodies need different information at different times.

The most common misconception: that communications planning is just creating a status report template or meeting calendar. In practice it is a broader design decision about information flow, transparency, timing, audience needs, confidentiality, and decision support.

How it shows up in real projects: a team defines weekly dashboards for leadership, daily coordination channels for delivery staff, issue escalation routes, decision logs, and tailored stakeholder updates across the project lifecycle.
Contrast: Communications management plan — Plan Communications Management is the process; the communications management plan is the documented output it produces.
Example: In an enterprise system rollout, the project manager maps executives to monthly steering updates, business users to change-readiness briefings, and the delivery team to daily coordination channels and weekly risk reviews.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Plan Communications Management, pp. 70, 72, 73

Core Meaning: The process of documenting project procurement decisions, specifying the approach, and identifying potential sellers.
Expansion: This process determines what will be procured, how procurement will be conducted, and what criteria will be used to evaluate sellers. It helps align procurement strategy with project needs, constraints, and governance requirements. The output supports later solicitation, selection, and contract administration activities.
Contrast: Conduct procurements — planning defines the approach and criteria; conducting procurements executes the seller selection and contracting work.
Example: A project team decides to outsource testing, defines the evaluation criteria, and identifies qualified vendors before issuing a request for proposal.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — Plan Procurement Management process, page 59

Core Meaning: Plan Resource Management is the process of defining how project resources will be estimated, acquired, managed, and controlled.
Expansion: This process establishes the approach for managing both team resources and physical resources across the project lifecycle. It clarifies roles, responsibilities, reporting relationships, acquisition methods, and control practices before intensive execution begins. PMI treats it as foundational because effective delivery depends on having the right people and tangible resources available when needed and governed appropriately.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Plan Resource Management sets the rules and approach for how the project will obtain, organise, use, and oversee resources. It covers both people and physical assets, including roles, responsibilities, resource calendars, and management expectations.

Why it matters and where it applies: resource problems are a common source of delay, overload, conflict, and cost variance. This process matters in any project where scarce skills, shared equipment, facilities, or materials must be coordinated across time.

The most common misconception: that it is only a staffing exercise focused on who is on the team. In practice it also addresses physical resources and defines the management framework that later supports estimating, acquiring, developing, and controlling resources.

How it shows up in real projects: the project team documents reporting lines, decision authority, training needs, equipment booking rules, resource release timing, and how shortages or conflicts will be escalated and resolved.
Contrast: Resource management plan — Plan Resource Management is the process; the resource management plan is the output that documents the agreed approach.
Example: For a manufacturing-line upgrade, the project manager defines how engineers, technicians, shutdown windows, cranes, and specialist tools will be requested, scheduled, approved, and tracked throughout the implementation.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Plan Resource Management, p. 9

Core Meaning: The process of defining how risk management activities will be conducted for a project.
Expansion: Plan Risk Management establishes the approach, roles, tools, reporting formats, and timing that will be used to manage project risk. In PMI's process framework, it is performed early so later risk identification, analysis, response planning, and monitoring are consistent and fit the project's context. It helps tailor risk work to the project's size, complexity, stakeholder expectations, and governance needs.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Plan Risk Management is the process that sets the rules of engagement for handling uncertainty on a project. It defines how the team will identify, analyse, respond to, monitor, and communicate risks.

Why it matters and where it applies: without an agreed risk approach, teams often handle threats and opportunities inconsistently or too late. The process is relevant across predictive, adaptive, and hybrid environments, though the degree of formality varies with context.

The most common misconception: that this process is the same as identifying risks or creating the risk register. In fact, it comes first and focuses on the method for managing risk, not on listing individual risks themselves.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager documents risk categories, probability and impact scales, escalation thresholds, reporting cadence, and ownership rules before the team begins detailed risk identification and analysis.
Contrast: Identify Risks — Plan Risk Management defines the approach for managing risk; Identify Risks discovers and documents specific uncertain events or conditions.
Example: Before starting a plant-upgrade project, the team agrees on risk workshops, scoring scales, reporting thresholds, and who must approve contingency use, then records that approach in the risk management plan.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / Process from PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition, Section 11.1, Plan Risk Management, p. 401

Core Meaning: The process of developing options, selecting strategies, and agreeing on actions to address overall project risk exposure and individual project risks.
Expansion: This process turns risk analysis into actionable response plans. It identifies strategies for threats and opportunities and assigns owners and timing for response actions. The result is a set of planned responses that can be integrated into project work and monitored over time.
Contrast: Identify risks — identifying risks finds uncertainty; planning risk responses decides what to do about it.
Example: After identifying a likely supplier delay, the team plans a response by qualifying a backup vendor and adding schedule contingency.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — Plan Risk Responses process, page 442

Core Meaning: The process of developing approaches to involve project stakeholders based on their needs, expectations, interests, and potential impact on the project.
Expansion: Plan Stakeholder Engagement is the planning process used to define how stakeholders will be engaged throughout the project. It translates stakeholder analysis into a practical approach for gaining appropriate support, participation, and alignment. PMI treats it as a distinct process within project stakeholder management, not merely a communications activity.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Plan Stakeholder Engagement is a planning process that determines how to engage stakeholders throughout the project or phase. Its main output is the stakeholder engagement plan, which sets out strategies for involving stakeholders in ways that support project success.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects succeed or fail partly through stakeholder behaviour, not just through technical execution. This process matters wherever stakeholder influence, expectations, resistance, or support can affect outcomes, which means it applies broadly across predictive, adaptive, and hybrid environments.

The most common misconception: that it is just about sending information to people. In PMI terms, it is broader than communications because it focuses on how to involve stakeholders, address their concerns, and build or maintain commitment.

How it shows up in real projects: a team may plan tailored approaches such as sponsor briefings, regulator consultations, user workshops, and targeted actions for resistant groups rather than relying on one generic engagement method for everyone.
Contrast: Manage Stakeholder Engagement — Plan Stakeholder Engagement defines the approach; Manage Stakeholder Engagement carries out that approach during project work.
Example: A hospital system implementation team creates a stakeholder engagement plan that includes executive steering reviews, nurse-superuser workshops, and targeted outreach to departments likely to resist workflow changes.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Plan Stakeholder Engagement, pp. 518-523

Core Meaning: The authorized budget assigned to scheduled work to be accomplished for an activity or work breakdown structure component.
Expansion: Planned value is the budgeted amount of work that should have been completed by a particular point in time. It is one of the core earned value measures used to compare what was planned against what was actually accomplished and spent. PMI also denotes it as PV and links it directly to the performance measurement baseline.
Contrast: Earned value — planned value is the budgeted value of scheduled work; earned value is the budgeted value of work actually completed.
Example: By the end of the second month, a data-center upgrade was scheduled to complete work budgeted at 80,000, so that amount is the project's planned value at that date.
Source: PMBOK Guide — planned value, pp. 169, 207

Core Meaning: The project management activity of defining how objectives will be achieved, including the work, resources, schedule, costs, and controls needed.
Expansion: Planning establishes the intended path from objectives to delivery by deciding what work will be done, when, by whom, with what resources, and under what constraints. PMI treats planning as both an ongoing performance domain and a set of processes that progressively elaborate scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, communications, risk, procurement, and stakeholder approaches. It is not limited to creating a single plan document; it includes integrating multiple plans and adapting them as the project learns and changes. Good planning improves predictability, alignment, decision quality, and the team's ability to respond coherently when conditions shift.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Planning turns intent into an actionable and coordinated approach for delivery. It includes selecting delivery methods, defining scope, sequencing work, estimating effort and cost, preparing baselines or adaptive plans, and setting the controls used to monitor progress.

Why it matters and where it applies: every project needs enough planning to support sound decisions, even when uncertainty is high. In predictive environments planning may be extensive upfront, while in adaptive environments it is more iterative and just-in-time, but in both cases it reduces ambiguity and aligns stakeholders.

The most common misconception: that planning is a one-time phase completed before real work begins. In practice planning continues throughout the life cycle as assumptions change, risks emerge, and the team gains better information.

How it shows up in real projects: charters are refined into management plans, backlogs are ordered and re-ordered, milestones and budgets are updated through integrated change control, and short-horizon plans guide the next increment or phase.
Contrast: Plan — planning is the ongoing activity of deciding and coordinating how work will be done; a plan is the documented output of that activity.
Example: For a warehouse automation project, the team plans the rollout by defining requirements, sequencing facility modifications, estimating vendor lead times, allocating technicians, budgeting contingencies, and revising the schedule as site constraints become clearer.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, Planning Performance Domain; also reflected throughout PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition planning processes

Core Meaning: A WBS component below the control account and above the work package that has known work content but no detailed schedule activities yet.
Expansion: A planning package is used when scope is understood at a higher level but the detailed activities are not yet defined. It helps preserve the WBS structure and budget allocation until the work can be decomposed into work packages. PMI places it within earned value and scope planning as a bridge between summary planning and detailed execution.
Contrast: Work package — a planning package is a placeholder for future detail; a work package contains the detailed activities needed for execution and control.
Example: A defense project reserves a planning package for future integration work that is known to be needed but cannot yet be broken into detailed tasks.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Planning package, p. 140

Core Meaning: The performance domain concerned with organising and coordinating work, resources, estimates, and approaches so the project can move toward intended outcomes.
Expansion: In PMBOK Seventh Edition, the Planning Performance Domain focuses on the ongoing activities needed to proactively prepare for delivering project outcomes rather than treating planning as a one-time phase. It addresses how scope, schedule, cost, resources, communications, risk, quality, procurement, and other considerations are thought through and integrated in a way appropriate to the project context. The domain emphasises that planning is iterative, proportionate, and closely tied to uncertainty, stakeholder needs, and the chosen development approach.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The Planning Performance Domain is one of the PMBOK Seventh Edition performance domains and describes the project activities that establish, refine, and coordinate how work will be carried out. It is not a single plan document but a continuing body of planning effort across the life of the project.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects need enough planning to align expectations, reduce avoidable uncertainty, and support coherent decision making. This domain applies on predictive, adaptive, and hybrid work alike, though the form and timing of planning differ by context.

The most common misconception: that planning means producing a large upfront plan and then following it rigidly. PMI's framing is broader: planning should be fit for purpose, progressively elaborated, and adjusted as the team learns.

How it shows up in real projects: teams define delivery approaches, sequence work, estimate effort and cost, prepare for risks, coordinate resources, and update plans as conditions change. In practice, this appears through roadmaps, release plans, iteration plans, integrated baselines, and regular replanning discussions.
Contrast: Project Work Performance Domain — The Planning Performance Domain focuses on preparing and coordinating how work should proceed, while the Project Work Performance Domain focuses on executing and adapting the work itself.
Example: A hybrid enterprise-system project sets an overall roadmap and procurement plan upfront, then refines release plans and team-level iteration plans as requirements become clearer.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition — Performance Domains, Planning Performance Domain

Core Meaning: A collaborative estimation technique in which team members use relative estimates and reveal them together to converge on effort or complexity.
Expansion: Planning poker is commonly used in Agile teams to estimate backlog items by discussion and simultaneous reveal, often with story-point values. Its purpose is to reduce anchoring bias, surface differing assumptions, and build shared understanding of the work. PMI discusses it as an Agile estimation practice rather than a formal glossary term.
Contrast: Expert judgment — planning poker is a structured group estimation technique with simultaneous reveal; expert judgment may come from one or more specialists without that facilitation format.
Example: A Scrum team estimates user stories for an upcoming iteration by selecting cards privately, revealing them at the same time, then discussing why one developer chose 3 while another chose 13.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The Process Group required to establish the scope of the project, refine objectives, and define the course of action required to attain the objectives that the project was undertaken to achieve.
Expansion: The Planning Process Group develops the coordinated approach for delivering the project and managing its work. It includes processes used to define scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, communications, risk, procurement, and stakeholder approaches. PMI presents it as iterative, meaning planning is revisited and refined as more information becomes available.
Deep Dive:
What it is: the Planning Process Group is the collection of processes used to build out the project management plan and related project documents. It turns high-level intent into a workable path for execution and control.

Why it matters and where it applies: without planning, teams may execute activity without a shared basis for scope, timing, resources, risks, or governance. It applies on every project, though the amount and formality of planning vary by context, uncertainty, delivery approach, and organizational needs.

The most common misconception: that planning is a one-time step completed at the beginning. PMI practice treats planning as iterative, with progressive elaboration as assumptions are tested and the project learns more.

How it shows up in real projects: after initiation, the team develops baselines, management plans, and supporting documents, then updates them as conditions change, risks materialise, or stakeholder needs evolve.
Contrast: Initiating Process Group — Initiating authorizes the project or phase and defines it at a high level; Planning develops the detailed path for doing the work.
Example: After approving a project charter for a manufacturing-line upgrade, the team creates the scope baseline, integrated schedule, cost estimates, risk responses, procurement approach, and stakeholder engagement plan.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 3.2, pp. 23-25

Core Meaning: The PMBOK Guide is PMI's foundational standard-and-guidance publication that organizes generally recognized project management principles, domains, and practices.
Expansion: The PMBOK Guide is PMI's flagship reference for the project management profession and has evolved across editions from a process-focused framework to a principles-and-performance-domains model. It does not prescribe a single way to run every project; instead, it provides a common vocabulary, structure, and body of guidance that practitioners tailor to context. In practice, it is widely used to support shared understanding, professional development, exam preparation, and alignment of project management practices across organizations.
Deep Dive:
What it is: the PMBOK Guide is a PMI-issued guide that captures broadly accepted project management knowledge and provides a common reference point for practitioners. It is not a methodology in itself, but a structured source of principles, concepts, and guidance.

Why it matters and where it applies: it creates a shared language across teams, industries, and governance environments, which helps when defining roles, tailoring approaches, and aligning delivery practices. It applies across predictive, adaptive, and hybrid environments, though teams must still tailor how they use it.

The most common misconception: that the PMBOK Guide tells teams to follow one fixed sequence of steps or mandates a single lifecycle. In reality, PMI presents it as guidance to be adapted, not a one-size-fits-all operating manual.

How it shows up in real projects: organizations use it to shape internal standards, orient new project managers, structure training, and anchor conversations about scope, risk, stakeholders, delivery approach, and governance.
Contrast: Methodology — the PMBOK Guide is a body of guidance and common reference, while a methodology is an organization's specific prescribed way of managing work.
Example: A PMO uses the PMBOK Guide to standardize terminology and tailor its internal project framework for both construction and software initiatives.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) / The Standard for Project Management, Seventh Edition, PMI, pp. 1-4

Core Meaning: A project management office, a structure that standardizes project-related governance processes and facilitates the sharing of resources, methodologies, tools, and techniques.
Expansion: A PMO supports consistency and governance across projects, programs, or portfolios. Its role can range from supportive to controlling, depending on the organization. It often provides standards, reporting, coaching, and oversight to improve delivery performance.
Contrast: Project manager — a PMO provides organizational support and governance; a project manager leads a specific project.
Example: An enterprise PMO publishes templates, reviews project status reports, and helps teams apply common scheduling and risk practices.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — PMO. See Project management office, page 153

Core Meaning: A policy is a high-level organizational statement that guides decisions and sets boundaries for action within which projects operate.
Expansion: In project management, policy shapes what a project may, must, or must not do in areas such as compliance, quality, security, procurement, or governance. It is not usually a project-created artifact but an organizational constraint or guide that the project must align with. PMI references policy in context, but not as a distinct core glossary term in the retrieved material.
Contrast: Procedure — a policy sets direction and decision boundaries, while a procedure defines the steps used to carry that direction out.
Example: A project developing a customer portal must comply with an organizational data-retention policy before its design can be approved.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Political awareness is the ability to recognise and interpret the informal power dynamics, interests, and relationships that influence project decisions and behaviour.
Expansion: In project work, political awareness helps a project manager understand who truly influences outcomes, where support or resistance may come from, and how stakeholder positions may shift. It is especially important in cross-functional or high-visibility environments where formal authority alone does not determine decisions. PMI references it as a relevant professional capability, but not as a distinct core glossary term in the retrieved material.
Contrast: Power — political awareness is the skill of understanding influence patterns, while power is the actual capacity to influence decisions or behaviour.
Example: A project manager realises that an influential architect with no formal approval role can still shape executive opinion, so she engages that person early before design reviews begin.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Projects, programs, subsidiary portfolios, and operations managed as a group to achieve strategic objectives.
Expansion: A portfolio is a management construct used to select, prioritize, and oversee work so it aligns with strategy. Unlike a program, a portfolio does not require the components to be directly related; the common thread is strategic value and governance. PMI treats portfolio management as a way to balance investment, risk, and performance across the organization.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A portfolio is a collection of work and operational components managed together at the strategic level. The components may be unrelated in content but are linked by the organization’s objectives and investment decisions.

Why it matters and where it applies: portfolios help leaders decide where to place resources, which initiatives to start or stop, and how to balance risk and return across the enterprise. They are central to strategic alignment and governance.

The most common misconception: that a portfolio is simply a list of all projects. In PMI terms, it is an actively managed group used to achieve strategic objectives, not just an inventory.

How it shows up in real projects: an organization reviews its portfolio quarterly, compares initiatives against strategic goals, and reallocates funding from lower-value work to higher-priority programs.
Contrast: Program — a portfolio is managed for strategic alignment across potentially unrelated work; a program coordinates related work for benefits.
Example: A healthcare company manages a portfolio that includes IT modernization, facility upgrades, and compliance initiatives to support its strategic plan.
Source: The Standard for Portfolio Management, Fourth Edition — Portfolio, page 4

Core Meaning: The centralized management of one or more portfolios to achieve strategic objectives.
Expansion: Portfolio management is the coordinated selection and oversight of portfolio components so that investments align with organizational strategy. It focuses on prioritization, balancing, resource allocation, and governance across a set of change initiatives and other work. PMI treats it as a strategic management discipline, not simply an aggregation of project tracking. It helps organizations direct limited capacity toward the work that offers the most strategic value.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Portfolio management is the centralized management of projects, programs, subportfolios, and operations or other work grouped to support strategy. Its focus is on choosing, authorizing, prioritizing, and overseeing the right mix of work.

Why it matters and where it applies: organizations usually have more ideas and demand than budget, capacity, or leadership attention can support. Portfolio management provides the decision framework to align investments with strategic objectives, risk tolerance, and available resources.

The most common misconception: that portfolio management is just reporting on a list of projects. In practice it is a strategic governance function that includes deciding what to start, continue, pause, reprioritize, or stop.

How it shows up in real projects: executives compare proposed initiatives against strategy, capacity, and expected value, then shift funding away from low-priority work to critical modernization, compliance, or growth initiatives.
Contrast: Program management — portfolio management selects and governs the mix of work for strategic alignment; program management coordinates related components to realize benefits.
Example: A healthcare organization reviews its enterprise portfolio and delays low-value departmental upgrades so it can fund a strategic patient-record modernization program.
Source: The Standard for Portfolio Management, Fourth Edition, portfolio management, page 4

Core Meaning: Positive risk is an uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has a favorable effect on one or more project objectives.
Expansion: In PMI usage, positive risk is the same underlying concept as an opportunity: uncertainty that could improve outcomes such as schedule, cost, quality, or performance. It is managed deliberately rather than passively, using responses intended to increase the probability or impact of beneficial outcomes. Treating positive risk seriously helps teams move beyond loss avoidance and actively pursue upside value.
Contrast: Threat — a positive risk can benefit project objectives, whereas a threat can harm them.
Example: A project team identifies that early access to a test environment could accelerate integration work and decides to pursue that opportunity to shorten the schedule.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, PMI Glossary, p. 719

Core Meaning: Power is the capacity to influence decisions, resources, priorities, or the behaviour of others in a project environment.
Expansion: In project management, power affects whose views carry weight, how quickly decisions are made, and whether support or resistance changes project outcomes. It may come from formal authority, expertise, relationships, control of resources, or personal influence. PMI discusses power in stakeholder and leadership contexts, but not as a distinct core glossary term in the retrieved material.
Contrast: Authority — power is the broader ability to influence outcomes, while authority is formally granted decision rights within a role or structure.
Example: A functional director who controls specialist resources influences project sequencing even though the project manager owns the integrated schedule.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A power interest grid is a stakeholder analysis tool that maps stakeholders by their level of authority and their level of concern or involvement to guide engagement strategy.
Expansion: Project teams use the power interest grid to decide how closely different stakeholders should be engaged, informed, or monitored. It supports stakeholder identification and engagement planning by highlighting which people or groups can influence outcomes and which are most affected by the work. The grid is especially useful early in a project and whenever the stakeholder landscape shifts.
Contrast: Salience model — a power interest grid classifies stakeholders by power and interest; a salience model classifies them by power, legitimacy, and urgency.
Example: A project manager maps the chief operations officer as high power and high interest and therefore schedules frequent briefings, while keeping low power and low interest external observers updated through periodic summaries.

Figure.caption: Power up the side, interest across the bottom; each quadrant has its own strategy.

Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A practice is a set of related activities that are commonly performed to achieve a specific project management objective.
Expansion: In PMI usage, a practice groups actions, considerations, and outputs that help practitioners address a recurring need in project work. Practices are broader than a single tool or technique and are intended to be applied with judgment based on context. They help translate principles into work that teams can actually perform across different delivery approaches.
Contrast: Process — a practice is a flexible body of related activities applied contextually; a process is a more defined sequence of actions with inputs and outputs.
Example: A project team uses a risk identification practice that combines workshops, expert input, and historical information to surface threats and opportunities throughout the project.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, p. 243

Core Meaning: A schedule network diagramming technique in which activities are represented by nodes and linked by logical relationships.
Expansion: Precedence Diagramming Method is a scheduling technique used to model the sequence of project activities with nodes and dependency arrows. It supports relationship types such as finish-to-start, start-to-start, finish-to-finish, and start-to-finish. PMI uses it to develop schedule logic, analyse dependencies, and support critical path analysis.
Contrast: Activity-on-arrow — Precedence Diagramming Method places activities in nodes and uses arrows for relationships; activity-on-arrow places activities on arrows.
Example: A software implementation schedule uses Precedence Diagramming Method to show that training can start once core configuration begins, while user acceptance testing cannot finish until defect fixes are complete.

Figure.caption: Boxes = activities; arrows = dependencies (FS shown).

Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Precedence Diagramming Method, page 699

Core Meaning: A precedence relationship is the logical dependency that determines the sequence in which activities occur in a schedule network.
Expansion: Precedence relationships connect predecessor and successor activities so the project team can model how work flows through the schedule. PMI uses them in schedule network analysis through relationship types such as finish-to-start, finish-to-finish, start-to-start, and start-to-finish. They are essential for identifying critical paths, evaluating schedule changes, and understanding how delays propagate.
Contrast: Dependency — a precedence relationship is the specific logical link used in the schedule network between activities, while dependency is the broader concept of one element relying on another.
Example: In a facility-upgrade project, server-rack installation cannot start until room-cooling modifications are completed, creating a finish-to-start precedence relationship.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Sixth Edition, section 6.3 Sequence Activities and glossary entries for precedence diagramming relationships, p. 189

Core Meaning: Precision is the degree to which repeated measurements or estimates under unchanged conditions show the same or very similar results.
Expansion: In project management, precision is used when discussing data quality, estimating, measurement, and control. A precise result is consistent across repetitions, even if it is not perfectly correct. PMI distinguishes precision from accuracy because project decisions can be affected by data that is tightly clustered but still off target.
Contrast: Accuracy — precision is about consistency of repeated results; accuracy is about closeness to the true or correct value.
Example: A cost analyst produces three independent estimates for the same work package and all are nearly identical, showing high precision even before validating whether the estimate is accurate.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, p. 704

Core Meaning: An activity that logically comes before another activity in the schedule and influences when that later activity can start or finish.
Expansion: A predecessor is the earlier activity in a logical relationship between scheduled activities. It provides the basis for sequencing work and determining dependencies in the schedule model. PMI uses predecessor relationships to analyse timing, identify the critical path, and understand how delays can propagate through the project.
Contrast: Successor — a predecessor comes before and constrains a later activity; a successor is the activity that follows.
Example: In an office-fitout project, 'Complete electrical rough-in' is the predecessor to 'Install drywall' because the walls cannot be closed before the wiring work is finished.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Predecessor activity, page 214

Core Meaning: An activity that logically comes before a dependent activity in a schedule.
Expansion: A predecessor activity is the earlier activity in a logical relationship. It determines when a successor activity can start or finish, depending on the dependency type. Predecessors are fundamental to schedule network logic and critical path analysis.
Contrast: Successor activity — a predecessor comes before; a successor comes after.
Example: Foundation excavation is the predecessor to concrete pouring in a construction schedule.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Predecessor activity, p. 127

Core Meaning: The use of historical and current data with analytical techniques to forecast likely future outcomes.
Expansion: In project management, predictive analytics supports forecasting such things as schedule slippage, cost overruns, defect trends, demand, or resource constraints. It matters because project decisions improve when teams use evidence-based projections rather than intuition alone. PMI treats it as an adjacent analytical concept that can inform project, program, or portfolio decisions, not as a formal core glossary term.
Contrast: Trend analysis — predictive analytics uses broader data and analytical models to forecast future outcomes; trend analysis typically extrapolates patterns from observed performance data more simply.
Example: A PMO uses past delivery data and current sprint performance to flag projects with a high likelihood of missing milestone dates next quarter.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A predictive approach is a development approach in which scope, time, and cost are determined early in the project life cycle.
Expansion: A predictive approach plans most work upfront and executes against a defined baseline, with changes managed through formal control. It is most effective when requirements are sufficiently stable, the product is well understood, and the cost of change is relatively high. PMI treats it as one of the major delivery approaches alongside adaptive and hybrid ways of working.
Deep Dive:
What it is: a predictive approach is a plan-driven way of delivering work in which requirements and plans are established as early as practical. The team sequences activities in advance and manages progress against approved baselines.

Why it matters and where it applies: it is valuable where outputs can be specified with reasonable confidence, such as construction, infrastructure, regulatory implementations, or equipment deployment. In these settings, upfront definition improves coordination, procurement, governance, and stakeholder expectation management.

The most common misconception: that predictive means rigid or incapable of handling change. In practice, predictive projects do manage change, but they do so through structured evaluation and control rather than frequent reprioritization.

How it shows up in real projects: teams develop detailed schedules, cost estimates, scope baselines, and milestone plans early, then monitor variance and process change requests through governance channels.
Contrast: Adaptive approach — a predictive approach defines and plans most work early, while an adaptive approach evolves scope and plans as learning occurs.
Example: A hospital expansion project finalizes design packages, procurement plans, budget baselines, and construction sequencing before major execution begins.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, PMI Glossary, p. 723

Core Meaning: A life cycle in which the project scope, time, and cost are determined in the early phases of the life cycle.
Expansion: A predictive life cycle relies on substantial upfront planning and early definition of scope, schedule, and cost. PMI uses the term for environments where work can be planned in detail before execution proceeds. It is most suitable when requirements are relatively stable and changes can be managed through formal control.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A predictive life cycle structures the project so major planning happens early and execution follows approved baselines. Work is usually organized into sequential or largely sequential phases with formal oversight.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because some projects need stable planning, clear handoffs, and disciplined change control. It is common in construction, engineering, infrastructure, and other contexts where uncertainty is lower or late change is expensive.

The most common misconception: that predictive means rigid or incapable of adapting. In practice, change still occurs, but it is handled through formal review and control rather than continuous reprioritization.

How it shows up in real projects: a facility build completes concept, design, and procurement planning before construction begins, then manages execution against approved baselines.
Contrast: Adaptive life cycle — a predictive life cycle defines scope, time, and cost early; an adaptive life cycle expects details to evolve through iteration.
Example: A bridge construction project completes detailed design and cost estimation before field work starts and then tracks progress against the baseline plan.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition — predictive life cycle, page 551

Core Meaning: Present value is the current worth of a future sum of money or stream of cash flows discounted at a specified rate.
Expansion: PMI uses present value in project selection, business case analysis, and financial evaluation. It helps compare options that generate costs or benefits at different times by converting future amounts into current terms. This supports decisions about whether an initiative is financially attractive relative to alternatives.
Contrast: Net present value — present value is the discounted value of a future amount or cash flow; net present value sums discounted benefits and costs to show overall financial gain or loss.
Example: A portfolio review discounts the expected savings from an automation project over three years to determine the present value of those future benefits before approving funding.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, p. 706

Core Meaning: Prevention refers to actions taken to keep errors, defects, or other unwanted outcomes from occurring in project work.
Expansion: Within PMI quality management, prevention focuses on designing and managing work so problems do not happen in the first place. It is generally more effective than relying only on inspection after the fact. Prevention supports process quality, lowers rework, and improves confidence in deliverables.
Contrast: Inspection — prevention aims to stop defects before they occur; inspection finds defects after work has already been performed.
Example: Before development begins, a team introduces coding standards, peer review checklists, and automated tests to prevent defects rather than depending only on final testing to catch them.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, p. 706

Core Meaning: The cost of activities performed to avoid defects and keep project outputs and processes within required quality standards.
Expansion: Prevention cost is part of the cost of quality and covers spending intended to stop errors before they occur. It includes actions such as training, process improvement, quality planning, and preventive reviews. PMI uses the concept to show that investing early in quality can reduce rework, failure costs, and downstream disruption.
Contrast: Appraisal cost — prevention cost is spent to avoid defects from happening; appraisal cost is spent to assess whether outputs already meet requirements.
Example: A project funds peer design reviews and staff training on coding standards to reduce defects before system testing begins.
Source: PMBOK Guide / Quality management references — prevention cost; exact standard page citation not available in retrieved PMI sources

Core Meaning: An intentional action to ensure the future performance of the project work is aligned with the project management plan.
Expansion: Preventive action is taken to reduce the probability of a potential negative effect before it occurs. It is different from corrective action because it addresses anticipated issues rather than existing variances. PMI treats it as part of project control and quality management, where proactive adjustments help keep work aligned with the plan.
Contrast: Corrective action — preventive action avoids a future problem; corrective action fixes an existing deviation.
Example: A project team adds an extra design review before release to prevent defects from reaching production.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Preventive action, p. 89

Core Meaning: A contractual limit that sets the maximum amount the buyer is obligated to pay under the terms of the contract.
Expansion: Price ceiling is commonly used in procurement and contract administration, especially in cost-reimbursable arrangements with incentive structures. It caps the buyer's financial exposure even if actual seller costs rise above expectations.
Contrast: Target cost — the target cost is the planned cost basis for incentive sharing; the price ceiling is the upper payment limit under the contract.
Example: A buyer awards a cost-plus-incentive-fee contract for plant automation with a negotiated maximum payable amount that cannot be exceeded without contract change.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The risk that changes in market prices will adversely affect project costs, procurement value, or financial outcomes.
Expansion: In project management, price risk most often arises when the project depends on commodities, materials, services, currencies, or other inputs whose prices may fluctuate over time. It is especially relevant in procurement, contracting, and cost risk analysis because external market movements can affect estimates and budgets. PMI discusses this as a risk consideration in project and procurement contexts rather than as a consistently formal standalone glossary term across core standards.
Contrast: Cost risk — cost risk is the broader possibility that project costs vary from plan; price risk is specifically driven by changes in market prices or rates.
Example: A construction project budgets structural steel at one market rate, then faces a sudden increase before purchase, forcing the team to use contingency or rebaseline costs.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The process of determining the relative importance or order of work, requirements, risks, or alternatives so that limited resources are applied to the highest-value items first.
Expansion: In project management, prioritization is used to rank competing work based on factors such as value, urgency, risk, dependency, and strategic alignment. PMI treats it as a practical decision process that supports scope selection, backlog ordering, issue response, and resource allocation. It is especially important when demand exceeds capacity or when change requires frequent re-evaluation of what should happen next.
Contrast: Sequencing — prioritization decides relative importance; sequencing decides the logical order in which work is performed.
Example: A product team ranks requested features by customer value, compliance need, and delivery effort, then schedules the highest-priority items into the next release.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A scatter diagram that plots effort against value so items can be classified by relative priority.
Expansion: A prioritization matrix is a visual decision tool that compares items using effort and value so the team can rank what to address first. It helps structure tradeoff discussions when there are more candidate actions than capacity. In practice it is often used for backlog items, improvement opportunities, requirements, or risks.
Contrast: Prioritization Schema — a prioritization matrix is the visual comparison tool; a prioritization schema is the rule set or classification method used to assign priority.
Example: A product team plots enhancement requests on a value-versus-effort grid and selects the high-value, low-effort items for the next release.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Methods used to prioritize portfolio, program, or project components, as well as requirements, risks, features, or other product information.
Expansion: A Prioritization Schema is the structured method used to rank or sequence work items, choices, or concerns so attention and resources go first to what matters most. PMI uses the term broadly because prioritization applies at multiple levels, from selecting portfolio components to ordering backlog items, features, risks, or change requests. The schema may be simple or formal, but it should reflect agreed decision criteria rather than personal preference alone.
Deep Dive:
What it is: a Prioritization Schema is a repeatable approach for deciding relative importance among competing items. It can rely on value, urgency, risk, strategic alignment, regulatory necessity, customer impact, effort, dependency, or a weighted combination of factors.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects and products almost always face more demand than available capacity. A clear schema improves transparency, reduces arbitrary decisions, and helps governance bodies and delivery teams make consistent trade-offs.

The most common misconception: that prioritization is only a backlog technique used by agile teams. In practice it is also central to portfolio selection, risk response ordering, issue escalation, and release planning across predictive, adaptive, and hybrid environments.

How it shows up in real projects: a team may use a weighted scoring model for candidate initiatives, MoSCoW categories for requirements, or a risk ranking model based on probability and impact. The key is that the method is explicit, understood, and tied to decision intent.
Contrast: Weighted scoring model — A Prioritization Schema is the broader method for ordering items, while a weighted scoring model is one specific technique that can be used within it.
Example: A product team ranks proposed features using customer value, revenue impact, implementation effort, and regulatory urgency so the next release includes the highest-value items first.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms

Core Meaning: The relative importance assigned to work, requirements, risks, or issues to determine the order and level of attention they receive.
Expansion: Priority is used in project management to rank competing items so that limited time, money, and attention are directed where they matter most. It supports decision-making in areas such as scope selection, backlog ordering, issue escalation, and risk response. In PMI practice, priority is a practical control concept rather than a standalone methodology term.
Contrast: Urgency — priority reflects relative importance for action and decision-making; urgency reflects how quickly attention is needed.
Example: A product owner places a security fix above lower-value enhancement requests so the team addresses the highest-priority backlog item first.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An estimating approach that represents possible cost or duration outcomes as probabilities rather than as a single fixed value.
Expansion: Probabilistic estimating expresses uncertainty explicitly by modeling a range of possible outcomes and their likelihoods. It is used when planners need to understand confidence levels, exposure, and the spread of possible dates or costs rather than rely on one deterministic estimate. It commonly appears with three-point estimates, probability distributions, and simulation-based forecasting.
Contrast: Deterministic estimating — probabilistic estimating models a range of possible outcomes with likelihoods; deterministic estimating provides one expected value without explicitly expressing uncertainty.
Example: A schedule analyst estimates a testing phase with optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic durations to forecast the probability of meeting a launch date.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The likelihood that a given event will occur.
Expansion: Probability expresses how likely a risk event or other uncertain event is to happen. In project management, it is commonly paired with impact to assess risk significance and prioritize responses. It helps teams compare uncertainties in a structured way rather than relying on intuition alone.
Contrast: Impact — probability is the chance that an event occurs; impact is the effect if it occurs.
Example: A project team rates the probability of a supplier delay as high because the vendor has already missed two prior delivery dates.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — probability

Core Meaning: A grid for mapping the probability of occurrence of each risk and its impact on project objectives if that risk occurs.
Expansion: A probability and impact matrix is used to prioritise risks by combining how likely they are with how much they would affect project objectives. PMI notes that organisations may define prioritisation rules in advance or tailor them to the project. It can represent both threats and opportunities, using positive impact definitions for opportunities and negative impact definitions for threats.
Contrast: Probability distribution — a probability and impact matrix is a qualitative prioritisation tool; a probability distribution is a quantitative representation of possible outcomes.
Example: A project team rates a supplier delay as high probability and high impact, so it is placed in the top-priority risk category for response planning.

Figure.caption: Probability x impact: red cells (top-right) are highest priority; green (bottom-left) a watch list.

Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — probability and impact matrix; A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, exact page citation not confirmed in current context

Core Meaning: A representation of the range of possible values for a variable and the likelihood of each value occurring.
Expansion: In PMI risk practice, probability distributions are used to model uncertainty in quantitative analysis. They help show not just one expected value, but the spread of possible outcomes and their likelihoods. This supports techniques such as Monte Carlo simulation and helps teams understand exposure and confidence levels.
Contrast: Probability and impact matrix — a probability distribution models numeric uncertainty; a probability and impact matrix ranks risks qualitatively by likelihood and impact.
Example: A schedule risk model uses a triangular distribution for activity duration to reflect optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic estimates.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — probability distribution; A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, exact page citation not confirmed in current context

Core Meaning: Problem solving is the structured identification, analysis, and resolution of issues that impede project objectives or performance.
Expansion: In project management practice, problem solving involves diagnosing causes, generating response options, selecting an appropriate solution, and implementing corrective action. PMI treats it as a practical leadership and team capability that supports decision making, issue resolution, and continuous improvement rather than as a standalone formal glossary term in every standard. Effective problem solving improves delivery flow, reduces recurring issues, and strengthens team learning.
Contrast: Decision making — problem solving focuses on resolving an issue or gap, while decision making is the act of choosing among alternatives and may occur with or without a problem context.
Example: When repeated defects appear during system testing, the project team performs root-cause analysis, identifies an interface specification gap, and updates both the design and review procedure.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A procedure is an established method for performing a specific activity or process.
Expansion: Within PMI usage, a procedure describes how work is to be carried out in a consistent and repeatable way. It is more specific than a policy and typically defines steps, responsibilities, and required controls for performing an activity. In projects, procedures support governance, quality, compliance, and operational consistency across recurring tasks.
Contrast: Policy — a procedure explains how work is performed, while a policy states governing intent or rules at a higher level.
Example: A PMO defines a change-control procedure that specifies how change requests are submitted, assessed, approved, documented, and communicated.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, PMI Glossary, p. 736

Core Meaning: A systematic series of activities directed toward causing an end result such that one or more inputs will be acted upon to create one or more outputs.
Expansion: A process is a defined set of activities that transforms inputs into outputs. In project management, processes help teams perform work consistently, communicate expectations, and manage complexity. PMI uses the term broadly for the structured actions that support planning, execution, monitoring, and control.
Contrast: Procedure — a process defines the flow of activities and intended transformation; a procedure gives more specific instructions for how to perform them.
Example: A change control process takes a submitted change request as input, evaluates its impact, and produces an approval or rejection decision as output.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition and Process Groups A Practice Guide — process, page 187

Core Meaning: A technique for examining a process to identify inefficiencies, variation, and opportunities for improvement.
Expansion: Process analysis is used to study how a project-related process works and where it can be improved. PMI applies it to examine process steps, handoffs, bottlenecks, waste, and non-value-adding work so the team can improve quality and efficiency. It is commonly used in quality management and continuous improvement efforts.
Contrast: Root cause analysis — process analysis examines the design and performance of a process; root cause analysis focuses on why a specific problem occurred.
Example: A project team reviews its change control workflow, finds duplicate approvals that add delay, and redesigns the process to shorten turnaround time.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Process analysis

Core Meaning: The measurable ability of a process to produce outputs that meet specified requirements in a stable and predictable way.
Expansion: Process capability is used to judge whether a process can consistently achieve required performance levels, quality limits, or control thresholds. In project work it is most relevant to quality management, process improvement, and operational handoff, where leaders need evidence that a process will perform reliably. PMI commonly treats it as part of quality and performance thinking, even when the exact phrasing comes from broader quality-management practice.
Contrast: Process performance — process capability describes what a process is able to achieve under stable conditions; process performance describes how the process is actually performing now.
Example: A manufacturing-improvement project measures whether a packaging line can consistently fill units within tolerance before declaring the new process ready for rollout.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A representation of the sequence of activities, decisions, inputs, and outputs in a process.
