Expansion: Process flow shows how work moves from one step to another, often including handoffs, decision points, and dependencies. In project management it supports planning, analysis, improvement, and communication by making the logic of the work visible. PMI materials use process-flow thinking in process descriptions, workflow analysis, and quality tools, even where the exact term may appear more as a technique than as a core glossary entry.
Contrast: Workflow — workflow usually emphasises the operational movement of tasks through people or systems; process flow is the mapped sequence and logic of the process itself.
Example: A PMO maps the change-control process from request submission through impact review, approval decision, implementation, and closure to identify bottlenecks.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A logical grouping of project management inputs, tools and techniques, and outputs.
Expansion: Process groups organize project management processes by their general purpose within the flow of project work. They provide a useful way to understand how initiation, planning, execution, monitoring and controlling, and closing activities relate to one another. PMI presents them as groupings, not as rigid sequential steps that occur only once.
Contrast: Project phase — a process group is a logical grouping of management processes; a project phase is a segment of the project life cycle.
Example: During a construction project, planning processes are revisited after design approval even though the project remains within the same overall phase.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition and Process Groups A Practice Guide — process group, page 188

Core Meaning: A deliberate effort to enhance the efficiency, effectiveness, or quality of a process.
Expansion: Process improvement focuses on making a process work better by reducing waste, variation, delays, or defects. In PMI practice, it is commonly associated with quality management and process analysis, where teams examine how work is performed and identify opportunities to improve it. It can be applied to project processes, product-related processes, or organizational processes that affect project delivery.
Contrast: Process analysis — process improvement is the act of making the process better; process analysis is the examination used to find improvement opportunities.
Example: A project team streamlines its change approval workflow by removing duplicate reviews and shortening turnaround time.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — process improvement, page 8

Core Meaning: The condition in which a process performs predictably over time and shows only expected, inherent variation rather than unusual disturbance.
Expansion: Process stability means a process is operating consistently enough that its performance can be understood, predicted, and improved rationally. In project quality management, it matters because unstable processes produce unreliable outputs and make root-cause analysis difficult. PMI-related quality practice treats stability as a prerequisite for meaningful control and continuous improvement.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Process stability describes a process whose variation stays within a consistent pattern caused by common, built-in factors rather than special disruptions. A stable process is not necessarily perfect, but it is predictable enough to analyse and manage.

Why it matters and where it applies: quality management depends on knowing whether performance changes are normal or signal a real problem. Stability matters in repetitive project work, testing, manufacturing-related projects, service transitions, and any environment where process control supports reliable delivery.

The most common misconception: that stable means good. A process can be stable and still perform poorly if it consistently delivers results outside stakeholder needs; stability only means the behavior is predictable.

How it shows up in real projects: a deployment process repeatedly completes within a narrow range of time and defect levels, allowing the team to forecast release readiness and improve the workflow with confidence.
Contrast: Process capability — process stability is about predictability over time; process capability is about whether the process can meet specification limits or performance requirements.
Example: A testing process produces a consistent defect-detection rate sprint after sprint, which lets the team identify whether a sudden drop is an actual issue or normal fluctuation.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The natural or assignable differences in process performance that cause outputs or results to vary over time.
Expansion: Process variation is the change observed in how a process performs or what it produces from one occurrence to another. In project quality management, understanding variation is essential for distinguishing routine fluctuation from signals that require intervention. PMI-related quality practice uses variation analysis to support control, forecasting, and continuous improvement.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Process variation refers to the fact that no process produces identical results every time. Some variation comes from common causes built into the system, while some comes from special causes such as breakdowns, mistakes, or external disruption.

Why it matters and where it applies: managers make better decisions when they know whether a result reflects random fluctuation or a meaningful shift. This applies in quality control, schedule performance of repetitive tasks, defect patterns, service response times, and production-oriented project environments.

The most common misconception: that every deviation from a target needs correction. Acting on normal variation can make performance worse, while failing to spot special-cause variation can leave real problems unresolved.

How it shows up in real projects: a team tracks cycle time on repeated work items and sees that most fluctuations are normal, but one major spike reveals an environment outage that needs corrective action.
Contrast: Process stability — process variation is the pattern of fluctuation in performance; process stability is the condition in which that variation is predictable and under control.
Example: A project team monitors turnaround time for change requests and uses the spread of results to distinguish normal workload effects from a breakdown in approvals.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The acquisition of products, services, or results from outside the project team.
Expansion: Procurement is the process of obtaining needed goods or services from external sources. In projects, it is used when the performing organization does not provide the required capability internally. Procurement work includes planning what to buy, selecting sellers, administering contracts, and closing procurements.
Contrast: Make-or-buy decision — procurement is the act of acquiring externally; make-or-buy is the decision about whether to acquire externally or perform the work internally.
Example: A project team procures specialized testing services from an external vendor because the capability is not available in-house.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition — Project Procurement Management, page 459

Core Meaning: A procurement contract is a mutually binding agreement that obligates a seller to provide specified products, services, or results and obligates a buyer to provide payment or other consideration.
Expansion: In PMI usage, a contract formalises the relationship between buyer and seller and sets the terms under which project work will be performed by an external party. It typically defines scope, deliverables, commercial terms, responsibilities, acceptance conditions, and mechanisms for change or dispute resolution. In project management, it is a core instrument of procurement management and governance whenever work is sourced outside the performing organisation.
Contrast: Agreement — a contract is a specific legally binding form of agreement with enforceable obligations, while agreement is the broader term.
Example: A hospital construction project signs a fixed-price procurement contract with a mechanical contractor to install HVAC systems by defined milestones and quality standards.
Source: PMBOK Guide — not confirmed to exact page in available search results

Core Meaning: The collection of records and supporting information used to plan, conduct, administer, and close procurements.
Expansion: Procurement documentation includes items such as statements of work, bid documents, source selection records, contracts, change records, and performance-related correspondence. It matters because these records support supplier selection, contract administration, auditability, dispute resolution, and formal closeout. PMI uses related terms such as procurement statement of work and procurement documents more specifically, while the broader label procurement documentation is not typically treated as a distinct formal glossary entry.
Contrast: Project documents — procurement documentation is the subset of records specifically tied to acquiring and managing external goods and services; project documents cover the wider set of project records.
Example: During a data-centre migration, the buyer maintains the request-for-proposal package, evaluation notes, signed contract, approved changes, and vendor performance reports in the procurement file.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The documents used to solicit proposals, bids, quotations, or other supplier responses for project procurements.
Expansion: Procurement documents are the formal materials a buyer issues to communicate requirements and invite sellers to respond in a consistent, comparable way. They may include statements of work, instructions to bidders, evaluation criteria, contract terms, and request packages such as an RFP, RFQ, or IFB. In PMI practice they are central to planning and conducting procurements because they shape seller understanding, competition quality, and contract outcomes.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Procurement documents are the buyer-facing package used to define what is needed and how prospective sellers should respond. They create a structured basis for market engagement and later source selection.

Why it matters and where it applies: clear procurement documents reduce ambiguity, improve fairness, and support stronger contract formation. They are especially important in projects that rely on external vendors, regulated purchasing environments, or complex statements of work.

The most common misconception: that procurement documents are just administrative forms or that they are the same as the final contract. In practice they are pre-award instruments that help the buyer compare sellers consistently and reduce downstream dispute risk.

How it shows up in real projects: a project team drafts an RFP package with scope requirements, service levels, response instructions, and evaluation criteria, then uses seller submissions to shortlist vendors and negotiate the contract.
Contrast: Contract — procurement documents are used to solicit and evaluate seller responses before award; a contract is the binding agreement formed after selection.
Example: A public-sector IT project issues an RFP containing technical requirements, data-security conditions, pricing instructions, and weighted evaluation criteria to select a systems integrator.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Seventh Edition, Glossary; Process Groups: A Practice Guide, Procurement Management section

Core Meaning: The processes required to acquire products, services, or results from outside the project team.
Expansion: Procurement management covers planning, conducting, and controlling procurements so external goods and services are obtained appropriately. It includes deciding what to buy, how to solicit sellers, how to evaluate offers, and how to manage contracts after award. PMI treats it as a project management knowledge area that supports external acquisition and contract oversight.
Contrast: Procurement management plan — procurement management is the broader set of processes; the procurement management plan is the document that describes how those processes will be carried out.
Example: A construction project uses procurement management to source steel, select a contractor, and monitor contract performance.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — procurement management, page 59

Core Meaning: A component of the project management plan that describes how a team will acquire goods and services from outside of the performing organisation.
Expansion: The procurement management plan explains how procurement activities will be planned, conducted, and controlled. It addresses what will be procured, how sellers will be selected, contract types, and how procurement will be managed through the project. PMI treats it as part of the overall management plan that guides external acquisition work.
Contrast: Procurement statement of work — the procurement management plan describes how procurement will be managed; the procurement statement of work describes the specific goods or services to be acquired.
Example: A construction project includes a procurement management plan that defines competitive bidding, contract approval steps, and vendor performance oversight.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — procurement management plan; A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, exact page citation not confirmed in current context

Core Meaning: A narrative description of the products, services, or results to be supplied under a procurement contract.
Expansion: The procurement statement of work describes the work that a seller is expected to perform and the deliverables to be provided. It gives prospective sellers enough detail to prepare bids or proposals and helps the buyer compare responses consistently. PMI treats it as a key procurement planning artifact that clarifies scope and contract expectations.
Contrast: Statement of work — a procurement statement of work is the buyer's procurement-specific description of contracted work; a statement of work can be used more broadly in project or contractual contexts.
Example: For a data-centre migration, the procurement statement of work specifies equipment removal, transport, rack installation, cable testing, and cutover support services.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Procurement statement of work, page 471

Core Meaning: The planned approach for acquiring products, services, or results from outside the project team in a way that supports project objectives and constraints.
Expansion: Procurement strategy defines how the project will source external goods or services, including contract approach, supplier engagement, allocation of risk, and timing of purchases. It connects make-or-buy thinking with market conditions, governance, and delivery needs. In PMI practice, it helps ensure procurement decisions support cost, schedule, quality, and risk objectives rather than being treated as isolated purchasing actions.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Procurement strategy is the overall logic behind how a project will obtain external support, materials, or capabilities. It shapes decisions about contract type, vendor relationship model, competition, packaging of work, and when procurement actions occur.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects often depend on outside parties for specialised skills, equipment, technology, or construction work. A sound procurement strategy reduces commercial risk, improves supplier alignment, and helps the project choose contracting approaches that fit uncertainty and performance needs.

The most common misconception: that procurement strategy is just selecting a vendor or writing a contract. In practice it comes earlier and guides those later choices by defining the sourcing approach and the balance of control, flexibility, and risk.

How it shows up in real projects: a project may choose fixed-price contracts for well-defined equipment purchases, while using time-and-materials arrangements for uncertain advisory work where scope will evolve.
Contrast: Procurement statement of work — procurement strategy defines the overall sourcing approach; the procurement statement of work describes the specific item or service to be procured.
Example: An infrastructure project decides to bundle civil works into one competitive bid package while procuring specialist design support separately under a more flexible contract model.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An artifact that is produced, is quantifiable, and can be either an end item in itself or a component item.
Expansion: A product is a result created by work that can be identified and measured. It may be the final item delivered to a customer or a component used within a larger solution. PMI uses the term broadly enough to include tangible and intangible outputs. This makes it useful across industries, from physical goods to digital services.
Contrast: Deliverable — a product is the produced item itself; a deliverable is any unique and verifiable product, result, or capability that must be produced to complete a process, phase, or project.
Example: A payroll application released to employees is the product, while the user guide and test report are supporting deliverables.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, product, page 725

Core Meaning: An ordered list of work to be done on a product.
Expansion: A product backlog contains the features, enhancements, fixes, and other work items that may be needed to evolve a product. It is ordered so the team can focus on the most valuable work first and refine items as understanding improves. In adaptive delivery, it is a living artifact that changes as feedback, learning, and priorities change.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A product backlog is the evolving source of work for a product team. It typically includes user stories, defects, technical work, and other items needed to deliver value.

Why it matters and where it applies: it gives teams a single prioritized view of what may be built next. It is central in agile and hybrid environments because it supports continuous reprioritization, refinement, and planning.

The most common misconception: that the backlog is a fixed requirements list. In practice it is intentionally changeable and is refined as the team learns more about customer needs, technical constraints, and business value.

How it shows up in real projects: a product owner reviews feedback from users, reorders backlog items before each iteration, and adds new work discovered during testing or stakeholder review.
Contrast: Requirements documentation — a product backlog is a dynamic ordered work list; requirements documentation is typically a more formal statement of needs and constraints.
Example: A mobile app team keeps a product backlog of login improvements, accessibility fixes, and new payment features, then selects the top items for the next sprint.
Source: Agile Practice Guide / PMI Lexicon — product backlog

Core Meaning: A hierarchical decomposition of a product into its component parts.
Expansion: A product breakdown structure organizes the product into progressively smaller components so the team can understand what the product contains. It supports product-scope definition, design coordination, and communication by showing the structure of the deliverable itself rather than the work to create it. Teams use it to clarify boundaries, interfaces, and completeness before or alongside detailed planning.
Contrast: Work Breakdown Structure — a product breakdown structure decomposes the product itself; a work breakdown structure decomposes the work required to create that product.
Example: A smart-meter project decomposes the product into enclosure, display, communications module, firmware, power unit, and installation kit.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The activity of defining how a product should function and be structured so it can satisfy stakeholder needs and intended use.
Expansion: In a project context, product design translates requirements and stakeholder expectations into an actionable design for the product, service, or result being created. It shapes features, performance characteristics, interfaces, and other solution attributes before or during delivery. Product design is especially relevant where the project's success depends on how well the final output solves the underlying need.
Contrast: Project design — product design focuses on the characteristics of the deliverable itself, while project design concerns how the project work is organised and executed.
Example: A medical-device project develops the product design by specifying user-interface layout, safety tolerances, and component interactions before prototyping.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The product goal describes a future state of the product that serves as the long-term objective for the Scrum Team.
Expansion: In PMI's agile and Scrum context, the product goal gives the team a durable target that guides backlog ordering and incremental delivery. It provides coherence across multiple iterations by tying near-term work to an intended product outcome. It is especially useful when teams need a shared direction without fixing all detailed requirements upfront.
Contrast: Sprint goal — a product goal is the longer-term objective for the product, while a sprint goal is the near-term objective for a single sprint.
Example: A digital-banking team sets a product goal to enable customers to open an account fully online, then orders backlog items over several sprints to achieve that outcome.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / The Standard for Project Management — glossary; Scrum Guide reproduced in Agile Practice Guide context, exact page citation requires source verification

Core Meaning: A series of phases that represent the evolution of a product from concept through delivery, growth, maturity, and retirement.
Expansion: The product life cycle describes how a product evolves over time after it is conceived and introduced. PMI uses it to distinguish the ongoing existence of a product from the temporary project that creates or changes it. This perspective helps organizations plan enhancement, support, and retirement decisions beyond initial delivery. It is especially useful where products remain in service long after a project closes.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The product life cycle is the full arc of a product's existence, from concept through retirement. It focuses on the product as an enduring business asset rather than on a temporary body of project work.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because value realization often continues long after a project ends. It applies in product development, operations, portfolio decisions, and any environment where teams must maintain, enhance, and eventually retire products.

The most common misconception: that it is the same as the project life cycle. A project life cycle ends when the temporary effort is complete, while the product life cycle continues as the product is used, supported, improved, and ultimately withdrawn.

How it shows up in real projects: one project may launch a product, later projects may add major features, operations may sustain it in production, and a later initiative may decommission it when strategy shifts.
Contrast: Project life cycle — the product life cycle covers the product's evolution over time; the project life cycle covers the temporary work to create or change it.
Example: A mobile banking app is conceived, launched through a delivery project, enhanced over several releases, sustained in operations, and later retired when replaced by a new platform.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, product life cycle, page 725

Core Meaning: The integration of people, data, processes, and business systems to create, maintain, and evolve a product or service throughout its life cycle.
Expansion: Product management is the coordinated management of a product or service across its life cycle, not just during one project. PMI frames it as integrating people, data, processes, and business systems so the offering can be created, sustained, and evolved over time. It is broader than project delivery because it continues after an initial release and focuses on ongoing customer value and business performance.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Product management directs the evolution of a product or service across its full life cycle, from concept and launch through enhancement and retirement. It connects market need, customer value, business goals, and delivery decisions into one continuous management discipline.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters when success depends on sustained value over time rather than just completing a one-time initiative. It is especially important in product-centric organisations where strategy, delivery, operations, analytics, and customer feedback all shape what happens next.

The most common misconception: that product management and project management are interchangeable. They overlap, but product management is centred on the life and performance of the product, while project management is centred on delivering a defined temporary effort.

How it shows up in real projects: teams use roadmaps, release decisions, feedback loops, usage data, lifecycle planning, and cross-functional governance to decide what to build, improve, maintain, or retire.
Contrast: Project Management — product management governs the product across its life cycle; project management governs the temporary effort undertaken to deliver a result.
Example: A digital payments team launches a wallet feature, then continues to refine onboarding, fraud controls, pricing options, and user experience based on market feedback and adoption data.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, page 270

Core Meaning: A person responsible for maximizing the value of the product and accountable for the end product.
Expansion: The product owner is the role accountable for maximizing product value by deciding what work should be done first and what should be deferred. In PMI usage, the role is central in adaptive and agile settings where priorities change as teams learn. It connects stakeholder needs and business goals to the team's ordered work. This makes it a key decision point for value-focused delivery.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The product owner is the person who makes prioritization decisions for the product and is accountable for the value of what is produced. The role turns stakeholder needs, feedback, and business objectives into an ordered flow of work.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because teams need a clear authority for tradeoff decisions when time, capacity, and demand compete. It applies most directly in agile and adaptive delivery, especially where products evolve through frequent feedback and incremental release.

The most common misconception: that the product owner is only a backlog administrator or stakeholder messenger. In practice the role carries real decision authority over value and priority, not just record keeping.

How it shows up in real projects: the product owner reviews customer feedback, reorders the backlog, clarifies acceptance expectations, and decides that a high-value checkout improvement should be built before lower-impact interface changes.
Contrast: Project manager — the product owner focuses on product value and prioritization; the project manager focuses on coordinating project work and delivery.
Example: On an e-commerce initiative, the product owner prioritizes payment reliability features ahead of cosmetic storefront updates because failed transactions hurt business value more.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, product owner, page 168

Core Meaning: The degree to which a product meets its intended functional, quality, and user expectations in operation.
Expansion: Product performance concerns how well the delivered product actually works against requirements, acceptance criteria, and stakeholder needs. It matters because project success is not only about delivering outputs on time and within budget but also about whether the resulting product performs effectively in use. PMI addresses this through product, quality, measurement, and value-delivery concepts rather than always as a single formal glossary term.
Contrast: Project performance — project performance concerns how the project is being managed and delivered; product performance concerns how the resulting deliverable performs for users or operations.
Example: A software project meets schedule targets, but the delivered platform still fails response-time thresholds under normal user load, revealing weak product performance.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A product roadmap is a high-level visual summary of a product's vision, direction, priorities, and progress over time.
Expansion: In PMI product management usage, a product roadmap communicates how planned work is intended to advance product goals and deliver value over time. It aligns stakeholders around likely sequencing, major outcomes, and strategic intent rather than serving as a fixed delivery schedule. It is commonly updated as learning, market conditions, and priorities change.
Contrast: Release plan — a product roadmap shows strategic direction and higher-level sequencing; a release plan details nearer-term delivery content and timing.
Example: A digital-banking program uses a roadmap to show that onboarding simplification comes before savings-goal features and later expands into small-business services.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The features and functions that characterize a product, service, or result.
Expansion: Product scope defines what the delivered product, service, or result includes. It focuses on the characteristics and capabilities of the outcome itself rather than the work required to create it. PMI distinguishes it from project scope so teams can separate the nature of the deliverable from the effort used to produce it. That distinction supports clearer requirements, validation, and stakeholder alignment.
Contrast: Project scope — product scope describes the deliverable's features and functions; project scope describes the work performed to create it.
Example: In a hospital portal project, product scope includes appointment booking, test-result viewing, and secure messaging for patients.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition and PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Product scope, page 271

Core Meaning: Product strategy is the integrated set of choices about how a product will create value, serve target users, and achieve desired business outcomes.
Expansion: Product strategy defines the logic behind a product's direction, including the users it serves, the problems it addresses, and the outcomes it is meant to achieve. In PMI-aligned practice, it connects organizational strategy to product decisions, investment choices, and prioritization. It guides tradeoffs across features, timing, market focus, and measures of success, especially when resources are limited and demand exceeds capacity.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Product strategy is the decision framework that explains why a product exists, for whom it creates value, and how it will win or remain relevant. It turns broad business intent into a coherent basis for prioritizing product work.

Why it matters and where it applies: without strategy, teams can deliver many outputs that do not add up to meaningful outcomes. It matters in product management, digital transformation, platform evolution, and any project or product environment where choices must be made among competing opportunities.

The most common misconception: that product strategy is just a feature list or roadmap. In practice, those are expressions of the strategy; the strategy itself is the reasoning about markets, users, value proposition, and outcomes.

How it shows up in real projects: teams use product strategy to screen ideas, shape roadmaps, justify funding, define success measures, and decide which requests to defer even when stakeholders are pressing for them.
Contrast: Product roadmap — product strategy explains the choices and value logic behind the product; a product roadmap shows how priorities are expected to unfold over time.
Example: A health-tech company defines a strategy to win mid-size clinics by reducing appointment no-shows through integrated reminders, analytics, and easy staff workflows, and then uses that logic to prioritize its delivery backlog.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A product vision is a concise description of the desired future state a product is intended to create for users and the organization.
Expansion: The product vision expresses the aspirational end state that gives the product direction and purpose. It helps teams and stakeholders align on what they are trying to achieve before debating detailed features or delivery sequencing. In PMI-aligned product and agile practice, it anchors prioritization and supports consistent decision-making as conditions change.
Contrast: Project vision — a product vision describes the enduring future of the product and its value; a project vision is tied to the temporary effort delivering change.
Example: A university defines the product vision for its student portal as one seamless digital front door for enrollment, advising, billing, and academic support.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Production rate is the quantity of work, units, or deliverable output produced in a given period of time.
Expansion: In project management, production rate is a planning and control measure used to estimate duration, resource needs, and work performance. It is common in construction, manufacturing, installation, and other execution-heavy environments where repeatable output can be measured per hour, shift, or day. Project teams use it to forecast progress, compare planned versus actual performance, and adjust capacity or methods when throughput changes.
Contrast: Productivity — production rate measures output over time, while productivity usually compares output to the resources or effort used to produce it.
Example: A construction project estimates that a crew can install 120 square meters of flooring per day and uses that production rate to schedule the finishing work.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Productivity is the efficiency with which project resources convert effort and inputs into useful output.
Expansion: In project work, productivity helps teams understand whether labor, equipment, or other resources are producing expected results. It is used in estimating, performance analysis, and continuous improvement because changes in productivity affect cost, schedule, and capacity. Productivity can improve through better methods, clearer requirements, fewer interruptions, or more capable tools and skills.
Contrast: Efficiency — productivity focuses on the amount of useful output produced from inputs, while efficiency more broadly concerns minimizing waste in performing work.
Example: An engineering team automates test execution and increases the number of validated features completed per week without adding staff, improving productivity.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Professional conduct is behavior that demonstrates ethical responsibility, respect, fairness, honesty, and accountability in project work.
Expansion: Within PMI's framework, professional conduct refers to acting in line with accepted ethical standards and professional responsibilities while managing projects, programs, or portfolios. It shapes how practitioners make decisions, use authority, communicate, handle conflicts, and protect stakeholder trust. It is foundational because project success depends not only on technical delivery but also on credible, principled behavior under pressure.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Professional conduct is the practical expression of ethics in day-to-day project behavior. It includes how practitioners exercise judgment, treat people, represent information, and respond to dilemmas.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects depend on trust across sponsors, teams, vendors, customers, and regulators. Professional conduct matters wherever decisions involve uncertainty, power, resource competition, reporting, confidentiality, or competing stakeholder interests.

The most common misconception: that professional conduct is only about avoiding obvious misconduct. In practice it also includes positive duties such as transparent reporting, respectful engagement, responsible escalation, and making decisions that uphold the profession's standards.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager reports schedule slippage accurately instead of masking it, avoids favoritism in procurement discussions, protects confidential data, and raises risks even when the message is unwelcome.
Contrast: Compliance — compliance is adherence to rules or requirements; professional conduct is the broader ethical and behavioral standard guiding how a practitioner acts.
Example: During a vendor evaluation, a project manager discloses a prior relationship with one bidder, recuses themselves from scoring that proposal, and documents the decision path for transparency.
Related Terms: Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct
Source: PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct, p. 2-4

Core Meaning: The ongoing enhancement of a practitioner's knowledge, skills, and capabilities to improve performance in project work and related roles.
Expansion: In a PMI context, professional development refers to structured and self-directed learning that strengthens competence across project, program, and portfolio domains. It supports both current job performance and longer-term career growth through education, experience, reflection, and skill building. The concept is important to the profession, but it is not typically treated as a tightly bounded project management glossary term in the way process or governance terms are.
Contrast: Training — training is a specific learning intervention; professional development is the broader, ongoing growth of capability over time.
Example: A project manager completes risk workshops, applies the learning on a live initiative, and reflects on lessons learned to improve future planning and stakeholder engagement.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Professionalism is the consistent demonstration of ethical conduct, responsibility, respect, and competent practice in project work.
Expansion: Within the PMI context, professionalism is expressed through accountability, ethical behavior, respectful stakeholder interaction, and commitment to competent practice. It shapes how project professionals make decisions, communicate, handle conflict, and exercise judgment under pressure. Professionalism is foundational because project success depends not only on methods and tools but also on trust, integrity, and responsible behavior.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Professionalism is the disciplined way a project practitioner applies knowledge, judgment, and ethical standards in day-to-day work. It combines competence with behavior, especially in situations involving uncertainty, pressure, or competing interests.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects rely on credibility, stakeholder trust, and responsible decision-making. Professionalism affects governance, team culture, escalation, procurement, reporting, and any moment where a project manager's conduct shapes confidence in the work.

The most common misconception: that professionalism is mainly about etiquette or formal appearance. In practice, it is much more about integrity, accountability, respect, and acting in line with ethical and professional standards even when doing so is inconvenient.

How it shows up in real projects: raising risks early instead of hiding bad news, giving accurate status reports, respecting confidential information, acknowledging mistakes, and making decisions that balance delivery pressure with safety, fairness, and long-term value.
Contrast: Ethics — professionalism is the broader pattern of competent and responsible conduct, while ethics focuses specifically on principles of right behavior and moral decision-making.
Example: A project manager reports a likely schedule slip as soon as evidence emerges, explains the impact honestly, and works with stakeholders on recovery options instead of masking the delay.
Related Terms: Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct
Source: PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct / A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition — professionalism-related concepts; exact page citation requires source verification

Core Meaning: Related projects, subsidiary programs, and program activities managed in a coordinated manner to obtain benefits not available from managing them individually.
Expansion: A program coordinates multiple related components so the organization can realize benefits that would be harder to achieve if each component were managed separately. PMI emphasizes benefits and coordination rather than simply grouping projects together. Programs often include governance, shared planning, and alignment to strategic objectives.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A program is a coordinated set of related work components managed together to achieve benefits. It may include projects, other programs, and ongoing activities that contribute to the same strategic outcome.

Why it matters and where it applies: programs are useful when benefits depend on integration, sequencing, or shared governance across multiple efforts. They are common in transformation, product line, and capability-building initiatives.

The most common misconception: that a program is just a large project. In PMI terms, the key difference is coordinated management for benefits, not size alone.

How it shows up in real projects: a digital transformation program may include separate projects for platform migration, process redesign, training, and change management, all managed together to realize enterprise benefits.
Contrast: Project — a program coordinates related work to realize benefits; a project delivers a unique output or result.
Example: A bank runs a program that includes mobile app upgrades, core system integration, and customer onboarding redesign to improve digital service adoption.
Source: The Standard for Program Management, Fifth Edition — Program, page 4

Core Meaning: A project, subsidiary program, or related activity that is included within a program and managed in a coordinated way to realize program benefits.
Expansion: A program component is one of the constituent parts of a program's structure. PMI uses the term to describe the projects, subprograms, and other work within the program boundary that together contribute to strategic outcomes and benefits. The concept matters because program management is performed through the coordination, alignment, and oversight of these components rather than through one undifferentiated body of work.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A program component is a discrete element inside a program, such as a project, a subsidiary program, or another coordinated activity. Each component has its own purpose and management needs, but it exists within the larger program structure.

Why it matters and where it applies: program managers do not deliver benefits by managing only one schedule at a high level; they do so by aligning multiple components toward shared outcomes. This is especially important in transformations, capability rollouts, infrastructure portfolios grouped as programs, and other environments where benefits emerge from coordinated change.

The most common misconception: that a program is simply a large project and its components are just tasks or work packages. In PMI terms, components are managed entities in their own right, often with separate leaders, plans, constraints, and deliverables.

How it shows up in real projects: a digital transformation program may include a CRM implementation project, a data-migration project, a training initiative, and a change-management effort, each treated as a program component that must be synchronized for benefits realization.
Contrast: Work package — a program component is a managed project, subprogram, or related activity within a program; a work package is a lower-level decomposition of project scope.
Example: A hospital modernization program includes a facility-upgrade project, an electronic-records rollout project, and a staff-readiness initiative, each managed as a separate program component.
Source: The Standard for Program Management, Fifth Edition — Program component, page 14

Core Meaning: The application of knowledge, skills, and principles to a program to achieve the program objectives and obtain benefits and control not available by managing program components individually.
Expansion: Program management coordinates related components so the organization can realize benefits and control that would not be achieved by managing each effort separately. It focuses on interdependencies, benefits realization, shared governance, and outcomes across the program. PMI treats it as distinct from single-project delivery because the emphasis is on coordinated value rather than isolated outputs. It is especially important when multiple projects must work together to produce organizational change.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Program management is the coordinated management of related projects, subsidiary programs, and program activities to achieve benefits and control. It works across component boundaries to manage dependencies, sequencing, and shared outcomes.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters when the intended value depends on combining several efforts into a larger change. It is common in transformation, infrastructure modernization, product family evolution, and enterprise capability-building initiatives.

The most common misconception: that a program is simply a very large project. The distinction is not size alone; program management is defined by coordinated benefits, interdependence, and integrated governance across related components.

How it shows up in real projects: a transformation program governs separate projects for process redesign, system implementation, training, rollout, and adoption measurement so they produce a combined business outcome rather than disconnected deliverables.
Contrast: Project management — program management coordinates related components for benefits and control; project management delivers a specific product, service, or result.
Example: A bank manages mobile app upgrades, core platform integration, training, and branch process redesign as one digital banking program to improve customer adoption and service quality.
Source: The Standard for Program Management, Fifth Edition, program management, page 4

Core Meaning: A structure that supports program governance, integration, oversight, and administrative coordination for a program.
Expansion: A program management office is the support and integration function established for a program to help coordinate its components and sustain effective governance. In PMI usage, it is distinct from a project management office because it is oriented to program-level integration, benefits, reporting, decision support, and cross-component coordination. It is foundational because many programs rely on this structure to maintain alignment, visibility, and control across multiple related efforts.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A program management office, often abbreviated PgMO, is an enabling structure that provides coordination, reporting, governance support, and management services for a program. It helps the program manager and component leaders operate as a coherent system.

Why it matters and where it applies: programs typically span multiple projects, stakeholders, dependencies, and benefit streams. A program management office creates consistency in planning, reporting, risk visibility, change coordination, and decision support, which is especially valuable in enterprise change programs and multi-vendor or multi-workstream initiatives.

The most common misconception: that a program management office is simply the same thing as a PMO at a larger scale. In practice, a PMO may support many projects across an organization, while a program management office is focused on one program and on integrating its components to realize benefits.

How it shows up in real projects: in a merger integration program, the program management office may consolidate status from workstreams, manage dependency logs, coordinate governance meetings, maintain issue escalation paths, and support benefits tracking for leadership.
Contrast: Project management office — a program management office supports a specific program and its integrated benefits delivery; a project management office typically provides broader project support, standards, or oversight across projects.
Example: A retailer's omnichannel transformation program uses a program management office to coordinate release timing, executive reporting, dependency management, and governance across e-commerce, store systems, and supply-chain projects.
Source: The Standard for Program Management, Fifth Edition — Program management office, page 133

Core Meaning: A meeting held to review current status, discuss progress against plan, surface issues, and coordinate next actions.
Expansion: In project practice, a progress meeting is a recurring status-oriented meeting used to compare actual progress with planned work and to align stakeholders on immediate actions. It supports monitoring and controlling by creating a forum for reporting, escalation, and coordination. The term is widely used in practice, but it is not usually treated by PMI as a distinct formal glossary entry with a stable technical definition.
Contrast: Progress update — a progress meeting is the live forum for discussing status; a progress update is the communicated content about that status.
Example: During a construction project, the site team holds a weekly progress meeting to review completed work, schedule slippage, safety issues, and actions needed from subcontractors.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A progress report is a document or communication that describes the current status of project work against the plan.
Expansion: In PMI practice, a progress report communicates how work is advancing, typically including completed work, work in progress, upcoming work, and notable variances or issues. It supports monitoring by making schedule, cost, scope, or performance information visible to stakeholders. It is a reporting artifact rather than the broader management activity of tracking and controlling performance.
Contrast: Status report — a progress report focuses on advancement of work against plan, while a status report may present a broader current-state snapshot including health, risks, and decisions needed.
Example: A construction project manager issues a weekly progress report showing foundation work completed, steel delivery delayed by three days, and the forecast effect on the milestone schedule.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Progress reporting is the ongoing activity of collecting, analysing, and communicating information about project performance and advancement.
Expansion: It is part of project monitoring and stakeholder communication, turning performance data into usable information for decision-making. In PMI terms, this activity draws on work performance data and produces work performance information and reports for stakeholders. It helps compare actual results with the baseline so that corrective action, escalation, or replanning can occur when needed.
Contrast: Performance reporting — progress reporting emphasises advancement of planned work, while performance reporting more broadly communicates analysed project performance across measures such as schedule, cost, quality, and forecast outcomes.
Example: On an ERP implementation, the PMO consolidates team updates each Friday and publishes progress reporting that compares completed configuration tasks and testing results with the approved baseline.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A communication that reports the current state of work, typically comparing actual progress with the plan and noting key issues or next steps.
Expansion: A progress update is a status communication artifact rather than a distinct PMI technical concept. It is commonly used to keep stakeholders informed about what has been completed, what is in progress, and what needs attention.
Contrast: Status report — a progress update is any communication on current progress; a status report is a more formal, structured reporting document or output.
Example: A project lead sends a Friday progress update summarizing completed milestones, delayed tasks, and the actions planned for the next week.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The iterative process of increasing the level of detail in a project management plan as greater amounts of information and more accurate estimates become available.
Expansion: Progressive elaboration means planning becomes more detailed as the team learns more about the work. PMI treats it as controlled refinement, not uncertainty being ignored or planning deferred carelessly. It helps teams begin with high-level direction and improve detail, estimates, and planning precision over time.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Progressive elaboration is the gradual refinement of plans, estimates, and supporting detail as more information becomes available. The team starts with what is known and adds precision as uncertainty decreases.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because many projects cannot be fully detailed at the start without wasting effort or making weak assumptions. It applies across predictive, hybrid, and adaptive environments, especially where complexity or uncertainty is significant.

The most common misconception: that progressive elaboration is the same as uncontrolled change. It is actually a disciplined planning practice, whereas uncontrolled change alters scope without proper evaluation.

How it shows up in real projects: a project begins with summary milestones and broad estimates, then breaks work into detailed packages after requirements, design, or vendor information becomes clearer.
Contrast: Scope creep — progressive elaboration is controlled refinement of known work; scope creep is uncontrolled expansion of scope.
Example: A product implementation starts with a high-level release roadmap, then adds detailed tasks and estimates after integration requirements are clarified.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition — progressive elaboration, page 559

Core Meaning: A temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result.
Expansion: A project is temporary because it has a defined start and finish, and it is unique because the work produces something new or distinct. PMI distinguishes projects from operations by emphasizing that projects are created to deliver change, not to perform ongoing repetitive work. Projects may be large or small, predictive or adaptive, but they always exist to achieve a specific outcome within constraints.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A project is a temporary effort organized to produce a unique outcome. It has a beginning and an end, and its work is directed toward a defined objective rather than routine operations.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects are the primary vehicle organizations use to create change, whether that change is a new product, a process improvement, a facility, or a service capability. The concept applies across all industries and delivery approaches.

The most common misconception: that a project is just any piece of work. In PMI terms, the defining features are temporary nature and uniqueness of the result.

How it shows up in real projects: a team is chartered to implement a new payroll system, deliver it, transition it to operations, and then close the project.
Contrast: Operations — a project is temporary and unique; operations are ongoing and repetitive.
Example: An organization launches a project to replace its legacy HR system with a new cloud-based platform.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Seventh Edition — Project, page 4

Core Meaning: A structured examination of a project's processes, controls, compliance, and performance to assess whether it is being managed effectively and according to required standards or expectations.
Expansion: A project audit is a formal review that evaluates selected aspects of a project such as governance, financial control, quality practices, risk management, procurement compliance, or adherence to organisational procedures. Unlike routine status monitoring, it is typically independent or semi-independent and is intended to provide assurance, identify gaps, and recommend corrective action or lessons for future work. It is commonly used in regulated, high-risk, or high-visibility projects where objective oversight is needed beyond normal project reporting.
Contrast: Project review — a project review assesses status, progress, or readiness; a project audit is a more formal assurance-focused examination of controls, compliance, and management effectiveness.
Example: During a public-sector IT modernization effort, an internal audit team reviews procurement records, change approvals, risk logs, and reporting controls to confirm the project is complying with governance requirements.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A concise early description of a proposed project used to summarize purpose, scope, context, and key expectations before fuller authorization or planning.
Expansion: Project brief is a commonly used practitioner term for a short document that outlines the project's rationale, objectives, major deliverables, constraints, and stakeholders at a high level. PMI materials use nearby formal concepts such as business case and project charter more consistently than project brief as a standard glossary term. In practice, a project brief often serves as a precursor or lightweight input to chartering, funding, or initiation decisions.
Contrast: Project charter — a project brief is often an informal or preliminary summary; a project charter formally authorizes the project and the project manager's use of organizational resources.
Example: Before funding a customer-portal redesign, a product sponsor circulates a two-page project brief describing the business problem, target users, expected outcomes, budget range, and initial timeline assumptions.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The project budget is the approved estimate for the funds needed to complete the project work.
Expansion: In PMI practice, the project budget aggregates estimated costs for project activities or work packages and establishes the authorised spending plan. It is typically time-phased to support cost control and is aligned with the cost baseline, with management reserves handled according to governance rules. The budget provides the reference point for monitoring expenditures, forecasting, and evaluating cost performance throughout the project life cycle.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The project budget is the authorised financial plan for delivering the project. It is built from cost estimates and rolled up into a level suitable for approval, funding, and control.

Why it matters and where it applies: every project needs a clear funding reference so the team can plan work, commit resources, and monitor cost performance. It is especially important in projects with procurement, phased funding, or executive oversight because spending must be tracked against an approved baseline.

The most common misconception: that the budget is just an early rough estimate. In practice, once approved it becomes a control reference, and changes to it normally require formal review and change control.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager compares actual costs and earned value against the time-phased budget, explains variances in monthly reviews, and requests budget changes when approved scope changes increase authorised spending.
Contrast: Cost baseline — the project budget is the broader approved funding plan, while the cost baseline is the approved time-phased version used specifically to measure and monitor cost performance.
Example: A data-centre migration project receives an approved budget covering hardware, vendor labour, internal staff time, contingency, and transition support, then tracks monthly actuals against the planned spend curve.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition / Process Groups: A Practice Guide, project cost management content and funding baseline discussion; exact glossary-style page citation varies by edition

Core Meaning: A calendar that identifies working days and shifts available for scheduled activities.
Expansion: A project calendar defines when project work can be performed. It captures working time, nonworking time, and shifts that affect schedule calculations. It is used with activity durations and resource availability to build realistic schedules.
Contrast: Resource calendar — a project calendar applies to the project schedule overall; a resource calendar applies to a specific resource or resource group.
Example: A construction project calendar excludes weekends and public holidays, so activity dates are calculated only on working days.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Project calendars, p. 81

Core Meaning: A document issued by the project sponsor that formally authorizes the existence of a project and gives the project manager authority to apply organizational resources.
Expansion: The project charter is the formal starting point that authorizes the project and identifies its purpose, high-level objectives, and initial authority. It typically names the project manager and establishes the sponsor's support for the work. PMI treats it as a key governance document that connects the project to business need and organizational commitment.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A project charter is the document that officially starts a project. It gives the project manager authority and records the high-level reason, objectives, and boundaries for the work.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because no project should begin without clear authorization and alignment to business need. It is used in predictive, agile, and hybrid environments as the formal basis for initiating work and clarifying accountability.

The most common misconception: that the charter is a detailed plan. It is not a full schedule or requirements document; it is a high-level authorization and direction document.

How it shows up in real projects: a sponsor signs the charter for a system replacement project, names the project manager, and approves the initial budget and high-level scope so planning can begin.
Contrast: Business case — a business case explains why the work should be done; a project charter formally authorizes the project to begin.
Example: A healthcare organization issues a charter for an electronic records upgrade, naming the sponsor, project manager, objectives, and initial authority to use resources.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — project charter

Core Meaning: Project closure is the process of formally completing the project or phase and transitioning, handing off, or ending the work in an orderly way.
Expansion: In PMI practice, project closure confirms that planned work is complete or that the project is being terminated appropriately, and that acceptance, records, lessons learned, and final transitions are handled. It includes administrative, contractual, financial, and knowledge-capture activities needed to close out the effort responsibly. Closure turns completed delivery into an accountable organisational outcome rather than simply stopping work.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Project closure is the formal set of actions that ends a project or phase. It verifies completion criteria, secures acceptance where required, closes procurements and accounts, archives records, and transfers ownership of deliverables or ongoing responsibilities.

Why it matters and where it applies: without closure, organisations lose lessons, leave obligations unresolved, and create confusion about support, ownership, or remaining risk. It applies to predictive, agile, and hybrid work alike, though the exact artifacts differ by life cycle and governance model.

The most common misconception: that closure is just a final meeting or a declaration that the team is done. In practice it is a governed transition activity that protects value, confirms accountability, and preserves organisational knowledge.

How it shows up in real projects: after deploying a new customer portal, the team obtains business sign-off, transitions support to operations, finalises vendor invoices, captures lessons learned, releases resources, and archives project records.
Contrast: Phase closure — project closure ends the entire project, while phase closure formally completes only one phase before the next begins or before a phase-gate decision.
Example: A hospital equipment-upgrade project closes by obtaining final acceptance from clinical leadership, transferring maintenance documents to operations, reconciling supplier payments, and recording lessons learned for future sites.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, Close Project or Phase, Section 4.7; also reflected in Process Groups: A Practice Guide

Core Meaning: Project communications are the purposeful generation, collection, distribution, storage, retrieval, management, monitoring, and disposition of project information.
Expansion: Project communications cover how information moves among stakeholders so work can be coordinated, decisions can be made, and expectations can be managed. PMI treats communication as both a management process and a practical capability that affects stakeholder engagement, risk visibility, and delivery performance. It includes selecting channels, tailoring messages to audiences, and ensuring information is timely, accurate, and usable.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Project communications are the structured and ongoing exchange of project information among team members, sponsors, customers, vendors, and other stakeholders. It includes both formal and informal communication, written and verbal communication, and pull, push, and interactive methods.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects fail when the right people do not get the right information at the right time. Communication is central in every delivery approach because alignment, escalation, approvals, stakeholder confidence, and issue resolution all depend on it.

The most common misconception: that project communications means sending status reports. In practice, it is much broader and includes planning communication needs, shaping messages for different stakeholders, confirming understanding, and managing information flow across the full project lifecycle.

How it shows up in real projects: communication plans, daily coordination, steering-committee updates, RAID reporting, vendor notices, decision logs, dashboards, demos, workshops, and stakeholder-specific briefings.
Contrast: Stakeholder engagement — project communications focuses on the flow and management of information, while stakeholder engagement focuses on building support, involvement, and relationships.
Example: On an ERP rollout, the project manager uses weekly team standups, monthly sponsor dashboards, change-impact briefings for department heads, and a central repository for decisions and risks.
Source: PMBOK Guide — not confirmed to exact page in available search results

Core Meaning: Project context is the internal and external environment of conditions, influences, and constraints that shape how a project is selected, planned, and delivered.
Expansion: PMI uses project context to describe the surrounding circumstances that affect project decisions, including organisational structure, culture, governance, market conditions, regulations, technology, and stakeholder environment. It explains why the same project-management approach is not suitable for every project. Understanding context helps tailor methods, life cycle choices, governance, communication, and risk responses to the situation at hand.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Project context is the setting in which the project exists, not just the work inside the project boundary. It includes organisational strategy, culture, stakeholder dynamics, compliance obligations, delivery environment, and other situational factors that influence decisions.

Why it matters and where it applies: context is central to tailoring. Teams that understand context choose more suitable delivery approaches, governance levels, and engagement methods, which improves the fit between project practices and real operating conditions.

The most common misconception: that context is background information with little effect on delivery. In practice it often determines decision speed, risk exposure, stakeholder expectations, and how much agility or control is realistic.

How it shows up in real projects: a regulated healthcare initiative uses heavier documentation and validation than an internal innovation pilot because the project context includes strict compliance, patient-safety concerns, and external oversight.
Contrast: Enterprise environmental factors — project context is the broader situational setting for the project, while enterprise environmental factors are specific internal and external conditions that influence project work.
Example: A global system rollout is tailored differently across countries because local regulations, vendor maturity, language needs, and organisational readiness create different project contexts.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition, sections on tailoring and project environment; exact glossary-style page citation varies by edition

Core Meaning: The monetary resources needed to complete project work and produce the project deliverables.
Expansion: Project cost refers to the estimated and actual spending associated with carrying out project activities and acquiring required resources. In PMI practice, it is planned, budgeted, monitored, and controlled as part of project cost management. It includes direct and indirect cost elements as defined by the project's accounting and governance approach.
Contrast: Project budget — project cost is the spending associated with the work; the project budget is the approved allocation against which that spending is managed.
Example: In a warehouse automation project, project cost includes software licences, contractor labour, equipment installation, training, and testing expenses.
Source: PMBOK Guide — confirm exact citation in Infinity

Core Meaning: Documents that are not part of the project management plan but are used to manage the project.
Expansion: Project documents are the working records and supporting artifacts used throughout the project. They include items such as logs, registers, schedules, assumptions, and issue records. These documents are updated as the project progresses and support planning, execution, monitoring, and control. PMI distinguishes them from the project management plan because they support the work rather than define the approved management approach.
Contrast: Project management plan — the project management plan defines the approved approach for managing the project; project documents are the supporting records used to run it.
Example: A team maintains an issue log, risk register, stakeholder register, and assumption log as project documents during delivery.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, project documents, page 244

Core Meaning: Forecasted expenditures for the project, by period and by funding source, that are needed to finance the project.
Expansion: Project funding requirements describe when money will be needed and from which sources it will come. They help align financing with the project schedule and expected cash flow. They can include periodic funding needs as well as approved reserves. They support budgeting, financing, and governance decisions.
Contrast: Cost baseline — the cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget for project work; project funding requirements describe the financing needed to support that spending.
Example: A capital project forecasts quarterly funding for design, procurement, and construction so finance can release funds at the right time.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, project funding requirements, page 243

Core Meaning: A framework, functions, and processes that guide project management activities in order to meet organizational or strategic goals.
Expansion: Project governance defines how direction, oversight, decision-making, and accountability are structured for a project. In PMI usage, it connects project work to organizational governance and strategy by establishing authorities, policies, escalation paths, and control mechanisms. It helps ensure that project decisions are made consistently, transparently, and in alignment with intended benefits and risk tolerances.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Project governance is the system through which a project is directed and overseen. It includes decision rights, review structures, roles, policies, and criteria used to steer the project and judge performance.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects operate within larger organizational systems, so they need clear authority and oversight to stay aligned with strategy, funding constraints, compliance expectations, and stakeholder priorities. Governance is especially important in complex, regulated, cross-functional, or high-visibility initiatives where unclear authority can slow decisions or increase risk.

The most common misconception: that project governance is just status reporting or executive approval gates. In practice, reporting is only one visible output; governance is the broader structure that clarifies who decides what, how trade-offs are made, when escalation occurs, and how alignment is maintained.

How it shows up in real projects: steering committees, sponsor decision forums, phase-gate reviews, delegated authorities, escalation thresholds, change approval rules, and clear accountability for benefits, risk, and value delivery.
Contrast: Governance framework — project governance applies that broader governing structure specifically to a project; a governance framework may exist at organizational, portfolio, program, or project level.
Example: In an enterprise ERP rollout, the sponsor approves scope trade-offs above a set threshold, a steering committee reviews benefit realization monthly, and the project manager escalates schedule risks through defined governance channels.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition and The Standard for Project Management, page 237

Core Meaning: Data that has been organized, interpreted, or communicated so it is meaningful for managing and governing a project.
Expansion: Project information includes status details, forecasts, decisions, assumptions, issues, and other content used to direct and control project work. It is more useful than raw data because it has been contextualised for stakeholders or management needs. Effective project information supports timely decisions, transparency, and alignment across the project environment.
Contrast: Work performance data — project information is processed and contextualised for use, while work performance data is the raw observation gathered during execution.
Example: A project manager converts task-completion data, cost figures, and risk updates into a weekly dashboard that helps the sponsor assess overall project health.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Seventh Edition, page 260

Core Meaning: The knowledge area concerned with identifying, defining, combining, unifying, and coordinating the various processes and project management activities so the project works as a coherent whole.
Expansion: Project Integration Management brings the many parts of a project together so decisions made in one area remain aligned with the project's overall objectives, constraints, and governance. In PMI's process-based framing, it includes developing foundational documents, directing work, managing knowledge, monitoring and controlling the project holistically, performing integrated change control, and closing the project or phase. It matters because projects fail less often from a single isolated mistake than from disconnected decisions, unresolved trade-offs, and weak coordination across planning, execution, and control. The concept is central to the project manager's role because it links strategy, delivery, stakeholder expectations, and change into one managed system.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Project Integration Management is the discipline of making the project function as one system rather than a collection of separate plans and specialist activities. It connects the charter, plans, work, changes, knowledge, and closure decisions into a unified management approach.

Why it matters and where it applies: every project contains interdependencies across scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, risk, procurement, and stakeholders. Integration matters wherever one decision affects several others, which means it applies on virtually all projects, whether predictive, adaptive, or hybrid.

The most common misconception: that integration is just administration or document consolidation. In practice, it is the active leadership work of balancing trade-offs, resolving conflicts, sequencing decisions, and ensuring that changes support the project's intended outcomes.

How it shows up in real projects: a change request that improves product scope may also affect delivery timing, funding, risk exposure, vendor commitments, and stakeholder communications. Integration management is the mechanism for evaluating that full impact and deciding coherently rather than in silos.
Contrast: Project coordination — coordination is an activity within delivery; Project Integration Management is the broader discipline of unifying decisions, processes, and change across the whole project.
Example: On an ERP implementation, the project manager assesses a requested reporting enhancement by examining its impact on scope, testing effort, training, deployment date, vendor work, and budget before approving it through integrated change control.
