Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, Project Integration Management, Chapter 4, pages 69-113

Core Meaning: A person who helps the project team to achieve the project objectives, typically by orchestrating the work of the project.
Expansion: A Project Lead coordinates and guides project work so the team can move toward planned outcomes. The term emphasizes helping the team achieve objectives through orchestration, alignment, and support rather than only through positional authority. Depending on the organization, a Project Lead may perform many functions associated with project management, team coordination, or delivery leadership.
Contrast: Project manager — A Project Lead focuses on helping orchestrate the team's work toward objectives, while a project manager is the formally assigned person accountable for applying project management processes and practices.
Example: On a digital transformation initiative, the Project Lead aligns work across business analysts, developers, and training staff to keep dependencies visible and milestones coordinated.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms

Core Meaning: The series of phases that a project passes through from its start to its completion.
Expansion: The project life cycle describes the broad phase structure a project follows from beginning to end. PMI uses it to frame how work is organized, governed, and reviewed across the duration of the project. The exact phases vary by domain and approach, but the concept provides a common structure for managing delivery.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The project life cycle is the overall sequence of phases a project moves through from initiation to closure. It provides the high-level structure within which planning, execution, monitoring, and transition occur.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because phase structure affects governance, resource timing, deliverable reviews, and decision points. It applies to all projects, whether the delivery approach is predictive, hybrid, or adaptive.

The most common misconception: that every project must use the same life cycle model. PMI treats the life cycle as tailored to the nature of the work, industry context, and delivery approach.

How it shows up in real projects: a team may define a life cycle with concept, design, build, test, deploy, and close phases, each with clear outputs and review points.
Contrast: Product life cycle — the project life cycle describes the phases of the project work; the product life cycle describes the stages of the product from creation through retirement.
Example: An enterprise software rollout follows initiation, planning, configuration, testing, deployment, and closure phases with approvals at key transitions.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition — project life cycle, page 554

Core Meaning: The application of knowledge, skills, tools, and techniques to project activities to meet project requirements.
Expansion: Project management is the disciplined practice of leading and coordinating work so that a temporary endeavor achieves its intended objectives. PMI frames it as applying knowledge, skills, tools, and techniques through an appropriate approach, whether predictive, adaptive, or hybrid. It matters because projects create change, and effective management improves the likelihood of delivering value, managing uncertainty, and aligning outcomes with stakeholder needs.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Project management is the professional practice used to initiate, plan, lead, monitor, and close project work in a way that achieves agreed objectives. It combines technical methods with leadership, decision-making, and context-sensitive judgment.

Why it matters and where it applies: organizations rely on projects to implement strategy, deliver products and services, enable transformation, and respond to risk or opportunity. Good project management improves coordination, clarifies scope and accountability, manages constraints, and increases the chance that benefits and value are realized.

The most common misconception: that project management is only scheduling and status tracking. In practice it also includes integrating work, engaging stakeholders, managing risk and uncertainty, tailoring ways of working, and supporting value delivery through the full life cycle.

How it shows up in real projects: defining objectives and success criteria, structuring the team, selecting a delivery approach, sequencing work, managing risks and changes, engaging stakeholders, and adapting decisions as the project environment evolves.
Contrast: Program management — project management focuses on delivering a specific temporary endeavor; program management coordinates related projects and activities to realize broader benefits.
Example: A hospital expansion project manager coordinates design, permitting, procurement, construction, risk responses, and stakeholder communications to deliver the new facility on the required timeline and quality standards.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition and The Standard for Project Management, page 4

Core Meaning: The sum of knowledge within the profession of project management that PMI organizes and publishes through its standards and guides.
Expansion: PMBOK refers to the body of knowledge associated with professional project management, not just a single book. PMI's PMBOK Guide is one major expression of that body of knowledge, capturing broadly recognized principles, domains, practices, and terminology. The concept matters because it gives practitioners a shared professional reference point while still allowing tailoring to context, industry, and delivery approach.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The Project Management Body of Knowledge is the collective professional knowledge relevant to managing projects. PMI curates and communicates that knowledge through standards, guides, and related publications, with the PMBOK Guide being the most widely recognized artifact.

Why it matters and where it applies: a shared body of knowledge creates common language, improves professional consistency, and supports education, certification, and practice across industries. It is useful in predictive, adaptive, and hybrid environments because it provides an organized reference rather than a one-size-fits-all method.

The most common misconception: that PMBOK means a mandatory step-by-step methodology or only the PMBOK Guide book. In practice, the body of knowledge is broader than any single publication and is intended to inform judgment, not replace tailoring.

How it shows up in real projects: teams use PMBOK-based concepts to structure charters, plans, risk approaches, stakeholder engagement, performance measurement, and governance while adapting the level of formality to the project's context.
Contrast: PMBOK Guide — the PMBOK is the broader body of professional knowledge; the PMBOK Guide is a published PMI guide that captures part of that knowledge.
Example: A PMO uses PMBOK-based terminology and practices to standardize risk management, stakeholder planning, and performance reporting across software, construction, and product projects.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition, page 3

Core Meaning: An organizational structure that standardizes the project-related governance processes and facilitates the sharing of resources, methodologies, tools, and techniques.
Expansion: A project management office, or PMO, provides centralized support and governance for projects and sometimes for programs and portfolios as well. Its role can be supportive, controlling, or directive depending on how much authority the organization assigns to it. In PMI practice, it commonly maintains standards, coordinates reporting, enables capability development, and improves consistency in delivery.
Contrast: Project manager — a PMO provides organizational governance and support across multiple initiatives, while a project manager leads the work of a specific project.
Example: A company PMO issues common schedule and risk templates, reviews monthly status reports, and helps project managers escalate decisions through governance channels.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition, Project management office, page 239

Core Meaning: The document that describes how the project will be executed, monitored and controlled, and closed.
Expansion: The project management plan integrates the subsidiary plans and baselines that guide how the project will be managed. It is the central reference for execution, monitoring, control, and closure rather than a simple schedule or task list. PMI treats it as the primary management document that brings scope, schedule, cost, risk, quality, resources, communications, and other management approaches into one coherent whole.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The project management plan is the master management document for the project. It consolidates approved baselines and subsidiary plans so the team has a clear, coordinated way to run the work.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because projects fail when teams manage scope, schedule, cost, risk, and governance in isolation. The plan creates integration across these areas and is used in predictive, hybrid, and adaptive environments, though the level of detail and formality may vary.

The most common misconception: that the project management plan is just a timeline. In practice, the schedule is only one component; the full plan also defines decision rules, change control, reporting, resource approaches, and how performance will be managed.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager uses the plan to align the schedule baseline, risk approach, communication rules, and change process so the team and stakeholders know how the project will operate from start to finish.
Contrast: Project schedule — the project management plan is the integrated guide for managing the whole project; the schedule is one component focused on timing and sequencing.
Example: For a factory upgrade, the team approves a project management plan that combines the scope baseline, schedule baseline, cost baseline, risk management approach, and change control process.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition and Process Groups A Practice Guide — project management plan, page 196

Core Meaning: A logical grouping of project management inputs, tools and techniques, and outputs used to achieve specific project objectives.
Expansion: A Project Management Process Group organises project management processes by purpose rather than by timing, so the grouped processes can be applied where needed across the project life cycle. In PMI usage, the classic groups are Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring and Controlling, and Closing. They are not project phases, and they may overlap or recur within a phase, release, or iteration.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A Project Management Process Group is a category of processes that serve a common management purpose. It provides a way to structure how work is authorised, planned, performed, tracked, controlled, and formally closed.

Why it matters and where it applies: the concept helps practitioners understand that project management work is not random or purely chronological. It is useful in predictive, hybrid, and adaptive environments because teams still need to start work intentionally, plan appropriately, coordinate execution, monitor performance, and close work responsibly.

The most common misconception: that Process Groups are the same as project phases. They are different concepts: phases describe segments of the project life cycle, while Process Groups describe types of management activity that can occur repeatedly within those segments.

How it shows up in real projects: a team may initiate the overall project, plan the first release, execute delivery, monitor progress and risks every week, and close a vendor package before the whole project ends. The same Process Group logic can recur at multiple levels.
Contrast: Project Phase — a Process Group is a category of management activity; a phase is a life-cycle segment of the project.
Example: On an ERP implementation, the team uses Initiating to authorise the project, Planning to build scope and schedule baselines, Executing to configure and test the system, Monitoring and Controlling to manage variance and risks, and Closing to obtain formal acceptance.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / Process Groups: A Practice Guide, page citation needed from PMI source

Core Meaning: The members of the project team who are directly involved in project management activities.
Expansion: The Project Management Team consists of the people responsible for leading, coordinating, and administering the project management work. It commonly includes the project manager and may include project coordinators, schedulers, risk leads, PMO support, or other roles assigned to planning, monitoring, reporting, and control. It is a subset of the broader project team and focuses on managing the project rather than only performing product or technical work.
Contrast: Project Team — the Project Management Team manages and coordinates the project; the Project Team includes all members performing project work, including technical and delivery roles.
Example: In a hospital construction project, the project manager, scheduler, cost controller, and risk coordinator form the Project Management Team, while architects, engineers, contractors, and inspectors are part of the wider project team.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), page citation needed from PMI source

Core Meaning: The person assigned by the performing organization to lead the team that is responsible for achieving the project objectives.
Expansion: The project manager leads the project team and is accountable for integrating people, process, and delivery toward the project objectives. PMI describes the role as one of leadership, coordination, decision making, and stakeholder engagement rather than simple task tracking. The role adapts to context and may emphasize predictive control, agile facilitation, hybrid coordination, or a combination of these.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The project manager is the individual responsible for leading the project toward its intended outcomes. The role includes aligning work, managing constraints, engaging stakeholders, and helping the team make progress through uncertainty.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects need a focal point for integration because objectives, dependencies, risks, and stakeholder expectations rarely manage themselves. The role applies across industries and delivery approaches, though the exact authority and style vary by organization and project context.

The most common misconception: that the project manager is only an administrator who updates schedules and status reports. In practice, effective project managers provide leadership, resolve tradeoffs, shape decisions, and create the conditions for the team to succeed.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager aligns business and technical stakeholders, manages change requests, responds to emerging risks, and keeps delivery focused on the agreed outcomes even when priorities shift.
Contrast: Sponsor — the project manager leads day to day integration and delivery; the sponsor provides authorization, strategic support, and escalation backing.
Example: On an ERP implementation, the project manager coordinates work across finance, IT, vendors, and operations while managing schedule impacts, risks, and stakeholder decisions.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition and Process Groups A Practice Guide — project manager, page 200

Core Meaning: The demonstrated capability of a project manager to apply knowledge, skills, behaviours, and business understanding to achieve project outcomes effectively.
Expansion: Project manager competence refers to more than technical project management knowledge; it includes leadership behaviour, strategic awareness, communication, decision making, and the ability to tailor ways of working to context. PMI's broader view of practitioner capability spans both performance in managing projects and the personal and business acumen needed to lead people and deliver value. The concept matters because project success depends not only on knowing methods, but on applying judgment appropriately across changing situations and stakeholder environments.
Contrast: Project manager role — the role describes assigned responsibilities and authority; competence describes the capability to perform that role effectively.
Example: A project manager leading a cross-border product launch demonstrates competence by tailoring planning, aligning regional stakeholders, resolving team conflict, and translating business goals into executable delivery decisions.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The arrangement of project roles, responsibilities, authority, and reporting relationships used to carry out project work.
Expansion: Project organization describes how people are structured to govern, manage, and execute a project. It clarifies who makes decisions, who performs the work, and how coordination occurs among the sponsor, project manager, team members, and other stakeholders. In PMI usage, it is shaped both by the needs of the project and by the wider organizational structure in which the project operates.
Contrast: Organizational structure — project organization is the project-specific arrangement of roles and authority, while organizational structure is the broader enterprise context that influences how projects are staffed and governed.
Example: A transformation project defines a sponsor, a project manager, business and technical workstream leads, and a steering committee with approval authority for major changes.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The extent to which a project is achieving its intended measures of progress, outcomes, and delivery effectiveness.
Expansion: Project performance is the assessed condition of a project against planned or agreed criteria such as scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk, and value delivery. It is understood through the interpretation of performance data, performance information, and performance reports rather than through raw figures alone. In PMI practice, the concept supports decision making, forecasting, and corrective or preventive action.
Contrast: Work performance data — project performance is the overall interpreted state of the project, while work performance data is the raw observation and measurement used to assess it.
Example: A project team reviews schedule variance, defect trends, budget consumption, and stakeholder acceptance to determine whether overall project performance is improving or deteriorating.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A group of related activities that are critical for the effective delivery of project outcomes.
Expansion: Project Performance Domains are the broad areas of focus used in PMI's principle-based PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition to describe what matters most in delivering project outcomes. Rather than organising guidance primarily around process groups and knowledge areas, the performance-domain model highlights interacting domains such as stakeholders, team, development approach and life cycle, planning, project work, delivery, measurement, and uncertainty. The concept matters because it shifts attention from mechanically following processes to understanding the conditions that drive value delivery and project success. It is especially useful in tailoring, since projects can emphasise different domains depending on context, complexity, and delivery approach.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Project Performance Domains are an organising construct in PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition that groups related capabilities and activities influencing project results. They are not sequential phases or isolated functions, but overlapping domains that interact throughout the project.

Why it matters and where it applies: the model helps practitioners focus on outcomes, adaptability, and system-level thinking instead of treating project management as a checklist. It applies across predictive, adaptive, and hybrid environments and supports tailoring to different industries, teams, and governance settings.

The most common misconception: that the performance domains replace planning discipline or eliminate process thinking. In practice, they broaden the lens, helping teams choose fit-for-purpose practices while still planning, measuring, governing, and controlling work appropriately.

How it shows up in real projects: a project team may tailor governance and delivery by strengthening the Stakeholder, Team, and Uncertainty domains early on, then intensifying Delivery and Measurement as increments are released and benefits evidence emerges.
Contrast: Knowledge areas — knowledge areas in earlier PMBOK editions organise processes by management discipline; Project Performance Domains organise attention around interacting outcome-critical areas of performance.
Example: In a digital-transformation project, the team deliberately tailors its approach by focusing early on stakeholder engagement, team ways of working, delivery cadence, and uncertainty responses instead of building a rigid one-time master plan.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition, Section 2, pages 21-128

Core Meaning: A collection of logically related project activities that culminates in the completion of one or more deliverables.
Expansion: A Project Phase is a structured segment of the project life cycle used to organise work toward a defined interim result. Phases can be sequential, overlapping, or iterative depending on the delivery approach and governance model. They help teams manage control points, funding decisions, reviews, and transitions from one major body of work to the next.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A Project Phase is a meaningful chunk of the project, grouped around related work and one or more significant deliverables. Common examples include concept, design, build, test, and deployment, though the labels vary by industry and life cycle.

Why it matters and where it applies: phases make complex work easier to govern by creating natural review points and decision gates. They are especially useful where sponsors need staged funding, formal approvals, or risk reduction before committing to the next tranche of effort.

The most common misconception: that phases are the same as Process Groups. A phase is a segment of the life cycle, while Process Groups are categories of project management activity that can occur within every phase.

How it shows up in real projects: a construction project may complete design before procurement and build, while a product initiative may move through discovery, MVP release, and scale-up. Each phase ends with a review, deliverable acceptance, or go no-go decision.
Contrast: Project Management Process Group — a phase is a life-cycle segment of work; a Process Group is a type of management activity applied within and across phases.
Example: A pharmaceutical project divides work into feasibility, clinical development, regulatory submission, and launch, with each phase ending in a formal review before the next begins.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), page citation needed from PMI source

Core Meaning: A structured examination of project status, deliverables, or readiness used to support governance decisions and improvement actions.
Expansion: A project review is a formal check on some aspect of project progress, quality, risk, or readiness at a defined point in time. It may be used to confirm continued alignment, approve movement into the next phase, or identify corrective action that is needed. In PMI practice, reviews are an important mechanism for governance, transparency, and organizational learning.
Contrast: Project audit — a project review is typically a management or governance examination of status or readiness, while a project audit is a more formal and often independent assessment of compliance, controls, or performance.
Example: At the end of design, a steering committee conducts a project review to assess scope completeness, unresolved risks, and readiness to authorize build work.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An output of a schedule model that presents linked activities with planned dates, durations, milestones, and resources.
Expansion: The project schedule shows when project activities are planned to occur and how they are sequenced. It is produced from the schedule model and reflects planned dates, durations, milestones, and resource assignments. PMI treats it as a key planning and control artifact for coordinating work over time. It supports execution, monitoring, forecasting, and communication across stakeholders.
Contrast: Schedule model — the schedule model is the analytical representation used to calculate timing; the project schedule is the resulting time-based plan used for management and communication.
Example: For a bridge repair project, the project schedule lays out inspection, design, permitting, material delivery, lane closures, repair work, and reopening milestones.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition and PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Project schedule, page 271

Core Meaning: Project Schedule Management is the knowledge area that includes the processes required to plan, develop, manage, execute, and control the project schedule.
Expansion: Project Schedule Management covers how the project team defines activities, sequences work, estimates durations, develops the schedule, and controls schedule performance over time. In PMI terms, it is not just the timeline itself but the full set of management processes and practices used to produce and maintain a realistic schedule. It is foundational because time performance affects delivery commitments, resource coordination, stakeholder expectations, and often cost and risk outcomes. It applies across predictive, hybrid, and adaptive environments, though the techniques and level of detail differ by approach.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Project Schedule Management is the body of project management work focused on turning scope into a time-based delivery plan and then keeping that plan useful as the project proceeds. It includes defining activities, sequencing them, estimating durations, building the schedule model, and controlling schedule changes and performance.

Why it matters and where it applies: schedules coordinate dependencies, resource timing, milestones, and stakeholder commitments. Without schedule management, teams may still work hard, but they are far less likely to align on when value will be delivered, where delays are emerging, and what corrective action is needed.

The most common misconception: that schedule management is just making a Gantt chart at the start of the project. In practice, PMI treats it as an ongoing management discipline that combines planning, analysis, monitoring, forecasting, and decision-making throughout the project life cycle.

How it shows up in real projects: teams create milestone lists, activity sequences, duration estimates, critical path analysis, sprint or release timing, baseline comparisons, and recovery plans when slippage occurs. Sponsors and stakeholders often experience it through status dates, forecast completion changes, and milestone commitments.
Contrast: Project schedule — Project Schedule Management is the management discipline and set of processes; the project schedule is the resulting time-based model and output.
Example: On a facility-upgrade project, the team sequences permitting, procurement, installation, and testing work, identifies the critical path, tracks milestone slippage weekly, and replans around a delayed equipment delivery.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 6, pp. 173-174

Core Meaning: A graphical representation of the logical relationships among the schedule activities of a project.
Expansion: A Project Schedule Network Diagram shows activities as nodes or arrows and displays the precedence relationships that determine sequencing. It is used to analyse dependencies, identify possible parallel work, and support methods such as critical path analysis. The diagram helps the team understand how schedule logic drives start and finish dates rather than treating the schedule as a simple list of tasks.
Contrast: Gantt chart — a network diagram emphasises logical dependencies among activities; a Gantt chart primarily emphasises timing and duration on a calendar scale.
Example: For a data-centre migration, the team maps server build, security review, data replication, cutover rehearsal, and production switch activities in a network diagram to identify the critical path and where parallel work is possible.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), page citation needed from PMI source

Core Meaning: The work performed to deliver a product, service, or result with the specified features and functions.
Expansion: Project scope defines the total work the project team must perform to create the agreed deliverable. It sets boundaries around what effort is included and excluded from the project. PMI uses the term to distinguish the project work from the characteristics of the product itself. Clear project scope helps control change, support planning, and reduce unnecessary work expansion.
Contrast: Product scope — project scope is the work to create the deliverable; product scope is the deliverable's features and functions.
Example: For an ERP rollout, project scope includes process design, configuration, testing, training, data migration, and deployment support.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition and PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Project scope, page 271

Core Meaning: The description of the project scope, major deliverables, assumptions, and constraints.
Expansion: The project scope statement documents the boundaries of the project and the major deliverables it will produce. It also records key assumptions and constraints that shape planning and execution. PMI uses it to create shared understanding before and during detailed planning. It becomes an important reference for alignment, scope control, and acceptance discussions.
Contrast: Project scope — the project scope statement is the documented description of scope and its conditions; project scope is the underlying body of work itself.
Example: In an office relocation project, the project scope statement identifies the departments moving, the target move date, facility limitations, and assumptions about vendor access.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition and PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Project scope statement, page 271

Core Meaning: Project selection methods are techniques used to evaluate and choose proposed projects or programs based on defined criteria such as value, risk, strategic fit, and feasibility.
Expansion: In PMI usage, project selection methods support decisions about which initiatives an organisation should authorise, fund, or prioritise. They commonly include benefit measurement methods and constrained optimisation methods, although organisations often combine financial, strategic, and qualitative criteria. The concept matters because not every possible project should be pursued, and disciplined selection improves portfolio value and strategic alignment.
Contrast: Proposal evaluation — project selection methods decide which candidate initiatives to pursue overall; proposal evaluation judges submitted vendor or supplier responses against procurement criteria.
Example: A company compares three proposed transformation projects using strategic alignment, expected benefits, risk exposure, and resource capacity before approving only the two that best support its strategy.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Fifth Edition, Section 1.2.6, pp. 30-33

Core Meaning: The project sponsor is the person or group that provides resources and support for the project, program, or portfolio and is accountable for enabling success.
Expansion: The sponsor champions the initiative at an organisational level, helps secure funding and authority, and supports key governance and decision-making needs. PMI treats the sponsor as distinct from the project manager: the sponsor owns high-level business backing and strategic alignment, while the project manager leads day-to-day management of delivery. This role is foundational because projects often fail not only from execution issues but from weak sponsorship, unclear escalation paths, or lack of executive support. The sponsor is especially visible at initiation, major decisions, risk escalation points, and benefit-realisation discussions.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The project sponsor is the senior individual or governing body that legitimises the project and connects it to organisational strategy, funding, and executive support. The sponsor helps establish direction, resolves escalated issues, and ensures the project has the backing needed to proceed.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects need more than a competent project manager; they need authority, alignment, and timely decisions from the business side. Sponsorship is critical in change initiatives, cross-functional work, and any project where priorities, resources, or business outcomes must be defended at leadership level.

The most common misconception: that the sponsor is just a ceremonial approver who signs the charter and disappears. In strong practice, the sponsor remains engaged, removes obstacles, supports governance, and helps maintain alignment between delivery and intended business value.

How it shows up in real projects: the sponsor approves the charter, supports funding, attends governance reviews, resolves escalated stakeholder conflicts, confirms major scope decisions, and advocates for adoption of the project's outcomes.
Contrast: Project manager — the sponsor provides executive ownership, support, and strategic accountability; the project manager leads planning, coordination, and day-to-day delivery management.
Example: In an enterprise ERP rollout, the CFO acts as sponsor by securing funding, resolving conflicts between business units, approving major decisions, and reinforcing adoption expectations with department leaders.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, Glossary, p. 369

Core Meaning: A governance body of senior stakeholders that provides strategic direction, oversight, and decision support for a project.
Expansion: A project steering committee is typically composed of sponsors, executives, and other senior stakeholders who guide the project at a governance level rather than manage day-to-day delivery. It reviews major risks, escalated issues, funding, priorities, and alignment with organisational strategy, and it helps resolve decisions beyond the project manager's authority. In practice it is most common on large, complex, politically sensitive, or high-investment initiatives where executive oversight materially affects success.
Contrast: Project team — the project team delivers the work; the steering committee provides strategic oversight, escalation support, and governance decisions.
Example: For a hospital construction project, the steering committee meets monthly to review milestone status, approve major scope trade-offs, and resolve conflicts between clinical operations and construction timelines.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A set of individuals performing the work of the project to achieve its objectives.
Expansion: The project team consists of the people who perform the project work and contribute directly to achieving the project's objectives. Team members may come from different functions, suppliers, or partner organizations depending on the delivery model. PMI uses the term to emphasize collaborative execution rather than just individual assignment. The composition of the team may change as the project moves through its life cycle.
Contrast: Project manager — the project team performs the work; the project manager leads and coordinates that work.
Example: A digital transformation project team includes a business analyst, developers, testers, change specialists, and a data architect working together on the release.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, project team, page 729

Core Meaning: A concise description of the desired future state or intended outcome that the project seeks to realise.
Expansion: A Project Vision Statement expresses the purpose and aspirational direction of the project in clear, outcome-focused language. It is used to align stakeholders, guide decision-making, and keep delivery focused on the value the project is meant to create. In PMI usage it appears more as a practitioner concept than as a formal core glossary term, especially in agile and product-oriented contexts.
Contrast: Project Objective — a vision statement describes the desired future state and overarching intent; an objective states a specific result to be achieved, often with measurable criteria.
Example: In a citizen-services transformation, the vision statement is to create a single digital entry point where residents can complete the most common services in minutes rather than across multiple offices and forms.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The Project Work Performance Domain addresses activities and functions associated with establishing project processes, managing physical resources, and fostering a learning environment to enable effective project work.
Expansion: In PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, a performance domain is a group of related activities that are critical for delivering project outcomes, and the Project Work Performance Domain focuses on how project work is organised and carried out. It includes process efficiency, resource stewardship, procurement integration, quality of execution, and continuous improvement in the working environment. It is foundational because it connects planning and governance to the actual doing of the work and influences delivery reliability, team effectiveness, and operational discipline. It applies regardless of delivery approach, though its practices appear differently in predictive, hybrid, and adaptive settings.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The Project Work Performance Domain is one of the PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition performance domains and concerns the system through which project work gets performed. It includes the way work is structured, monitored, improved, supplied with resources, and supported through appropriate processes and controls.

Why it matters and where it applies: even with clear goals and stakeholders, projects underperform when execution systems are weak. This domain matters wherever teams must coordinate people, materials, suppliers, methods, and operational practices to produce reliable outputs and maintain flow.

The most common misconception: that this domain is only about task tracking or individual productivity. In PMI terms, it is broader, covering the environment and management approach that allow the work itself to be performed effectively, consistently, and adaptively.

How it shows up in real projects: teams establish ways of working, manage handoffs and procurement inputs, address bottlenecks, improve process flow, monitor execution quality, and adapt operating practices when actual work reveals friction or waste.
Contrast: Measurement performance domain — the Project Work Performance Domain focuses on enabling and managing the doing of project work; the Measurement Performance Domain focuses on assessing performance and supporting decisions with metrics and insight.
Example: During a product-development project, the team redesigns its handoff process between design and testing, improves supplier coordination, and standardises work intake to reduce delays and rework.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, Section 2.4.8, pp. 113-126

Core Meaning: Physical or electronic representations of work performance information compiled in project documents and intended to generate decisions, actions, or awareness.
Expansion: Project work performance reports present analysed project information in a form suited to decision making and communication. They draw on work performance information and may include status reports, memos, explanations, and recommendations. In PMI usage, they are outputs used to inform stakeholders and support monitoring and control.
Contrast: Work performance information — information is analysed performance data; reports package that information into communicated outputs for stakeholders and decisions.
Example: A project manager issues a monthly dashboard showing schedule variance, cost performance, forecast completion dates, major risks, and recommended corrective actions for the steering committee.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, p. 105 and p. 722

Core Meaning: An organizational structure in which the project manager has full authority over the project.
Expansion: In a projectized organization, resources are assigned primarily to projects and report to the project manager. This structure supports fast decision-making, clear accountability, and strong focus on project objectives. It is common where project delivery is the main way the organization creates value. Functional specialties may still exist, but project authority remains central.
Contrast: Matrix organization — a projectized organization gives the project manager primary authority; a matrix organization shares authority between project and functional managers.
Example: A consulting firm assigns analysts, designers, and developers directly to a client implementation under one project manager with full delivery authority.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, projectized organization, page 715

Core Meaning: Proposal evaluation is the assessment of seller or supplier responses against defined criteria to support source selection in procurement.
Expansion: In PMI practice, proposal evaluation is used during procurement to compare submitted bids or proposals in a structured and defensible way. Evaluation criteria may include technical capability, past performance, price, schedule, risk, compliance, and overall value depending on the acquisition strategy. The concept matters because sound evaluation improves supplier selection quality and reduces bias, dispute risk, and downstream delivery problems.
Contrast: Bidder conference — proposal evaluation compares and scores submitted responses after receipt; a bidder conference is a pre-submission event used to clarify procurement requirements for potential sellers.
Example: A public-sector project team scores vendor proposals for a data-platform implementation using weighted criteria for solution fit, implementation approach, security compliance, support model, and delivery risk before awarding the contract.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A working model used to obtain early feedback on the expected product before actually building it.
Expansion: A prototype is an early working model created to explore requirements, test assumptions, and gather feedback before full development. It helps teams reduce uncertainty by making ideas tangible and reviewable. PMI uses prototyping as a practical way to clarify stakeholder expectations and improve requirement quality. Prototypes may be discarded after learning or evolved into later solution versions depending on the approach.
Contrast: Mock-up — a prototype is a working model used to test and learn; a mock-up is usually a static representation of appearance or layout.
Example: A team creates a clickable prototype of a customer self-service portal so users can react to workflow and navigation before development begins.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, prototype, page 726

Core Meaning: A technique for creating a preliminary model of a product or solution so stakeholders can explore requirements and provide feedback.
Expansion: PMI identifies prototyping as an elicitation technique used to make ideas tangible and testable early. Instead of relying only on verbal descriptions or written requirements, teams present a simplified representation that stakeholders can react to directly. This is especially useful when requirements are unclear, user experience matters, or learning through feedback reduces risk.
Contrast: Mock-up — a prototype is used to explore and learn about requirements or behaviour; a mock-up is usually a static representation focused mainly on appearance.
Example: A project team creates a clickable prototype of a customer self-service portal so users can validate navigation and screen flow before full development starts.
Source: The PMI Guide to Business Analysis, Section 6.3.2.6 Prototyping

Core Meaning: A communication method used to provide large volumes of information to a large audience that accesses it at their own discretion.
Expansion: Pull communication makes information available for recipients to retrieve when needed. It is useful when the audience is large, the content is extensive, or different stakeholders need different levels of detail. Common examples include dashboards, repositories, portals, and intranet sites. It supports self-service access and reduces unnecessary message traffic.
Contrast: Push communication — pull communication is accessed on demand by the recipient; push communication is sent directly to recipients.
Example: A project team posts status reports, decision logs, and schedule updates on a shared portal for stakeholders to review when needed.
Source: PMIstandards+ Navigator, pull communication, page 377

Core Meaning: A communication method used to distribute information directly to specific recipients who need to receive it.
Expansion: Push communication sends information to intended recipients rather than requiring them to retrieve it. It is effective when messages are targeted and timely delivery matters, such as approvals, escalations, or status updates. Common examples include email, memos, voice mail, and direct messaging. It helps ensure key stakeholders receive information that is relevant to their role.
Contrast: Pull communication — push communication is sent directly to recipients; pull communication is made available for recipients to access on demand.
Example: A project manager emails a risk escalation and revised milestone date directly to the sponsor, steering committee, and affected workstream leads.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, push communication, page 727

Core Meaning: The standards used to determine whether a seller, resource, or candidate is capable of performing the required work.
Expansion: Qualification criteria define the minimum conditions that must be met before a party is considered suitable for selection. They are commonly used in procurement and staffing decisions to screen for capability, experience, certifications, or other required attributes. In PMI usage, they act as a filter that helps ensure only qualified options proceed to evaluation or award.
Contrast: Source selection criteria — qualification criteria determine whether a candidate is eligible to be considered; source selection criteria compare qualified candidates for final selection.
Example: A government project requires bidders to have completed three similar infrastructure projects before their proposals can move forward for evaluation.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Qualification criteria

Core Meaning: A procurement source selection method in which the buyer chooses the seller based on competence, experience, and qualifications rather than price as the primary deciding factor.
Expansion: This method is used when the work is specialised and the quality of expertise matters more than competing on price at the outset. The buyer evaluates suppliers on capability, relevant experience, and professional standing, then typically negotiates scope and price with the highest-ranked firm. It is common in consulting, design, and other knowledge-intensive procurements where weak capability creates outsized project risk.
Contrast: Quality-based selection method — qualifications-only selection emphasises supplier capability first and may negotiate commercial terms afterward, while quality-based selection focuses on the quality of the technical proposal for a defined need.
Example: A public-sector project selects an engineering advisory firm based on specialist bridge-renewal experience and credentialed staff before entering fee negotiations.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge — Qualifications-only selection method, p. 249

Core Meaning: Estimating techniques that rely on expert judgement, relative comparison, or descriptive assessment rather than detailed quantitative calculation.
Expansion: These techniques are useful when information is limited, speed matters, or early planning does not support precise numeric analysis. They often draw on analogy, experience, pattern recognition, and comparative sizing to produce estimates that are directionally useful. PMI treats them as practical tools for forming reasonable expectations when the project team lacks enough detail for more rigorous estimating.
Contrast: Quantitative estimating techniques — qualitative estimating techniques rely more on judgement and relative assessment, while quantitative techniques depend more on numerical models, detailed data, or statistical calculation.
Example: Early in a digital-transformation project, the team judges that a customer-portal feature is roughly twice the effort of a previously delivered feature and uses that comparison to size the work.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge — Qualitative estimating techniques, p. 170

Core Meaning: The process of prioritizing individual project risks for further analysis or action by assessing their probability of occurrence and impact.
Expansion: Qualitative risk analysis ranks risks using relative scales rather than detailed numerical modeling. It helps the team focus attention on the risks most likely to affect project objectives. PMI uses it as a practical early step in risk management to guide response planning and determine which risks need deeper quantitative analysis.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Qualitative risk analysis evaluates identified risks by comparing their probability and impact, often with supporting factors such as urgency or detectability. The result is a prioritized view of which risks deserve the most attention.

Why it matters and where it applies: it helps teams use limited time and resources on the risks that matter most. It is widely used in all project types because it is fast, scalable, and useful even when detailed data is limited.

The most common misconception: that it is less important because it is not numerical. In PMI practice, qualitative analysis is often the key filter that determines which risks move on to quantitative analysis or immediate response planning.

How it shows up in real projects: teams use probability-impact matrices, risk ranking, and expert judgment to sort risks into high, medium, and low priority categories before assigning owners and responses.
Contrast: Quantitative risk analysis — qualitative risk analysis prioritizes risks using relative assessment; quantitative risk analysis numerically models overall uncertainty and outcomes.
Example: A project team ranks supplier delay, design rework, and permit approval as high-priority risks because they combine high impact with moderate to high probability.
Source: The Standard for Risk Management in Portfolios, Programs, and Projects, qualitative risk analysis, pages 28-29 and 33-34

Core Meaning: The degree to which a set of inherent characteristics fulfills requirements.
Expansion: Quality in PMI terms is not simply excellence or luxury; it is conformance to requirements and fitness for intended use. It applies to both project deliverables and the processes used to create them, linking customer expectations, prevention, assurance, and control. Effective quality management reduces defects, rework, and dissatisfaction while increasing the likelihood that outcomes will be accepted and perform as intended.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Quality is the extent to which deliverables and processes meet defined requirements and are suitable for their intended purpose. It is managed through planning quality standards, building quality into the work, and checking results against those standards.

Why it matters and where it applies: Quality affects customer satisfaction, operational performance, compliance, safety, and total cost of delivery. It applies across all industries, from software and product design to construction, healthcare, and service transformation.

The most common misconception: that quality means adding premium features or inspecting defects out at the end. In practice, PMI treats quality as requirements-based and emphasises prevention, process capability, and continuous improvement over late detection alone.

How it shows up in real projects: teams define acceptance criteria, set quality metrics, conduct reviews and testing, analyse defects and root causes, and adjust processes to improve consistency and reduce rework.
Contrast: Grade — quality is how well requirements are met; grade is a category or rank assigned to deliverables with different technical characteristics.
Example: In a water-treatment plant project, quality means the installed system consistently meets regulatory purity thresholds and documented performance tolerances, not that it contains the most advanced optional equipment.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Quality; PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 8.1, p. 277

Core Meaning: A planned and systematic set of activities used to provide confidence that the project will satisfy relevant quality requirements.
Expansion: Quality assurance focuses on whether the processes used to create deliverables are appropriate, followed, and effective. It is preventive rather than detective, aiming to reduce defects by improving how work is performed. PMI distinguishes it from inspection-oriented activities by emphasizing process evaluation, audits, and continuous improvement.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Quality assurance is the process-focused side of quality management. It examines whether the methods, standards, and procedures used by the team are capable of producing outputs that meet requirements.

Why it matters and where it applies: strong quality assurance reduces rework, increases predictability, and improves stakeholder confidence. It is especially important in regulated, high-risk, or repeatable delivery environments such as construction, engineering, healthcare, and software development.

The most common misconception: that quality assurance means testing the final deliverable. Testing and inspection are usually part of quality control, while quality assurance asks whether the work system itself is sound and being followed.

How it shows up in real projects: process audits, peer reviews of methods, compliance checks, lessons-learned driven process improvements, and verification that teams are using approved standards and procedures.
Contrast: Quality control — quality assurance focuses on improving and confirming the process used to create deliverables; quality control focuses on inspecting the deliverables themselves.
Example: On a medical device project, an internal audit checks whether design reviews, traceability, and verification steps are being performed according to the approved development process before release testing is completed.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, section 8.2 Manage Quality, p. 288

Core Meaning: A structured, independent review used to determine whether project activities comply with organizational and project policies, processes, and procedures.
Expansion: A quality audit examines how work is being performed, not just whether the final output meets requirements. It identifies good practices, gaps, inefficiencies, and opportunities for improvement in the quality management system. In PMI usage, it sits within quality assurance because it focuses on process compliance and process improvement rather than product inspection.
Contrast: Inspection — a quality audit reviews the process and compliance system; inspection checks the deliverable itself.
Example: On a pharmaceutical project, an internal auditor reviews whether design reviews, document control, and validation steps were performed according to the approved quality procedures.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, section 8.2.2.5, page 291

Core Meaning: The process of monitoring and recording results of executing the quality activities to assess performance and recommend necessary changes.
Expansion: Quality control focuses on the deliverables and work results to determine whether they meet quality requirements. It is primarily detective, using inspection, testing, and measurement to identify defects or nonconformance. PMI treats it as part of monitoring and controlling because it provides evidence for corrective action and process improvement.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Quality control is the product-focused side of quality management. It checks outputs against requirements and uses tools such as inspections, tests, and measurements to determine conformance.

Why it matters and where it applies: it helps teams catch defects before delivery, reduce rework, and protect customer satisfaction. It is used in nearly every project type, especially where deliverable quality can be verified through testing or inspection.

The most common misconception: that quality control is the same as quality assurance. Quality assurance is process-focused and preventive, while quality control is output-focused and detective.

How it shows up in real projects: test execution, inspection of completed work, defect logging, acceptance sampling, and review of quality control measurements that trigger corrective action when results fall outside expectations.
Contrast: Quality assurance — quality control checks deliverables and results; quality assurance evaluates and improves the process used to create them.
Example: A construction team inspects welded joints and performs load tests to confirm the finished structure meets specification before handover.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, section 8.3 Control Quality, p. 298

Core Meaning: The documented results of quality control activities used to assess performance and support decisions.
Expansion: Quality control measurements are the recorded outputs of inspections, tests, and other control activities. They provide evidence about whether deliverables or processes meet quality requirements and help identify trends, defects, or nonconformance. PMI uses them as inputs to quality reports and corrective action decisions.
Contrast: Quality metrics — quality metrics define what will be measured; quality control measurements are the actual recorded results of those measurements.
Example: A software team records defect counts, test pass rates, and response times as quality control measurements during system testing.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — quality control measurements, page 300

Core Meaning: The processes and activities used to translate quality policy into planning, assurance, control, and continuous improvement so project results meet relevant requirements and are fit for use.
Expansion: Quality management in PMI is broader than testing or defect detection. It includes defining the quality standards that matter, building them into project work, checking results against those standards, and improving the system that produces those results. Its purpose is not only conformance, but also ensuring that deliverables satisfy stakeholder needs and support intended benefits.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Quality management is the integrated approach for planning, managing, and controlling quality across the project life cycle. It connects standards, acceptance criteria, process discipline, verification activities, and improvement actions so that quality is designed into the work rather than inspected in at the end.

Why it matters and where it applies: poor quality drives rework, delays, cost growth, stakeholder dissatisfaction, and operational failure after handoff. It applies in predictive, agile, and hybrid environments alike, whether the deliverable is software, construction work, a service rollout, or an organisational change initiative.

The most common misconception: that quality management is mainly about final inspection or a specialist quality team. In practice, it starts during planning, belongs to the whole team, and is strongest when requirements, processes, and acceptance criteria are clear from the beginning.

How it shows up in real projects: teams define acceptance criteria, set quality metrics, perform reviews and testing, analyse defects and process variation, and use lessons learned to reduce recurring problems across iterations or phases.
Contrast: Quality control — quality management is the full system of planning, assurance, control, and improvement, while quality control focuses on monitoring and verifying results against requirements.
Example: In a hospital-system implementation, the project team defines data-accuracy thresholds, review procedures, test scripts, and defect-resolution workflows to ensure the new patient-record process is reliable before go-live.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge — Quality management, pp. 22-23

Core Meaning: A component of the project management plan that describes how applicable policies, procedures, and guidelines will be implemented to achieve the quality objectives.
Expansion: The quality management plan explains how the project will plan, manage, and verify quality across its work and deliverables. It sets expectations for standards, metrics, roles, review activities, and acceptance approaches. In practice, it provides the operating rules for how the team will meet quality requirements throughout the project lifecycle.
Contrast: Quality assurance — the quality management plan defines how quality will be managed; quality assurance is one category of activity performed under that plan.
Example: A bridge project documents inspection hold points, material testing criteria, audit responsibilities, and defect escalation rules in its quality management plan before construction begins.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, section 8.1.3.1 Quality Management Plan, p. 282

Core Meaning: Project or product attributes and how they will be measured to verify compliance with quality requirements.
Expansion: Quality metrics define what aspect of quality will be measured and how the measurement will be taken. They make quality expectations observable and support consistent verification during quality assurance and quality control activities. Examples include defect rates, test coverage, customer satisfaction scores, and other measurable attributes tied to requirements.
Contrast: Quality requirements — quality metrics define how quality will be measured; quality requirements define what quality characteristics must be achieved.
Example: A software project tracks percentage of requirements covered by tests and number of defects found per day as quality metrics.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, section 8.1.3.2 Quality Metrics, p. 282

Core Meaning: Specific, measurable outcomes that define the required level of quality for project deliverables and processes.
Expansion: Quality objectives translate stakeholder needs, requirements, and acceptance expectations into concrete targets that the project can plan, manage, and verify. They help the team decide what quality means in practical terms, such as defect thresholds, performance levels, compliance criteria, or service levels. In PMI practice, they are used to guide quality planning, quality assurance, and quality control so that fitness for use and conformance to requirements can be assessed consistently.
Contrast: Quality requirements — quality objectives state the target outcomes to be achieved, while quality requirements specify the detailed criteria or conditions that deliverables must meet.
Example: In a hospital-system implementation project, the team sets quality objectives that patient-record retrieval must complete within two seconds, critical defects must be zero at go-live, and user training satisfaction must exceed 90%.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Seventh Edition / Process Groups: A Practice Guide — not directly listed as a formal PMI glossary term; aligns with project quality planning concepts

Core Meaning: The overall intentions and direction of an organization with regard to quality, as formally expressed by top management.
Expansion: A quality policy states the organization-wide commitment to quality and provides the framework for quality objectives. It guides how projects define and manage quality expectations, standards, and responsibilities. In PMI usage, it is an organizational input that shapes project quality planning and execution.
Contrast: Quality management plan — the quality policy states the organization-wide direction on quality; the quality management plan explains how a specific project will implement it.
Example: A construction firm has a quality policy requiring approved materials and documented inspections, and the project team builds those controls into execution plans.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Quality policy

Core Meaning: A project document that records quality management issues, quality control measurements, and recommendations for corrective action.
Expansion: A quality report summarises the results of quality activities and highlights trends, exceptions, and actions needed. It helps stakeholders understand whether quality objectives are being met and where corrective or preventive action may be required. In PMI usage, it is a communication and control artifact within project quality management.
Contrast: Quality control measurements — quality control measurements are the raw data; a quality report interprets and communicates that data.
Example: A software project issues a weekly quality report showing defect density, test pass rates, and open critical defects along with recommended fixes.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, section 8.3.3.2, page 300

Core Meaning: The documented conditions or criteria that a product, service, or result must satisfy to meet stakeholder expectations and project objectives.
Expansion: Quality requirements specify the standards or characteristics that the deliverable must meet. They are used to guide planning, testing, inspection, and acceptance decisions. In PMI practice, they are distinct from quality metrics, which describe how those requirements will be measured.
Contrast: Quality metrics — quality requirements state what must be achieved; quality metrics state how it will be measured.
Example: A medical device project defines sterility, reliability, and traceability requirements that the final product must satisfy before release.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, section 8.1.3.3 Quality Requirements, p. 282

Core Meaning: A procurement source selection method in which the buyer evaluates sellers using both the quality of the proposal and the offered cost.
Expansion: This method is used when the project needs a balanced decision that reflects technical merit as well as commercial value. The buyer scores proposals using weighted criteria so that neither capability nor price is ignored. It supports more nuanced trade-off decisions than choosing only the lowest bid or only the strongest technical proposal. It is especially useful when acceptable performance can be achieved by more than one supplier approach.
Contrast: Quality-based selection method — quality-and-cost-based selection balances technical quality and price, while quality-based selection gives primary importance to technical quality.
Example: A system-implementation project compares vendors using weighted scores for solution fit, delivery approach, support capability, and total evaluated cost before contract award.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge — Quality-and-cost-based selection method, p. 249

Core Meaning: A procurement source selection method in which the buyer chooses the seller primarily on the quality of the proposed technical solution and capability rather than on price alone.
Expansion: This method is used when technical excellence, specialist expertise, or solution quality has greater importance than selecting the lowest-cost offer. The buyer evaluates proposals mainly on competence, methodology, and likely performance against project needs. Price may still be considered, but it is not the principal driver of the decision. It is common where poor technical choices would create significant delivery, safety, or performance risk.
Contrast: Quality-and-cost-based selection method — quality-based selection gives primary weight to technical quality, while quality-and-cost-based selection balances quality with price in the final evaluation.
Example: A transport authority selects a tunnel-design consultant based mainly on engineering approach, safety record, and specialist experience, with commercial terms considered only after technical ranking.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge — Quality-based selection method, p. 249

Core Meaning: The process of numerically analyzing the combined effect of identified individual project risks and other sources of uncertainty on overall project objectives.
Expansion: Quantitative risk analysis uses numeric techniques to estimate the impact of uncertainty on objectives such as schedule, cost, and performance. It goes beyond relative ranking by modeling probabilities, ranges, and likely outcomes. PMI treats it as an optional but powerful analysis process when a project needs deeper understanding of overall exposure or decision consequences.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Quantitative risk analysis converts uncertainty into numerical insight. It commonly uses techniques such as simulations, decision tree analysis, and sensitivity analysis to estimate possible outcomes and their probabilities.

Why it matters and where it applies: it helps leaders make better commitments, set realistic reserves, and understand the likelihood of meeting key targets. It is most useful on large, complex, high-uncertainty, or high-consequence projects where rough prioritization is not enough.

The most common misconception: that it is simply assigning numbers to risks. In PMI practice, the point is not just scoring risks but analyzing how multiple uncertainties interact to affect whole-project outcomes.

How it shows up in real projects: teams run Monte Carlo simulations on schedules, model cost contingencies, compare response options quantitatively, and present confidence levels such as the probability of finishing by a target date or within a target budget.
Contrast: Qualitative risk analysis — quantitative risk analysis numerically models overall uncertainty and probable outcomes; qualitative risk analysis prioritizes risks using relative scales such as probability and impact.
Example: A program team models schedule uncertainty across interdependent work packages and finds only a 45 percent chance of completing by the contractual milestone, leading to added contingency and resequencing.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, section 11.4 Perform Quantitative Risk Analysis, p. 428

Core Meaning: A data-gathering technique that uses a structured set of written questions to collect information from respondents.
Expansion: In projects, questionnaires are used to gather consistent input from stakeholders, users, or subject matter experts at scale. They are especially useful when broad participation is needed and direct interviews would be impractical.
Contrast: Interviews — questionnaires collect standardised written responses from many people efficiently, while interviews allow deeper probing and clarification with individuals or small groups.
Example: A project team distributes a questionnaire to regional office managers to capture current process pain points before designing a new workflow system.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge — Questionnaires, p. 192

Core Meaning: A responsibility assignment matrix that maps project work to roles using Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, and Informed designations.
Expansion: A RACI chart is a structured way to clarify who does the work, who owns the result, who provides input, and who must be kept aware for a task, deliverable, or decision. It is widely used in project planning and governance to reduce ambiguity in roles and handoffs across teams. In PMI terms, it is a common form of responsibility assignment matrix used to define roles and responsibilities at an actionable level. It is especially useful where work crosses functions, vendors, or governance bodies.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A RACI chart is a matrix that links activities, deliverables, or decisions to stakeholder roles so ownership is visible and explicit. Each row typically represents work, and each column represents a role or person.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects often fail through unclear ownership rather than lack of effort. A RACI chart is especially useful in cross-functional delivery, governance-heavy environments, system implementations, and transitions where many parties must coordinate.

The most common misconception: that every task needs many Responsible or Accountable parties. In practice, the chart works best when accountability is clear, usually with one accountable role per item and only the involvement needed for effective execution.

How it shows up in real projects: teams use it in planning workshops, governance packs, onboarding, and decision mapping to prevent duplicated effort, missed approvals, and stakeholder confusion.
Contrast: Responsibility assignment matrix — a RACI chart is a specific type of responsibility assignment matrix using the R, A, C, and I pattern.
Example: In an ERP deployment, the data lead is Responsible for data migration, the project sponsor is Accountable for go-live approval, cybersecurity is Consulted on controls, and regional operations managers are Informed of cutover timing.

Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The state of being adequately prepared to proceed to the next stage or to go live.
Expansion: Readiness is assessed against defined criteria covering people, processes, systems, and support before a transition. Confirming readiness reduces the chance of a failed launch or hand-over.
Contrast: Completion — completion means the work is built; readiness means the organization is prepared to use and support it.
Example: An operational readiness review confirms staff are trained and support is in place before go-live.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; defined for glossary completeness

Core Meaning: An assessment performed as the organization approaches solution deployment to determine how prepared it is to integrate and sustain the solution.
Expansion: A readiness assessment checks whether the organization is prepared for transition, adoption, and ongoing support of the solution. It looks at people, processes, training, support, and operational capability before deployment. PMI uses it to reduce transition risk and confirm that the organization can absorb the change.
Contrast: Acceptance testing — readiness assessment evaluates organizational preparedness for deployment; acceptance testing evaluates whether the solution meets requirements.
Example: Before launching a new service desk platform, the team confirms that support staff are trained, procedures are updated, and escalation paths are in place.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The systematic control, storage, retention, retrieval, and disposition of project and organisational records.
Expansion: Records management is the disciplined handling of records so that project information remains accurate, accessible, protected, and auditable over time. In project settings it supports compliance, traceability, knowledge retention, and defensible decision histories. It commonly covers items such as approvals, contracts, change records, correspondence, and lessons learned.
Contrast: Configuration management — records management governs retention and control of records broadly, while configuration management focuses on the integrity and control of deliverable and product-related baselines and changes.
Example: On an infrastructure project, signed permits, inspection logs, approved change orders, and vendor correspondence are retained under a controlled filing and retention process to satisfy audit and legal obligations.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Ongoing, repetitive operational activity that continues in a repeated pattern rather than being a temporary project effort.
Expansion: Recurring work refers to repeatable activities performed as part of operations, services, or business-as-usual delivery instead of a temporary initiative with a defined end state. In PMI usage, it is important because projects are temporary and unique, while recurring work is sustained and repeatable. The distinction helps organizations choose the right management approach, governance model, staffing pattern, and performance measures.
Contrast: Project work — recurring work is repetitive and ongoing, while project work is temporary and creates a unique product, service, or result.
Example: A PMO tracks the monthly production of routine compliance reports as recurring work, while the one-time rollout of a new reporting platform is managed as a project.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The disciplined improvement of a product's internal structure without changing its external behavior.
Expansion: Refactoring is commonly used in agile and software development environments to improve maintainability, readability, flexibility, or technical quality while preserving the functionality experienced by users.
