It reduces technical debt and makes future changes easier, safer, and faster. Within PMI-aligned agile practice, refactoring supports sustainable delivery by helping teams keep the evolving solution healthy over time.
Contrast: Defect repair — refactoring improves internal design without changing external behavior, while defect repair corrects behavior that is wrong or failing.
Example: During a product increment, a development team reorganizes duplicated code modules and simplifies interfaces so future features can be added faster without altering what users see in the application.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A document used to capture and maintain a defined set of project information that requires ongoing tracking and updating.
Expansion: In PMI usage, a register is a structured project record for a specific category of information, such as stakeholders, risks, or assumptions. It is maintained over time rather than issued once. Its value is that it provides a current reference for ownership, status, and follow-up as the project evolves.
Contrast: Report — a register is a living tracking record for a defined class of items; a report communicates status, analysis, or results to an audience.
Example: A project team updates its stakeholder register whenever new stakeholders are identified or existing stakeholders change in influence or interest.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, Project artifacts section, p. 153

Core Meaning: An analytical method in which input variables are examined in relation to their corresponding output variable.
Expansion: Regression analysis is used to understand the relationship between one dependent variable and one or more independent variables. In project management, it can support estimating, forecasting, and trend analysis by showing how changes in inputs relate to outcomes. PMI includes it as a quantitative analytical method for examining patterns in project data.
Contrast: Correlation analysis — regression analysis examines how input variables relate to an output variable; correlation analysis measures the strength of association between variables without modeling prediction.
Example: A project team analyzes whether defect counts are related to code size, test coverage, and developer experience to improve future estimates.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, regression analysis

Core Meaning: A rule or requirement issued by an authority that governs conduct or compliance in a given context.
Expansion: Regulation affects projects by setting mandatory requirements that may influence scope, schedule, cost, procurement, and acceptance criteria. Projects must identify applicable regulations early so compliance obligations are built into planning and execution. In PMI practice, regulation is part of the external environment that can constrain or shape project work.
Contrast: Policy — regulation is an externally imposed rule with legal or authoritative force; policy is an internal directive or guideline.
Example: A healthcare project designs its data handling procedures to comply with privacy regulations before system deployment.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A mandated condition, constraint, or obligation imposed by an external authority that the project or its deliverables must satisfy.
Expansion: A regulatory requirement is an externally imposed requirement arising from laws, regulations, codes, or governing bodies. It shapes project scope, quality criteria, approvals, and acceptance conditions because noncompliance can block delivery or create legal and financial exposure. In PMI practice it is typically handled as part of requirements, constraints, compliance planning, and risk management.
Contrast: Business requirement — a business requirement expresses organisational need or desired outcome, while a regulatory requirement is mandatory because of external compliance obligations.
Example: A healthcare software project includes data privacy controls and audit logging because the release must satisfy applicable patient-information regulations before deployment.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The intentional development and maintenance of productive interactions with stakeholders, teams, and other parties that affect project outcomes.
Expansion: Relationship management is the practice of building trust, credibility, and effective working connections among the people and groups involved in or affected by a project. PMI treats these interpersonal capabilities as central to leadership, stakeholder engagement, collaboration, and influence across formal authority boundaries. It matters because projects succeed not only through plans and controls but through the quality of cooperation that enables decisions, alignment, and sustained commitment.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Relationship management is the active management of human connections that shape project performance. It includes communication style, trust building, conflict navigation, influence, empathy, and ongoing stakeholder awareness.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects depend on coordinated action across sponsors, customers, team members, vendors, and functional managers. Strong relationships improve issue resolution, accelerate decisions, reduce resistance, and help the project manager work effectively even when authority is limited.

The most common misconception: that relationship management is mainly being personable or maintaining harmony. In practice it is a disciplined leadership activity that supports candour, accountability, negotiation, and constructive escalation when interests conflict.

How it shows up in real projects: the project manager tailors communication to stakeholder needs, repairs trust after missed commitments, aligns cross-functional leaders around trade-offs, and creates enough psychological safety for risks and bad news to surface early.
Contrast: Stakeholder engagement — stakeholder engagement focuses on gaining and sustaining stakeholder support for project objectives, while relationship management is the broader capability of building effective working relationships across those interactions.
Example: A project manager delivering an ERP rollout meets regularly with finance, operations, and IT leaders to resolve tensions over process changes and maintain sponsorship through a difficult cutover period.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An estimating approach that sizes work by comparison with other work items rather than by assigning absolute units directly.
Expansion: Relative estimating compares one item with another to judge size, effort, or complexity. It is especially useful when exact detail is limited or when a team wants faster, more consistent sizing across a backlog or work set. PMI commonly associates it with agile ways of working, where the focus is on comparative understanding more than precision early on.
Contrast: Analogous estimating — relative estimating compares work items to each other for size; analogous estimating predicts an effort or duration using data from a previous similar activity or project.
Example: A delivery team sizes new backlog items against a reference story and assigns story points based on comparative complexity.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, Estimate Size section, p. 104

Core Meaning: A sizing technique that estimates work by comparing items to one another rather than predicting absolute duration or effort directly.
Expansion: Relative estimation is used to assess the comparative size, complexity, or effort of backlog items by placing them against reference items or agreed scales such as story points. It is especially common in agile environments where uncertainty makes precise early estimates unreliable. By focusing on comparison rather than exact prediction, teams can estimate faster, reduce false precision, and improve planning consistency over time.
Contrast: Bottom-up estimating — relative estimation compares work items against each other, while bottom-up estimating aggregates detailed estimates of individual components into a total.
Example: A Scrum team judges one user story as roughly twice as complex as a previously completed story and assigns it 8 points instead of estimating exact hours upfront.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A deployable version of a product, service, or result that is made available to users or customers.
Expansion: A release is the point at which completed work is packaged and made available for use, deployment, or transition. In PMI contexts, it often refers to a planned delivery of features or capability rather than every internal increment the team completes. Release decisions consider readiness, quality, stakeholder expectations, and operational timing. The concept is especially common in agile, hybrid, and product delivery environments.
Contrast: Increment — an increment is completed usable work; a release is the act or event of making that work available to users or customers.
Example: A software team completes several tested features across three iterations and then combines them into a quarterly release for customer deployment.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A high-level plan for delivering a product, deliverable, or increment into use.
Expansion: A release plan identifies what will be released, when it will be released, and the major conditions that must be met. It helps coordinate development, testing, readiness, and stakeholder communication. PMI uses it to align delivery timing with business and operational needs.
Contrast: Roadmap — a release plan is more detailed and delivery-focused; a roadmap is a higher-level strategic timeline of major milestones and decision points.
Example: A software team plans a beta release in June, a pilot release in August, and full production release after security sign-off.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — release plan

Core Meaning: The activity of determining what functionality or scope will be delivered in a release and when that release is intended to occur.
Expansion: Release planning is commonly used in agile and hybrid delivery to map backlog items or features into a target release. It connects product priorities, capacity, dependencies, and timing so teams can communicate what is likely to be delivered together. PMI discusses release planning in agile practice, but it is generally used as a practitioner term rather than a formal PMI glossary entry.
Contrast: Iteration planning — release planning looks across multiple iterations toward a deployable release; iteration planning details the work for the next short cycle.
Example: A digital-banking team groups authentication improvements, account alerts, and profile updates into a quarter-end mobile-app release based on team capacity and regulatory deadlines.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The degree to which a system, product, service, or component performs its intended function consistently under stated conditions for a specified period of time.
Expansion: Reliability concerns dependable performance over time, not just initial correctness at the moment of delivery. In project management, it often appears as a quality, engineering, product, or operational requirement that influences design decisions, testing strategy, risk response, and acceptance criteria. It is especially important in projects involving infrastructure, software, manufacturing, healthcare, transportation, defense, and any environment where failure has material consequences.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Reliability is the likelihood that an item will continue to perform as intended over a defined period and under defined conditions. It focuses on stability and consistency in use, not merely whether the item works once.

Why it matters and where it applies: reliability drives stakeholder confidence, lifecycle cost, safety, and service continuity. It is central in projects that produce assets or systems expected to operate predictably after handoff, especially where downtime, failure, or maintenance burden create major business impact.

The most common misconception: that reliability is the same as quality in general or the same as availability. Reliability is a specific performance attribute over time; a product may be high quality in many respects yet still fail too often, and a system may be available through redundancy without each component being highly reliable.

How it shows up in real projects: teams translate reliability needs into requirements, test regimes, design tolerances, maintenance assumptions, risk analyses, and vendor criteria. It appears in measures such as failure rates, mean time between failures, warranty expectations, and service-level commitments.
Contrast: Availability — reliability concerns consistent failure-free performance over time, while availability concerns whether the system is up and usable when needed.
Example: In a rail-signaling modernization project, the sponsor requires the installed control system to maintain consistent operation with extremely low failure rates, so the team designs redundant components, runs endurance testing, and sets acceptance thresholds tied to operational performance.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Seventh Edition — reliability is treated as a product and quality characteristic; confirm exact glossary citation in PMI Lexicon

Core Meaning: Work performed in a recurring, similar pattern rather than as a one-off effort.
Expansion: Repetitive work is characterised by repeated execution of similar tasks under comparable conditions. In PMI usage, it is commonly contrasted with project work, which is temporary and unique.
Contrast: Project work — repetitive work recurs in a similar form, while project work creates a unique product, service, or result.
Example: A facilities team performs the same monthly preventive-maintenance checklist across all office locations.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A communication artifact that presents project information in a form suited to stakeholder awareness, analysis, or decision making.
Expansion: A report conveys project information for a purpose such as status visibility, performance review, risk awareness, or forecasting. It is audience-oriented and may be produced in narrative, visual, dashboard, or summary form. In PMI terms, reports support transparency and informed decision making rather than acting as the primary record of tracked items.
Contrast: Register — a report communicates information to an audience; a register stores and maintains a tracked set of project entries over time.
Example: The project manager sends a monthly performance report showing milestone progress, cost trends, major risks, and forecast completion dates.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, Project artifacts section, p. 153

Core Meaning: A procurement document used to solicit price proposals from sellers for a clearly defined product or service.
Expansion: A request for bid is used when the buyer can specify the requirement clearly and wants sellers to compete primarily on price. It is typically associated with well-defined scope and standardized deliverables. PMI treats it as a formal procurement solicitation document.
Contrast: Request for proposal — a request for bid focuses on price for a clearly defined need; a request for proposal is used when solution approach and seller expertise matter more.
Example: A city issues a request for bid for road resurfacing work with exact specifications and completion dates.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, request for bid

Core Meaning: A procurement document used to collect information from potential sellers about available products, services, or capabilities.
Expansion: A request for information is typically used early in procurement to understand the market before issuing a more formal solicitation. It helps the project team clarify what suppliers can provide, how solutions differ, and what requirements are realistic. In PMI practice, it supports market research and better-informed procurement planning rather than serving as a binding request for bids.
Contrast: Request for Proposal — a request for information gathers market knowledge, while a request for proposal asks sellers to submit a proposed solution for evaluation.
Example: A hospital project issues an RFI to several scheduling-system vendors to understand integration options before preparing detailed procurement documents.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms / A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition — procurement terminology, exact page citation not confirmed

Core Meaning: A document used to solicit proposals from prospective sellers for products, services, or results.
Expansion: A request for proposal is used when the buyer wants sellers to explain how they would meet a need, not just what they would charge. It is common when scope is clear enough to compete the work but solution approach, capability, and experience still matter. PMI treats it as a procurement document that supports structured evaluation of competing proposals.
Contrast: Request for quotation — a request for proposal seeks a proposed solution and approach; a request for quotation mainly seeks price for a clearly specified need.
Example: A public agency issues a request for proposal for a digital permitting platform and compares vendors on implementation approach, support model, and delivery capability.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, request for proposal

Core Meaning: A procurement document used to solicit price quotations from prospective sellers for a defined product, service, or result.
Expansion: A request for quotation is used when the buyer can define the requirement clearly and wants sellers to compete primarily on price and commercial terms. It is best suited to standardized goods or well-specified services. PMI treats it as a formal procurement solicitation document for obtaining comparable quotations.
Contrast: Request for bid — a request for quotation asks for quotations against a defined requirement; a request for bid is a closely related solicitation term often used where formal bid competition is emphasized.
Example: A project team sends a request for quotation for backup servers with fixed technical specifications and compares supplier pricing and delivery dates.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, request for quotation

Core Meaning: A procurement document used to obtain price quotations from sellers for a well-defined requirement.
Expansion: A request for quote is used when the buyer can specify the needed product, service, or result clearly enough that suppliers mainly compete on price and delivery terms. It is most suitable for standardised or tightly specified purchases rather than situations requiring sellers to propose substantially different solution approaches. In PMI procurement practice, it is one of the common bidder conference and solicitation-related instruments used in conducting procurements.
Contrast: Request for proposal — a request for quote seeks pricing for a defined need, while a request for proposal seeks a proposed solution and approach.
Example: A project team issues a request for quote for 200 laptops with fixed technical specifications and compares supplier responses on cost and lead time.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Request for quote, page 248

Core Meaning: Conditions or capabilities that must be present in a product, service, result, or project outcome to satisfy an agreement, standard, specification, or stakeholder need.
Expansion: Requirements define what must be delivered or satisfied for a project to be considered successful. In PMI usage, they can apply to product features, quality expectations, regulatory obligations, transition needs, or project management constraints, and they become the basis for planning, verification, and acceptance. Clear requirements support scope definition, prioritisation, traceability, and change control across the project life cycle.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Requirements are documented needs and expectations that the project and its deliverables must meet. They may be functional or nonfunctional, explicit or implied, and are refined, analysed, and managed as understanding improves.

Why it matters and where it applies: Requirements are central to scope, quality, stakeholder engagement, procurement, testing, and acceptance. Weak requirements create rework, disputes, scope creep, and misaligned solutions, especially in complex or change-heavy environments.

The most common misconception: that requirements are just a fixed list of features collected once at the start. In practice, they exist at multiple levels, need validation and prioritisation, and may evolve through progressive elaboration or adaptive delivery.

How it shows up in real projects: teams elicit stakeholder needs, document acceptance criteria, maintain traceability between requirements and deliverables, and assess proposed changes against approved baselines and business value.
Contrast: Scope — requirements state the needs and conditions to be met; scope defines the work and deliverables undertaken to meet them.
Example: In a hospital records project, requirements include role-based access, audit logging, response-time thresholds, and compliance with health-data regulations before the system can be accepted.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Requirements; PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 5.2, p. 137

Core Meaning: The systematic examination of elicited requirements to clarify, prioritise, refine, and validate what the project or product must deliver.
Expansion: Requirements analysis turns raw stakeholder needs into clear, actionable, and testable requirements that can support scope definition and delivery decisions. PMI places it within requirements management as the work of assessing completeness, consistency, feasibility, priority, and alignment with business objectives. It is foundational because weak analysis leads directly to rework, misaligned deliverables, conflict over acceptance, and uncontrolled scope change.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Requirements analysis is the step where gathered needs are interpreted, decomposed, compared, and structured into usable requirements. It often includes modelling, prioritisation, dependency analysis, acceptance criteria definition, and resolution of ambiguity or conflict.

Why it matters and where it applies: almost every project depends on a shared understanding of what must be delivered and how success will be judged. Requirements analysis is especially important when many stakeholders are involved, needs conflict, or the solution is complex and carries significant downstream cost if misunderstood.

The most common misconception: that requirements analysis is just writing down what stakeholders ask for. In practice it tests those asks against business value, feasibility, constraints, interfaces, assumptions, and hidden contradictions before they become commitments.

How it shows up in real projects: analysts and project teams review stakeholder inputs, model workflows or features, define acceptance criteria, resolve conflicts between groups, and produce a prioritised and traceable set of requirements that informs scope, schedule, design, and validation.
Contrast: Requirements elicitation — elicitation gathers stakeholder needs and expectations, while requirements analysis interprets and refines that input into coherent, usable requirements.
Example: For a new customer portal, the team interviews sales, service, and compliance stakeholders, then analyses their inputs to separate mandatory security requirements from desired usability features and define measurable acceptance criteria.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, p. 137 to p. 145

Core Meaning: A collection of requirements that the project has agreed to deliver.
Expansion: Requirements documentation records the requirements, their attributes, and supporting detail needed to understand and manage them. It provides a basis for scope definition, validation, and change control. PMI uses it as the working record of what the project must satisfy.
Contrast: Requirements management plan — requirements documentation records the requirements themselves; the requirements management plan explains how they will be managed.
Example: A business system project records functional, nonfunctional, interface, and regulatory requirements before detailed design begins.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Requirements documentation

Core Meaning: The process of drawing out, discovering, and identifying stakeholder needs and requirements.
Expansion: Requirements elicitation is the work of uncovering what stakeholders need, want, and expect from a product, service, or result. In PMI practice, it is part of the broader requirements work that informs analysis, documentation, and prioritisation. Common techniques include interviews, workshops, observation, and document analysis. Its purpose is to surface both stated and unstated needs early enough to support sound decisions.
Contrast: Requirements analysis — elicitation discovers and captures requirements; analysis examines, refines, and prioritises them.
Example: A business analyst interviews call-centre staff and observes live support interactions to elicit requirements for a new customer-service portal.
Source: The PMI Guide to Business Analysis — requirements elicitation; PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition — Collect Requirements

Core Meaning: The disciplined handling of requirements so they are elicited, analysed, documented, traced, prioritised, and controlled throughout the project life cycle.
Expansion: Requirements management is the ongoing practice of making sure stakeholder and product requirements are clear, agreed, traceable, and governed as work progresses. In PMI practice, it connects elicitation, documentation, prioritisation, traceability, change control, and validation so that scope remains aligned to business need. It matters because poorly managed requirements are one of the fastest paths to rework, stakeholder conflict, and scope instability.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Requirements management is not a single document but a continuing management discipline that spans from initial discovery of needs through approval, tracing, change handling, and verification. It ensures that requirements remain connected to objectives, deliverables, and acceptance criteria.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects fail when teams build the wrong thing, build too much, or lose control of changes. Requirements management is especially important in product development, software, engineering, regulatory initiatives, and any effort with multiple stakeholder groups or evolving needs.

The most common misconception: that requirements management is just writing requirements once at the start. In practice, it also includes governance over changes, maintaining traceability, resolving conflicts, and confirming that delivered outputs satisfy the intended need.

How it shows up in real projects: teams use a requirements management plan, traceability matrix, prioritisation decisions, baseline control, and structured reviews to keep requirements usable and aligned from planning through delivery.
Contrast: Requirements documentation — requirements management is the end-to-end discipline of governing requirements, while requirements documentation is the recorded content of the requirements themselves.
Example: On a digital-banking project, the team traces each compliance, customer, and security requirement to design elements, test cases, and approval decisions so that scope changes are assessed before implementation.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A component of the project management plan that describes how requirements will be analyzed, documented, and managed.
Expansion: The requirements management plan defines how the project will collect, analyze, prioritize, trace, and control requirements. It sets expectations for change handling, version control, and approval responsibilities. PMI uses it to keep requirements work disciplined and aligned with scope management.
Contrast: Requirements documentation — the requirements management plan explains how requirements will be managed; requirements documentation records the requirements themselves.
Example: A software project defines in its requirements management plan that all changes must be logged, reviewed by the change control board, and traced to test cases.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Requirements management plan

Core Meaning: A structured representation used to depict, organise, and analyse requirements and their relationships.
Expansion: A requirements model expresses requirements in a form that helps stakeholders understand scope, behaviour, relationships, or constraints more clearly than narrative text alone. In PMI practice, models may be graphical, analytical, or hierarchical, depending on the kind of requirement being examined. They are useful because they improve communication, reveal gaps or inconsistencies, and support analysis before detailed solution work begins.
Contrast: Requirements documentation — a requirements model is a structured representation of requirements, while requirements documentation is the broader recorded set of requirement information.
Example: A business-analysis team uses process and data models to show how customer onboarding requirements translate into workflow steps and data fields.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The process of ranking requirements by relative importance to maximise value and guide scope and delivery decisions.
Expansion: Requirements prioritisation determines which requirements matter most so the team can make informed decisions about scope, sequencing, and trade-offs. It is used to align product or project work with stakeholder value, risk, urgency, and constraints. In PMI practice, prioritisation commonly appears within requirements management, backlog management, and progressive elaboration rather than as a standalone delivery method.
Contrast: Requirements grouping — prioritisation ranks requirements by importance, while grouping organises them into meaningful categories or sets.
Example: On a customer-portal project, the team ranks secure login and payment processing above profile-customisation features so the first release delivers core business value.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A grid that links product requirements from their origin to deliverables and test cases.
Expansion: The requirements traceability matrix allows each requirement to be tracked across the project life cycle. It supports coverage analysis, change impact assessment, and confirmation that requirements are built and verified. PMI uses it to strengthen scope control and product acceptance.
Contrast: Requirements documentation — the requirements traceability matrix links requirements across the project; requirements documentation records the requirements themselves.
Example: A software team maps each business requirement to design components, build tasks, and test cases so every requirement can be verified before release.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition — Requirements traceability matrix

Core Meaning: An amount of time or budget set aside to address uncertainty and protect project objectives.
Expansion: In PMI usage, a reserve is planned capacity in time or cost that is not intended for routine work but for uncertainty. It exists because estimates are never perfectly certain and projects operate under risk. The concept includes forms such as contingency reserve and management reserve, which are used for different kinds of uncertainty and are governed differently.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A reserve is deliberate allowance built into the plan or budget to absorb uncertainty without immediately destabilising the project. It is not extra scope and it is not a planning error; it is a controlled response to the reality of risk.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects face variability in effort, cost, supply, approvals, weather, quality outcomes, and many other factors. Reserves help teams remain credible, avoid constant replanning, and respond to risk in a disciplined way across schedule, cost, and delivery commitments.

The most common misconception: that any unused reserve is waste or padding. In sound project management, reserve is justified by risk and uncertainty, governed explicitly, and released or consumed through defined controls rather than hidden inside estimates.

How it shows up in real projects: a cost baseline may include contingency for identified risks while senior management holds a separate management reserve for unforeseen work. Teams monitor reserve consumption as part of risk review, forecasting, and change control.
Contrast: Contingency reserve — reserve is the broader concept; contingency reserve is the portion set aside for identified risks within defined limits.
Example: A capital project includes contingency in the cost baseline for identified ground-condition risks and keeps separate management reserve for unforeseen events outside the approved baseline.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, reserve analysis and reserve terminology, pp. 442 and 705

Core Meaning: An analytical technique used to determine the amount of contingency and management reserves needed for the project.
Expansion: Reserve analysis evaluates whether current reserves are sufficient to address remaining uncertainty and risk. It compares reserve levels with the project s current exposure and can lead to adjustments in contingency or management reserve. PMI uses it in schedule and cost control to keep reserves aligned with actual risk conditions.
Contrast: Contingency reserve — reserve analysis is the technique used to assess reserves; contingency reserve is one type of reserve that may be evaluated.
Example: During execution, a construction team reviews weather and procurement risks and decides whether the remaining contingency reserve is still adequate.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Seventh Edition — Reserve analysis

Core Meaning: The risk that remains after risk responses have been implemented.
Expansion: Residual risk is the portion of risk left after planned responses have reduced or addressed the original threat or opportunity. It may still need monitoring, acceptance, or further action if it remains above tolerance. PMI distinguishes it from the original risk because it exists after the response is applied.
Contrast: Secondary risk — residual risk is what remains after a response; secondary risk is a new risk created by the response itself.
Example: After adding a backup supplier, a project still faces a small chance of delayed materials because customs clearance can still disrupt delivery.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Residual risk

Core Meaning: A person, team, facility, equipment, material, or other asset used to complete project work.
Expansion: In PMI usage, a resource is anything needed to perform project work, not just people. It includes human capability as well as equipment, facilities, materials, funding support, and other assets that enable delivery. Because projects depend on finite resources, planning and managing them is central to estimating, scheduling, cost control, and execution.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A resource is any asset applied to project activities or work packages to produce deliverables. PMI uses the term broadly so teams do not treat staffing as the only constraint that matters.

Why it matters and where it applies: resource availability shapes schedule realism, cost estimates, sequencing decisions, and delivery risk. It appears across planning and execution in staffing, equipment booking, material supply, and access to specialist capability.

The most common misconception: that resource means only a named person or role on the team. In practice, critical resources often include scarce environments, machinery, funding, data, or physical space, and these can constrain delivery as much as labour does.

How it shows up in real projects: teams estimate resource requirements, acquire people and assets, track utilisation, resolve conflicts, and adjust plans when key resources are overallocated, delayed, or unavailable.
Contrast: Resource assignment — a resource is the asset itself; resource assignment is the allocation of that asset to specific work.
Example: A software project uses developers, testers, a cloud test environment, and a cybersecurity lab as project resources needed to deliver the release.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — resource; A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, exact page citation not confirmed in current context

Core Meaning: The assignment of available resources to specific project activities, work packages, or time periods.
Expansion: Resource allocation matches people, equipment, facilities, funding, or materials to the work that needs to be performed. It supports realistic planning by making visible who or what is needed, when it is needed, and where constraints may affect delivery. In PMI practice, it is closely tied to activity resource estimating, schedule development, and ongoing monitoring of resource use.
Contrast: Resource optimisation — allocation assigns resources to work, while optimisation adjusts the plan to use those resources more effectively within constraints.
Example: A construction project manager assigns two electricians and one inspection window to the wiring activities scheduled for the third week of site execution.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The allocation of a resource to a specific project activity or work package.
Expansion: Resource assignment connects available resources to the work they will perform. It supports accountability, scheduling, and workload balancing by showing who or what is committed to each task. PMI uses it to make execution responsibilities explicit and to support schedule and resource management.
Contrast: Responsibility assignment matrix — resource assignment allocates resources to work; a responsibility assignment matrix maps deliverables or activities to levels of responsibility across roles.
Example: A project manager assigns a database specialist and a staging server to the data migration work package during the final two weeks of system testing.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — resource assignment; A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, exact page citation not confirmed in current context

Core Meaning: The extent to which needed resources are obtainable and ready for use when project work requires them.
Expansion: Resource availability affects whether activities can start and finish as planned because people, equipment, materials, facilities, or funding may be constrained. In PMI scheduling and planning practice, it is a key input to estimating durations, sequencing work, and developing realistic schedules. Limited availability often drives trade-offs among scope, timing, cost, and quality.
Contrast: Resource calendar — resource availability is the actual condition of a resource being obtainable when needed, while a resource calendar records the times when that resource is expected to be available.
Example: A construction project pushes back commissioning because the specialist inspection team is not available during the original handover window.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, availability of resources, pages 66 and 120

Core Meaning: A hierarchical representation of resources organized by category and type.
Expansion: A Resource Breakdown Structure groups project resources into logical categories such as people, equipment, materials, or facilities. It helps the team understand resource classes, assess capacity needs, and support planning and control. PMI uses it as a way to structure resource information distinctly from the work itself.
Contrast: Work Breakdown Structure — a Resource Breakdown Structure organizes resources by type or category; a WBS organizes deliverables and the work needed to create them.
Example: An infrastructure project structures resources into civil engineers, site supervisors, heavy machinery, concrete materials, and testing facilities before detailed assignment planning.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, 9.2.1.3 Resource Breakdown Structure, p. 323

Core Meaning: A calendar that identifies the working days and shifts on which a resource is available.
Expansion: A resource calendar shows when a person, team, or physical resource can work on the project. It is used to align scheduling with actual availability, including holidays, shifts, and time off. PMI treats it as a scheduling input that helps prevent unrealistic assignments and date conflicts.
Contrast: Project calendar — a resource calendar shows availability for a specific resource; a project calendar shows the working and nonworking time for the project as a whole.
Example: A project schedules a specialist only on weekdays because the resource calendar shows that the person is unavailable on weekends.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition / PMI Lexicon — Resource calendar

Core Meaning: A limitation on the availability, capacity, timing, or type of resources that restricts how project work can be planned or executed.
Expansion: A resource constraint exists when needed people, equipment, materials, funding, or facilities are not available in the amount or timeframe the project would ideally require. It affects sequencing, duration, cost, and delivery choices because work must be adapted to finite capacity. In practice, resource constraints are among the most common drivers of schedule trade-offs and prioritisation decisions.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A resource constraint is any cap or shortage that limits the project's ability to use resources exactly when and how it wants. The constraint may involve quantity, skills, timing, location, or competing demand from other work.

Why it matters and where it applies: resource constraints directly shape schedules, budgets, and delivery strategies across predictive, agile, and hybrid environments. They are especially important in matrix organisations, capital projects, and multi-project portfolios where the same critical resources are shared.

The most common misconception: that a resource constraint is only a staffing problem. In reality, constrained resources can include specialist equipment, scarce materials, approval capacity, funding windows, or access to key stakeholders.

How it shows up in real projects: teams delay tasks until a specialist becomes available, level workloads to avoid over-allocation, split releases to fit funding limits, or re-plan dependencies because a shared test environment cannot support concurrent work.
Contrast: Constraint — a resource constraint is a specific limitation tied to resource availability or capacity, while a constraint can be any limiting factor such as scope, schedule, cost, quality, or regulation.
Example: An ERP implementation must stagger configuration and testing because the same database architect is assigned part-time across three concurrent projects.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Seventh Edition, page 264

Core Meaning: A resource optimization technique in which adjustments are made to the project schedule to optimize the allocation of resources and which may affect the critical path.
Expansion: Resource leveling adjusts activity dates to resolve over-allocation and align the schedule with actual resource availability. Unlike techniques that preserve planned dates where possible, it may extend durations and change the critical path if constrained resources cannot support the original sequence. PMI treats it as a practical scheduling response when resources are limited or fixed and schedule realism matters more than protecting the initial timeline.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Resource leveling is a schedule adjustment technique used when key people, equipment, or materials are overcommitted. The project manager shifts activities within or beyond float, or even delays work, so resource demand better matches actual capacity.

Why it matters and where it applies: plans often look feasible until the same specialist, machine, or crew is assigned to overlapping tasks. Resource leveling matters in projects with scarce expertise, shared resources across multiple projects, or hard staffing limits, because it produces a schedule that can actually be executed.

The most common misconception: that resource leveling is the same as smoothing and should not change end dates. In fact, leveling is specifically used when resource limits drive the schedule, so finish dates and the critical path may change.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager may delay noncritical engineering tasks because the same senior architect is also needed on a regulatory design review. The updated schedule becomes longer, but it reflects real capacity instead of an impossible staffing plan.
Contrast: Resource smoothing — resource leveling resolves over-allocation even if it changes the critical path or finish date; resource smoothing adjusts within existing schedule limits where possible.
Example: A construction project postpones some interior work by two weeks because the only certified electrical crew is already committed to critical installation tasks on another building area.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition — Resource leveling, p. 215

Core Meaning: The processes used to identify, acquire, manage, and control the resources needed for the project.
Expansion: Resource management covers how a project plans for and uses people, equipment, materials, facilities, and other needed resources. PMI treats it as a full management domain that includes estimating needs, acquiring resources, assigning them, developing team capability, and monitoring utilisation. It matters because resource limits often shape schedule, cost, feasibility, and risk. In practice, it connects planning decisions to day-to-day execution so the right resources are available at the right time.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Resource management is the coordinated planning and control of all resources required to deliver project work. It includes identifying what is needed, securing it, allocating it, and adjusting use as conditions change.

Why it matters and where it applies: most projects are constrained by scarce skills, shared teams, specialist equipment, or material availability. Effective resource management helps prevent bottlenecks, supports realistic schedules, and improves the chance that commitments can actually be met.

The most common misconception: that resource management only means assigning people to tasks. In PMI terms it also covers physical resources, acquisition timing, capacity constraints, team development, and ongoing control of resource use.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager negotiates for specialist engineers, sequences use of shared test equipment, updates resource calendars, and resolves conflicts when multiple projects need the same capability at once.
Contrast: Resource calendar — resource management is the overall discipline of planning and controlling resources; a resource calendar only shows when a specific resource is available.
Example: A construction project coordinates trades, cranes, and concrete deliveries so each required resource is available in sequence without delaying critical work.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition — Project Resource Management, pp. 307-362

Core Meaning: A component of the project management plan that describes how project resources are acquired, allocated, managed, and controlled.
Expansion: The resource management plan sets out the approach for planning, acquiring, managing, and releasing team and physical resources. It typically addresses roles and responsibilities, organisational charts, resource calendars, training, recognition, and compliance considerations. In PMI practice it provides the rules and structure for managing the resources needed to deliver the project.
Contrast: Resource requirements — the resource management plan explains how resources will be managed overall; resource requirements specify the types and quantities needed for particular work.
Example: On a hospital expansion project, the resource management plan defines specialist trade roles, equipment booking rules, onboarding, shift constraints, and how scarce crane time will be scheduled.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 9.1.3.1, p. 318

Core Meaning: The adjustment of activity dates, sequencing, or assignments to use available resources as effectively as possible within project constraints.
Expansion: Resource optimisation is a scheduling and planning concept used to balance resource demand with resource availability. PMI commonly discusses it through techniques such as resource levelling and resource smoothing, which change the schedule or assignment pattern to reduce overloads and improve feasibility. It matters when the initial plan exceeds available capacity or creates inefficient usage patterns.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Resource optimisation is the deliberate reshaping of planned work so the project uses limited resources in a practical, sustainable way. It usually involves changing timing, sequencing, or assignment patterns rather than changing project objectives.

Why it matters and where it applies: most projects face shared specialists, limited equipment, budget ceilings, or hard calendar constraints. Optimisation helps produce schedules that can actually be executed, especially in matrix organisations, construction, engineering, and multi-project environments.

The most common misconception: that resource optimisation always means making people fully utilised at every moment. In practice, the goal is not constant utilisation but a workable plan that respects constraints, reduces harmful overloads, and supports delivery performance.

How it shows up in real projects: a scheduler delays noncritical tasks to avoid overbooking a testing team, or shifts work within float so a scarce subject-matter expert can support multiple deliverables without causing repeated bottlenecks.
Contrast: Resource allocation — allocation places resources onto work, while optimisation refines that plan to resolve conflicts and improve feasibility.
Example: In an IT rollout, the project manager shifts several training tasks by one week because the same network engineer was assigned to three parallel activities on the same days.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 6.5.2.3 and Section 6.6.2.4

Core Meaning: A scheduling technique used to adjust activity start and finish dates so resource usage fits resource constraints or balances demand.
Expansion: Resource optimization is the use of scheduling methods such as resource leveling and resource smoothing to create a more achievable schedule. PMI treats it as a way to resolve overallocations, capacity limits, and uneven workloads that make a schedule unrealistic. It is foundational because even well-defined scope and sequencing can fail if the plan ignores actual resource availability.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Resource optimization is the deliberate adjustment of a schedule so the planned work better matches available people and physical resources. It is most often achieved through resource leveling, which can change the critical path and end date, or resource smoothing, which stays within float and tries to preserve the completion date.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects rarely have unlimited access to specialist staff, equipment, facilities, or environments. Optimization makes schedules executable by reducing overload, avoiding impossible overlaps, and improving the reliability of commitments in construction, technology, engineering, operations, and other delivery settings.

The most common misconception: that resource optimization is just about making people busy all the time. In practice, its purpose is not maximum utilisation for its own sake but a feasible flow of work that respects constraints, protects delivery dates where possible, and reduces burnout and conflict.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager shifts testing work because the same test environment is booked by multiple teams, delays a task until a certified engineer is available, or smooths design work across weeks so the core team is not overloaded in one period and idle in the next.
Contrast: Resource planning — resource planning determines what resources are needed; resource optimization adjusts the schedule to use those resources realistically.
Example: In an ERP rollout, the schedule is reworked because the same data-migration specialist was assigned to three parallel activities, so tasks are leveled across two additional weeks.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 6.5.2.3, p. 191

Core Meaning: The process of determining what resources are needed for project work and when they will be required.
Expansion: Resource planning is the practical activity of identifying required people, equipment, materials, facilities, and related capacity over time. In PMI terms, this idea is expressed through estimating activity resources and planning resource management rather than as a single formal glossary entry. It is widely used in practice to align demand, availability, and delivery commitments before execution begins.
Contrast: Resource management plan — resource planning determines needed resources and timing; the resource management plan documents the policies and approach for managing them.
Example: A manufacturing-automation project forecasts when electricians, PLC engineers, test rigs, and commissioning windows will be needed for each phase of the implementation.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The types and quantities of resources needed to complete project work.
Expansion: Resource requirements describe what resources are needed, in what amount, and sometimes when they are needed. They support planning, estimating, and acquisition decisions. PMI uses them to translate scope and schedule needs into staffing, equipment, and material demands.
Contrast: Activity duration estimates — resource requirements define what resources are needed; activity duration estimates define how long the work is expected to take.
Example: A project plan states that two testers, one business analyst, and a dedicated test environment are required for the final release cycle.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Resource requirements

Core Meaning: A schedule optimization technique in which activities are adjusted within their float so resource demand is kept at a more even level.
Expansion: Resource smoothing shifts work within available float to reduce extreme peaks and valleys in resource usage. It aims to make utilization more stable without changing the project end date or violating major schedule constraints. PMI treats it as a distinct optimization technique from resource leveling because it works within existing timing limits.
Contrast: Resource leveling — resource smoothing uses available float and preserves the planned finish date; resource leveling may delay activities and change the critical path.
Example: A project manager moves noncritical training tasks by a few days so the same specialist is not double-booked during the busiest week.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Seventh Edition — Resource smoothing, p. 194

Core Meaning: The amount of a resource's capacity assigned to an activity, usually expressed as a percentage or similar measure of availability.
Expansion: Resource units indicate how much of a person, team, equipment item, or other resource is committed to a specific activity. They are used in scheduling and planning to model partial or full allocation of available capacity.
Contrast: Resource assignment — resource units describe how much capacity is assigned, while a resource assignment identifies which resource is allocated.
Example: A business analyst is assigned at 50 percent resource units to requirements workshops while supporting another project half time.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A project schedule whose planned dates are adjusted to reflect the expected availability of required resources.
Expansion: A resource-constrained schedule reflects the fact that activities cannot always start as soon as logic alone would permit because needed resources are limited or unavailable. It is the resulting schedule after timing has been aligned to actual resource capacity. PMI treats this as the same concept as a resource-limited schedule.
Contrast: Resource leveling — resource leveling is the technique used to adjust dates for limited resources, while a resource-constrained schedule is the resulting schedule.
Example: A project delays integration testing by one week because the only qualified test engineer is committed to another critical activity.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — resource-limited schedule

Core Meaning: The scheduling practice of adjusting activity timing to fit limited resource availability.
Expansion: Resource-constrained scheduling builds a workable schedule by considering when needed resources are actually available rather than relying only on activity logic. In PMI-aligned practice, it is closely associated with resource leveling and the creation of a resource-limited schedule.
Contrast: Resource smoothing — resource-constrained scheduling may extend the project finish date to respect resource limits, while resource smoothing generally works within available float.
Example: A construction scheduler moves overlapping inspection tasks because the same certified inspector is required for each one.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A schedule that is constrained by the availability of resources needed to perform the work.
Expansion: A resource-limited schedule reflects the fact that activities can proceed only when the required people, equipment, materials, or facilities are available. It is shaped by resource constraints rather than target dates alone. In practice, this often leads to sequencing changes, delayed starts, or resource leveling so the plan remains feasible.
Contrast: Resource smoothing — a resource-limited schedule is driven by hard availability constraints; resource smoothing adjusts work within float to reduce peaks without necessarily changing the critical path.
Example: A systems implementation project pushes several testing tasks into the following week because the only certified security tester is unavailable until then.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The person assigned responsibility for carrying out a specific planned action in response to a risk.
Expansion: In PMI usage this role is typically expressed as a risk action owner: the individual responsible for implementing one or more agreed response actions. The role is execution-focused and distinct from overall accountability for the risk itself. Assigning a response owner prevents planned actions from remaining unowned in the risk register.
Contrast: Risk owner — the risk owner is accountable for monitoring and managing the overall risk; the response owner carries out the specific response action.
Example: For a supplier-delay risk, the procurement manager is assigned as the response owner for qualifying a backup vendor within two weeks.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Risk action owner, pp. 38, 167

Core Meaning: The obligation to perform assigned project work and answer for completing that work.
Expansion: Responsibility is the duty assigned to a person or role to carry out specific project work. In PMI practice, it is used to clarify who performs tasks, produces outputs, or supports decisions within the project structure. It is especially visible in role definitions and responsibility assignment matrices, where clear ownership reduces confusion and delay.
Contrast: Accountability — responsibility is about doing the work or owning an assigned duty; accountability is about ultimate answerability for the result.
Example: In a system-implementation project, the data-migration lead is responsible for cleansing legacy data and loading it into the new platform.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — exact page citation unavailable in retrieved context

Core Meaning: A grid that shows project resources assigned to each work package.
Expansion: A responsibility assignment matrix links work packages or activities to the people or roles responsible for performing them. It is used to clarify who does what and to reduce gaps, duplication, and confusion in execution. PMI presents it as a practical way to align work definitions with assigned resources and accountability.
Contrast: RACI matrix — a responsibility assignment matrix is the broader category; a RACI matrix is one specific form that labels involvement as responsible, accountable, consulted, and informed.
Example: In an ERP implementation, the matrix shows that finance owns chart-of-accounts design, IT owns system configuration, and the vendor supports data migration tasks.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, responsibility assignment matrix

Core Meaning: A result is an output, outcome, or other consequence produced by work that can be evaluated for its contribution to project objectives.
Expansion: In project management, result refers broadly to what project work produces, whether that is a deliverable, a performance effect, or a realised benefit. PMI usage often distinguishes among types of results rather than treating the word as a tightly bounded standalone method term. It matters because projects are judged not only by completing activities but by the value and effects those activities create.
Contrast: Deliverable — a deliverable is a specific, verifiable product, service, or capability; a result can also include broader outcomes or consequences beyond the deliverable itself.
Example: A data-migration project produces a converted database as a deliverable, while the reduced reporting delay experienced by business users is a result of the project.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The planned withdrawal of a product, service, asset, or capability from operational use at the end of its useful life.
Expansion: Retirement is the end-of-life transition in which something is formally taken out of service in a controlled manner. In projects, it often includes migration, archival, disposal, decommissioning, or handoff to replacement solutions.
Contrast: Decommissioning — retirement is the broader end-of-life state or decision, while decommissioning is one action used to execute it.
Example: A legacy payroll platform is retired after employee records are migrated, users are trained on the replacement system, and the old environment is shut down.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A meeting held to review what happened in an iteration or project phase and identify improvements.
Expansion: A retrospective helps the team inspect its process, collaboration, and outcomes so it can improve future performance. It is commonly used in adaptive and iterative environments, but the underlying idea of learning from experience applies broadly. PMI treats it as a structured opportunity for continuous improvement.
Contrast: Lessons learned — a retrospective is a live team reflection used to improve the next cycle; lessons learned are the recorded insights that may be captured for future use.
Example: After a two-week sprint, the team agrees to improve backlog refinement and reduce handoff delays before the next iteration.
Source: PMI Agile Practice Guide — retrospective

Core Meaning: A measure of how much value or benefit is gained relative to the cost of the investment.
Expansion: Return on investment is used to compare expected or actual benefits against the money and effort spent. In project management, it helps justify project selection and supports business cases and portfolio decisions. It is a financial indicator of whether a project is worth pursuing from a value perspective.
Contrast: Net present value — return on investment compares gain to cost as a ratio or percentage, while net present value converts future cash flows into a present-value amount.
Example: An organisation approves an automation project after estimating that lower operating costs and faster processing will produce a strong return on investment over three years.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An assessment of a product, document, or project work to determine whether it is complete, correct, and acceptable.
Expansion: A review is a structured examination of a work product, deliverable, or project status to judge quality, completeness, or readiness. In PMI practice, reviews are used to identify defects, confirm alignment with expectations, and support decisions before work proceeds. They may be formal or informal depending on governance needs and delivery approach. Reviews appear across quality management, requirements work, governance, and iterative delivery.
Contrast: Inspection — a review is a broader evaluation of work or status; an inspection is a more detailed check for conformance to specified criteria.
Example: Before releasing a training package, the team reviews the materials with subject matter experts and the sponsor to confirm accuracy and completeness.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — review

Core Meaning: Action taken to bring a defective or nonconforming component into compliance with requirements or specifications.
Expansion: Rework is corrective effort performed after work has failed to meet defined requirements, acceptance criteria, or quality standards. In PMI usage, it represents avoidable additional work that consumes time, cost, and resources and often signals weaknesses in planning, quality management, requirements definition, or execution control. It is tracked because it affects performance, productivity, and the likelihood of meeting project objectives.
Contrast: Repair — rework restores work to meet the original requirement; repair modifies a defective item so it becomes usable, which may or may not fully restore original conformity.
Example: A software team rebuilds a reporting feature after testing shows it does not meet the approved performance and security requirements.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Rework; PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 8.2, p. 305

Core Meaning: An uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has a positive or negative effect on one or more project objectives.
Expansion: Risk is PMI's core concept for uncertainty that can affect project outcomes. It includes both threats and opportunities, so it is broader than the idea of something going wrong. It is managed continuously through identification, analysis, response planning, and monitoring so the team can protect objectives and improve outcomes.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Risk is uncertainty that matters because it can affect scope, schedule, cost, quality, or other objectives. PMI defines it broadly enough to include upside as well as downside effects.

Why it matters and where it applies: Every project operates with imperfect information, changing conditions, assumptions, and dependencies. Risk management helps teams make better decisions under uncertainty in predictive, agile, and hybrid environments.

The most common misconception: that risk means only threats or hazards. In PMI usage, a risk can also be an opportunity that creates a beneficial effect if it occurs.

How it shows up in real projects: A team may track the risk of a supplier delay, the risk that a regulation changes, or the opportunity that a reusable component could shorten development time and reduce cost.
Contrast: Issue — a risk is uncertain and may or may not occur; an issue has already happened and needs immediate management.
Example: A digital-transformation project identifies the risk that a third-party interface may not be ready on time and the opportunity that an existing internal tool could be reused to accelerate delivery.
Source: Practice Standard for Project Risk Management, 2nd Edition — Risk, p. 7; PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Risk, p. 720

Core Meaning: A risk response strategy that involves acknowledging the risk and not taking proactive action unless the risk occurs.
Expansion: Risk acceptance is used when the cost or effort of another response is not justified by the level of exposure. The team consciously decides to live with the risk, sometimes with contingency reserves or a fallback plan in place. PMI treats it as an intentional response choice rather than passive neglect.
Contrast: Risk avoidance — risk acceptance leaves the threat in place; risk avoidance changes the plan to eliminate the threat or protect the objective from it.
Example: A project accepts a small chance of minor weather delay and keeps schedule reserve available in case the delay happens.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Risk acceptance

Core Meaning: The person responsible for carrying out the approved risk actions when responding to a given risk.
Expansion: A risk action owner is assigned to execute one or more approved actions tied to a risk response. The role focuses on doing the work, tracking progress, and keeping the risk owner informed. PMI also notes that this role is sometimes called the response owner.
Contrast: Risk owner — the risk owner is accountable for the risk itself; the risk action owner carries out the approved response actions.
Example: The procurement lead is named as risk action owner for the backup-supplier qualification tasks tied to a delivery-delay risk.
Source: The Standard for Risk Management in Portfolios, Programs, and Projects — Risk Action Owner

Core Meaning: The assignment of responsibility for a risk to the party best able to manage or bear it.
Expansion: Risk allocation places a risk with the internal or external party that has the most control over its causes or consequences. In project practice it often appears in procurement and contracting, where terms specify which party carries particular risks. Good allocation improves accountability and can reduce total project exposure by aligning responsibility with capability.
Contrast: Risk transference — risk allocation is the broader assignment of responsibility for a risk; risk transference is a specific response strategy that shifts ownership of a threat to a third party.
Example: In a construction project, ground-condition risk is allocated to the owner while equipment-maintenance risk is allocated to the contractor through contract terms.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition / procurement and risk management discussion, exact page citation not confirmed in current context

Core Meaning: The process of examining and evaluating risks to determine their characteristics and significance.
Expansion: Risk analysis helps the team understand which risks deserve the most attention and why. It can be qualitative, quantitative, or both, depending on the decision context and the available data. PMI uses it to support prioritisation, response planning, and reserve decisions.
Contrast: Risk data quality assessment — risk analysis evaluates the risks themselves; risk data quality assessment evaluates whether the underlying risk data is good enough to support that analysis.
Example: A project team rates identified risks for probability and impact, then applies quantitative analysis to the highest-priority schedule risks.
Source: Practice Standard for Project Risk Management, 2nd Edition — Risk analysis, p. 167; PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Risk analysis, p. 720

Core Meaning: The degree of uncertainty an organization or individual is willing to accept in anticipation of a reward.
Expansion: Risk appetite describes how much uncertainty a stakeholder or organization is prepared to take on in pursuit of value. It shapes decision making about which options are acceptable and how aggressively opportunities or threats should be managed. PMI treats it as a foundational concept for setting risk strategy and interpreting acceptable exposure.
Contrast: Risk threshold — risk appetite is the general willingness to accept uncertainty; risk threshold is the measurable boundary that expresses that willingness.
Example: A sponsor accepts greater technical uncertainty on an innovative digital product than on a mandatory regulatory reporting project.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Seventh Edition — Risk appetite, p. 94

Core Meaning: The evaluation of identified risks to understand their probability, impact, and relative priority for response and monitoring.
Expansion: Risk assessment is the broader practical activity of examining project risks to judge their significance and decide where attention is needed. In PMI practice, this is primarily carried out through qualitative and, when appropriate, quantitative risk analysis rather than as one single formal glossary term. It is foundational because projects cannot manage uncertainty well unless they first assess which risks matter most and how they could affect objectives.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Risk assessment is the disciplined evaluation of threats and opportunities after they are identified. Teams consider likelihood, impact, urgency, detectability, and sometimes quantitative exposure to prioritise action and improve decision making.

Why it matters and where it applies: every project faces uncertainty in scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, procurement, and stakeholder conditions. Risk assessment helps direct limited management attention toward the events and conditions most likely to influence outcomes instead of treating every risk as equally important.

The most common misconception: that risk assessment is just making a risk register or assigning red-amber-green labels once. In practice it is an ongoing analytical activity that should be revisited as assumptions change, responses are implemented, and new information emerges.

How it shows up in real projects: a team scores cyber-security risks for a product launch, models schedule exposure from supplier delays, elevates a high-impact interface risk to governance review, and updates priorities after a failed prototype test changes the probability of rework.
Contrast: Risk identification — risk identification finds and describes risks; risk assessment evaluates their significance and priority.
Example: A construction team assesses weather, permitting, and subcontractor risks, then prioritises the permitting risk for immediate response because it has moderate likelihood but severe schedule impact.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An examination of the effectiveness of risk responses and the effectiveness of the risk management process.
Expansion: A risk audit reviews whether planned risk responses were appropriate, executed, and effective. It also checks whether the overall risk management approach is being followed and producing useful control over uncertainty. PMI uses it to strengthen governance, improve future responses, and identify weaknesses in how risks are being managed.
Contrast: Risk review — a risk audit formally evaluates the effectiveness of responses and process; a risk review is a recurring check on current risks, status, and actions.
Example: During a large infrastructure project, the PMO conducts a risk audit to verify whether contingency triggers, mitigation actions, and escalation routes are working as intended.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, risk audit

Core Meaning: A risk response strategy in which the project team acts to eliminate the threat or protect the project from its impact.
Expansion: Risk avoidance changes the project approach so the threatening event can no longer occur or can no longer harm a project objective. This may involve changing scope, method, supplier, technology, or sequence of work. PMI uses it when the threat is serious enough that removing the exposure is preferable to reducing or transferring it.
Contrast: Risk mitigation — risk avoidance removes the threat or its exposure; risk mitigation reduces the probability or impact but leaves the risk in place.
Example: A team avoids a data privacy risk by removing a planned feature that would store sensitive personal information.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Risk avoidance

Core Meaning: A hierarchical representation of risks according to risk categories.
Expansion: A risk breakdown structure groups risks into categories and subcategories so the team can identify patterns and coverage gaps. It helps make risk identification more systematic and supports analysis by showing where uncertainty is concentrated. PMI uses it as a structuring device rather than a replacement for detailed risk records.
Contrast: Risk register — the risk breakdown structure organizes risks by category; the risk register records detailed information about individual risks.
Example: A construction project organizes risks under design, procurement, labor, weather, and regulatory branches before running identification workshops.
Source: The Standard for Portfolio Management, Fourth Edition — Risk breakdown structure, pp. 135-136

Core Meaning: A group of potential causes of risk.
Expansion: Risk categories organise risks into meaningful source groupings such as technical, external, organisational, or project management causes. They make identification more systematic and help teams detect patterns across related risks. PMI uses them to structure risk discussions and support further analysis.
Contrast: Risk breakdown structure — a risk category is a grouping concept; a risk breakdown structure is the hierarchical structure built from those categories.
Example: In a construction project, the team groups identified risks under design, procurement, labour, weather, and regulatory categories during risk workshops.
Source: Practice Standard for Project Risk Management, 2nd Edition — Risk category, p. 25; PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Risk category, p. 720

Core Meaning: A technique used to evaluate the degree to which data about risks is useful for risk management.
Expansion: This assessment checks whether risk data is accurate, reliable, complete, and timely enough to support analysis and decisions.
