Example: A PMO aligns its delivery governance to PMI's Standard for Project Management so project teams use a shared basis for planning, oversight, and decision-making.
Source: The Standard for Project Management in A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) – Seventh Edition, pp. 3-4

Core Meaning: A statistical measure of how widely values are dispersed around the mean.
Expansion: Standard deviation indicates the degree of variability in a set of values. In projects, it is used in estimating and quantitative analysis to describe uncertainty in durations, costs, or other forecasted results. A lower standard deviation means results cluster more tightly around the average, while a higher one indicates greater spread and less predictability.
Contrast: Variance — variance measures average squared dispersion, while standard deviation is its square root and is expressed in the same units as the original values.
Example: A project analyst calculates the standard deviation of task duration estimates to understand how much schedule uncertainty exists around the planned completion date.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition Glossary, standard deviation, page not provided in available search results

Core Meaning: A point in time associated with the start of a schedule activity.
Expansion: PMI uses start date as a schedule term for when an activity begins or is planned to begin. It can refer to several schedule states, such as actual, early, late, scheduled, or target start dates. The term is used in planning, sequencing, and schedule control.
Contrast: Project start date — start date applies to an activity; project start date applies to the project as a whole.
Example: The system-integration task has a scheduled start date of 12 August in the approved project schedule.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Start date, p. 28

Core Meaning: A logical relationship in which a successor activity cannot finish until a predecessor activity has started.
Expansion: Start-to-Finish is one of PMI's precedence relationship types used in schedule network analysis. It ties the finish of the successor to the start of the predecessor rather than to the predecessor's finish. It is valid but less common than Finish-to-Start in most project schedules.
Contrast: Finish-to-Start relationship — Start-to-Finish constrains when the successor can finish; Finish-to-Start constrains when the successor can start.
Example: A legacy support task cannot finish until the new operations team has started its takeover work, so the activities are linked Start-to-Finish.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition — Start-to-Finish relationship, p. 243

Core Meaning: A logical relationship in which a successor activity cannot start until a predecessor activity has started.
Expansion: A Start-to-Start relationship is a dependency type used in schedule network analysis when two activities may proceed in parallel once the first one begins. It allows overlap rather than requiring the predecessor to finish. PMI includes it as one of the standard precedence relationships used to model realistic sequencing, often with lead or lag.
Contrast: Finish-to-Start relationship — Start-to-Start allows the successor to begin after the predecessor starts; Finish-to-Start requires the predecessor to finish first.
Example: Once excavation starts on a construction project, dewatering operations can begin and continue in parallel rather than waiting for excavation to finish.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, section 6.3.3.1, page 157

Core Meaning: A narrative description of products, services, or results to be delivered by the project.
Expansion: A statement of work, or SOW, describes what is to be delivered and often provides the basis for procurement or project authorisation. It may include scope, deliverables, timing, and other expectations depending on the context. PMI uses it as a key input for defining and contracting work.
Contrast: Scope statement — a statement of work is a narrative description of requested work or deliverables; a scope statement is the project document that defines the project and product scope in more detail.
Example: A vendor SOW for a website redesign specifies the pages, content migration, testing support, and delivery milestones expected from the supplier.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — statement of work

Core Meaning: A method of selecting part of a population for inspection or analysis in order to draw conclusions about the whole.
Expansion: Statistical sampling is used when checking every item would be inefficient, costly, or unnecessary. In project work it is most often applied in quality planning and quality control to inspect a representative subset. The approach supports evidence-based decisions while reducing the effort of full inspection.
Contrast: Inspection — statistical sampling examines a subset of items, while inspection may refer to examining all items or the inspection activity more broadly.
Example: A construction project samples concrete test cylinders from each pour rather than testing every unit produced.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition — statistical sampling, p. 304

Core Meaning: A regularly scheduled meeting to exchange and analyze information about the current progress of the project and its performance.
Expansion: A Status Meeting is a recurring forum used to review where the project stands against plan or current expectations. Participants share updates, assess progress, surface issues, and interpret performance information so decisions or follow-up actions can be taken. Its value comes from turning scattered updates into shared understanding and coordinated next steps.
Contrast: Steering committee meeting — A Status Meeting focuses on current progress and performance exchange, while a steering committee meeting focuses more on oversight, escalation, and governance decisions.
Example: Every Monday, the project team reviews completed tasks, milestone status, schedule variance, open risks, and decisions needed for the coming week.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms

Core Meaning: A project document or communication that describes current performance and condition at a specific point in time.
Expansion: A status report summarises where the project stands against the plan at a given moment. It commonly covers progress, key issues, risks, schedule or cost condition, and near-term next steps.
Contrast: Progress report — a status report shows the current state at a point in time, while a progress report emphasises work completed during a reporting period.
Example: A weekly status report to the sponsor shows that testing is on track, two defects remain high priority, and the deployment decision is due on Friday.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A recurring review of current project progress, issues, and near-term actions used to support coordination and control.
Expansion: A status review is a common project-management review practice for assessing where work stands against plan. It typically covers completed work, upcoming activities, issues, risks, and decisions needed from stakeholders. Although widely used in PMI-aligned practice, it is better treated as a practitioner term than as a distinct formal glossary concept.
Contrast: Status report — a status review is the discussion or meeting, while a status report is the documented output or input for that discussion.
Example: Each Friday the project manager runs a status review with workstream leads to confirm milestone health and escalate unresolved blockers.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An advisory body of senior stakeholders who provide guidance, oversight, and decision support for a project, program, or portfolio.
Expansion: A steering committee is a governance body made up of senior stakeholders with authority or influence over the work. It provides direction, resolves escalated issues, and helps keep the initiative aligned with organizational priorities. PMI uses the term for groups that support decision making beyond the authority of the delivery team. It is especially common in complex, cross-functional, or high-visibility initiatives.
Contrast: Project team — the steering committee governs and guides the work, while the project team performs and manages the day to day delivery.
Example: A bank's digital transformation project uses a steering committee of business, technology, and compliance executives to approve major scope decisions and resolve conflicts across departments.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, steering committee, page 27

Core Meaning: A visual arrangement of user stories that shows the flow of user activities and helps plan releases or iterations around slices of value.
Expansion: A story map organises backlog items by the user journey or workflow rather than as a flat priority list. In agile practice, it helps teams see the big picture, identify meaningful increments, and decide what belongs in an initial release versus later iterations. It is especially useful when teams need both customer-context visibility and practical release planning.
Contrast: Product backlog — a story map arranges stories in the context of user activities and flow; a product backlog is the ordered list of work items to be delivered.
Example: A product team maps the customer journey for online booking, then selects the minimum set of stories across search, selection, payment, and confirmation for the first release.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A visual sequence of frames depicting a process, user journey, or interface flow.
Expansion: Storyboards lay out steps or screens in order so stakeholders can see and discuss an experience before it is built. They are a low-cost way to align understanding and surface gaps early.
Contrast: Prototype — a storyboard sketches the sequence at low fidelity; a prototype is an interactive working model.
Example: A team storyboards the six screens of a checkout flow to validate it with users before development.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; defined for glossary completeness

Core Meaning: The degree to which project work supports the organization’s strategy, goals, and priorities.
Expansion: Strategic alignment is the connection between project outcomes and the organisation's intended direction. PMI uses it to frame why work is selected, prioritised, continued, changed, or stopped based on contribution to strategic objectives. It matters because successful delivery alone does not guarantee meaningful value if the work does not support enterprise priorities. In practice, strategic alignment guides investment decisions, portfolio choices, and major scope trade-offs.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Strategic alignment expresses whether a project, program, or portfolio component supports organisational strategy. It links planned outcomes to goals such as growth, compliance, resilience, efficiency, or customer impact.

Why it matters and where it applies: organisations have limited funding, capacity, and executive attention, so alignment helps direct those resources toward work that matters most. It is especially important in portfolio management, business case evaluation, governance reviews, and prioritisation across competing initiatives.

The most common misconception: that strategic alignment matters only at project approval. In reality it should continue throughout delivery, because changes in strategy, market conditions, or performance may alter whether the work still deserves investment.

How it shows up in real projects: teams reference strategic objectives in charters, business cases, benefits plans, and steering reviews, and leaders reassess whether scope, funding, and sequencing still support the intended enterprise outcome.
Contrast: Business value — strategic alignment is about fit with organisational strategy; business value is the benefit the work is expected to produce.
Example: A retailer funds a customer-data platform because it directly supports its strategy to improve personalised marketing and increase repeat purchases.
Source: The Standard for Portfolio Management, Fourth Edition — strategic alignment; PMBOK Guide, Seventh Edition — System for Value Delivery

Core Meaning: Strategic goals are the high-level outcomes an organization intends to achieve to realize its strategy and guide investment and change decisions.
Expansion: In PMI usage, strategic goals express the future results the organization is pursuing and provide the basis for aligning portfolios, programs, projects, and operations. They help decision-makers select, prioritize, and govern work according to expected contribution to strategy rather than local activity alone. In practice, they connect enterprise vision and strategy to measurable outcomes and benefits.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Strategic goals translate organizational strategy into concrete target outcomes. They sit above individual initiatives and define what success should look like at an enterprise or business-unit level.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects and programs create the most value when they are selected and governed for their contribution to strategic goals. This is especially important in portfolio management, benefits realization, investment prioritization, and executive decision-making.

The most common misconception: that strategic goals are just broad aspirations with no operational effect. In practice, they shape funding, sequencing, governance attention, benefit measures, and decisions to continue, redirect, or terminate work.

How it shows up in real projects: a project business case, charter, benefits register, or portfolio review will often state which strategic goals the work supports and how progress toward them will be measured.
Contrast: Objectives — strategic goals set the higher-level intended outcomes, while objectives are more specific and often more immediately measurable targets supporting them.
Example: A healthcare organization sets a strategic goal to reduce avoidable readmissions, then prioritizes projects for remote monitoring, discharge process redesign, and analytics because they directly support that outcome.
Source: The Standard for Portfolio Management, Fourth Edition, Glossary section for strategic goals

Core Meaning: High-level organizational goals that guide investment decisions and align projects with the enterprise's intended direction.
Expansion: Strategic objectives express the outcomes an organization seeks to achieve over time and provide the basis for selecting, prioritising, and governing change initiatives. In PMI thinking, projects exist not just to produce deliverables but to advance these larger organizational aims. They connect strategy to portfolios, programs, projects, benefits, and measures of success.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Strategic objectives are the enterprise-level goals that define where the organization intends to go and what value it intends to create. They are broader than any single project and serve as a reference point for choosing which work should be funded and sustained.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects that are clearly linked to strategic objectives are easier to justify, prioritize, and govern. This matters especially in portfolio management, business case development, benefits realization, and executive decision-making where the question is not just whether a project can be done, but whether it should be done.

The most common misconception: that strategic objectives and project objectives are interchangeable. They are not; strategic objectives belong to the organization, while project objectives define the specific outcomes a particular project is expected to deliver in support of strategy.

How it shows up in real projects: a project charter, business case, or portfolio review often ties the initiative to goals such as market growth, regulatory compliance, cost reduction, resilience, or customer experience improvement. That traceability helps leaders decide scope, funding, sequencing, and success measures.
Contrast: Project objectives — strategic objectives are organization-level ends; project objectives are the specific results a project is chartered to achieve.
Example: A bank funds a customer-identity modernization project because it supports strategic objectives around digital growth, fraud reduction, and regulatory compliance.
Source: The Standard for Portfolio Management, Fourth Edition, section on strategic objectives and strategic alignment, exact page not confirmed in current context

Core Meaning: A high-level document that defines an organization's long-term direction, goals, and priorities and guides how initiatives should align to them.
Expansion: A strategic plan sets out where the organization intends to go and the objectives it seeks to achieve over time. In PMI usage, projects and programs are expected to align with this broader direction so that work contributes to intended benefits and business value. It matters because project selection, prioritisation, and success criteria are shaped by strategy rather than by isolated delivery performance alone.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A strategic plan is the organization's high-level blueprint for achieving its mission and future objectives. It defines priorities, desired outcomes, and directional choices that frame investment decisions.

Why it matters and where it applies: Projects do not exist in isolation. The strategic plan is central in portfolio selection, program justification, business case development, and benefits alignment because it helps determine which work should be started, funded, accelerated, or stopped.

The most common misconception: that strategy is separate from day-to-day project management. In practice, strategic alignment is a core reason projects are authorised, and a well-run project that does not support strategy may still be the wrong project.

How it shows up in real projects: It appears in business cases, project charters, portfolio reviews, and benefits plans where teams must show how a proposed initiative supports strategic objectives such as growth, compliance, cost efficiency, resilience, or customer experience.
Contrast: Project management plan — a strategic plan sets organizational direction and priorities; a project management plan explains how a specific project will be executed, monitored, and controlled.
Example: A healthcare provider's strategic plan calls for expanded virtual care, and that direction drives approval of a telehealth platform project and the benefits expected from it.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, p. 32

Core Meaning: A high-level plan of how objectives will be achieved over the longer term.
Expansion: Strategy sets direction and priorities, guiding which initiatives are undertaken and how resources are invested to reach goals. In projects, alignment to strategy ensures the work delivers value the organization actually wants.
Contrast: Tactics — strategy is the long-term approach and direction; tactics are the specific short-term actions used to execute it.
Example: An organization's growth strategy drives a portfolio of projects expanding into new regions.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; defined for glossary completeness

Core Meaning: Documents created prior to or at the start of the project that address strategic, business, or high-level information about the project.
Expansion: Strategy Artifacts are early documents that explain why a project exists, what strategic purpose it serves, and what high-level context should guide it. They are created before delivery work is fully underway or at project initiation, and they provide the business and strategic framing for later planning and execution. These artifacts help connect project activity to organizational intent rather than treating the work as isolated task execution.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Strategy Artifacts capture the high-level rationale, direction, and context that justify a project. They may include business cases, benefits information, strategic alignment documents, vision material, roadmaps, or other initiation-stage references that shape downstream decisions.

Why it matters and where it applies: teams make better scope, schedule, funding, and change decisions when the strategic purpose is explicit. These artifacts help sponsors, project leaders, and stakeholders stay aligned on intended value and on the broader outcomes the project is expected to support.

The most common misconception: that strategy artifacts are the same as detailed planning documents. In practice they come earlier and operate at a higher level, defining the business and strategic frame within which detailed plans are later developed.

How it shows up in real projects: before building a new customer platform, an organization may create a business case, product vision, target benefits statement, and strategic roadmap. Those documents then guide the charter, roadmap decisions, release priorities, and success measures.
Contrast: Project management plan — Strategy Artifacts provide strategic and high-level business framing at or before project start, while the project management plan defines how the project will be executed, monitored, and controlled.
Example: Before approving an enterprise data project, executives prepare a business case, benefits statement, and strategic roadmap that explain how the effort supports regulatory compliance and faster decision making.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms

Core Meaning: An organizational structure in which the project manager has high authority and full-time responsibility while functional managers retain some administrative control over resources.
Expansion: A strong matrix is a matrix organizational form that operates much like a projectized structure for day-to-day project leadership. The project manager has substantial authority over planning, decision-making, and coordination, while team members often work full time on the project. Functional managers still exist, but their role is more limited than in a weak or balanced matrix.
Contrast: Projectized organization — both give the project manager high authority, but a strong matrix still retains functional departments and dual reporting relationships.
Example: In an enterprise system rollout, the project manager directs budget, schedule, and assigned specialists full time, while department heads continue to manage technical standards and personnel administration.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, Section 2.3.2, pages 47-48

Core Meaning: A person with specialized knowledge in a discipline, function, technology, market, or process who provides expertise to support project work and decisions.
Expansion: A subject matter expert contributes deep domain knowledge that the project team may not possess internally. SMEs help refine requirements, assess options, validate deliverables, and reduce technical or business uncertainty. They may support planning, execution, quality review, risk analysis, or stakeholder engagement depending on the project's needs.
Contrast: Project team member — a subject matter expert is defined by specialized expertise contributed to the project, while a project team member may have broader delivery responsibilities without being the recognized authority in a domain.
Example: On a healthcare software project, a clinical coding expert reviews workflow requirements and confirms that the proposed design aligns with regulatory and operational practice.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, pages 43 and 82

Core Meaning: A smaller, more manageable component of a larger project that is planned and managed as part of the overall project structure.
Expansion: A subproject breaks a complex project into discrete parts so work can be organized, assigned, and controlled more effectively. It is commonly used when a major deliverable, phase, specialty area, or externally managed portion needs focused management attention. Each subproject remains aligned to the parent project's objectives, governance, and integration needs.
Contrast: Project phase — a subproject is a separately managed component of a larger project, while a phase is a collection of related activities within the project's life cycle.
Example: In a new airport construction program, the baggage handling system is managed as a subproject with its own lead, schedule, and vendor coordination integrated into the main project.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, page 4

Core Meaning: A component of the project management plan that describes how a specific aspect of the project will be planned, managed, monitored, and controlled.
Expansion: A subsidiary plan is one of the supporting plans that together make up the project management plan. Each subsidiary plan addresses a particular management domain such as scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, communications, risk, procurement, or stakeholder engagement. PMI uses the concept to show that integrated project management is built from coordinated domain-specific planning rather than from a single undifferentiated plan. The term is foundational because it explains how planning detail is structured and governed across the project.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A subsidiary plan is a formal part of the project management plan devoted to one management area. It sets the rules, methods, thresholds, and responsibilities for that area so the team can manage work consistently.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects quickly become difficult to control if planning exists only at a high level. Subsidiary plans create usable management guidance for delivery teams and are especially important on projects with multiple workstreams, compliance needs, external suppliers, or significant risk exposure.

The most common misconception: that a subsidiary plan is just a detailed schedule or an optional attachment. In PMI usage, it is broader than scheduling and is not merely supplemental notes; it is an approved planning component that supports integrated management.

How it shows up in real projects: a project may include a risk management plan defining probability-impact scales, a communications management plan defining reporting cadence, and a scope management plan defining how changes will be evaluated, all under the umbrella of the project management plan.
Contrast: Project management plan — a subsidiary plan is one component plan for a specific domain; the project management plan is the integrated, approved whole.
Example: On an ERP implementation, the project manager maintains a stakeholder engagement plan, risk management plan, and quality management plan as separate subsidiary plans within the overall project management plan.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition, page 239

Core Meaning: A program sponsored and conducted to pursue a subset of goals important to a primary program.
Expansion: A subsidiary program, also called a subprogram, is managed as a program in its own right while supporting the outcomes of a larger program. It is used when part of the overall change is substantial enough to need its own coordinated governance, benefits focus, and component oversight. The primary program aligns and monitors the subsidiary program so its results contribute to the larger strategic intent.
Contrast: Project — a subsidiary program coordinates a related subset of program goals, while a project delivers a specific output or result within temporary constraints.
Example: In a national rail-modernisation program, the signalling upgrade is run as a subsidiary program because it includes multiple interdependent projects, benefits, and transition activities.
Source: The Standard for Program Management, Fourth Edition, glossary entry for subsidiary program; exact page varies by edition formatting

Core Meaning: A dependent activity that logically follows another activity in the project schedule.
Expansion: A successor is the activity that comes later in a dependency relationship. Its timing is linked to a predecessor through a defined logical relationship.
Contrast: Predecessor — a predecessor comes before another activity; a successor comes after it.
Example: In a construction schedule, roof installation is the successor to structural framing.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition Glossary, page 240

Core Meaning: A dependent activity that logically comes after another activity in a schedule.
Expansion: A successor activity is the later activity in a logical relationship. Its timing depends on the predecessor activity according to the defined dependency type.
Contrast: Predecessor activity — a successor activity follows the dependent relationship, while a predecessor activity comes before it.
Example: Concrete curing is the successor activity to concrete pouring in a construction schedule.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, successor activity, page 726

Core Meaning: A schedule activity that aggregates lower-level activities to present a higher-level view of work.
Expansion: A summary activity rolls up related detailed activities into one higher-level schedule line. It is used for communicating structure and status without replacing the detailed tasks underneath.
Contrast: Milestone — a summary activity represents grouped work over time, while a milestone marks a significant point or event with zero duration.
Example: A project schedule shows 'System Testing' as a summary activity that contains unit, integration, performance, and user acceptance testing activities.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, summary activity; exact page varies by edition formatting

Core Meaning: A summary schedule is a schedule presentation that shows major activities, milestones, or summary work packages at a high level rather than detailed task-level planning.
Expansion: It is used to communicate the overall timing of a project or phase to sponsors, customers, and senior stakeholders who do not need detailed activity logic. PMI treats it as a condensed view of the schedule model or schedule baseline rather than a replacement for detailed planning. It is especially useful in governance reporting, executive communication, and stage-gate reviews.
Contrast: Milestone schedule — a summary schedule can include major activities and summary elements as well as milestones, while a milestone schedule shows only key milestones.
Example: For a factory expansion, the project manager presents an executive summary schedule showing design complete, permitting, procurement, construction, commissioning, and go-live dates instead of hundreds of underlying activities.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / prior PMBOK glossary usage for summary schedule

Core Meaning: A cost that has already been incurred and cannot be recovered.
Expansion: Sunk cost refers to money or effort already spent that cannot be changed by current decisions. In project management, it matters because continuation or termination decisions should be based on future costs, benefits, and strategic value rather than past expenditure. Treating sunk cost correctly helps leaders avoid escalating commitment to work that no longer makes sense.
Contrast: Opportunity cost — sunk cost is unrecoverable past spending, while opportunity cost is the value of the best alternative forgone when one option is chosen.
Example: After spending months on a prototype that no longer fits market needs, the steering group evaluates whether to stop the effort based on future return rather than the money already spent.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition Glossary, sunk cost, page not provided in available search results

Core Meaning: An external party that provides products, services, or results needed by a project, program, or portfolio.
Expansion: A supplier is the organisation or party that furnishes contracted goods or services to support delivery. In PMI usage, suppliers are external stakeholders whose performance can directly affect quality, schedule, cost, and risk.
Contrast: Seller — supplier is the broader external source of goods or services, while seller is the contracting party in a specific buyer-seller relationship.
Example: A cloud-platform provider acts as the supplier for a migration project that depends on hosted infrastructure and support services.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, supplier; exact page varies by edition formatting

Core Meaning: The end-to-end network involved in sourcing, producing, moving, and delivering materials, components, or services.
Expansion: A supply chain links the organisations, flows, and dependencies that move needed inputs from source to point of use. In projects, it matters because delays, shortages, transport issues, or quality failures anywhere in that network can affect delivery outcomes. It is especially relevant in construction, manufacturing, infrastructure, and any project relying on external materials or specialised vendors.
Contrast: Logistics — supply chain is the broader network of sourcing and delivery relationships, while logistics focuses on the movement and storage of items within that network.
Example: A hospital-build project manages the supply chain for steel, chillers, medical gas systems, and imported imaging equipment to prevent installation delays.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Supply chain risk is the possibility that disruptions, constraints, or variability in suppliers, logistics, materials, or external networks will affect project objectives.
Expansion: As a project-management concept, it addresses threats and sometimes opportunities arising from sourcing, transportation, dependencies on vendors, and market or geopolitical conditions. PMI discusses these issues within broader project risk management and procurement contexts rather than as a universally formal standalone glossary term. It is most relevant in projects with critical external dependencies, long-lead items, global sourcing, or regulated supply environments.
Contrast: Procurement risk — supply chain risk is broader, covering the full external flow of materials and services beyond the contract transaction itself.
Example: A renewable-energy project identifies that a sole-source inverter supplier faces shipping delays and export restrictions, so the team adds alternate sourcing, buffer stock, and schedule contingency.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The possibility that required goods or services will be unavailable, delayed, disrupted, or fail to meet needed quality or performance.
Expansion: Supply risk is a form of project or procurement risk tied to the reliability and continuity of external supply. It can arise from shortages, transport disruption, geopolitical events, supplier insolvency, single-source dependency, or quality failures. Its impact is often seen in schedule slippage, cost escalation, rework, and inability to complete planned scope.
Contrast: Procurement risk — supply risk focuses on availability and continuity of supplied items or services, while procurement risk is broader and also includes contracting, compliance, and sourcing-process issues.
Example: A semiconductor shortage creates supply risk for an electronics project, forcing the team to redesign around alternate components and resequence testing.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A data-gathering technique that collects information from a sample or population through questions.
Expansion: Surveys are used to gather opinions, preferences, facts, or feedback from stakeholders, users, or team members. In PMI practice, they are a common elicitation and assessment technique when information must be collected from many people efficiently. They can be used to understand needs, test assumptions, or measure satisfaction and outcomes. Their usefulness depends heavily on sound question design and adequate response rates.
Contrast: Interviews — surveys collect standardised input from many people efficiently; interviews allow deeper exploration with individual participants.
Example: A project team sends a short survey to pilot users after a new service rollout to measure satisfaction and identify the most common pain points.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A way of working that enables a team to maintain effective delivery over time without burnout or chronic overextension.
Expansion: In PMI's agile context, sustainable pace means matching work demand to the team's real capacity so delivery remains steady, quality stays acceptable, and people can continue performing effectively. It supports predictable flow, healthier teams, and better long-term value delivery rather than short bursts of output followed by fatigue or rework. The idea is closely associated with agile and adaptive environments where cadence, capacity, and team wellbeing directly affect outcomes.
Contrast: Overtime — sustainable pace is a repeatable delivery rhythm; overtime is a temporary increase in effort that cannot be relied on as a normal operating model.
Example: A product team limits sprint commitments to its observed throughput so it can deliver every iteration without weekend work or rising defect rates.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A collaborative technique in which multiple team members focus together on one high-priority work item to complete it faster and reduce delay.
Expansion: Swarming is commonly used in agile and flow-based environments when finishing a critical item is more valuable than keeping everyone on separate tasks. Instead of maximising individual utilisation, the team temporarily concentrates capacity on shared completion. This helps reduce work in progress, accelerate feedback, and expose blockers quickly.
Contrast: Pair programming — swarming involves several people collaborating on one item more broadly; pair programming is a specific two-person development practice.
Example: When a production defect threatens a release, a developer, tester, business analyst, and UX specialist work together on the same issue until it is resolved and validated.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A technique for examining strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats to understand a situation or option in project and business analysis work.
Expansion: PMI identifies SWOT analysis as a technique used in current state assessment. It helps teams evaluate internal factors, such as strengths and weaknesses, alongside external factors, such as opportunities and threats. In project work, it supports framing a problem or decision before selecting actions, requirements, or solution options.
Contrast: Root cause analysis — SWOT analysis assesses a situation broadly across internal and external factors; root cause analysis investigates why a specific problem is occurring.
Example: A project team performs a SWOT analysis before deciding whether to modernise a legacy platform or replace it with a cloud solution.

Figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='99' y='20' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Helpful</text><text x='217' y='20' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Harmful</text><text x='16' y='66' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 16 66)'>Internal</text><text x='16' y='138' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 16 138)'>External</text><rect x='40' y='28' width='118' height='72' fill='#2F6F5E' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='158' y='28' width='118' height='72' fill='#B5462F' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='40' y='100' width='118' height='72' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='158' y='100' width='118' height='72' fill='#C98A1F' fill-opacity='0.15' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><text x='99' y='68' fill='currentColor' font-size='12' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>Strengths</text><text x='217' y='68' fill='currentColor' font-size='12' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>Weaknesses</text><text x='99' y='140' fill='currentColor' font-size='12' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>Opportunities</text><text x='217' y='140' fill='currentColor' font-size='12' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>Threats</text></svg>
Figure.caption: Internal strengths and weaknesses; external opportunities and threats.

Source: The PMI Guide to Business Analysis, Section 4.2.2.10 SWOT Analysis, p. 72

Core Meaning: The definition and organisation of a system's components, interfaces, and overall structure so it can meet intended requirements.
Expansion: In project and product work, system design refers to determining how a system will be structured and how its parts will interact to achieve required functionality and performance. It typically translates stakeholder and technical requirements into an implementable solution architecture, design specifications, and interface decisions. The concept is most visible in engineering, construction, information technology, and product-development projects where solution structure must be deliberately planned.
Contrast: Requirements — requirements describe what the system must do or constrain; system design defines how the system will be structured to satisfy those requirements.
Example: On a manufacturing automation project, engineers produce the control-system design showing sensors, controllers, network interfaces, and fail-safe logic before build and integration begin.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The degree to which a system operates effectively against its expected functional and nonfunctional measures under real conditions.
Expansion: In project settings, system performance refers to how well a completed or evolving system meets expected levels such as speed, reliability, capacity, accuracy, or stability. It is assessed against defined criteria, operational needs, and acceptance thresholds to determine whether the delivered solution is fit for use. The concept matters particularly in technical and engineering projects where benefits depend not just on delivery of the system, but on how well it actually performs.
Contrast: Project performance — system performance concerns how the delivered solution operates; project performance concerns how the project itself performs against scope, schedule, cost, and similar measures.
Example: After deploying a customer portal, the team verifies that average response times remain within target levels even during peak user traffic.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A test performed on the complete integrated system to evaluate whether it meets specified requirements.
Expansion: System test examines the fully combined system rather than isolated components to confirm that end-to-end behaviour aligns with functional and nonfunctional expectations. In project delivery, it is typically conducted after integration and before final acceptance or release so defects can be identified at the whole-system level. It is especially important where multiple subsystems, interfaces, and operational scenarios must work together reliably.
Contrast: Integration test — integration testing checks interactions among combined components; system testing evaluates the complete integrated system against overall requirements.
Example: Before go-live on an ERP implementation, the team runs system tests covering order entry, inventory updates, invoicing, security roles, and batch processing across the full platform.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An approach to understanding a project or organisational context by examining the relationships and interactions among its parts.
Expansion: Systems thinking looks beyond isolated tasks or components and focuses on how elements influence one another within a larger whole. In PMI practice, it helps practitioners understand dependencies, feedback loops, constraints, and unintended consequences. It is especially important in complex environments where local decisions can create broader effects across portfolios, programmes, projects, and operations.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Systems thinking is a way of viewing work as part of an interconnected system rather than as a set of independent pieces. It asks how structure, relationships, flows, and feedback shape outcomes over time.

Why it matters and where it applies: Projects rarely operate alone; they sit inside business, technical, regulatory, and human systems. Systems thinking improves decisions in complex delivery, transformation, integration, portfolio balancing, and change-heavy environments where second-order effects matter.

The most common misconception: that systems thinking is abstract theory with little day-to-day value. In practice it is highly practical because it helps teams anticipate ripple effects, spot hidden dependencies, and avoid solving one problem by creating another.

How it shows up in real projects: A change to scope may affect architecture, staffing, vendor contracts, training needs, compliance work, and customer adoption at the same time. Teams using systems thinking trace those interactions before making decisions.
Contrast: Reductionist thinking — systems thinking emphasises interrelationships and whole-system effects, while reductionist thinking examines parts in isolation.
Example: A portfolio team evaluating a new enterprise platform considers not only delivery cost and schedule, but also downstream impacts on support teams, data governance, user training, cybersecurity, and dependent projects.
Source: The Standard for Portfolio Management Fourth Edition, page 15

Core Meaning: A capability profile that combines deep expertise in one area with broad working knowledge across related areas.
Expansion: A T-shaped skill set describes someone who can contribute deeply in a specialty while also collaborating effectively across disciplines. In PMI agile guidance, the concept supports flexibility, reduced handoffs, and stronger collaboration in cross-functional teams. It is especially valuable where teams need to adapt quickly and members must help beyond narrow role boundaries. The horizontal breadth improves teamwork, while the vertical depth preserves specialist strength.
Contrast: Generalist — a T-shaped contributor combines breadth with one area of deep expertise; a generalist is broad across areas but may lack the same specialist depth.
Example: On a product team, a tester with strong automation expertise also helps refine requirements, review code, and support release planning.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, p. 154

Core Meaning: Knowledge that is personal, experience-based, and difficult to fully articulate or document.
Expansion: Tacit knowledge consists of know-how, judgment, intuition, and practical insight developed through experience. PMI distinguishes it from explicit knowledge, which can be more easily codified in documents, repositories, and procedures. In project work, tacit knowledge is often transferred through observation, mentoring, collaboration, and shared practice rather than through formal records alone.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Tacit knowledge is the unwritten expertise people carry in their heads, shaped by experience, pattern recognition, and context. It includes practical know-how such as how to navigate a stakeholder, spot early warning signs, or sequence work effectively in a specific environment.

Why it matters and where it applies: many critical project decisions rely on judgment that is not fully captured in templates or lessons-learned files. It matters in estimating, risk identification, stakeholder engagement, technical troubleshooting, and onboarding, especially where local context or specialist expertise strongly affects outcomes.

The most common misconception: that documenting lessons learned fully captures what experts know. Documentation helps, but much high-value knowledge remains embedded in behavior, relationships, and situational judgment that must be shared through interaction.

How it shows up in real projects: a veteran construction manager may know which subcontractor warning signs predict delay, or an experienced product owner may sense when stakeholder resistance will surface despite positive status reports. Teams surface tacit knowledge through peer reviews, shadowing, communities of practice, and retrospective discussion.
Contrast: Explicit knowledge — tacit knowledge is hard to codify and transfer directly; explicit knowledge can be documented, stored, and retrieved more easily.
Example: An experienced scheduler adjusts a dependency plan based on past vendor behavior and local permitting realities that are not captured in the formal schedule guidance.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, page 181

Core Meaning: The deliberate adaptation of a project management approach, governance, methods, and artifacts to fit the context of a specific project.
Expansion: Tailoring is the process of selecting and adjusting project practices so they are appropriate for the work, environment, stakeholders, and level of uncertainty involved. PMI treats it as a core responsibility of effective project delivery rather than an exception to a standard method. It helps teams avoid both under-governing complex work and overburdening simple work with unnecessary process.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Tailoring is the intentional fit-for-purpose design of how a project will be managed. It includes adapting life cycle choices, governance controls, reporting, planning detail, and delivery practices to the needs of the initiative.

Why it matters and where it applies: no single approach works equally well for every project. Tailoring matters in all domains because project size, risk, complexity, regulatory pressure, team capability, and delivery cadence differ across efforts.

The most common misconception: that tailoring means skipping discipline or ignoring standards. In practice, it means applying discipline thoughtfully so that project management effort supports outcomes instead of becoming mechanical overhead.

How it shows up in real projects: a regulated construction project may require formal stage gates and heavy documentation, while a digital product effort may use rolling-wave planning, lightweight decision logs, and iterative delivery within the same organizational framework.
Contrast: Customization — tailoring adapts the project management approach for context; customization more broadly changes features or components of a product, service, or method.
Example: A company uses its standard governance model for all projects, but for a six-week internal automation effort it reduces approval layers, uses a simplified risk register, and replaces monthly status packs with a live dashboard.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Seventh Edition, section 3 Model, Methods, and Artifacts and section 4 Tailoring

Core Meaning: The deliberate growth of people's capabilities so they can perform current roles effectively and build capacity for future organisational needs.
Expansion: In PMI's people-focused view of project work, talent development concerns strengthening knowledge, skills, and abilities through learning, coaching, experience, and career growth. It matters because project outcomes depend heavily on capable teams, effective leadership, and an environment that supports continuous improvement. The concept applies at team, PMO, and organisational levels wherever project capability must be built and sustained.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Talent development is the intentional development of individual and team capability through education, mentoring, stretch assignments, feedback, and practical experience. In project environments, it includes both technical project-management capability and interpersonal or leadership capability.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects are delivered by people, so capability gaps directly affect decision quality, collaboration, delivery speed, and resilience. It applies in project teams, PMOs, transformation efforts, and organisations trying to improve delivery maturity over time.

The most common misconception: that talent development is just formal training. In practice, it is broader and often more effective when it combines learning with coaching, role clarity, communities of practice, and real project experience.

How it shows up in real projects: organisations create development plans for project managers, pair less experienced staff with mentors, rotate people through challenging assignments, and use retrospectives or lessons learned to strengthen future performance.
Contrast: Training — training is one development method; talent development is the broader capability-building system that also includes coaching, mentoring, experience, and career growth.
Example: A PMO creates a development pathway in which new project managers take targeted courses, shadow senior PMs, lead small initiatives, and receive coaching tied to competency assessments.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The planned cost objective established for a product, component, or work effort and used as a benchmark for design, delivery, or control decisions.
Expansion: Target cost is the cost level a team aims to achieve while meeting scope, quality, and performance requirements. In project management practice, it is used to guide estimating, trade-off decisions, and cost control rather than to describe actual cost incurred. It is especially relevant where cost constraints shape design or procurement decisions.
Contrast: Cost baseline — a target cost is an objective or goal for cost performance; a cost baseline is the approved time-phased basis for measuring and controlling project cost.
Example: A product-development project sets a target cost for a new device so engineering and procurement can choose materials and features that keep the offering commercially viable.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A desired date by which a schedule activity or project is intended to finish.
Expansion: A target finish date expresses the planned or desired completion point for work. It is used as a management objective and may differ from calculated schedule dates such as early finish or late finish. Teams use it to align expectations, monitor progress, and compare actual performance against intent.
Contrast: Finish date — a target finish date is the desired completion date, while a finish date is the calculated or recorded completion point in the schedule.
Example: The team sets a target finish date of 30 September for user acceptance testing so the release can still occur in October.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — target finish date

Core Meaning: A desired date by which a schedule activity or project is intended to start.
Expansion: A target start date identifies when work is intended to begin. It is a planning objective rather than a calculated schedule result, and it helps coordinate dependencies, resources, and stakeholder expectations. Project teams use it to align readiness and timing before execution begins.
Contrast: Start date — a target start date is the desired start point, while a start date is the actual or calculated start point in the schedule.
Example: The procurement team sets a target start date of 1 March for vendor onboarding so contract approval can be completed beforehand.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — target start date

Core Meaning: The defined future condition that a project, program, or change initiative is intended to achieve.
Expansion: Target state describes the desired future operating, process, organisational, or technical condition after change is implemented. It helps align scope, requirements, transition planning, and benefit expectations around a clear end condition. In PMI-aligned practice it is commonly used in transformation, business analysis, and benefits-focused change work.
Contrast: Current state — the target state defines the desired future condition; the current state describes how work or capability exists today.
Example: A process-improvement project defines the target state as a fully digital approval workflow with standard cycle times and role-based routing across all regions.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A unit of work performed to complete part of an activity, deliverable, or planned effort.
Expansion: A task is a smaller piece of work that contributes to completing project work. In project practice, tasks are used to break work into manageable components for assignment, estimation, sequencing, and tracking. They help teams clarify who does what and when, especially in detailed planning and execution. The term is widely used in practice even when PMI standards more often formalise related terms such as activity or work package.
Contrast: Activity — an activity is a schedule-level element of work that may encompass multiple tasks; a task is typically a smaller practical unit used to carry out that work.
Example: For a training rollout, tasks include drafting the facilitator guide, booking the room, and sending attendee invitations.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A visual representation of the progress of planned work that allows everyone to see the status of tasks.
Expansion: A task board makes work visible by showing tasks and their current status in a shared visual format. PMI describes it as a way for everyone to see the status of the tasks, and notes its connection to a kanban board. It is commonly used to support coordination, transparency, and flow in team-based work.
Contrast: Kanban board — a task board is the general visual display of task status, while a kanban board is a specific implementation used to manage flow.
Example: A software team uses columns for To Do, In Progress, Review, and Done so the whole team can see where each task stands.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, task board

Core Meaning: A team is a set of individuals who work interdependently to achieve a common objective.
Expansion: In PMI usage, a team is more than a reporting grouping because its members coordinate effort, share accountability, and combine complementary skills to produce outcomes. In projects, the project team may include people with different roles, disciplines, and authority relationships, but they are aligned around delivering the project's objectives and intended value. Team effectiveness directly influences delivery speed, quality, stakeholder collaboration, and the ability to respond to change.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A team is an interdependent group formed to accomplish shared goals through coordinated effort. In project management, this usually means the people who perform, support, and guide the work needed to create project outputs and outcomes.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects are delivered through people, so team structure, clarity, trust, and collaboration affect performance in every domain, whether predictive, agile, or hybrid. Strong teams improve decision making, problem solving, knowledge sharing, and resilience under uncertainty.

The most common misconception: that a team is simply any collection of people assigned to the same project. In practice, a true team depends on shared purpose, mutual accountability, and active coordination rather than mere co-assignment.

How it shows up in real projects: cross-functional members plan work together, resolve dependencies, surface risks, adapt priorities, and coordinate with stakeholders to deliver increments, milestones, or final deliverables.
Contrast: Working group — a team is interdependent and jointly accountable for shared outcomes; a working group may share information but members mainly complete individual tasks.
Example: On an ERP implementation, business analysts, developers, testers, a change lead, and a project manager coordinate daily to configure modules, manage dependencies, and deliver a staged rollout.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Team Performance Domain, pp. 241-267

Core Meaning: The deliberate strengthening of trust, cohesion, and collaboration so a project team can perform more effectively.
Expansion: Team building is the intentional development of relationships, norms, and working habits that improve how people perform together on a project. In PMI practice, it is not limited to informal bonding activities; it also includes actions such as clarifying roles, setting ground rules, resolving tensions, and creating shared commitment to goals. Strong team building improves communication, psychological safety, and accountability, which directly affects delivery performance. It is relevant across predictive, agile, and hybrid environments because projects depend on coordinated human effort.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Team building is the set of actions used to turn a collection of individuals into a functioning project team. It includes forming shared expectations, strengthening trust, and improving the way people communicate and solve problems together.

Why it matters and where it applies: Projects succeed through coordinated execution, so weak team dynamics quickly become schedule, quality, and stakeholder problems. Team building matters in virtual teams, cross-functional teams, newly formed teams, and teams under pressure, where alignment and trust do not happen automatically.

The most common misconception: that team building means only social events or morale activities. In practice, the most effective team building is often built into the work itself through role clarity, working agreements, constructive feedback, and conflict management.

How it shows up in real projects: kickoff workshops, ground rules, team charters, retrospectives, collaborative planning sessions, and manager actions that remove friction and reinforce healthy behaviours.
Contrast: Team development — team building focuses on cohesion and ways of working together; team development is broader and also includes growth in capability and performance over time.
Example: At project kickoff, the project manager facilitates a workshop where the team defines decision rules, communication norms, escalation paths, and shared expectations for collaboration.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A document created by the team that describes its values, agreements, and ways of working together.
Expansion: A team charter is a working agreement that makes explicit how the team will collaborate, communicate, make decisions, and handle conflict. It gives the team a shared reference point for behavioural norms and operating expectations, especially in cross-functional or newly formed groups. PMI presents it as a practical aid for strengthening team performance rather than as the formal authorization for the project itself.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A team charter is a concise, team-owned document that captures the norms and agreements that shape day-to-day collaboration. It often covers meeting expectations, communication channels, decision rules, conflict handling, and shared commitments.

Why it matters and where it applies: teams perform more consistently when expectations are discussed early instead of assumed. A team charter is especially useful when the team is newly formed, distributed, cross-functional, or working in a fast-changing environment where misalignment can slow delivery.

The most common misconception: that a team charter is the same as a project charter. A project charter formally authorizes the project and assigns authority, while a team charter focuses on how people will work together once the project is underway.

How it shows up in real projects: teams establish response-time norms, define how priorities are clarified, agree on escalation paths, and revisit the charter during retrospectives or phase transitions when collaboration starts to drift.
Contrast: Project charter — a team charter defines how the team will work together; a project charter formally authorizes the project and names authority.
Example: A hybrid product team agrees in its team charter to use one shared backlog, raise blockers within 24 hours, make design decisions by consent unless a deadline forces escalation, and revisit the agreement at the end of each release cycle.
Source: The Standard for Project Management and A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, section 2.4.3 Team Charter, page 36

Core Meaning: The coordinated way team members work together to share information, solve problems, and deliver project outcomes.
Expansion: Team collaboration is the practical interaction through which project team members align work, communicate, and integrate their contributions. It supports faster issue resolution, stronger commitment, and better-quality outcomes across predictive, agile, and hybrid environments. PMI treats collaboration as a key enabler of team performance rather than as a standalone process artifact.
Contrast: Communication — communication is the exchange of information; collaboration is the broader joint work of making decisions and producing results together.
Example: A cross-functional project team uses daily coordination, shared visual boards, and joint planning sessions to resolve dependencies between design, testing, and operations.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The process of improving team members' competencies, interaction, and overall team environment to enhance project performance.
Expansion: Team development focuses on strengthening how people work together as well as improving individual capability. In PMI project management practice, it includes actions that build trust, clarify collaboration, improve communication, and raise competence over time. Its purpose is to help the team perform more effectively and sustain productive working relationships throughout the project.
Contrast: Manage Team — Team development is about improving capability and team effectiveness; Manage Team is the broader process of tracking performance, resolving issues, and taking corrective action.
Example: A project manager introduces peer learning sessions, team working agreements, and retrospective actions so a newly formed cross-functional team can collaborate more effectively during delivery.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition / not enough verified page detail available from retrieval

Core Meaning: The pattern of interpersonal behaviours, relationships, and interactions that influences how a project team functions.
Expansion: Team dynamics describes how trust, conflict, influence, motivation, and working norms shape team behaviour and effectiveness. Strong dynamics can improve decision making, learning, and delivery performance, while poor dynamics can create friction, delay, and disengagement. PMI project leadership guidance treats this as an important consideration in developing and leading teams.
Contrast: Team development — team dynamics refers to the interaction patterns within a team; team development is the deliberate effort to improve the team's capability and cohesion.
Example: A project manager notices tension between engineering and procurement and facilitates working agreements to improve team dynamics before the conflict delays delivery.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A person who coordinates and guides the day-to-day work of a team or workstream to support delivery of project objectives.
Expansion: A team lead typically provides technical or functional direction, helps organise work, and supports issue resolution within a team. The role may exist within projects, but it is not a distinct formal PMI glossary concept with a single standard definition.
Contrast: Project manager — a team lead usually guides a specific team or discipline; a project manager is accountable for integrating the full project across scope, schedule, cost, risk, and stakeholders.
Example: In an infrastructure project, the network team lead coordinates engineers' tasks and escalates cross-team dependencies to the project manager.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Agreed behaviours and working expectations that guide how a team collaborates and makes decisions.
Expansion: Team norms define the practical rules a team follows for communication, meetings, responsiveness, conflict handling, and accountability. They help create predictability and reduce friction, especially when team members come from different functions, locations, or delivery approaches. PMI discusses them as part of team working agreements and team chartering rather than as a standalone core glossary term.
Contrast: Team charter — team norms are the behavioural expectations the team follows, while a team charter is the broader document that can capture those agreements.
Example: A project team agrees to respond to internal messages within one business day, raise blockers in the daily sync, and keep meeting decisions in a shared log.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The Team Performance Domain addresses the people, interactions, culture, and capabilities needed for a project team to deliver value.
Expansion: In PMI's performance-domain model, the Team Performance Domain focuses on how team members and other contributors work together to achieve project outcomes. It includes shared ownership, leadership, collaboration, emotional intelligence, decision making, and the development of a supportive team environment. Rather than prescribing a single structure, it emphasises principles and desired outcomes that help teams perform effectively across predictive, adaptive, and hybrid approaches.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The Team Performance Domain is one of the project performance domains described in PMI's principle-based standard. It concerns team culture, structure, leadership, communication, and the interpersonal conditions that enable effective delivery.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects succeed through people as much as through plans. This domain matters in any setting where coordination, trust, accountability, and learning affect delivery, from traditional capital projects to agile product teams.

The most common misconception: that it is only about team morale or human-resources topics. In practice it is directly tied to execution because poor collaboration, unclear roles, or weak leadership quickly turn into schedule, quality, and risk problems.

How it shows up in real projects: establishing working agreements, clarifying decision rights, coaching team members, resolving conflict, building psychological safety, and adapting leadership style to the project's context and constraints.
Contrast: Stakeholder Performance Domain — the Team Performance Domain focuses on people doing the project work; the Stakeholder Performance Domain focuses on individuals and groups affected by or able to influence the project.
Example: A project manager on a hybrid ERP implementation sets team working agreements, defines decision paths across business and IT leads, and uses regular retrospectives to improve collaboration between vendor and internal staff.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition — Team Performance Domain section, pp. 33-40

Core Meaning: The human resources assigned to or needed by the project team to perform project work and produce deliverables.
Expansion: Team resources refers to the people who carry out project activities, including project team members and, depending on context, subject matter experts and support roles. In PMI usage, this concept is central to estimating resource needs, acquiring people, developing the team, and managing performance. It connects planning decisions about skills, availability, roles, and responsibilities to execution outcomes.
Contrast: Physical resources — team resources are people and their capabilities; physical resources are equipment, materials, facilities, and infrastructure.
Example: For a construction project, the manager plans team resources such as site engineers, schedulers, safety specialists, procurement staff, and supervisors needed at different phases.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Project Resource Management chapter and glossary; exact page citation should be verified against the edition in use

Core Meaning: The way project team roles, responsibilities, reporting relationships, and collaboration patterns are arranged to deliver the work.
Expansion: Team structure describes how a project team is organised, including role design, authority distribution, reporting lines, and how different disciplines work together. In PMI practice, team structure affects decision speed, communication flow, accountability, and the team's ability to respond to change. It is shaped by the delivery approach, organisational context, and the complexity of the work, so the right structure varies by project. Good team structure supports clarity and coordination without creating unnecessary handoffs or bottlenecks.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Team structure is the design of the team as a working system, not just a list of names and titles. It defines who is involved, how responsibilities are grouped, how authority is shared, and how work moves across the team.

Why it matters and where it applies: Structure influences how quickly issues are resolved, how information flows, and whether specialists can work as an integrated team. It matters in all projects, but especially in matrix organisations, agile product delivery, vendor-heavy efforts, and complex programmes where unclear interfaces create delay and rework.

The most common misconception: that team structure is only an HR or org-chart issue. In projects, it is also a delivery design choice that directly shapes collaboration, responsiveness, and execution risk.

How it shows up in real projects: selecting a cross-functional team instead of siloed functional support, defining product owner and scrum master responsibilities, setting up integrated design-and-build squads, or creating clear workstream leads in a large transformation.
Contrast: Organisational structure — organisational structure describes the wider enterprise arrangement of authority and reporting; team structure describes how the project team itself is set up to do the work.
Example: For a digital transformation project, the sponsor creates a cross-functional team with business, architecture, development, testing, and change leads who work through a single integrated backlog rather than separate department queues.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The collaborative effort of a group working together toward a shared goal.
Expansion: Teamwork combines individual skills through communication, trust, and coordination to achieve more than members could alone. It is fostered by clear goals, shared norms, and psychological safety.
Contrast: Collaboration — teamwork is the joint working of a defined team; collaboration is the broader act of working together, including across teams.
Example: Daily coordination and shared ownership help the team resolve a blocker quickly through teamwork.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; defined for glossary completeness

Core Meaning: The implied future cost of rework created when a team chooses a limited or expedient technical solution instead of a more robust one.
Expansion: Technical debt accumulates when teams defer code quality, architecture, testing, or design improvements in order to move faster in the short term. PMI recognizes it as a practical concern in iterative and technology-enabled delivery because it can reduce future velocity, increase defects, and raise change costs. Managing it requires visibility, conscious tradeoff decisions, and periodic repayment through refactoring or remediation work.
Contrast: Defect — a defect is a flaw needing correction in a deliverable; technical debt is the broader future burden created by expedient technical choices, whether or not a visible defect exists.
Example: A software team hardcodes business rules to meet a release date, then later spends several sprints redesigning the rules engine so new products can be added without repeated code changes.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, glossary Technical Debt

Core Meaning: The degree to which project work and deliverables meet the required technical specifications and performance criteria.
Expansion: Technical performance refers to how well the product, service, or result performs against defined technical requirements. It is monitored to confirm that the project is producing outputs that satisfy design intent, quality expectations, and acceptance criteria. PMI includes technical performance analysis as a way to compare actual technical results with planned or required performance.
Contrast: Technical debt — technical performance measures current conformance to requirements, while technical debt reflects future rework or compromise created by expedient design choices.
Example: A software release is tested to confirm response time, throughput, and error rates meet the agreed performance targets before acceptance.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition / Process Groups A Practice Guide — Technical performance analysis, p. 205

Core Meaning: Technical performance measures are indicators that track whether a product, system, or project deliverable is achieving required technical parameters over time.
Expansion: These measures compare actual technical achievement against planned specifications or thresholds, such as weight, speed, capacity, reliability, or response time. They help teams detect whether solution performance is drifting away from requirements before the issue becomes a late-stage quality or acceptance problem. In practice they are especially useful in engineering, construction, defense, technology, and product-development projects where technical compliance must be monitored progressively.
Contrast: Key performance indicator — a technical performance measure tracks achievement of technical characteristics of the deliverable; a KPI may track broader business or project performance.
Example: On an aerospace project, the team tracks engine temperature tolerance, fuel efficiency, and structural weight against design thresholds at each prototype review.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition — Monitor Communications and Monitor Risks glossary usage; also formal term in PMI Lexicon, exact page varies by edition

Core Meaning: A defined systematic procedure used to perform an activity and produce a product, result, or service.
Expansion: A technique is a specific way of carrying out work. It is narrower than a process or methodology and focuses on how an activity is performed.
Contrast: Tool — a technique is the procedure used to do the work, while a tool is the instrument or aid used in applying it.
