Example: A project manager uses brainstorming as a technique to identify risks during a workshop.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Technique

Core Meaning: A platform, system, tool, or technical capability used to create, enable, or support project deliverables and project work.
Expansion: In project management, technology refers to the technical means through which work is performed or value is delivered, including products, infrastructure, software, equipment, and enabling systems. It matters because project approaches, risks, skills, constraints, and stakeholder expectations are often shaped by the technologies involved. PMI treats technology as a contextual factor rather than a universal process artifact, so it is important but not consistently a formal glossary term across standards.
Contrast: Tool — technology is the broader technical environment or capability; a tool is a specific instrument or application used within it.
Example: A hospital-implementation project depends on cloud hosting, API integrations, and cybersecurity controls as core technologies that shape scope, schedule, and risk responses.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An analysis of whether the needed technology exists in the organisation or can be acquired and operated successfully.
Expansion: Technology feasibility assesses whether a proposed solution can be supported by available or obtainable technology. It considers whether the organisation has, or can realistically gain, the technical capability to implement and sustain the solution. This helps decision makers avoid committing to options that are attractive in concept but impractical in execution.
Contrast: Time feasibility — technology feasibility tests whether the solution is technically achievable, while time feasibility tests whether it can be delivered within the needed timeframe.
Example: Before approving a cloud analytics initiative, the team assesses whether existing infrastructure, security controls, and support skills can handle the platform.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Technology feasibility

Core Meaning: Partially completed documents in a predefined format used to collect, organise, and present information.
Expansion: Templates provide a standard structure for recurring project documents. They improve consistency and reduce the effort needed to create common artefacts.
Contrast: Checklist — a template provides a structured document format, while a checklist provides items to verify or complete.
Example: A PMO issues a risk register template so every project captures risks using the same fields and layout.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Templates

Core Meaning: A project is a temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result.
Expansion: Temporary endeavor is one of PMI's defining characteristics of a project. It means a project has a definite beginning and end, even when the duration is long or the end date evolves through approved planning and control. The idea separates projects from ongoing operations and helps frame governance, staffing, funding, and success criteria around a bounded effort.
Deep Dive:
What it is: In PMI usage, temporary means the work is not indefinite. The project is initiated for a specific purpose and concludes when objectives are achieved, the effort is terminated, or the work is transitioned.

Why it matters and where it applies: this distinction is central to deciding whether an effort should be managed as a project, program, portfolio component, or operational process. It affects how leaders define scope, establish timelines, allocate resources, and plan closure and benefits transition.

The most common misconception: that temporary means short. A project can last years and still be temporary if it has a defined lifecycle and an intended endpoint.

How it shows up in real projects: a factory expansion, ERP rollout, or regulatory compliance implementation has a chartered start, planned phases, formal acceptance criteria, and closure activities, after which ongoing support moves into operations.
Contrast: Operations — a temporary endeavor is bounded and ends; operations are ongoing and repetitive.
Example: A company runs a 14-month project to implement a new procurement platform, then closes the project once deployment, training, and transition to support are complete.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, section 1.1; also reflected in PMI Lexicon entry for project

Core Meaning: A test plan is a document that describes the scope, approach, resources, schedule, and criteria for testing a product or deliverable.
Expansion: It defines what will be tested, how testing will be performed, who is responsible, and what conditions must be met for acceptance or completion. In project management it supports quality planning and control by making verification activities explicit rather than ad hoc. Test plans are especially visible in software, systems, product-development, and regulated environments, but the concept applies wherever deliverables require structured validation.
Contrast: Quality management plan — a test plan focuses specifically on testing activities for deliverables; a quality management plan governs the broader approach to quality policies, standards, assurance, and control.
Example: For a payroll-system implementation, the team creates a test plan covering unit, integration, user acceptance, data-migration, and regression testing, with entry and exit criteria for each phase.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A test-first approach that defines requirements as test cases before constructing the solution.
Expansion: Test-driven development begins by writing tests that express the expected behaviour of a solution before the solution itself is built. The team then develops just enough functionality to pass those tests and refactors as needed. This approach improves requirement clarity, supports quality, and creates a growing regression test suite as development progresses.
Contrast: Behavior-driven development — test-driven development centres on writing tests first to drive implementation, while behavior-driven development expresses expected behaviour in a more business-readable form.
Example: A software team writes automated tests for password reset rules before implementing the reset feature.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Test-driven development

Core Meaning: The evaluation of a product, deliverable, or component to determine whether it meets specified requirements and works as intended.
Expansion: Testing is a quality-related activity used to verify whether deliverables conform to defined requirements, acceptance criteria, or performance expectations. In project management it commonly appears in product development, implementation, transition, and validation work. PMI treats it as part of assuring and controlling quality rather than as a substitute for clear requirements or stakeholder acceptance.
Contrast: Inspection — testing is a specific evaluative technique to check behaviour or performance, while inspection is the broader examination of a work product for compliance.
Example: Before a new payroll system goes live, the project team executes user acceptance testing to confirm pay rules, approvals, and reports function correctly.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A body of knowledge focused on improving the system-level performance of an organization, end-to-end process, project portfolio, or project.
Expansion: Theory of constraints holds that every system has at least one limiting factor that governs its overall performance. Instead of trying to optimize every activity equally, it directs attention to the current constraint because improving that point usually improves the whole system. In PMI usage, it is closely associated with flow-based thinking and with critical chain applications in project environments.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Theory of constraints is a management approach that identifies the factor that most limits overall system performance and then manages work around that limit. The aim is to improve the throughput of the whole system rather than the local efficiency of isolated parts.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects often slow down because of one scarce resource, one approval gate, or one persistent dependency rather than because every task is equally problematic. This makes theory of constraints useful in schedule planning, resource management, portfolio flow, and environments where too much simultaneous work overwhelms a bottleneck.

The most common misconception: that any delay or difficult task is automatically the constraint. In practice, the true constraint is the element that most restricts end-to-end performance at that moment, and it can move once the current bottleneck is relieved.

How it shows up in real projects: a team may identify a constrained testing group, protect it from interruptions, sequence work to keep it productive, and limit work in progress upstream so the queue does not become chaotic.
Contrast: Critical path — theory of constraints focuses on the system bottleneck that limits overall flow, while critical path focuses on the longest path of dependent activities that determines schedule duration.
Example: A release program is repeatedly delayed by a single cybersecurity review team, so the project manager restructures handoffs and limits parallel submissions to keep that team focused on the highest-value items first.
Source: The Standard for Project Management and A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, glossary entry for theory of constraints; confirm exact page in your edition

Core Meaning: A type of risk that would have a negative effect on one or more project objectives if it occurs.
Expansion: In PMI risk language, a threat is the downside form of uncertainty. It represents a possible event or condition that could harm scope, schedule, cost, quality, or other objectives. Identifying threats enables teams to choose response strategies such as avoid, mitigate, transfer, accept, or escalate.
Contrast: Opportunity — a threat is a risk with negative effect, while an opportunity is a risk with positive effect.
Example: A supplier bankruptcy is logged as a threat because it could delay material delivery and cause schedule and cost impacts on the project.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition Glossary, page 722

Core Meaning: A planned action taken to address a negative risk and reduce its probability or impact.
Expansion: Threat response is part of risk management and focuses on dealing with risks that could harm project objectives. PMI identifies common strategies such as avoid, mitigate, transfer, accept, and escalate. The purpose is to choose a response that is proportionate to the threat and aligned with project priorities.
Contrast: Opportunity response — threat response addresses downside risk, while opportunity response addresses upside risk.
Example: A project team mitigates a supplier-delay threat by qualifying a backup vendor and adding schedule contingency.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition / Process Groups A Practice Guide — Threats, strategies for, p. 203-204

Core Meaning: An estimating technique that uses three values to represent uncertainty in cost or duration.
Expansion: Three-point estimating uses optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic values to improve an estimate under uncertainty. It helps teams move beyond false precision from a single-number estimate. PMI places it within estimation practice for schedule and cost planning.
Contrast: Single-point estimating — three-point estimating models uncertainty with a range of values, while single-point estimating gives only one value.
Example: A project team estimates an integration task at 4 days optimistic, 7 days most likely, and 12 days pessimistic before setting the planned duration.

Source: PMBOK Guide — Three-point estimating, page 44

Core Meaning: A predefined value, level, or point that signals when action, escalation, or a decision is required.
Expansion: A threshold defines acceptable limits for performance or conditions such as cost, schedule, quality, or risk exposure. When actual or forecast results cross that limit, the project team typically investigates, responds, or escalates according to governance rules. Thresholds make control more objective by linking management attention to agreed trigger points rather than informal judgment alone.
Contrast: Tolerance — a threshold is the specific trigger point for action; tolerance is the permitted degree of variation that may exist around a target or baseline.
Example: A project sets a cost threshold of 10 percent variance from the approved budget, and any forecast beyond that point must be escalated to the steering committee.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, glossary Threshold

Core Meaning: The number of items passing through a process.
Expansion: Throughput measures how much work a system completes over a defined period. It is commonly used in flow-based delivery to understand capacity and performance trends.
Contrast: Cycle time — throughput measures how many items are completed in a period, while cycle time measures how long a single item takes to move through the process.
Example: A service desk tracks that it completed 38 approved change requests in one month and uses that throughput trend to forecast near-term delivery capacity.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A visual chart that shows how many work items are completed over a period of time, helping teams understand delivery rate and flow.
Expansion: A throughput chart tracks the number of items passing through a process over time. In PMI-aligned flow and agile contexts, it is used to inspect whether delivery is stable, to spot trends in output, and to support forecasting. It is a practical performance view rather than a formal PMI glossary entry.
Contrast: Cycle time chart — a throughput chart shows how many items are completed over time, while a cycle time chart shows how long individual items take to move through the process.
Example: A Kanban team reviews weekly throughput and sees that completed work drops whenever a specialist approval step is added, prompting a workflow change.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Time in project management is the duration framework within which activities, milestones, and delivery commitments are planned, sequenced, and controlled.
Expansion: Time is a core planning and control dimension of project work because projects operate within finite durations and expected completion dates. PMI typically addresses it through schedule-related concepts such as activity duration, milestones, sequencing, and deadlines rather than as a standalone glossary term. In practice, time influences prioritisation, resource use, risk exposure, and trade-off decisions across the project lifecycle.
Contrast: Schedule — time is the underlying duration dimension; the schedule is the structured model and baseline used to manage it.
Example: In a data-center migration, the available outage window of eight hours becomes the critical time factor driving sequencing, rehearsals, and contingency planning.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A fixed period of time allocated for an activity, event, or iteration, after which the work stops or is reviewed.
Expansion: A time box limits how long a team spends on a task or cycle so that work stays focused and decisions are made within a known window. It is commonly used in adaptive and iterative approaches to encourage discipline, feedback, and regular inspection. PMI treats timeboxing as a practical planning and control technique rather than a separate formal deliverable.
Contrast: Deadline — a time box limits the duration of the work itself, while a deadline is the latest acceptable completion date.
Example: A product team uses a two-week time box for each iteration and reviews the increment at the end of every cycle.
Source: Agile Practice Guide — Timeboxing, p. 126

Core Meaning: A time constraint is a limiting schedule condition that restricts when work can start, finish, or be completed.
Expansion: A time constraint narrows the schedule flexibility available to the project team by imposing dates, windows, deadlines, dependencies, or regulatory timing requirements. It matters because constrained time reduces float, increases coordination pressure, and often drives trade-offs among scope, cost, quality, and risk. PMI commonly treats this through schedule constraints and schedule model logic rather than as a universally separate glossary entry.
Contrast: Deadline — a time constraint is any limiting timing condition; a deadline is one specific required completion point within that broader category.
Example: A construction project must finish noisy demolition work before a city permit window closes, making the permit date a binding time constraint.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Seventh Edition, glossary term constraint; schedule-related treatment also appears in Practice Standard for Scheduling

Core Meaning: A time-and-materials contract is a type of contract that pays the seller for actual labor time and materials used, typically with agreed rates and terms.
Expansion: In project management, a time-and-materials contract blends features of fixed-price and cost-reimbursable arrangements by compensating the seller for actual effort and materials consumed. It is often used when the scope cannot be fully defined at the outset but work must begin before all details are known. Because cost can grow if the work expands, buyers usually pair it with rate schedules, oversight, and sometimes not-to-exceed limits.
Contrast: Cost-reimbursable contract — a time-and-materials contract pays agreed labor rates plus materials, while cost-reimbursable arrangements reimburse allowable actual costs with a fee structure.
Example: A company hires a specialist integration vendor at agreed hourly rates plus hardware pass-through costs to support a system rollout while final interface requirements are still emerging.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Seventh Edition / Process Groups: A Practice Guide, procurement contract types entry; exact page citation requires source verification

Core Meaning: A fixed period of time allocated to an activity or increment of work, with the end date held constant.
Expansion: A timebox constrains work within a set duration so teams focus on completing the most valuable feasible scope by a known deadline. It is common in agile ways of working for iterations, planning events, workshops, and spikes. By fixing time and adjusting scope as needed, it improves predictability, decision speed, and feedback cadence.
Contrast: Milestone — a timebox is a fixed duration for performing work; a milestone is a zero-duration marker that indicates a significant point or event.
Example: A Scrum team runs a two-week sprint and commits only to the user stories it believes can be completed within that fixed window.
Source: Agile Practice Guide, glossary Timebox

Core Meaning: A measure of the cost performance needed on the remaining work to meet a specified management target.
Expansion: To-complete performance index, or TCPI, shows the cost efficiency required from now forward rather than the efficiency already achieved. It is calculated against a target such as budget at completion or estimate at completion. Project managers use it to judge whether the remaining work must be performed at a realistic or unrealistic rate of cost efficiency.
Contrast: Cost Performance Index — TCPI shows the cost efficiency still required on remaining work, while CPI shows the cost efficiency achieved so far.
Example: After several overruns, the project manager calculates TCPI to determine whether the team can still finish within the approved budget target.
Source: PMBOK Guide — To-complete performance index, pages 206-210

Core Meaning: The permissible variation in a project objective that is acceptable before escalation is required.
Expansion: Tolerance sets the acceptable range of deviation around a target for variables such as cost, schedule, scope, or quality. It helps distinguish routine variation from exception conditions that need higher-level attention. In practice it supports governance by clarifying when the project manager can act within delegated authority and when sponsor or governance involvement is needed.
Contrast: Threshold — tolerance is the allowed range of variation; a threshold is the specific limit that triggers action or escalation.
Example: A sponsor sets a schedule tolerance of plus or minus 10 days, allowing the project manager to recover small delays without formal escalation unless the forecast exceeds that range.
Source: The Standard for Project Management and A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition Glossary, page 243

Core Meaning: A tool is a tangible aid or instrument used to perform an activity, while a technique is a systematic procedure employed to accomplish a task or produce an output in project management.
Expansion: PMI uses 'tools and techniques' to describe the practical means by which project work is carried out, analysed, or controlled. Tools may include software, templates, or physical aids, whereas techniques include methods such as brainstorming, decomposition, or variance analysis. In practice, project management guidance often presents them together because project managers apply both to transform inputs into outputs within a process or performance activity.
Contrast: Methodology — tools and techniques are specific means used to perform work; a methodology is a broader system of practices, principles, and governance.
Example: A project manager uses a scheduling application as the tool and critical path method as the technique to develop and analyse the project schedule.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Sixth Edition, Section 1.2.3, p. 25

Core Meaning: A graphical display used in sensitivity analysis to show which variables have the greatest effect on an outcome.
Expansion: A tornado diagram ranks uncertain variables by their influence on a result such as project cost or duration. The longest bars appear at the top, making the highest-impact drivers easy to identify. PMI places it within quantitative risk analysis and sensitivity analysis practice.
Contrast: Cause-and-effect diagram — a tornado diagram ranks uncertainty drivers by impact on an outcome, while a cause-and-effect diagram organises possible causes of a problem.
Example: A team analyses a construction estimate and uses a tornado diagram to identify that labour rates and steel prices drive the largest variation in total cost.
