Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / Practice Standard for Work Breakdown Structures, Third Edition — Work Breakdown Structure

Core Meaning: A document that provides detailed deliverable, activity, and scheduling information about each component in the work breakdown structure.
Expansion: The WBS dictionary supplements the work breakdown structure by adding detail for each component. It can include descriptions, assumptions, acceptance criteria, responsible parties, milestones, and other planning information needed to manage the work. PMI treats it as part of the scope baseline and a key companion to the WBS for planning and control.
Contrast: Work breakdown structure — the WBS shows the hierarchical scope breakdown; the WBS dictionary adds the detailed information for each component.
Example: For a data-center migration, the WBS dictionary defines each work package with scope notes, acceptance criteria, and schedule milestones.
Source: The Standard for Business Analysis, WBS Dictionary, page 417

Core Meaning: A matrix organizational structure in which the functional manager has more authority than the project manager.
Expansion: In a weak matrix, the project manager has limited formal authority and often serves mainly as a coordinator or expediter. Functional managers control most resources, priorities, and staff decisions, which can constrain project speed and decision-making. PMI uses the term as one end of the matrix spectrum, contrasted with balanced and strong matrix structures.
Contrast: Strong matrix — a weak matrix leaves authority primarily with functional managers, while a strong matrix gives the project manager substantially greater authority over resources and decisions.
Example: In an IT department with a weak matrix structure, the project manager coordinates a system upgrade but depends on functional managers to assign engineers and approve schedule changes.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, organizational structure type weak matrix, exact page not confirmed

Core Meaning: A progress measurement technique that assigns weighted values to predefined milestones so earned value can be credited as interim accomplishments are completed.
Expansion: The weighted milestone method measures performance for longer-duration work by assigning portions of value to specific milestone completions within a work package or control account. PMI uses it in earned value management when taking credit only at full completion would hide meaningful interim progress. It improves visibility while still tying progress to objectively verifiable events.
Contrast: Percent complete method — weighted milestone method credits progress only when defined milestones are achieved, while percent complete estimates progress as a proportion of total work.
Example: On a system integration package, a team assigns 20 percent to design approval, 30 percent to build completion, 30 percent to test completion, and 20 percent to client acceptance for earned value credit.
Source: Practice Standard for Earned Value Management, weighted milestone method, page 167

Core Meaning: A technique used to evaluate how different assumptions, events, or conditions could affect project outcomes.
Expansion: What-If-Scenario Analysis tests hypothetical situations to understand their effect on schedule, cost, resources, or other objectives. PMI includes it as a schedule analysis technique for exploring alternatives and possible responses before decisions are made. It is valuable when a team needs to understand the consequences of uncertainty or proposed changes.
Contrast: Sensitivity analysis — What-If-Scenario Analysis tests specific hypothetical situations, while sensitivity analysis examines which variables drive the greatest impact.
Example: A scheduler models the effect of a two-week supplier delay and compares whether resequencing work or adding resources would better protect the target finish date.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, page 222

Core Meaning: A consensus-based estimating technique that combines expert judgment, structured discussion, and iterative refinement.
Expansion: Wideband Delphi brings multiple experts together to estimate work while reducing bias and improving convergence on a shared view. PMI references it as a variation of the Delphi technique used in estimating and planning. It is especially useful when uncertainty is high and a single expert estimate would be too fragile.
Contrast: Delphi Technique — Wideband Delphi adds structured group discussion and iterative feedback to the basic Delphi approach.
Example: A software team estimates a complex integration feature by having several engineers estimate independently, discuss differences, and then re-estimate until the range narrows.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, page 416

Core Meaning: A defined time period during which a task, event, or opportunity can occur or be scheduled.
Expansion: A window is a bounded time frame in which work or access is possible. In projects it commonly appears in scheduling, deployment, testing, maintenance, and stakeholder availability constraints.
Contrast: Milestone — a window is an allowable time period, while a milestone is a specific significant point or event in time.
Example: A production release must be deployed during a Saturday night maintenance window when business systems are offline.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A constraint on the amount of work allowed to be in progress at a given time so flow can be managed and bottlenecks exposed.
Expansion: A WIP limit, or work-in-progress limit, caps how many items a person, team, or workflow stage may handle simultaneously. In Kanban-based systems, it helps reduce multitasking, reveal congestion, and improve throughput by encouraging teams to finish work before starting more. PMI treats it as a practical flow-management mechanism used in adaptive delivery environments.
Contrast: Batch size — a WIP limit restricts how much work is active at once, while batch size describes how much work is grouped together for processing or delivery.
Example: A software team sets a WIP limit of three items in the testing column so developers stop starting new features and help clear testing bottlenecks.
Source: Agile Practice Guide / Kanban-focused guidance on limiting work in progress — confirm exact page in PMI source

Core Meaning: The effort, actions, or tasks performed to create deliverables, produce outcomes, or achieve project objectives.
Expansion: In PMI usage, work is the actual activity undertaken to turn requirements, plans, and resources into deliverables and results. It may be organised into packages, activities, tasks, or iterations depending on the life cycle and delivery approach. Managing work means defining it clearly, sequencing it appropriately, assigning responsibility, monitoring progress, and controlling changes so execution remains aligned to scope and objectives.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Work is the body of effort performed by people, teams, and sometimes automated systems to produce project outputs. In project management it is not just busyness or time spent; it is purposeful effort tied to defined deliverables, milestones, and intended value.

Why it matters and where it applies: all planning, scheduling, estimating, resourcing, and control depend on how work is defined and structured. Whether a project uses predictive planning, iterative delivery, or hybrid execution, the project team still needs to identify what work must be done and how it will be managed.

The most common misconception: that work is the same as scope. Scope defines what is included, while work is the effort required to deliver that scope. A project can have well-defined scope but still fail if the work is decomposed poorly, sequenced badly, or executed without control.

How it shows up in real projects: work appears in work breakdown structures, activity lists, sprint backlogs, work packages, schedules, task boards, and progress reporting. Teams estimate work, assign owners, track completion, and adjust execution when risks, defects, or changes affect delivery.
Contrast: Scope — scope defines what the project will deliver; work is the effort and activities needed to deliver it.
Example: On an office-relocation project, work includes surveying the new site, configuring network infrastructure, moving equipment, testing systems, and training employees before go-live.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) / PMI Lexicon — exact page citation not confirmed in available context

Core Meaning: A formal approval to begin work on a project, phase, or activity.
Expansion: Work authorization is the approval that permits work to start in line with project governance and planning controls. It helps ensure that execution begins only when prerequisites, approvals, and timing conditions have been satisfied. In practice it is used to prevent premature or unauthorized work and to align release of effort with approved plans.
Contrast: Work authorization system — work authorization is the approval to proceed; the system is the method used to issue and control that approval.
Example: A construction manager receives written authorization to start foundation work only after design review and permit approval are complete.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, work authorization

Core Meaning: A collection of formal, documented procedures that defines how project work is authorized so it is performed at the right time and in the proper sequence.
Expansion: A work authorization system is the governance mechanism used to release work in a controlled way. It links approvals, sequencing, and organizational procedures so teams do not begin work before the required conditions are met. PMI treats it as an important control for coordinating execution across phases, teams, and dependencies.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A work authorization system is the formal process or set of procedures used to approve work before it begins. It may include written approvals, electronic workflows, release gates, or other documented controls that govern when work can start.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters because projects often involve interdependent work, limited resources, compliance obligations, and multiple decision makers. It is especially useful in predictive and hybrid environments, and in regulated or capital-intensive projects where timing and sequence must be tightly controlled.

The most common misconception: that it is just a signature or a one-time approval. In practice it is a broader control system that can govern the release of work throughout the project life cycle, not merely the initial start of the project.

How it shows up in real projects: A sponsor approves a phase, the PMO issues a formal release, procurement confirms prerequisites, and the team starts only after the required authorizations are in place. The system creates traceability for who approved the work, when it was released, and under what conditions.
Contrast: Work authorization — work authorization is the approval itself; the work authorization system is the formal process that issues and controls that approval.
Example: Before a vendor begins installation, the project office requires a signed release form, completed safety checks, and phase approval through the organisation's authorization workflow.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, work authorization system

Core Meaning: A hierarchical decomposition of the total scope of work to be carried out by the project team.
Expansion: The work breakdown structure organises the total project scope into smaller, manageable components. PMI describes it as a deliverable-oriented breakdown of the work included in the project scope baseline and uses it as a core basis for planning, estimating, and control. It helps create shared understanding of what the project will produce and what work is required to produce it.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A work breakdown structure, or WBS, breaks the full project scope into progressively smaller components until the work can be planned and managed at a practical level. It is hierarchical and focuses on project scope, not just on the sequence of tasks.

Why it matters and where it applies: The WBS matters because it gives the team a common structure for estimating cost and duration, assigning accountability, identifying risks, and tracking progress. It applies broadly across predictive, hybrid, and many adaptive environments whenever the team needs a clear way to organise deliverables and scope.

The most common misconception: that a WBS is simply a long task list or a project schedule. The distinction is that the WBS defines and organises scope, while the schedule sequences activities over time.

How it shows up in real projects: Teams create top-level deliverables, decompose them into lower-level components, define work packages, and then use that structure to support estimating, budgeting, reporting, and change control.
Contrast: Work package — the WBS is the full hierarchical decomposition of scope; a work package is the lowest level within that structure used for planning and control.
Example: On a hospital expansion project, the team structures the scope into site works, structural works, clinical systems, commissioning, and handover, then decomposes each into manageable work packages for estimating and control.

Source: Practice Standard for Work Breakdown Structures, Third Edition, work breakdown structure, page 195

Core Meaning: A set of values and attitudes that influence how diligently and responsibly a person approaches work.
Expansion: Work ethic refers to personal conduct and commitment in performing work. It is a general behavioral concept rather than a formal PMI glossary term.
Contrast: Professional responsibility — work ethic is a personal attitude toward work; professional responsibility is an obligation tied to role and standards.
Example: A team member consistently meets commitments and follows through on assigned tasks without needing repeated reminders.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Work that has started but has not yet been completed.
Expansion: Work in progress refers to items currently being worked on within a workflow or delivery system. In project and agile practice, it is often tracked to understand flow, expose bottlenecks, and prevent overloading the team with too many simultaneous tasks. Limiting work in progress helps improve throughput, focus, and predictability.
Contrast: Backlog — Work in progress has already been started; backlog items are approved or candidate work not yet in active execution.
Example: A Kanban team limits itself to five active tasks at a time so developers finish current stories before pulling additional work into execution.
Source: PMI Lexicon / Agile Practice Guide — confirm exact citation in Infinity

Core Meaning: A limit on the number of items allowed to be in progress at one time.
Expansion: Work in progress limits help control flow by preventing too much work from starting before existing work is finished. They are used to reduce multitasking, expose bottlenecks, and improve throughput. PMI references them in adaptive and flow-based contexts as a way to manage capacity and maintain focus.
Contrast: Work in progress — work in progress is the work currently underway; work in progress limits are the cap placed on how much can be underway at once.
Example: A software team sets a limit of three items in testing so the team finishes existing defects before starting more test cases.
Source: The PMI Guide to Business Analysis, glossary, work in progress limits, page 417

Core Meaning: The work defined at the lowest level of the work breakdown structure for which cost and duration can be estimated and managed.
Expansion: A work package is the smallest WBS component used for practical planning and control. PMI uses it as the level where cost and duration are estimated, responsibility can be assigned, and performance can be tracked. It connects scope decomposition to the later definition of schedule activities and execution work.
Contrast: Activity — a work package is a scope decomposition element in the WBS, while an activity is a scheduled action used to perform the work.
Example: In an ERP implementation, data migration is defined as a work package and later broken into extraction, cleansing, validation, and load activities.
Source: Practice Standard for Work Breakdown Structures, Third Edition, work package, page 195

Core Meaning: The raw observations and measurements identified during activities performed to carry out the project work.
Expansion: Work performance data is unprocessed factual project data captured while work is being performed. PMI treats it as the basic input for monitoring and controlling, before analysis or interpretation has occurred. Typical examples include actual dates, actual costs, defect counts, and completed work quantities.
Contrast: Work performance information — work performance data is raw observation and measurement, while work performance information is that data after analysis and contextualisation.
Example: A project team records actual labour hours, number of test failures, and percentage of foundation concrete poured during the reporting period.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, work performance data, page 143

Core Meaning: Performance data collected from various controlling processes, analysed in context and integrated based on relationships across areas.
Expansion: Work performance information is project performance data that has been processed so it can support interpretation and decision making. PMI distinguishes it from raw data because it has been analysed, combined, or compared against baselines, thresholds, or expectations. It is commonly used to identify trends, variances, and emerging issues that need management attention.
Contrast: Work performance reports — work performance information is analysed performance insight, while work performance reports are the formal physical or electronic representations used to present that information.
Example: After comparing actual progress and spending against the baseline, the project manager identifies that the project is two weeks late on critical path work but still within cost tolerance.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, work performance information, page 144

Core Meaning: Physical or electronic representations of work performance information compiled to generate decisions, actions, or awareness.
Expansion: Work performance reports package analysed project performance into formats stakeholders can use for oversight and action. PMI distinguishes them from raw data and analysed information because reports are the communicated output, such as dashboards, status reports, or trend views. They support governance, escalation, and decision making across the project life cycle.
Contrast: Work performance information — information is the analysed performance content; reports are the representations used to communicate it.
Example: A project manager issues a weekly dashboard showing schedule variance, defect trends, and forecast completion dates to the sponsor and team leads.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, work performance reports, page 144

Core Meaning: Tangible or intangible outputs produced as part of project or business analysis work.
Expansion: Work products are the outputs created during analysis or project work, such as models, documents, diagrams, or other deliverables. They support communication, validation, and decision making by making the work visible and reviewable. PMI uses the term in business analysis contexts to describe outputs that help progress the work and confirm understanding.
Contrast: Deliverable — a work product is any output of the work; a deliverable is a work product specifically intended to be handed over or accepted.
Example: A business analyst produces a process map and requirements model as work products for stakeholder review.
Source: The PMI Guide to Business Analysis, glossary, work products, page 417

Core Meaning: The sequence of steps, handoffs, and decisions through which work moves from initiation to completion.
Expansion: Workflow describes how work progresses across people, roles, or systems. In project settings, it helps clarify how items are processed, reviewed, approved, and completed. It is commonly used to improve visibility, consistency, and efficiency, especially where recurring work or handoffs create delay or confusion.
Contrast: WBS — workflow shows how work moves through steps and handoffs, while a WBS shows the hierarchical decomposition of project scope.
Example: A change-control workflow routes a request through submission, impact review, approval, implementation, and closure.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A set of explicit team norms that defines how project participants will work together day to day.
Expansion: A working agreement sets expectations for collaboration, communication, decision making, meeting behaviour, and conflict handling within a project team. It is usually created by the team rather than imposed externally, and it can be revised as the team learns what improves performance. In PMI-aligned practice, it is closely related to ground rules and team norms that support effective team development and delivery.
Contrast: Ground rules — a working agreement is the team's broader, living set of collaboration norms; ground rules are the specific behavioural rules within it.
Example: At project kickoff, a cross-functional team agrees to raise blockers within 24 hours, keep meetings to agenda and timebox, and resolve design disputes through a designated decision owner.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The days identified in a project or resource calendar as available for scheduled work.
Expansion: Working days are the days on which project work is planned to occur, excluding nonworking periods such as weekends, holidays, or calendar exceptions. They are used in schedule development, duration calculation, and resource planning because elapsed time and workable time are not always the same. The exact pattern of working days depends on the project calendar, location, contract terms, and organisational practices.
Contrast: Calendar days — working days count only scheduled work time; calendar days count every day in the elapsed period.
Example: A task estimated at five working days starts on Thursday and finishes the following Wednesday because the weekend is nonworking time in the project calendar.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The amount of project work assigned to a person, team, or other resource over a defined period.
Expansion: Workload reflects how much effort or how many assignments a resource is expected to handle relative to available time and capacity. It is important in resource planning because excessive workload creates delays, multitasking inefficiency, and burnout, while too little workload leaves capacity unused. In PMI practice, the idea is handled through resource allocation, capacity, and overallocation management even when the term itself is used informally.
Contrast: Capacity — workload is the work assigned; capacity is the work a resource can realistically perform.
Example: A project manager sees that one business analyst is assigned to requirements work on three projects in the same week and redistributes tasks to avoid overallocation.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A structured, facilitated session in which participants work together to produce a defined outcome.
Expansion: A workshop is a focused group session used to explore issues, generate ideas, resolve questions, or create specific outputs. In project management, workshops are commonly used for requirements elicitation, planning, risk identification, and stakeholder alignment. They are valuable when rapid collaboration and shared understanding are more effective than sequential one-to-one discussions.
Contrast: Meeting — a workshop is more deliberately facilitated and output-oriented than a general meeting.
Example: During planning, a project manager runs a risk workshop with engineering, procurement, and operations leads to identify threats, assess causes, and agree on initial responses.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An earned value chart that plots cost and schedule performance over time to show trends relative to planned and earned value measures.
Expansion: A worm chart is a visual earned value display used to show how performance is trending across reporting periods. It helps teams quickly spot whether cost and schedule performance are improving or deteriorating.
Contrast: S-curve — a worm chart focuses on performance trend movement between earned value measures, while an S-curve typically shows cumulative values over time.
Example: A PMO reviews a worm chart each month to see whether a troubled infrastructure project is recovering schedule performance after corrective action.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The horizontal axis on a chart or graph.
Expansion: The X-axis is the horizontal reference line used to display one dimension of data. In project visuals it often represents time, sequence, or categories.
Contrast: Y-axis — the X-axis runs horizontally, while the Y-axis runs vertically.
Example: On a burnup chart, the X-axis shows the iteration timeline across the release.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A control chart used to monitor the average of a process over time.
Expansion: An X-bar control chart plots sample means against control limits to show whether a process average is stable or shifting. It is used in quality management to distinguish normal variation from signals that may require investigation. In project work, it supports monitoring and controlling process performance where repeated measurements are available.
Contrast: Control chart — an X-bar control chart is a specific control chart for monitoring sample means, while control chart is the broader category.
Example: In a construction materials project, the quality team tracks the average compressive strength of sampled concrete batches each day to detect process drift.

Figure.caption: The red point above the UCL is out of control.

Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition — see glossary entry for control chart and discussion of quality data representation

Core Meaning: A high-level plan that outlines expected releases over a year, including timing and major delivery objectives.
Expansion: A yearly release plan is a long-horizon version of release planning used to sequence expected deliveries across the year. It helps align product, delivery, business, and support expectations without locking every detail too early. Teams typically revise it as priorities, capacity, and market conditions change.
Contrast: Release plan — a yearly release plan is the longer-horizon version of a release plan, while release planning is the activity of creating and updating it.
Example: A digital product team maps four planned quarterly releases so engineering, training, and customer support can prepare in advance.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A pricing and capacity management approach used to maximize revenue or value from limited resources by adjusting availability and allocation based on demand.
Expansion: In project contexts, yield management is an operational or business concept rather than a core PMI glossary definition. It focuses on getting the highest value from constrained capacity by shaping allocation decisions. It is most common where demand varies and available capacity cannot expand quickly.
Contrast: Resource leveling — yield management optimizes value from limited capacity; resource leveling adjusts schedules to deal with resource constraints.
Example: A consulting firm assigns its limited senior architects first to the highest-margin client initiatives during a peak delivery period.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A schedule characteristic indicating that an activity or event occupies no measurable time span.
Expansion: Zero duration is used in scheduling to mark a point in time rather than work that consumes time. It commonly appears in milestones and other logic points that must be shown in the network without adding elapsed duration.
Contrast: Milestone — zero duration describes the time attribute; a milestone is the event that often has that attribute.
Example: The schedule shows contract award as a zero-duration point that must occur before vendor onboarding can begin.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An activity that has no time span and is used to represent a point in time or a logical event in the schedule.
Expansion: A zero duration activity does not consume working time, but it can still be important in the schedule model. It is often used to show milestones, handoffs, or logic points that need to appear in the network. PMI distinguishes it from normal activities that have duration greater than zero.
Contrast: Milestone — a zero duration activity is a schedule element with no time span; a milestone is a significant point or event that is often represented with zero duration.
Example: A regulatory-approval point is inserted as a zero duration activity before procurement can begin.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — zero duration activity

Core Meaning: The amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project finish date.
Expansion: Zero float means an activity has no scheduling flexibility. If it slips, the project finish date slips unless corrective action is taken. PMI uses float to assess schedule flexibility and identify activities that require close control.
Contrast: Total float — zero float means no delay is possible without affecting the finish date; total float is the general measure of allowable delay.
Example: A final data-migration task before cutover has zero float, so any delay pushes the planned go-live date.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — zero float

Core Meaning: A relationship timing condition in which the successor proceeds with no waiting time after the predecessor as defined by the logic link.
Expansion: Zero lag means there is no added delay between linked activities. It is the default timing in many finish-to-start relationships when no offset is needed. PMI uses lag to model waiting time, so zero lag indicates that no such delay is applied.
Contrast: Lag — zero lag has no delay between activities; lag introduces a deliberate waiting period.
Example: Once test scripts are approved, execution starts immediately with zero lag.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — zero lag

Core Meaning: A budgeting method that starts from a zero base and requires each cost to be justified for the current planning period.
Expansion: Zero-based budgeting does not assume that prior budgets should roll forward unchanged. Each planned expense is reviewed and justified against current objectives, constraints, and expected value. It is used when tighter cost control or stronger alignment between spending and strategy is needed.
Contrast: Cost baseline — zero-based budgeting is a method for building or reviewing budgets from scratch; a cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget used for control.
Example: A transformation program requires each workstream to justify its staffing, software, and vendor costs for the next release cycle instead of carrying forward last quarter's allocations.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An informal review approach in which work is checked in alternating passes or perspectives to improve completeness and consistency.
Expansion: Teams may use a zigzag review pattern to move back and forth across sections, disciplines, or criteria instead of reviewing strictly from start to finish. It is a practitioner expression rather than a formal PMI glossary entry.
Contrast: Sequential review — zigzag review alternates across areas or viewpoints; sequential review follows one linear pass from start to finish.
Example: A team reviews a requirements package by alternating between business rules and system rules to catch mismatches early.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The bargaining range within which negotiating parties can reach a mutually acceptable agreement.
Expansion: The zone of possible agreement is the overlap between what one party is willing to accept and what the other is willing to offer. If no overlap exists, agreement is unlikely without changing assumptions, scope, value, or constraints. It is especially relevant in procurement, stakeholder negotiation, conflict resolution, and contract discussions.
Contrast: BATNA — the zone of possible agreement is the overlap where a deal can be made; a BATNA is the fallback option if no deal is reached.
Example: A buyer can approve a delivery contract up to a certain ceiling and a supplier can profitably deliver above a lower threshold, creating a range in which both can agree on price and terms.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The consensus view, across beneficiaries and stakeholders, that a project delivered value worth the effort and expense.
Expansion: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition reframes project success beyond the traditional 'on time, on budget, in scope' triple constraint. A project is considered successful when its intended beneficiaries, other stakeholders, and the project participants agree that the value delivered was worth the effort and expense invested. This shifts the emphasis from outputs to outcomes and realized value, and it underpins the 2026 ECO's focus on value-based delivery.
Contrast: Triple constraint — meeting scope, schedule, and cost is necessary but no longer sufficient; success now hinges on perceived value.
Example: A platform ships slightly late and over budget, yet stakeholders agree it transformed the business, so it is judged a success.
Source: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition, Glossary.

Core Meaning: A condition or capability that must be present in a product, service, or result to satisfy a business need.
Expansion: A requirement specifies what a deliverable must do or possess to meet a stakeholder or business need. Requirements are elicited and analyzed, documented, prioritized, and traced through delivery and validation. They may be functional (what it does), nonfunctional (qualities such as performance or security), or constraints, and together they form the basis of scope and acceptance criteria.
Contrast: Scope — a requirement is a single needed condition or capability; scope is the total set of work and deliverables that satisfies the requirements.
Example: 'The portal must authenticate users within two seconds' is a nonfunctional requirement that feeds the scope baseline.
Source: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition, Glossary.

Core Meaning: A predictive, sequential delivery approach where each phase is largely completed before the next begins.
Expansion: Waterfall is the common informal name for a fully predictive, plan-driven life cycle in which requirements, design, build, test, and deploy proceed as distinct sequential phases with formal hand-offs. It suits work with stable, well-understood requirements and low expected change, where up-front planning and baselines provide control. It contrasts with adaptive approaches that deliver iteratively and welcome change.
Contrast: Agile — waterfall front-loads planning and delivers once at the end; agile delivers in increments and adapts to change throughout.
Example: A regulated construction project runs design, build, then commission in sequence, with sign-off gates between phases.
Source: Common industry term; aligns with the predictive development approach in the PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition.

Core Meaning: A process-based project management methodology (PRojects IN Controlled Environments) used widely in the UK and internationally.
Expansion: PRINCE2 is a structured, process-driven method organized around defined principles, themes, and processes, with strong emphasis on continued business justification, clearly defined roles, and managing by stages and by exception. It is agnostic about technical delivery and is often adopted as an organizational standard alongside or instead of PMI-based approaches. The PMP exam is not based on PRINCE2, but practitioners may encounter it in governance-heavy environments.
Contrast: PMBOK Guide — PRINCE2 is a prescriptive methodology with set processes; the PMBOK Guide is a principles-and-domains body of knowledge, not a methodology.
Example: A UK government programme mandates PRINCE2 stage boundaries and a project board for governance decisions.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; widely used industry methodology (AXELOS).

Core Meaning: The amount by which actual project costs exceed the budgeted or baselined cost.
Expansion: A cost overrun occurs when the money actually spent surpasses the cost baseline, signalling unfavorable cost performance — a negative cost variance and a CPI below 1.0. Overruns may stem from poor estimating, scope creep, realized risks, or inefficiency, and are detected through earned value analysis and variance monitoring. The response is to analyze the cause and apply corrective or preventive action through the change-control and governance processes.
Contrast: Sunk cost — a cost overrun is spend above plan; a sunk cost is money already spent that should not influence future decisions.
Example: Halfway through, $600k is spent against a $500k earned-value baseline, a $100k cost overrun with CPI below 1.0.
Source: Common practitioner term; relates to cost variance and CPI in the PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition.

Core Meaning: Obtaining the people and physical resources needed to carry out project work.
Expansion: Acquisition is the act of securing the human and physical resources a project requires, whether from inside the organization or through external sources such as procurement. It covers negotiating for team members, hiring, and obtaining materials, equipment, and facilities.
Contrast: Procurement - procurement specifically obtains goods or services from outside sellers, whereas acquisition also includes sourcing internal resources.
Example: A project manager negotiating with a functional manager to assign two developers, and renting specialized testing equipment, is acquiring resources.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Acquire Resources

Core Meaning: The set of detailed characteristics associated with each schedule activity.
Expansion: Activity attributes extend the activity list by capturing the details needed to build and manage the schedule. They typically include an activity identifier and code, predecessors and successors, logical relationships, leads and lags, resource requirements, imposed dates, constraints, and assumptions. The attributes evolve as planning progresses and support schedule development, reporting, and filtering.
Contrast: Activity list - the activity list simply names the scheduled activities, whereas activity attributes hold the supporting detail for each one.
Example: For the activity 'Pour foundation,' the attributes record its predecessor 'Excavate site,' a 2-day lag for curing, and a crew of three laborers.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Define Activities (activity attributes)

Core Meaning: An alphanumeric value that classifies a schedule activity so activities can be sorted and filtered.
Expansion: An activity code is a numeric or alphanumeric tag assigned to schedule activities to categorize them, for example by phase, location, or responsible department. Codes let planners filter, group, and order activities within schedule reports.
Contrast: Activity identifier - an identifier uniquely names one activity, whereas an activity code is a shared category used to group many activities.
Example: Tagging all electrical activities with the code 'ELEC' so a report can show only electrical work.
Source: PMBOK Guide - schedule activity attributes

Core Meaning: The quantitative assessments of the likely number of work periods needed to complete an activity.
Expansion: Activity duration estimates state how many work periods (hours, days, weeks) an activity is expected to take, given the planned resources. They may be expressed as a single value or as a range with confidence levels, and often note the assumptions behind them. These estimates feed schedule development and are produced by techniques such as analogous, parametric, and three-point estimating.
Contrast: Effort - effort is the number of labor units (e.g., person-hours) required, whereas duration is the elapsed work periods, which depend on how many resources are applied.
Example: Estimating that testing a module will take 5 days, assuming one tester works on it full time.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Estimate Activity Durations

Core Meaning: A unique label distinguishing one schedule activity from all others in the schedule.
Expansion: An activity identifier is a short unique code or number assigned to a single schedule activity so it can be referenced unambiguously in network diagrams, reports, and software. Unlike a category code, it points to exactly one activity.
Contrast: Activity code - a code groups many activities by category, whereas an identifier uniquely names one activity.
Example: Labeling 'Install drywall' as activity A1040 in the scheduling tool.
Source: PMBOK Guide - schedule activity attributes

Core Meaning: The real elapsed work time an activity has taken, measured from its actual start.
Expansion: Actual duration is the number of work periods between an activity's actual start date and either the data date (for in-progress work) or the actual finish date (for completed work). It is compared against the planned duration to assess schedule performance.
Contrast: Remaining duration - actual duration is time already spent, whereas remaining duration is the time still estimated to finish.
Example: An activity that started Monday and is still running on Thursday's data date has an actual duration of 4 days.
Source: PMBOK Guide - schedule terms

Core Meaning: An iterative, adaptive approach to delivering projects through short cycles of work and frequent feedback.
Expansion: Agile project management delivers value incrementally through short iterations rather than a single long sequence of phases, emphasizing collaboration, customer feedback, and responsiveness to change. It is grounded in the values and principles of the Agile Manifesto and is well suited to projects with high uncertainty or evolving requirements. Common frameworks include Scrum, Kanban, and XP, and progress is measured by working deliverables rather than documentation alone.
Contrast: Predictive (waterfall) project management - predictive approaches plan scope and schedule in detail up front, whereas agile embraces change and refines the plan iteration by iteration.
Example: A software team delivering working features in two-week sprints, reprioritizing the backlog after each demo with the customer.
Source: Agile Practice Guide - agile approaches

Core Meaning: Any document or understanding that defines the initial intentions and terms between project parties.
Expansion: In project management, agreements capture the intentions binding parties together and can take many forms - contracts, memoranda of understanding, letters of agreement, service level agreements, or even verbal or email understandings. They are a key input to developing the project charter and often define obligations with external sellers. Contracts are the most formal type, carrying legal enforceability.
Contrast: Contract - a contract is a specific, legally binding type of agreement, whereas 'agreements' is the broader category that also includes informal understandings.
Example: A signed memorandum of understanding between two departments to share testing facilities for a project.
Source: PMBOK Guide - agreements

Core Meaning: Uncertainty that arises from a lack of knowledge or understanding about aspects of the project.
Expansion: Ambiguity risk is uncertainty caused by imperfect knowledge - gaps in understanding requirements, technology, the market, or future conditions. Unlike risks tied to natural variation, ambiguity risks stem from what the team does not yet know, and they are addressed by acquiring knowledge: research, prototyping, expert consultation, and incremental development. It is one of the emergent risk types discussed in PMI risk guidance, alongside variability and emergent risk.
Contrast: Variability risk - variability risk comes from known events whose outcome varies, whereas ambiguity risk comes from incomplete knowledge of the situation itself.
Example: Uncertainty about how a newly released technology will perform because the team has never used it before.
Source: PMI Practice Standard for Project Risk Management - ambiguity risk

Core Meaning: Resolving a dispute by submitting it to a private neutral third party whose decision the parties accept.
Expansion: Arbitration is an alternative dispute resolution method in which conflicting parties present their case to a neutral arbitrator (or panel) outside the courts, and the arbitrator renders a decision. It is typically faster, cheaper, and more private than litigation, and procurement contracts often specify it as the agreed mechanism for settling claims. The decision may be binding or non-binding depending on the agreement.
Contrast: Mediation - a mediator facilitates the parties toward their own agreement, whereas an arbitrator actually decides the outcome.
Example: A buyer and seller disputing a contract scope change agree to binding arbitration rather than going to court.
Source: PMBOK Guide - claims administration / dispute resolution

Core Meaning: A quality measurement that records whether each inspected item simply has or lacks a given characteristic.
Expansion: Attribute sampling is a quality control method that evaluates each unit on a pass/fail or present/absent basis for a particular attribute, rather than measuring a continuous value. The result is conforming or nonconforming, which makes it useful for go/no-go decisions. It contrasts with variables sampling, which measures the degree of conformance on a scale.
Contrast: Variables sampling - variables sampling measures a continuous quantity (e.g., length in millimeters), whereas attribute sampling only notes whether a characteristic is present or absent.
Example: Inspecting a batch of bolts and recording each simply as 'defective' or 'not defective.'
Source: PMBOK Guide - quality control measurements

Core Meaning: A matrix structure where authority is shared roughly equally between the project manager and functional managers.
Expansion: In a balanced matrix organization, power over project resources and decisions is split fairly evenly between the project manager and the functional managers. The project manager has a recognized role but does not fully control the budget or staff, which can blur authority and create competing priorities. It sits between the weak matrix (functional managers dominate) and the strong matrix (project managers dominate).
Contrast: Strong matrix - in a strong matrix the project manager holds most of the authority and resources, whereas in a balanced matrix that authority is shared with functional managers.
Example: A project manager who co-decides a team member's assignments and reviews with that person's department head, neither side having final say.
Source: PMBOK Guide - organizational structures

Core Meaning: The practice of identifying, planning, measuring, and sustaining the benefits a project or program is meant to deliver.
Expansion: Benefits realization management (BRM) is the collective set of processes for ensuring that the intended benefits of a project, program, or portfolio are clearly defined, actively pursued, and actually achieved and sustained over time. It shifts the focus from delivering outputs (a product) to realizing outcomes and value (the benefit). BRM spans identifying benefits, planning how and when they will be measured and by whom, and sustaining them after the project closes, connecting project work back to organizational strategy.
Deep Dive:
Benefits realization management is the discipline that makes sure the value a project promised actually materializes. A benefit is a measurable improvement - higher revenue, lower cost, better compliance - that stakeholders perceive as valuable. BRM tracks each benefit through its life cycle: identify, execute (plan, measure, and report), and sustain.

It matters because projects routinely deliver their scope yet fail to deliver value; an on-time, on-budget product that nobody adopts produces no benefit. By assigning benefit owners, target metrics, and realization dates, BRM keeps the organization honest about whether the investment paid off.

The most common misconception is that benefits are realized the moment the project is delivered. In reality most benefits accrue after closure, as the product is operated and adopted, so measurement must continue beyond the project's end.

On real projects BRM shows up as a benefits management plan that names each benefit, its metric and baseline, the owner accountable for it, and the date by which it should appear - reviewed at governance gates and after handover to operations.
Contrast: Scope management - scope management ensures the project delivers the agreed outputs, whereas BRM ensures those outputs translate into realized organizational value.
Example: After launching a new CRM, the organization tracks a targeted 15% reduction in customer churn over the following year and assigns the sales director to own that benefit.
Source: PMI - Benefits Realization Management

Core Meaning: The document defining how and when a project's benefits will be delivered, measured, and sustained.
Expansion: A benefits realization plan (or benefits management plan) describes the target benefits, how they will be measured, who owns them, and the timeline over which they are expected to be realized and sustained. It links project deliverables to the outcomes that justify the investment and is used to track value beyond project closure. It is typically created early and maintained alongside the business case.
Contrast: Business case - the business case justifies whether to undertake the project, whereas the benefits realization plan governs how the promised benefits will actually be achieved and tracked.
Example: A plan stating that a process-automation project should cut invoice-processing time by 40% within six months, measured monthly by the finance manager.
Source: PMI - benefits management plan

Core Meaning: Events or conditions that halt work or prevent further progress.
Expansion: Blockers are obstacles that stop a team member or the team from making progress on their work until they are resolved. In agile practice they are surfaced quickly - often in the daily standup - so they can be removed promptly, frequently by the Scrum Master or team lead.
Contrast: Impediment - the terms are often used interchangeably, but 'blocker' usually denotes a hard stop on a specific work item, whereas 'impediment' is any broader hindrance to team progress.
Example: A developer cannot continue coding because the test environment is down - a blocker raised at the daily standup.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A variation of brainstorming where participants generate ideas individually in writing before group discussion.
Expansion: Brain writing is a refinement of brainstorming in which participants are given time to think about and write down their ideas individually before the group convenes to share and build on them. This reduces the dominance of louder voices and the anchoring effect of the first idea spoken, producing a wider and more independent set of inputs. It is used as a data-gathering and creativity technique during requirements and risk work.
Contrast: Brainstorming - classic brainstorming generates ideas out loud as a group, whereas brain writing has people record ideas privately first to reduce groupthink.
Example: Before a risk workshop, each participant writes down five potential risks on cards, which are then collected and discussed together.
Source: PMBOK Guide - data gathering (brainstorming / brain writing)

Core Meaning: The rate at which an agile team consumes effort or budget relative to the work remaining.
Expansion: Burn rate measures how quickly a team is using up its available hours or budget to complete work. In agile contexts it often refers to the pace at which the team is consuming the effort set aside for the iteration or release, and it can be read from burndown data. A burn rate higher than planned warns that capacity or budget will be exhausted before the work is done.
Contrast: Velocity - velocity measures how much work the team completes per iteration, whereas burn rate focuses on how fast effort or money is being consumed.
Example: A team that has spent 60% of its sprint hours with only 40% of stories done has a burn rate that signals trouble.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A risk that can result in either a gain or a loss.
Expansion: A business risk carries the possibility of either a positive (opportunity) or negative (threat) outcome, unlike a pure risk that can only cause loss. Organizations accept business risks deliberately in pursuit of profit or strategic advantage. The concept distinguishes speculative, two-sided uncertainties from insurable, one-sided ones.
Contrast: Pure risk - a pure (insurable) risk offers only the chance of loss, whereas a business risk can yield either gain or loss.
Example: Investing in developing a new product line that may either capture market share or fail to sell.
Source: PMI risk management - business (speculative) risk

Core Meaning: An office layout combining shared social spaces ('commons') with quiet private spaces ('caves').
Expansion: Caves and commons is a workspace design for agile teams that provides two kinds of zones: open 'commons' areas that encourage osmotic communication and collaboration, and quieter 'caves' where individuals can do focused work without interruption. The idea balances the agile preference for high-bandwidth interaction with the need for concentration. It supports both teamwork and individual productivity in a colocated setting.
Contrast: Osmotic communication - osmotic communication is the information overheard in a shared space, whereas caves and commons is the physical layout that deliberately enables it alongside quiet focus.
Example: A team room with a central table for collaboration plus side nooks where developers retreat to concentrate.
Source: Agile Practice Guide - team space (caves and commons)

Core Meaning: The maximum price a buyer will pay under a contract, regardless of the seller's costs.
Expansion: The ceiling price is the highest amount a buyer agrees to pay under a contract, regardless of the seller's actual costs. In fixed-price incentive contracts it caps the buyer's exposure: above the ceiling, any further cost overrun is borne entirely by the seller. It is a key input to calculating the point of total assumption.
Contrast: Target price - the target price is the expected negotiated cost-plus-fee, whereas the ceiling price is the absolute maximum the buyer will pay.
Example: A fixed-price incentive contract with a ceiling price of $1.2M means the buyer never pays more than that even if the seller's costs balloon.
Source: PMBOK Guide - contract types (ceiling price)

Core Meaning: Manual or automated tools that support managing and tracking change requests and configuration.
Expansion: Change control tools are the manual or software tools used to record, evaluate, route, and track change requests and configuration items. At minimum they support the change control board's decisions and keep an auditable history of changes. They help maintain consistency in how changes are managed across the project.
Contrast: Change control board - the board is the group that approves or rejects changes, whereas change control tools are the means used to log and track those changes.
Example: A web-based change request system that logs each request, its impact analysis, and the change control board's decision.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Perform Integrated Change Control

Core Meaning: Systematically reviewing items against a predefined list to ensure accuracy and completeness.
Expansion: Checklist analysis uses a structured list - often built from historical information and lessons learned - to systematically verify that required items have been addressed. In risk management it helps identify risks by walking through categories that have arisen on past similar projects. Its strength is consistency; its weakness is that it can blind teams to risks not on the list.
Contrast: Brainstorming - brainstorming openly generates new ideas, whereas checklist analysis checks against a fixed, predefined list and may miss novel items.
Example: Using a standard risk checklist from previous construction projects to confirm common risks such as permit delays have been considered.
Source: PMBOK Guide - checklist analysis

Core Meaning: Contracts that have been formally completed, settled, and signed off.
Expansion: Closed procurements are contracts or other procurement agreements that have been formally finalized - all work accepted, claims settled, and closure documented by the authorized party. Closing a procurement includes verifying deliverables, settling open items, and archiving records. It is an output of the Control Procurements process.
Contrast: Close Project or Phase - closing procurements finalizes individual contracts, whereas Close Project or Phase finalizes the entire project or phase administratively.
Example: A signed completion certificate from the buyer formally closing a contractor's construction contract.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Control Procurements (closed procurements)

Core Meaning: Influence based on the ability to impose penalties or negative consequences.
Expansion: Coercive power (also called penalty or punishment power) is a form of organizational power rooted in the capacity to discipline or impose negative outcomes such as reprimands, undesirable assignments, or withheld benefits. It can compel short-term compliance but tends to damage trust, morale, and engagement when relied upon. It is widely viewed as the weakest basis for influence and a last resort.
Contrast: Reward power - reward power motivates with positive incentives, whereas coercive power compels through the threat of penalties.
Example: A project manager assigning undesirable tasks to a team member who repeatedly misses commitments, as a form of pressure.
Source: PMBOK Guide - types of power

Core Meaning: A conflict-resolution approach that integrates differing viewpoints to reach a consensus that satisfies everyone.
Expansion: Collaborating (also called problem solving or confronting) resolves conflict by openly incorporating multiple perspectives and working toward a solution that genuinely addresses everyone's concerns. It treats conflict as a shared problem to solve, requires dialogue and trust, and produces a durable win-win outcome. It is generally regarded as the most effective conflict-resolution style, though it takes the most time and commitment.
Contrast: Compromise - compromising has each party give up something for a partial (lose-lose) settlement, whereas collaborating seeks a solution that fully satisfies all parties (win-win).
Example: Two leads disagreeing on an architecture meet, surface their underlying needs, and design a hybrid that meets both sets of requirements.
Source: PMBOK Guide - conflict resolution (collaborate / problem solve)

Core Meaning: Structured group activities that spark collaboration and creativity to elicit requirements or ideas.
Expansion: Collaborative games are facilitated activities designed to encourage participants to share knowledge, build on one another's ideas, and reach a shared understanding in a creative, low-pressure way. They are used in requirements elicitation and team work to surface needs that conventional interviews might miss. Common examples include Product Box, Speedboat (Sailboat), and Spider Web.
Contrast: Interviews - interviews gather information one-on-one through direct questioning, whereas collaborative games draw it out through interactive group play.
Example: Running a Product Box game where stakeholders design the packaging for the product to reveal the features they value most.
Source: Agile Practice Guide / PMI Guide to Business Analysis - collaborative games

Core Meaning: A negotiated contract between an employer and a unionized group of employees governing employment terms.
Expansion: A collective bargaining agreement is a contract negotiated between an organization and a labor union representing its workers, setting wages, hours, working conditions, and other terms. For a project manager it acts as a constraint and an enterprise environmental factor, shaping how and when team members can be assigned and worked. Honoring it is both a legal obligation and a stakeholder consideration.
Contrast: Enterprise environmental factors - a collective bargaining agreement is one specific factor/constraint, whereas enterprise environmental factors are the broad set of internal and external conditions influencing the project.
Example: A union agreement limiting overtime forces the project manager to schedule additional shifts rather than extend the workday.
Source: PMBOK Guide - enterprise environmental factors

Core Meaning: Physically placing project team members close together to improve communication and teamwork.
Expansion: Colocation is the practice of locating team members in the same physical place - sometimes a dedicated 'war room' or 'tight matrix' - to strengthen communication, relationships, and productivity. Proximity enables spontaneous, high-bandwidth interaction and is especially valued on agile teams. For distributed teams, virtual colocation tools attempt to recreate some of these benefits.
Contrast: Virtual teams - colocation gathers people in one place for face-to-face interaction, whereas virtual teams collaborate across locations using technology.
Example: Moving all members of a critical project into a single shared project room for the duration of the work.
Source: PMBOK Guide - team resource management (colocation)

Core Meaning: Representations of how the communication process works, used to plan effective project communication.
Expansion: Communication models describe the process by which information is encoded by a sender, transmitted through a medium, and decoded by a receiver, including the noise and feedback that affect understanding. They help project managers anticipate where messages can be distorted and design communications that account for the audience and barriers. The basic sender-receiver model can be extended to reflect cross-cultural and interactive nuances.
Contrast: Communication methods - communication methods are the channels used (push, pull, interactive), whereas communication models explain the underlying encode-transmit-decode process.
Example: Recognizing from the sender-receiver model that 'noise' such as jargon may distort a status report, the project manager simplifies the language.
Source: PMBOK Guide - communication models

Core Meaning: Determining the information needs of project stakeholders in order to plan communications.
Expansion: Communication requirements analysis identifies what information each stakeholder needs, in what format, how often, and through which channel, so that communications are valuable and not wasteful. It considers the number of potential communication channels, which grows rapidly as stakeholders increase. Its results drive the communications management plan.
Contrast: Stakeholder analysis - stakeholder analysis identifies and assesses who the stakeholders are, whereas communication requirements analysis focuses on what information those stakeholders need.
Example: Analyzing that executives need a monthly one-page summary while the team needs daily standup updates.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Plan Communications Management

Core Meaning: A technique for identifying stakeholders' preferred communication methods and styles in order to tailor engagement.
Expansion: A communication styles assessment is a technique used to identify the preferred communication style, format, and content for planned communications, especially for stakeholders whose support is needed. It helps the project manager adapt messages to how different people best receive information. It is often used together with stakeholder engagement planning.
Contrast: Communication requirements analysis - requirements analysis determines what information stakeholders need, whereas a styles assessment determines how they prefer to receive it.
Example: Assessing that a key sponsor prefers brief verbal briefings over lengthy written reports, and adjusting communications accordingly.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Plan Stakeholder Engagement (communication styles assessment)

Core Meaning: A technique for studying an organization's external competitors and market environment.
Expansion: Competitive analysis gathers and evaluates information about an organization's external environment - particularly its competitors, their strengths, weaknesses, and strategies - to inform decisions. In a business-analysis and project context it helps shape requirements, product direction, and the business case by clarifying where the organization stands in its market. It is one of several strategic-analysis techniques.
Contrast: Market analysis - market analysis examines overall market conditions and demand, whereas competitive analysis focuses specifically on rival organizations.
Example: Reviewing competitors' product features and pricing before defining the requirements for a new mobile app.
Source: PMI Guide to Business Analysis - competitive analysis

Core Meaning: A conflict-resolution technique where each party gives up something to reach a mutually acceptable middle ground.
Expansion: Compromising (also called reconciling) resolves conflict by finding a middle position that gives each party partial satisfaction; everyone gains something but also concedes something. It can settle disputes quickly and feels fair, but because no party is fully satisfied it is often characterized as a lose-lose outcome that may not address the root cause. It is useful when a temporary or expedient resolution is acceptable.
Contrast: Collaborate - collaborating works to fully satisfy all parties (win-win), whereas compromising has each side give up something for a partial (lose-lose) settlement.
Example: Two managers who each want a shared resource full time agree to split the person's time 50/50.
Source: PMBOK Guide - conflict resolution (compromise / reconcile)

Core Meaning: The process of obtaining seller responses, selecting a seller, and awarding a contract.
Expansion: Conduct Procurements is the process of collecting proposals or bids from prospective sellers, evaluating them against source selection criteria, choosing a seller, and awarding a contract. It includes activities such as bidder conferences, proposal evaluation, and negotiation. Its main output is a signed agreement that defines the working relationship with the selected seller.
Contrast: Plan Procurement Management - planning decides what and how to procure, whereas Conduct Procurements actually solicits, selects, and contracts a seller.
Example: Issuing an RFP, evaluating the three proposals received, and signing a contract with the chosen vendor.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Conduct Procurements

Core Meaning: The system for tracking and controlling the characteristics of a project's products and documents.
Expansion: A configuration management system is the collection of procedures and tools used to identify and document the functional and physical characteristics of a product or component, control changes to those characteristics, and record and report change status. It ensures that the team always knows the current approved version of deliverables and documents. It works alongside, but is broader than, the change control system, which focuses on approving changes.
Contrast: Change control system - the change control system governs how change requests are reviewed and approved, whereas the configuration management system tracks and controls the product's versions and characteristics.
Example: A document control system that versions every revision of the design specification and records which version is approved.
Source: PMBOK Guide - configuration management system

Core Meaning: The configuration management activity that records and reports the status of configuration items and their changes throughout the project.
Expansion: Configuration status accounting is one of the core configuration management activities. It captures, stores, and reports information about each configuration item - its approved configuration, the status of proposed changes, and the implementation status of approved changes. This gives stakeholders an accurate, traceable record of what the product currently is and how it reached that state.
Contrast: Configuration identification - identification defines and labels the configuration items, whereas status accounting tracks and reports their evolving status over time.
Example: On a software project the team keeps a log showing each baselined component version, every change request raised against it, and whether each change is pending, approved, or implemented.
Source: PMBOK Guide - configuration management activities

Core Meaning: The configuration management activity that confirms configuration items meet their requirements and that records accurately reflect the actual product.
Expansion: Configuration verification and audit is a core configuration management activity that independently checks two things: that each configuration item conforms to its specified requirements, and that the configuration records correctly describe the as-built product. It provides assurance before a baseline is released or accepted, surfacing gaps between what was documented and what was actually delivered.
Contrast: Configuration status accounting - status accounting records and reports item status, whereas verification and audit independently checks that the items and their documentation are correct and complete.
Example: Before release, an auditor verifies the delivered build matches the approved baseline and that every change request was properly documented and approved.
Source: PMBOK Guide - configuration management activities

Core Meaning: Work performed to prevent defects and meet quality requirements correctly the first time.
Expansion: Conformance work is the effort spent up front to do project work correctly and avoid defects. It consists of prevention activities (such as training, documented processes, and good design) and appraisal activities (such as inspection and testing). Investing in conformance work reduces the more expensive nonconformance work of fixing failures later.
Contrast: Nonconformance work - nonconformance work deals with the consequences of failures after they occur, whereas conformance work prevents and detects them up front.
Example: Training the team, running peer reviews, and inspecting deliverables before handover are conformance work that reduces later rework.
Source: PMBOK Guide - cost of quality

Core Meaning: A visual model showing how a business system interacts with the people and external systems that surround it.
Expansion: A context diagram depicts the product or business system as a single unit and shows the actors - people and other systems - that exchange information with it. It is used during requirements work to define scope boundaries and clarify what is inside versus outside the system. By keeping the system as one box, it focuses attention on external interactions rather than internal detail.
Contrast: Data flow diagram - a context diagram shows the whole system as one box with its external interactions, whereas a data flow diagram decomposes internal processes and data movement.
Example: An analyst draws a context diagram for a payroll system showing employees, the tax authority, and the bank as external actors that exchange data with it.
Source: PMBOK Guide - data representation techniques (Collect Requirements)

Core Meaning: Risk responses planned in advance but executed only if specific trigger conditions occur.
Expansion: Contingent response strategies are responses designed for use only under certain predefined conditions. The team agrees in advance what action to take and what trigger or warning sign will set it in motion, then holds the response in reserve. This keeps the team prepared for risks that may not happen, without spending effort until a trigger event actually occurs.
Contrast: Active risk response - an active response is implemented right away, whereas a contingent response is held in reserve and executed only when a predefined trigger fires.
Example: A project lines up a backup supplier in advance, to be engaged only if the primary supplier misses a defined delivery milestone.
Source: PMBOK Guide - contingent response strategies

Core Meaning: The process of ensuring physical resources are available as planned and monitoring their actual versus planned utilization.
Expansion: Control Resources is the monitoring and controlling process focused on physical resources - materials, equipment, supplies, and facilities. It checks that resources are available when and where the plan requires, compares planned against actual usage, and triggers corrective action when they diverge. It does not deal with managing the project team, which is handled separately.
Contrast: Acquire Resources - Acquire Resources obtains the team and physical resources, whereas Control Resources monitors and adjusts physical resource usage during execution.
Example: A construction PM tracks whether delivered concrete and rented equipment match the plan and reallocates them when usage starts to drift.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Control Resources process

Core Meaning: Summing work package cost estimates up through control accounts to build the total project cost.
Expansion: Cost aggregation is a technique in the Determine Budget process. Individual activity and work package estimates are rolled up into control accounts, and the control accounts are then rolled up into the overall project cost. This bottom-up summation produces the figures that feed the cost baseline.
Contrast: Cost estimating - estimating produces the cost of individual activities or packages, whereas aggregation rolls those estimates up into control accounts and the project total.
Example: A PM totals the costs of all work packages under each control account, then sums the control accounts to establish the project budget.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Determine Budget (cost aggregation)

Core Meaning: Predictions of a project's future cost performance, typically expressed as EAC, ETC, and TCPI.
Expansion: Cost forecasts project where a project's costs are heading based on performance data available at the time. They are usually expressed through earned value measures such as estimate at completion (EAC), estimate to complete (ETC), budget at completion (BAC), and the to-complete performance index (TCPI). They give stakeholders an early, data-based warning of overruns or underruns so they can act.
Contrast: Cost baseline - the baseline is the approved planned spend, whereas a cost forecast projects where actual costs are now trending.
Example: Midway through the project, the PM calculates an EAC showing the work will finish about 10 percent over budget at the current spend rate.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Control Costs (work performance information)

Core Meaning: Money spent during the project to prevent defects and to meet quality requirements.
Expansion: Cost of conformance is the part of the cost of quality spent up front to avoid failures. It combines prevention costs (training, documenting processes, quality planning) and appraisal costs (testing, inspection, audits). Spending sensibly on conformance generally lowers the larger cost of nonconformance incurred when defects slip through.
Contrast: Cost of nonconformance - cost of conformance is spent up front to avoid failures, whereas cost of nonconformance is incurred after failures occur, through rework and warranty costs.
Example: Spending on quality training, written procedures, and pre-release inspections is all cost of conformance.
Source: PMBOK Guide - cost of quality

Core Meaning: Understanding differences among individuals, groups, and organizations and adapting communication to fit them.
Expansion: Cultural awareness is a communication and interpersonal skill that involves recognizing how people from different backgrounds, organizations, and regions differ in values, expectations, and styles. The project manager uses that understanding to adapt the communication strategy, reducing misunderstanding and building trust across a diverse team. It is especially important on global and virtual projects.
Contrast: Ethnocentrism - cultural awareness recognizes and adapts to other cultures, whereas ethnocentrism judges them against one's own.
Example: A PM leading a team across India, Germany, and Brazil adjusts meeting times, directness of feedback, and communication channels to suit each culture.
Source: PMBOK Guide - communication skills (cultural awareness)

Core Meaning: A reference that defines the data fields, attributes, and allowable values for a data object.
Expansion: A data dictionary catalogs the detailed definitions behind a data model - each field's name, format, meaning, and permitted values. It gives analysts, developers, and stakeholders a single authoritative description of the data, reducing ambiguity and inconsistency. It is typically paired with a data model, which shows how the data entities relate.
Contrast: Data model - a data model depicts data entities and their relationships visually, whereas a data dictionary catalogs the field-level definitions.
Example: A business analyst documents a Customer record's fields - name length, ID format, and valid status codes - in a data dictionary so developers interpret them the same way.
Source: PMI Guide to Business Analysis - data dictionary

Core Meaning: A model depicting how data moves among external entities, processes, and data stores.
Expansion: A data flow diagram (DFD) shows how information enters a system, is transformed by processes, and is stored or sent out. It decomposes the system into processes and traces the data passing between them, external entities, and data stores. It is used in requirements and business analysis to understand and communicate how data is handled.
Contrast: Flowchart - a flowchart shows the sequence of process steps and decisions, whereas a data flow diagram emphasizes the movement and transformation of data.
Example: A DFD for an order system shows customer data flowing into an order-validation process and then into an inventory data store.
