Contrast: Project planning - initiation authorizes and frames the project, while planning works out the detailed how, when, and how much.
Example: A sponsor signs a charter authorizing a warehouse-automation project and names the project manager, completing initiation.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Initiating Process Group

Core Meaning: The collection of tools and techniques used to gather, integrate, and disseminate the outputs of project management processes.
Expansion: A PMIS is part of the organization's enterprise environmental factors and provides automated tools such as scheduling software, work authorization systems, configuration management systems, and information repositories. It supports planning, executing, and monitoring by giving the team access to schedules, budgets, and performance data. It is used throughout the project to collect and distribute information.
Contrast: Work performance information - the PMIS is the system, while work performance information is the analyzed data that flows through it.
Example: A team uses MS Project plus a shared dashboard and document repository as its PMIS to track schedule and cost status.
Source: PMBOK Guide - project management information system

Core Meaning: An identified area of project management defined by its knowledge requirements and described in terms of its component processes.
Expansion: In the PMBOK Guide (6th edition and earlier), knowledge areas group related processes by subject matter, such as Scope, Schedule, Cost, Quality, Resource, Communications, Risk, Procurement, Stakeholder, and Integration Management. There are ten knowledge areas, each with defined inputs, tools and techniques, and outputs. They cut across the process groups to organize the discipline.
Contrast: Process group - knowledge areas group processes by subject, while process groups group them by the point in the project lifecycle where they occur.
Example: Estimate Activity Durations belongs to the Project Schedule Management knowledge area but the Planning process group.
Source: PMBOK Guide (6th ed.) - Knowledge Areas

Core Meaning: A set of interrelated actions and activities performed to manage a project and produce a pre-specified result.
Expansion: A project management process takes inputs and applies tools and techniques to produce outputs, and these processes are organized into process groups and knowledge areas. Each process is performed at least once on a project, and many are repeated as the work progresses.
Contrast: Product-oriented process - project management processes manage the project, while product-oriented processes create the project's deliverables.
Example: Determine Budget is a project management process that converts cost estimates into an approved cost baseline.
Source: PMBOK Guide - project management process

Core Meaning: A graphic display of the project team members and their reporting relationships for a specific project.
Expansion: A project organization chart shows who is on the project team and how they report to one another, clarifying lines of authority and communication. It is a hierarchical-type resource representation produced during resource planning.
Contrast: Responsibility assignment matrix (RAM) - the org chart shows reporting structure, while a RAM links work packages to the people responsible for them.
Example: A project org chart shows the PM at the top with leads for design, build, and test reporting beneath.
Source: PMBOK Guide - project organization chart

Core Meaning: The overall effort of defining scope, objectives, and the course of action needed to achieve project goals.
Expansion: Project planning develops the project management plan and its subsidiary plans and baselines, defining how the project will be executed, monitored, and closed. Good planning is iterative and progressively elaborated as more information becomes available.
Contrast: Planning Process Group - planning as a concept is the broad activity, while the Planning Process Group is the formal grouping of planning processes.
Example: During planning, a team defines scope, builds a WBS, sequences activities, estimates costs, and sets baselines.
Source: Concept aligned with the PMBOK Guide Planning Process Group

Core Meaning: The knowledge area that applies the organization's quality policy to plan, manage, and control project and product quality requirements.
Expansion: Project Quality Management comprises Plan Quality Management, Manage Quality (quality assurance), and Control Quality. It works to ensure the project satisfies the needs for which it was undertaken, emphasizing prevention over inspection and continuous improvement. It addresses both the quality of the project's deliverables and the quality of the management processes used.
Contrast: Manage Quality vs Control Quality - Manage Quality is process- and assurance-focused, while Control Quality inspects deliverables for conformance.
Example: A team writes a quality management plan, audits processes, and inspects deliverables against checklists to keep defects within tolerance.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Project Quality Management knowledge area

Core Meaning: The act of collecting and distributing project information to stakeholders.
Expansion: Project reporting gathers performance data and packages it into reports such as status, progress, and forecast reports for the right audiences at the right time. It is a key part of communications management and supports informed decision-making.
Contrast: Work performance reports - project reporting is the activity, while work performance reports are the physical or electronic artifacts produced.
Example: A PM issues a weekly status report summarizing schedule, cost, and risk to the steering committee.
Source: PMBOK Guide - project reporting

Core Meaning: The knowledge area covering processes to identify, acquire, and manage the team and physical resources needed to complete the project.
Expansion: Project Resource Management includes Plan Resource Management, Estimate Activity Resources, Acquire Resources, Develop Team, Manage Team, and Control Resources. It addresses both human (team) resources and physical resources such as equipment, materials, and facilities. Its aim is to have the right resources available at the right time and place and to optimize their use.
Contrast: Acquire Resources vs Develop Team - acquiring obtains the resources, while developing builds the team's competencies and cohesion.
Example: A PM estimates needed roles, secures three engineers and lab equipment, then runs team-building and tracks utilization.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Project Resource Management knowledge area

Core Meaning: Methods used to score and rank seller proposals against established selection criteria to choose a contractor.
Expansion: Proposal evaluation techniques are used in Conduct Procurements to assess seller responses objectively. They often apply a weighting system, screening systems, independent estimates, and expert judgment to rate proposals on cost, technical merit, capability, and past performance. The goal is a defensible, consistent selection that reflects the buyer's priorities.
Contrast: Source selection criteria - the criteria define what is judged, while evaluation techniques are the methods used to apply those criteria.
Example: A buyer scores three bids on a weighted matrix covering price, experience, and approach, selecting the highest total.
Source: PMBOK Guide - proposal evaluation techniques

Core Meaning: A lean scheduling technique in which work or materials are produced only in response to actual downstream demand.
Expansion: A pull system limits work in progress by starting new work only when there is a signal of demand, rather than pushing work based on a forecast or schedule. Often visualized with kanban boards and signals, it reduces waste, overproduction, and inventory while exposing bottlenecks. It is a foundational concept in lean and many agile approaches.
Contrast: Push system - a push system produces to forecast and pushes output downstream, while a pull system produces only to actual demand.
Example: A kanban team pulls the next user story into development only when a slot frees up, capping work in progress at three items.
Source: Agile Practice Guide - lean and kanban concepts

Core Meaning: A risk that can only result in a loss or no loss, with no possibility of gain; also called insurable risk.
Expansion: Pure risk presents only downside outcomes, such as fire, theft, or accidents, and is typically transferable through insurance. It contrasts with business or speculative risk, which carries both upside and downside potential. Recognizing pure risks helps a project decide what to insure versus what to actively manage for opportunity.
Contrast: Business (speculative) risk - business risk can yield profit or loss, while pure risk offers only loss or no loss.
Example: The risk that a flood damages on-site equipment is a pure risk the project transfers via insurance.
Source: Classic risk-management classification referenced in PMI risk guidance

Core Meaning: Structured lists of items to inspect or steps to perform, used to verify that deliverables meet quality requirements.
Expansion: Quality checklists are component- or activity-specific lists created during Plan Quality Management and used in Control Quality to confirm that required steps were performed and that deliverables conform to standards. They promote consistency and completeness in inspections.
Contrast: Quality metrics - a checklist confirms steps and items, while a quality metric defines a measurable attribute and its tolerance.
Example: An installation team uses a checklist to confirm every wiring step before sign-off.
Source: PMBOK Guide - quality checklists

Core Meaning: The organizational framework of policies, processes, and procedures used to plan, execute, and control quality.
Expansion: A quality management system (QMS) provides the structure through which an organization directs and coordinates its quality activities, often aligned to standards such as ISO 9000. On projects it supplies the policies, procedures, and resources that the quality management plan draws upon. A QMS supports continuous improvement and consistent, repeatable quality outcomes.
Contrast: Quality management plan - the QMS is the organization-wide framework, while the quality management plan applies and tailors it to a specific project.
Example: A manufacturer's ISO 9001 QMS supplies the documented procedures the project team follows for inspections and audits.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Manage Quality (organizational quality system)

Core Meaning: A condition or capability used to assess conformance by validating the acceptability of an attribute for the quality of a result.
Expansion: A quality requirement specifies the standard a deliverable's attribute must meet to be acceptable, giving an objective basis for validation. It is captured during requirements collection and quality planning and verified during Control Quality.
Contrast: Acceptance criteria - quality requirements define attribute conformance, while acceptance criteria are the broader set of conditions for a deliverable to be accepted.
Example: A requirement that a web page load in under two seconds is a quality requirement validated by performance testing.
Source: PMBOK Guide - quality requirement

Core Meaning: Written sets of questions designed to gather information from a large number of respondents quickly.
Expansion: Questionnaires and surveys are a data-gathering technique used to accumulate information from many people, often geographically dispersed, when statistical analysis is appropriate. On projects they support requirements collection, stakeholder analysis, and quality or satisfaction measurement.
Contrast: Interviews - surveys reach many respondents with standardized questions, while interviews gather deeper, individualized input from a few.
Example: A team surveys 500 users to prioritize desired features before planning the release.
Source: PMBOK Guide - questionnaires and surveys

Core Meaning: Influence derived from a person's charisma, character, and the respect and admiration others hold for them.
Expansion: Referent power is a basis of organizational power grounded in personality, reputation, and the relationships a leader builds, rather than position or rewards. People follow because they like, respect, or want to identify with the leader. It tends to be durable and to foster genuine commitment, though it takes time to develop.
Contrast: Expert power - expert power comes from specialized knowledge, while referent power comes from personal charisma and respect.
Example: Team members go the extra mile for a respected PM whose integrity they admire, reflecting referent power.
Source: PMBOK Guide - types of power

Core Meaning: Two team members working together on the same task from different locations using virtual collaboration tools.
Expansion: Remote pairing replicates the benefits of face-to-face pair programming or pair working when team members are distributed, using shared screens, voice, and video links. It supports knowledge sharing, real-time review, and collective ownership across dispersed agile teams.
Contrast: Co-location - co-location places people physically together, while remote pairing achieves close collaboration virtually.
Example: Two developers in different cities share a screen over video to write and review code together in real time.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A bar chart that displays the amount of a resource scheduled to work over a series of time periods.
Expansion: A resource histogram shows resource allocation over time, with bars representing planned usage per period and often a line marking maximum availability. It helps identify over-allocation and supports resource leveling and smoothing decisions.
Contrast: Gantt chart - a histogram shows resource quantity over time, while a Gantt chart shows activity timing and duration.
Example: A histogram reveals a tester is booked 60 hours in a 40-hour week, signaling over-allocation.
Source: PMBOK Guide - resource histogram

Core Meaning: An individual with management authority over one or more resources used by the project.
Expansion: A resource manager, often a functional or line manager, owns the people or physical resources the project needs and negotiates their availability with the project manager. In matrix organizations this shared authority makes the relationship between the PM and resource managers critical to securing committed resources.
Contrast: Project manager - the PM directs the project work, while the resource manager controls the supply of the resources that perform it.
Example: A functional engineering manager who lends two developers to a project is acting as their resource manager.
Source: PMBOK Guide - functional / resource manager role

Core Meaning: Scheduling techniques that adjust the schedule based on resource supply and demand, including resource leveling and resource smoothing.
Expansion: Resource optimization techniques modify the project schedule to address resource availability constraints. Resource leveling adjusts start and finish dates to balance demand with supply and can change the critical path and the project end date. Resource smoothing adjusts activities only within their available float, so it does not delay completion but may not fully resolve over-allocation.
Contrast: Resource leveling vs resource smoothing - leveling can extend the project end date to honor resource limits, while smoothing stays within float and protects the end date.
Example: A planner levels resources to avoid double-booking an engineer, accepting a one-week slip.
Source: PMBOK Guide - resource optimization techniques

Core Meaning: A core value in the PMI Code of Ethics: showing high regard for ourselves, others, and the resources entrusted to us.
Expansion: Respect is one of the four core values of the PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct, alongside responsibility, fairness, and honesty. It calls for an environment of mutual trust and courtesy, listening to others' viewpoints, and stewarding the people, money, and reputation entrusted to us. It includes respecting cultural differences and negotiating in good faith.
Contrast: Fairness - respect concerns regard and courtesy toward people and resources, while fairness concerns impartial and objective decision-making.
Example: A PM who acknowledges a dissenting team member's view and protects company assets is demonstrating respect.
Source: PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct - Respect

Core Meaning: The elapsed time an item waits in a queue before active work on it begins.
Expansion: Response time is a kanban and flow metric measuring the delay between when an item is requested or committed and when work actually starts on it. Reducing response time, alongside cycle time, improves a team's overall lead time and responsiveness to demand.
Contrast: Cycle time - response time is the wait before work starts, while cycle time is the active duration once work has begun.
Example: A support ticket sits in the backlog for two days before a developer picks it up, a two-day response time.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Influence a project manager exerts through the ability to grant benefits or incentives.
Expansion: Reward power is based on the leader's capacity to provide positive outcomes such as recognition, bonuses, promotions, or desirable assignments in exchange for performance. It can effectively motivate when rewards are valued and fairly distributed, but it weakens if rewards are seen as unfair or beyond the leader's control. It is one of the classic bases of organizational power.
Contrast: Coercive power - reward power motivates with positive incentives, while coercive power compels through the threat of penalties.
Example: A PM offers the top performer a choice assignment and public recognition, exercising reward power.
Source: PMBOK Guide - types of power

Core Meaning: Grouping identified risks by source, project area affected, or other category to find concentrations of risk.
Expansion: Risk categorization organizes risks using structures such as the risk breakdown structure (by source), the WBS (by affected area), or by project phase. It reveals where risk exposure is concentrated, helping target risk responses and root-cause analysis. It is performed during Perform Qualitative Risk Analysis.
Contrast: Risk breakdown structure - the RBS is the hierarchical taxonomy of risk sources, while risk categorization is the act of grouping risks using it or other schemes.
Example: Sorting project risks by RBS reveals that most exposure comes from external supplier sources.
Source: PMBOK Guide - risk categorization

Core Meaning: The degree to which an individual risk is related to or interconnected with other risks.
Expansion: Risk connectivity is one of the parameters assessed during qualitative risk analysis to prioritize risks beyond probability and impact. Highly connected risks can trigger or amplify others, so they may warrant elevated attention even if their standalone impact is moderate.
Contrast: Risk propinquity - connectivity measures links to other risks, while propinquity measures how much stakeholders perceive a risk to matter.
Example: A supplier-delay risk that would cascade into testing and launch risks is rated high in connectivity.
Source: PMBOK Guide - assessment of other risk parameters

Core Meaning: The degree to which the risk owner is able to control the risk's outcome.
Expansion: Risk controllability is a qualitative-analysis parameter indicating how much influence the owner has over whether and how a risk materializes. Low-controllability risks may push a team toward acceptance or contingency planning, while high-controllability risks favor active mitigation.
Contrast: Risk manageability - controllability concerns influencing the outcome itself, while manageability concerns how easily the occurrence or impact can be handled.
Example: Weather risk on an outdoor event has low controllability, so the team plans contingencies rather than mitigation.
Source: PMBOK Guide - assessment of other risk parameters

Core Meaning: The ease with which the occurrence, or imminent occurrence, of a risk can be detected and recognized.
Expansion: Risk detectability is a qualitative parameter describing how readily warning signs of a risk can be sensed. Low detectability is dangerous because a risk may strike with little warning, raising its priority for monitoring or early-warning indicators.
Contrast: Risk dormancy - detectability is about how easily a risk is noticed, while dormancy is the delay between occurrence and discovery of its impact.
Example: A latent code defect with no obvious symptoms has low detectability and is flagged for extra testing.
Source: PMBOK Guide - assessment of other risk parameters

Core Meaning: The period that may elapse after a risk occurs before its impact becomes apparent.
Expansion: Risk dormancy measures the lag between a risk event happening and the project discovering its consequences. Long dormancy is concerning because damage can accumulate undetected, so such risks may justify additional monitoring or detection mechanisms.
Contrast: Risk proximity - dormancy is the delay after occurrence before impact is seen, while proximity is the time before the risk might occur at all.
Example: A subtle data-corruption risk whose effects surface only months later has high dormancy.
Source: PMBOK Guide - assessment of other risk parameters

Core Meaning: The ease with which the risk owner can manage the occurrence or impact of a risk.
Expansion: Risk manageability is a qualitative parameter reflecting how practical it is to respond to a risk. Easily managed risks can be addressed efficiently, while hard-to-manage risks may need earlier action, escalation, or contingency reserves.
Contrast: Risk controllability - manageability is about how easily a response can be applied, while controllability is about influence over the outcome itself.
Example: A risk with a well-known, inexpensive workaround is rated highly manageable.
Source: PMBOK Guide - assessment of other risk parameters

Core Meaning: The additional return demanded above the risk-free rate to compensate a party for accepting risk.
Expansion: A risk premium is the extra compensation, over and above a risk-free rate of return, that an investor or contractor expects for bearing uncertainty. In projects it appears when risk is transferred, for example a contractor pricing in a margin for accepting a fixed-price commitment. Larger or less predictable risks command larger premiums.
Contrast: Contingency reserve - a risk premium is compensation paid to a party for taking on risk, while a contingency reserve is budget the project sets aside for known risks.
Example: A contractor adds a 10 percent risk premium to a fixed-price bid to cover the chance of cost overruns it must absorb.
Source: Risk and procurement finance concept referenced in PMI guidance

Core Meaning: The degree to which a risk is perceived to matter by one or more stakeholders.
Expansion: Risk propinquity is a qualitative parameter capturing the subjective importance stakeholders attach to a risk, which can differ from its objective probability and impact. A risk seen as highly significant by key stakeholders may receive priority attention for political or reputational reasons.
Contrast: Risk connectivity - propinquity is about perceived significance to stakeholders, while connectivity is about a risk's links to other risks.
Example: A minor data-privacy risk that the CEO cares deeply about has high propinquity and is escalated.
Source: PMBOK Guide - assessment of other risk parameters

Core Meaning: The length of time before a risk might have an impact on one or more project objectives.
Expansion: Risk proximity indicates how soon a risk could affect the project, helping teams prioritize near-term risks for earlier response planning. Risks with close proximity generally demand more immediate attention than those further in the future.
Contrast: Risk urgency - proximity is when the risk might occur, while urgency is how soon a response must be taken to remain effective.
Example: A risk that could strike during next week's cutover has high proximity and is addressed first.
Source: PMBOK Guide - assessment of other risk parameters

Core Meaning: The time available to implement a risk response before the opportunity to respond effectively is lost.
Expansion: Risk urgency assesses how quickly a response must be put in place, so risks needing near-term action can be flagged and prioritized. It is often combined with proximity to identify risks for accelerated response planning.
Contrast: Risk proximity - urgency is how soon a response must act, while proximity is how soon the risk itself might occur.
Example: A risk whose mitigation must be procured within days to be useful is marked high urgency.
Source: PMBOK Guide - assessment of other risk parameters

Core Meaning: A high-level early estimate of cost or duration with a wide accuracy range, typically -25 percent to +75 percent.
Expansion: A ROM estimate is produced early in the project, often during initiation, when little detail is known, to give stakeholders a ballpark figure for go/no-go decisions. Its accuracy range is broad, commonly cited as -25 percent to +75 percent, and it is refined into more precise estimates as the project progresses. It supports early budgeting and feasibility rather than firm commitments.
Contrast: Definitive estimate - a ROM is a wide-range early estimate, while a definitive estimate (-5 percent to +10 percent) is precise and made with detailed information.
Example: At charter time a PM gives a ROM of 500K to 1.5M for a new system before requirements are detailed.
Source: PMBOK Guide - estimate accuracy ranges

Core Meaning: A business analysis model that captures the business rules and policies a solution must enforce.
Expansion: A rule model documents the constraints, conditions, and logic that govern how a business operates, such as eligibility criteria, calculations, and decision policies. It is one of the analysis models used in business analysis to make implicit policies explicit so they can be validated and built into a solution. Separating rules from process makes them easier to maintain when policies change.
Contrast: Process Model - a process model shows the sequence of activities, whereas a rule model defines the policies and constraints that govern those activities.
Example: Documenting that a loan applicant must be at least 18 and have a credit score above 650 as enforceable rules in a lending system.
Source: PMI Guide to Business Analysis - rule model

Core Meaning: A control chart heuristic that flags seven consecutive points on the same side of the mean as a likely non-random pattern.
Expansion: The rule of seven is a rule of thumb used in statistical process control: when seven or more consecutive data points fall on one side of the mean, the process is treated as out of control even if no point breaches the control limits. The pattern suggests an assignable (special) cause rather than normal random variation and should be investigated. It helps detect drift or bias that single-point checks would miss.
Contrast: Control limits - a point outside the control limits signals an out-of-control process directly, whereas the rule of seven detects non-random runs that stay within the limits.
Example: On a control chart tracking weld defects, seven straight measurements above the mean prompt the team to investigate a possible equipment problem.
Source: PMBOK Guide - control chart / rule of seven

Core Meaning: The collection of information used to describe and control the project schedule.
Expansion: Schedule data is the supporting information behind the schedule model, including milestones, activities, attributes, assumptions, and constraints, along with items such as resource histograms and alternative schedules. It provides the detail needed to analyze, report, and control the schedule over the life of the project.
Contrast: Project schedule - the project schedule is the output that presents planned dates, whereas schedule data is the broader supporting information behind it.
Example: The resource requirements, scheduling assumptions, and contingency reserves stored alongside a project's network diagram.
Source: PMBOK Guide - schedule data

Core Meaning: A tool that provides schedule component names, definitions, relationships, and formats to support a scheduling method.
Expansion: A scheduling tool is the software or system used to build and maintain the schedule model by applying a chosen scheduling method such as the critical path method. Combined with the scheduling method and manual inputs, it generates and updates the project schedule.
Contrast: Scheduling method - the scheduling method is the technique (e.g., critical path method), whereas the scheduling tool is the instrument that applies it.
Example: Using Microsoft Project or Primavera P6 to calculate the critical path and produce a Gantt chart.
Source: PMBOK Guide - scheduling tool

Core Meaning: A business analysis model that structures and bounds the features, functions, and limits of the domain under analysis.
Expansion: Scope models organize what is and is not included in a solution, clarifying boundaries, interfaces, and major capabilities. Examples include context diagrams, ecosystem maps, feature models, and goal models. They give stakeholders a shared, visual understanding of scope before detailed requirements are written.
Contrast: Process Model - a process model details how work flows, whereas a scope model defines the boundaries and high-level content of the domain.
Example: Drawing a context diagram to show which external systems and actors interact with a new order-management application.
Source: PMI Guide to Business Analysis - scope model

Core Meaning: The sellers chosen, after evaluation, to provide contracted products or services.
Expansion: Selected sellers are the vendors judged to be in a competitive range based on proposal evaluation and with whom the buyer is prepared to negotiate or has awarded a contract. They are an output of conducting procurements.
Contrast: Prospective sellers - prospective sellers are all candidates invited to bid, whereas selected sellers are those chosen after evaluation.
Example: After scoring three vendor proposals, the buyer names the top vendor as the selected seller and awards the contract.
Source: PMBOK Guide - selected sellers

Core Meaning: The agreed split of cost savings or overruns between buyer and seller in an incentive contract.
Expansion: In incentive-fee contracts, the sharing ratio defines how a difference between target cost and actual cost is divided between buyer and seller, written as buyer:seller (for example 80:20). It motivates the seller to control costs because they keep a share of any savings and absorb a share of any overrun. The ratio is central to calculating final fee and the point of total assumption in fixed-price incentive contracts.
Contrast: Point of Total Assumption - the sharing ratio sets how over/underruns are split, whereas the point of total assumption is the cost level above which the seller bears all additional cost.
Example: With an 80:20 sharing ratio, a 10,000 dollar underrun gives the buyer 8,000 dollars and the seller 2,000 dollars.
Source: PMBOK Guide - incentive contracts / share ratio

Core Meaning: A conflict-resolution technique that stresses areas of agreement and downplays differences.
Expansion: Smoothing, also called accommodating, manages conflict by emphasizing points of agreement and yielding one's position to maintain harmony. It can calm a heated situation temporarily but does not resolve the underlying issue, which may resurface later. It is most useful when preserving the relationship matters more than the specific point in dispute.
Contrast: Collaborate - collaborating works through differences to reach a win-win solution, whereas smoothing glosses over them for short-term harmony.
Example: A project manager defuses a tense meeting by focusing on the team's shared goal and setting the disagreement aside for later.
Source: PMBOK Guide - conflict resolution techniques

Core Meaning: A source of process variation that is not inherent to the system and signals an assignable problem.
Expansion: A special cause (or assignable cause) is variation arising from a specific, unusual factor rather than the normal operation of the process. On a control chart it shows up as points outside the control limits or as non-random patterns within them, indicating the process is out of control and needs investigation. Identifying and removing special causes restores the process to a stable, predictable state.
Contrast: Common cause - common-cause variation is the random noise inherent in a stable process, whereas special-cause variation comes from an identifiable, external disturbance.
Example: A sudden spike in defects traced to a miscalibrated machine is a special cause.
Source: PMBOK Guide - control chart / special cause variation

Core Meaning: A short, time-boxed effort to research or prototype in order to reduce uncertainty.
Expansion: In agile, a spike is a time-boxed investigation used to answer a technical or functional question, gain knowledge, or reduce risk before committing to an estimate or design. Spikes produce learning or a throwaway prototype rather than shippable product. They are common when a team faces an unfamiliar technology or an ambiguous requirement.
Contrast: User story - a user story delivers customer-facing value, whereas a spike produces information needed to deliver that value.
Example: A team spends two days building a throwaway proof of concept to test whether a new API can handle the expected load.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A logical relationship in which a successor activity must start before its predecessor can finish.
Expansion: Start-to-finish is the rarest of the four precedence relationships: the predecessor (from) activity cannot finish until the successor (to) activity has started. It is typically used to model handovers, such as a new shift starting before the previous one ends. Most schedules rely on finish-to-start instead.
Contrast: Finish-to-start - in finish-to-start the predecessor must finish before the successor starts, the reverse logic of start-to-finish.
Example: The new security guard's shift must begin before the outgoing guard's shift can end.
Source: PMBOK Guide - start-to-finish relationship

Core Meaning: A logical relationship in which a successor activity cannot start until its predecessor has started.
Expansion: In a start-to-start relationship, the successor (to) activity can begin only once the predecessor (from) activity has begun, often with a lead or lag. It is used when two activities can run partly in parallel once the first is underway.
Contrast: Finish-to-start - finish-to-start ties the successor's start to the predecessor's finish, whereas start-to-start ties it to the predecessor's start.
Example: Pouring concrete can start two days after leveling the ground begins (SS with a 2-day lag).
Source: PMBOK Guide - start-to-start relationship

Core Meaning: A technique that arranges user stories by user workflow and priority to build shared understanding of a product.
Expansion: Story mapping organizes user stories into a two-dimensional map: a backbone of user activities across the top in the order users perform them, with supporting stories prioritized vertically beneath each. It helps teams visualize the whole product, identify gaps, and slice releases that deliver coherent value. The map keeps the user journey visible rather than reducing the backlog to a flat list.
Contrast: Product backlog - a product backlog is a flat prioritized list, whereas a story map adds the dimension of user workflow and helps shape that list.
Example: A team maps the steps of an online checkout across the top and stacks detailed stories under each step to plan its first release.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A relative unit estimating the effort to implement a backlog item rather than its calendar duration.
Expansion: Story points express the relative size of a user story by combining effort, complexity, and uncertainty, often using a Fibonacci-like scale. Because they are relative and team-specific, they let teams estimate quickly and compute velocity without committing to exact hours. Point values should not be compared across different teams.
Contrast: Ideal Time - ideal time estimates effort in absolute hours or days, whereas story points are relative and unitless.
Example: A team scores a simple login screen at 2 points and a complex reporting feature at 13.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A prototyping technique that shows sequence or navigation through a series of images.
Expansion: Storyboarding illustrates how a user moves through a process or interface as an ordered set of sketches or screens, making flow and interactions tangible before building. It is used to elicit feedback and clarify requirements early.
Contrast: Wireframes - a wireframe is a static blueprint of a single screen, whereas a storyboard strings screens together to show a flow.
Example: Sketching the five screens a customer steps through to register and place a first order.
Source: PMBOK Guide - prototypes / storyboarding

Core Meaning: A method that expenses an asset's cost in equal amounts over its useful life.
Expansion: Straight-line depreciation spreads the depreciable cost of an asset evenly across each period of its useful life, producing a constant annual expense. It contrasts with accelerated methods that load more depreciation into the early years. Project managers encounter it in business cases and lifecycle cost analysis when justifying capital investments.
Contrast: Accelerated depreciation - accelerated methods expense more in early years, whereas straight-line spreads the cost evenly across the asset's life.
Example: A 50,000 dollar machine with a 5-year life depreciates at 10,000 dollars per year.
Source: PMBOK Guide - depreciation (cost management)

Core Meaning: A matrix structure in which the project manager holds most of the authority over resources and budget.
Expansion: In a strong matrix, project managers are typically full-time, have significant authority, and may have administrative staff, while functional managers retain ownership of their staff between assignments. It sits at the project-manager-favoring end of the matrix spectrum, with the balanced and weak forms giving the PM progressively less power. It improves project focus while keeping functional expertise intact.
Contrast: Weak matrix - in a weak matrix the functional manager holds most authority and the PM acts more as a coordinator, the opposite of a strong matrix.
Example: A PM who controls the budget and assigns engineers full-time to a project while they still report to engineering managers for career matters.
Source: PMBOK Guide - organizational structures

Core Meaning: The tendency to delay starting work until just before a deadline, consuming any safety margin.
Expansion: Named in critical chain theory, student syndrome describes people putting off effort until the last possible moment, so any padding built into an estimate is wasted and a late surprise leaves no recovery time. It is one reason individual task buffers tend to disappear. Critical chain counters it by removing local safety and pooling it into shared project and feeding buffers.
Contrast: Parkinson's Law - Parkinson's law says work expands to fill available time, whereas student syndrome is specifically the delayed start that burns the buffer up front.
Example: A team member with a two-week task does nothing for the first week and a half, then scrambles and still finishes late.
Source: Critical chain / Theory of Constraints (Goldratt)

Core Meaning: An agile practice where multiple team members focus together on one work item to finish it.
Expansion: Swarming has the whole team or several members converge on a single backlog item or blocked task to complete it quickly, rather than each working separate items in parallel. It reduces work in progress, accelerates flow, and helps clear impediments. It is common in Scrum and Kanban when finishing started work is more valuable than starting new work.
Contrast: Mobbing - mobbing has the team work together continuously as a default way of working, whereas swarming is the targeted convergence on one item to get it done.
Example: Near the end of a sprint, three developers drop their own tasks to jointly finish the last story needed for the increment.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Deep expertise in one specialty paired with broad competence across other areas.
Expansion: A T-shaped person has deep skill in a core discipline (the vertical bar) plus enough breadth to contribute outside it (the horizontal bar). Agile teams value T-shaped members because they can swap in to help across the workflow, reducing bottlenecks and improving cross-functional flow. It contrasts with narrow specialists who can only do one job.
Contrast: I-shaped Skills - I-shaped people have deep skill in one area only, whereas T-shaped people add broad skills across others.
Example: A tester who is an expert in automation but can also help with requirements analysis and basic coding.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The part of the resource management plan covering how and when team members are acquired, managed, and released.
Expansion: Sometimes called the staffing management plan, this component describes how human resources will be brought onto the project, their roles and timing, training and recognition approaches, and how they will be released when no longer needed. It helps ensure the right people are available at the right time and that resource costs end when work ends. It is part of broader resource planning rather than a standalone plan.
Contrast: Team charter - a team charter sets the team's ground rules and values, whereas the team management plan addresses staffing logistics and timing.
Example: A plan showing testers join in month three, ramp to full time during system testing, and roll off after acceptance.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Evaluations of a team's effectiveness as a unit, used to improve how it works together.
Expansion: Team performance assessments are the project manager's appraisal of how well the team functions together, looking at indicators such as improved skills, lower staff turnover, and greater cohesion. They are an output of developing the team and inform actions to strengthen collaboration. They focus on the team as a whole rather than on individual appraisals.
Contrast: Performance Reviews - individual performance reviews assess a single member's work, whereas team performance assessments gauge the effectiveness of the team as a unit.
Example: After team-building, the PM notes faster decision-making and lower turnover as evidence of improved team performance.
Source: PMBOK Guide - team performance assessments

Core Meaning: Comparing actual technical achievement against planned technical measures to detect deviations.
Expansion: Technical performance analysis (or measurement) compares delivered technical metrics, such as weight, capacity, or response time, against the values planned during planning. Significant variances signal scope or quality risks and may indicate the project will not meet requirements. It is used in risk monitoring and quality control to catch problems early.
Contrast: Earned value analysis - earned value tracks cost and schedule performance, whereas technical performance analysis tracks achievement of technical requirements.
Example: Measuring that a prototype drone weighs 1.4 kg against a 1.2 kg target, flagging a design risk.
Source: PMBOK Guide - technical performance analysis (data analysis)

Core Meaning: Project documents that define how deliverables will be checked against quality requirements.
Expansion: Test and evaluation documents describe the activities, criteria, and records used to verify that deliverables meet quality goals, and they capture results during control quality. They are created when managing quality and provide evidence of conformance.
Contrast: Quality Checklists - a quality checklist is a simple list of items to verify, whereas test and evaluation documents are the broader set defining and recording how testing is done.
Example: Test plans, inspection sheets, and evaluation reports prepared for a new medical device.
Source: PMBOK Guide - test and evaluation documents

Core Meaning: Fixing a short, set duration for a piece of work and stopping when the time is up.
Expansion: Timeboxing allocates a fixed, usually short period to an activity; when the box expires, work stops and results are reviewed regardless of completeness. It limits scope creep, forces prioritization, and creates a predictable cadence, as in sprints and spikes. The deadline is fixed while the amount of work delivered flexes.
Contrast: Spike - a spike is a specific time-boxed investigation, whereas timeboxing is the general practice of fixing a duration for any activity.
Example: Capping a brainstorming session at 30 minutes and moving on when the time runs out.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An organization-wide philosophy of continuous improvement that engages everyone in raising quality.
Expansion: TQM is a management approach in which all members of an organization continuously improve processes, products, and culture to meet or exceed customer expectations. It stresses customer focus, employee involvement, fact-based decisions, and ongoing process improvement. It influenced modern quality practice and underlies tools such as the Deming cycle.
Contrast: Six Sigma - Six Sigma is a data-driven methodology focused on reducing defects and variation, whereas TQM is a broader organization-wide quality philosophy.
Example: A manufacturer empowers every employee to suggest process improvements and tracks customer satisfaction as a company-wide goal.
Source: PMBOK Guide - quality management theories (TQM)

Core Meaning: A group decision in which everyone agrees on a single course of action.
Expansion: Unanimity is reached when every member of a group supports the same decision, often pursued through techniques like the Delphi method or fist-of-five to surface and resolve dissent. It carries strong buy-in but can be slow or hard to achieve.
Contrast: Majority - majority needs only more than half to agree, whereas unanimity requires everyone's agreement.
Example: A change control board approving a baseline change only when all members consent.
Source: PMBOK Guide - decision-making (voting)

Core Meaning: Analyzing a product's functions to deliver them at the lowest cost without losing performance.
Expansion: Value analysis examines the required functions of a product or process and finds ways to provide those functions more economically while maintaining quality and performance. Closely tied to value engineering, it targets unnecessary cost rather than simply cutting features. It is often applied to improve an existing design or product.
Contrast: Value Engineering - value engineering typically applies this thinking during design to optimize value up front, whereas value analysis often applies it to an existing product; the terms are frequently used interchangeably.
Example: Redesigning a bracket to use less material and a cheaper process while still meeting its load requirement.
Source: PMBOK Guide - value analysis / value engineering

Core Meaning: A systematic approach to optimizing value by improving function relative to cost.
Expansion: Value engineering analyzes the functions of a project or product to achieve required performance at the lowest lifecycle cost, usually during design. It seeks to eliminate unnecessary cost while preserving or enhancing function, quality, and reliability. It is also called value analysis or value management.
Contrast: Value Analysis - value analysis usually refers to applying the same technique to an existing product, whereas value engineering is typically applied during design; the terms overlap heavily.
Example: During design, a team substitutes a standard component for a custom one, cutting cost while meeting all specifications.
Source: PMBOK Guide - value engineering

Core Meaning: Uncertainty about a key characteristic of a planned event, activity, or decision.
Expansion: Variability risk arises when a feature of the work could vary within a range, such as productivity, weather, or defect rates, but the exact value is unknown. It is addressed with techniques like sensitivity analysis and Monte Carlo simulation that model the range of possible outcomes. It is one type of uncertainty within the broader category of non-event risks.
Contrast: Ambiguity Risk - ambiguity risk comes from lack of knowledge or understanding, whereas variability risk comes from known factors whose values can fluctuate within a range.
Example: The number of defects found in testing could be anywhere from 10 to 100, affecting rework effort.
Source: PMI Standard for Risk Management - variability risk

Core Meaning: An actual condition that differs from the expected condition in the baseline plan.
Expansion: Variation is any difference between what was planned or expected and what actually occurs, whether in cost, schedule, scope, or a process measurement. In quality management, variation is analyzed to distinguish normal common-cause noise from special-cause problems. Understanding variation helps decide whether corrective action is needed.
Contrast: Special Cause - special cause is one assignable source of variation, whereas variation is the broader difference from expected conditions.
Example: A task planned for five days that actually takes seven shows a schedule variation.
Source: PMBOK Guide - quality / variation

Core Meaning: A concise description of the desired future end state a project or product aims to achieve.
Expansion: A vision articulates the aspirational outcome and purpose that gives a project or product direction and aligns stakeholders. In agile, a product vision keeps the team focused on the bigger goal behind the backlog and informs prioritization. A clear vision lets people make consistent decisions without constant direction.
Contrast: Objective - an objective is a specific measurable target, whereas a vision is the broader aspirational end state that objectives serve.
Example: The statement 'make local public transport so easy that residents prefer it to driving' framing a transit app project.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A static, low-detail blueprint of a user interface showing layout and basic functionality.
Expansion: Wireframes are schematic diagrams that map the structure, content placement, and key functions of a screen without final styling or color. They let stakeholders review layout and flow cheaply before visual design or development begins.
Contrast: Storyboarding - a wireframe shows the static layout of a single screen, whereas a storyboard links screens to show a sequence or flow.
Example: A gray-box sketch of an app's home screen showing where the search bar, menu, and results list go.
Source: PMBOK Guide - prototypes (wireframes)

Core Meaning: A conflict-resolution technique of retreating from or avoiding the conflict.
Expansion: Withdrawing, also called avoiding, handles conflict by stepping back and postponing the issue rather than engaging it. It can be appropriate to let tempers cool or when the issue is trivial, but it leaves the underlying problem unresolved and is generally a temporary measure. It is usually considered the least effective long-term approach.
Contrast: Collaborate - collaborating confronts the issue to find a mutually agreeable solution, whereas withdrawing avoids it altogether.
Example: A project manager sidesteps a heated argument by tabling it until a later meeting, without resolving it.
Source: PMBOK Guide - conflict resolution techniques

Core Meaning: An Extreme Programming practice of using a shared, simple story or analogy to describe how a system works.
Expansion: The metaphor in Extreme Programming gives the team and stakeholders a common, plain-language picture of the system's architecture and behavior, guiding naming and design decisions. It substitutes a vivid shared mental model for heavy upfront design documentation. A good metaphor keeps everyone's understanding aligned as the system evolves.
Contrast: Architecture document - a formal architecture document specifies structure in detail, whereas an XP metaphor conveys a shared conceptual picture informally.
Example: Describing an e-commerce system as a 'shopping cart and checkout lane' so everyone shares the same mental model.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: PMBOK 8's renaming of the five Process Groups into Focus Areas that organize the 40 project management processes.
Expansion: In the PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition, the five traditional Process Groups (Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring and Controlling, Closing) are reframed as Focus Areas. They group the 40 processes by the kind of work being performed rather than implying a strict sequence, reinforcing that project work is iterative and overlapping. The terminology sits alongside the 7 performance domains carried forward from the principles-based structure.
Contrast: Performance domain - performance domains are outcome-oriented groupings of project activity, while Focus Areas re-label the process-based Process Groups.
Example: A team maps each of the 40 processes to one of the five Focus Areas when tailoring its methodology for a new construction project.
Source: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition - Focus Areas

Core Meaning: A PMO positioned at the top of the organization that aligns the entire portfolio of projects and programs with enterprise strategy.
Expansion: An Enterprise PMO operates at the strategic, organization-wide level rather than within a single department or project. It connects executive strategy to portfolio prioritization, governance, and benefits realization, and typically reports to a C-level executive. Unlike a delivery-focused departmental PMO, the EPMO emphasizes strategic alignment and value delivery across the whole enterprise.
Contrast: Project management office - a standard PMO usually supports projects within a unit, whereas an EPMO governs strategy and portfolio decisions enterprise-wide.
Example: The EPMO reviews every proposed initiative against corporate strategic objectives before funding is released.
Source: PMI Pulse of the Profession - enterprise PMO

Core Meaning: A strategic performance framework that measures organizational health across financial, customer, internal process, and learning-and-growth perspectives.
Expansion: Developed by Kaplan and Norton, the Balanced Scorecard translates strategy into a balanced set of objectives and metrics beyond purely financial measures. The four perspectives - financial, customer, internal business process, and learning and growth - give a holistic view of performance. In a project context it helps ensure benefits map to strategic outcomes rather than only cost and schedule.
Contrast: Key performance indicator - a KPI is a single measure of progress, while the Balanced Scorecard is a structured framework organizing many measures across four perspectives.
Example: A program reports its outcomes on a balanced scorecard so executives see customer satisfaction and capability growth alongside financial return.
Source: Kaplan and Norton - the Balanced Scorecard

Core Meaning: A visual chart where team members record their daily mood to surface morale trends over time.
Expansion: A Niko-Niko calendar, also called a mood board, is an information radiator on which each team member marks a simple happy, neutral, or unhappy face each day. Tracking these moods across a sprint reveals patterns that can prompt the team to address morale or impediment issues in retrospectives.
Contrast: Mood Chart - the two are essentially synonymous, though Niko-Niko specifically refers to the smiley-face daily logging that originated in agile practice.
Example: After two weeks the Niko-Niko calendar shows a dip in morale every Thursday, prompting the team to investigate the recurring late-day deployment crunch.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A practice where a business representative, developer, and tester jointly review a requirement before development to build shared understanding.
Expansion: The Three Amigos meeting brings together three perspectives - business or product, development, and testing or quality - to examine a user story or its acceptance criteria before work begins. It surfaces ambiguities, edge cases, and differing assumptions early, and is closely associated with behavior-driven development.
Contrast: Backlog refinement - refinement prepares and estimates many backlog items, while the Three Amigos focuses three roles on clarifying a single story's acceptance criteria.
Example: Before coding the payment story, the analyst, developer, and tester hold a Three Amigos session and discover an unhandled currency-conversion case.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A Scrum metaphor distinguishing those committed to the work (pigs) from those merely involved (chickens).
Expansion: Drawn from a fable about a pig and chicken opening a restaurant, the metaphor labels people committed and accountable for delivering the sprint as pigs, and interested but not accountable stakeholders as chickens. Historically it governed who could speak in the daily scrum, though modern Scrum has largely retired the terminology as potentially demeaning.
Contrast: Stakeholder - all chickens and pigs are stakeholders; the metaphor specifically separates committed delivery team members from merely involved observers.
Example: During the daily standup the Scrum Master reminds attending managers they are chickens and should observe rather than redirect the team.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A facilitation technique for capturing off-topic but important points to address later without derailing the current discussion.
Expansion: A parking lot is a visible list where a facilitator records tangential issues, questions, or ideas that arise during a meeting so they are not lost but do not consume current agenda time. The captured items are revisited at the meeting's end or assigned as follow-up actions.
Contrast: Issue log - an issue log is a formal project artifact tracking issues to resolution, while a parking lot is an informal in-meeting holding place for deferred topics.
Example: When a discussion about hosting costs erupts during sprint planning, the facilitator adds it to the parking lot and returns the team to story estimation.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The servant-leader and chief facilitator of an Agile Release Train in the Scaled Agile Framework.
Expansion: In SAFe, the Release Train Engineer coordinates the Agile Release Train, the set of multiple agile teams working toward a common set of objectives. The RTE facilitates program-level events such as PI planning, manages risks and dependencies across teams, and removes impediments, acting in effect as a chief Scrum Master at scale.
Contrast: Scrum Master - a Scrum Master serves one team, whereas the RTE serves the entire release train of multiple teams.
Example: The RTE facilitates quarterly PI planning so eight teams align their commitments and surface cross-team dependencies.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A four-stage coaching framework structured around Goal, Reality, Options, and Will.
Expansion: The GROW model guides a coaching conversation through four steps: clarifying the Goal, examining the current Reality, exploring Options, and confirming the Will or way forward. Project managers use it to coach team members toward their own solutions rather than directing them, supporting a servant-leadership style.
Contrast: OSCAR Model - both are coaching frameworks, but OSCAR adds explicit Affirm-style review and follow-up steps that GROW does not name separately.
Example: A project manager uses the GROW model to help a developer define a personal growth goal and commit to concrete next steps.
Source: Whitmore - Coaching for Performance

Core Meaning: A coaching framework built on Outcome, Situation, Choices, Actions, and Review.
Expansion: The OSCAR coaching model structures a development conversation around the desired Outcome, the current Situation, the Choices available, the Actions to take, and a Review of progress. It is solution-focused and emphasizes ongoing follow-up to sustain change.
Contrast: GROW Model - GROW ends at securing the will to act, while OSCAR explicitly builds in a Review stage for accountability and reinforcement.
Example: A team lead applies the OSCAR model to coach a tester, agreeing on actions and scheduling a review to track improvement.
Source: Gilbert and Whittleworth - OSCAR coaching model

Core Meaning: A leadership model that matches leader behavior to a follower's competence and commitment on a specific task.
Expansion: Situational Leadership II, developed by Blanchard building on the original Hersey and Blanchard model, holds that effective leaders flex between directing, coaching, supporting, and delegating styles based on the follower's development level for the task at hand. There is no single best style; the leader diagnoses the individual's competence and commitment and adapts accordingly.
Contrast: Servant leadership - servant leadership is a consistent values-based orientation toward serving the team, whereas Situational Leadership II prescribes deliberately switching styles by follower readiness.
Example: A project manager directs a new analyst closely but delegates fully to a seasoned engineer, applying Situational Leadership II.

Figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='60' y='28' width='105' height='72' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.08' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='165' y='28' width='105' height='72' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.12' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='60' y='100' width='105' height='72' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.05' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='165' y='100' width='105' height='72' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><text x='112' y='60' fill='currentColor' font-size='9.5' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>S3</text><text x='112' y='73' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Supporting</text><text x='217' y='60' fill='currentColor' font-size='9.5' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>S2</text><text x='217' y='73' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Coaching</text><text x='112' y='132' fill='currentColor' font-size='9.5' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>S4</text><text x='112' y='145' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Delegating</text><text x='217' y='132' fill='currentColor' font-size='9.5' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>S1</text><text x='217' y='145' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Directing</text><line x1='48' y1='172' x2='48' y2='24' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><polygon points='48,20 45,28 51,28' fill='currentColor'/><line x1='60' y1='184' x2='274' y2='184' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><polygon points='278,184 270,181 270,187' fill='currentColor'/><text x='40' y='100' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 40 100)'>Supportive behaviour</text><text x='167' y='200' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>Directive behaviour</text></svg>
Figure.caption: Match style to follower readiness: directive vs supportive behaviour.

Source: Blanchard - Situational Leadership II

Core Meaning: A goal-oriented change management model describing the five sequential outcomes an individual must achieve to adopt a change: Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, and Reinforcement.
Expansion: ADKAR, from Prosci, frames change as something that happens one person at a time. The five elements are sequential building blocks: Awareness of the need to change, Desire to participate, Knowledge of how to change, Ability to implement, and Reinforcement to sustain it. Identifying which element a person is stuck on pinpoints the barrier and the targeted intervention required.
Deep Dive: Because ADKAR is sequential, a gap in any earlier element blocks the later ones; for instance, no amount of training (Knowledge) helps if the person lacks Desire. Change practitioners diagnose the first weak element, the barrier point, and focus effort there rather than pushing later stages. ADKAR complements organization-level frameworks like Kotter by operating at the individual level, making it useful for assessing readiness and tailoring communication, sponsorship, and coaching per stakeholder group.
Contrast: Kotter 8-Step Model - Kotter is an organization-level sequence led from the top, while ADKAR targets the individual's personal transition.
Example: A rollout stalls until the team realizes users have Awareness and Knowledge but lack Ability, so hands-on coaching is added.
Source: Prosci - ADKAR model

Core Meaning: An eight-step process for leading organizational change, from creating urgency to anchoring the change in the culture.
Expansion: John Kotter's model sequences large-scale change through eight steps: create urgency, build a guiding coalition, form a strategic vision, enlist a volunteer army, enable action by removing barriers, generate short-term wins, sustain acceleration, and institute change in the culture. It emphasizes top-down leadership and momentum, treating the early steps as essential groundwork often skipped in failed change efforts.
Contrast: ADKAR - Kotter operates at the organizational level through leadership action, whereas ADKAR diagnoses and supports change within each individual.
Example: A PMO uses Kotter's model, securing executive sponsorship and quick wins before rolling a new methodology across the organization.
Source: Kotter - Leading Change

Core Meaning: A change model distinguishing the external event of change from the internal psychological transition people pass through in three phases.
Expansion: William Bridges argued that change is situational and external, while transition is the internal three-phase psychological process people experience: Ending or letting go, the Neutral Zone of uncertain in-between, and the New Beginning. Leaders support people through each phase, recognizing that resistance often stems from incomplete endings rather than the change itself.
Contrast: Lewin Change Model - Lewin's unfreeze-change-refreeze describes managing the change process, while Bridges focuses on the emotional transition individuals undergo.
Example: When a reorganization is announced, the manager helps staff acknowledge what they are losing before pushing the new structure, honoring the Ending phase.
Source: Bridges - Managing Transitions

Core Meaning: A five-stage model describing how performance dips and then recovers as a group absorbs a disruptive change.
Expansion: Virginia Satir's model traces change through five stages: Late Status Quo, Resistance triggered by a foreign element, Chaos, the Transforming Idea, and a New Status Quo at higher performance. It explicitly predicts a temporary performance and morale dip during Chaos, helping leaders normalize that decline rather than abandon the change prematurely.
Contrast: Bridges Transition Model - Satir emphasizes the performance dip and recovery curve of a system, while Bridges focuses on the individual's psychological phases of letting go.
Example: A team's velocity drops after adopting a new tool; the coach cites the Satir model to reassure them the Chaos stage precedes higher performance.
Source: Satir - the Satir change model

Core Meaning: A three-stage change model of unfreezing the status quo, changing, and refreezing the new state.
Expansion: Kurt Lewin's model frames change as unfreeze (creating motivation and loosening current behaviors), change (implementing the new way), and refreeze (stabilizing and embedding the new state so it becomes the norm). It is one of the earliest and most cited change frameworks and underpins many later models.
Contrast: Kotter 8-Step Model - Lewin offers three broad phases, while Kotter expands the change journey into eight prescriptive, leadership-driven steps.
Example: Before introducing remote work, leadership builds the case for change (unfreeze), pilots new norms (change), then formalizes policy (refreeze).
Source: Lewin - field theory in social science

Core Meaning: A Japanese concept of skill mastery in three stages: follow the rules, break the rules, and transcend the rules.
Expansion: Originating in martial arts, Shu-Ha-Ri describes a learning progression: Shu (obey and follow established practices exactly), Ha (consciously adapt and break from the rules as understanding grows), and Ri (transcend rules and operate intuitively). Agile coaches use it to set the expectation that new teams should first follow a framework faithfully before tailoring it.
Contrast: Dreyfus Model - Dreyfus describes five graded stages of skill acquisition, while Shu-Ha-Ri compresses mastery into three stages centered on one's relationship to the rules.
Example: A coach insists a new team practice Scrum by the book (Shu) before customizing ceremonies once they understand the intent (Ha).
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A visual risk analysis technique that maps the causes and consequences of a risk event around a central knot with preventive and mitigating barriers.
Expansion: The bowtie method diagrams a single hazard or risk event in the center, with threats and their preventive controls on the left and consequences with their mitigating controls on the right, forming a bowtie shape. It makes the relationship between causes, the event, controls, and outcomes visible, supporting both qualitative analysis and barrier management.
Contrast: Cause and effect diagram - a fishbone diagram explores only the causes of a problem, while the bowtie also maps consequences and the barriers on both sides of the risk event.
Example: A safety team builds a bowtie for a data-breach risk, listing preventive controls like access management and mitigating controls like incident response.

Figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='34' y1='44' x2='148' y2='80' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.1'/><line x1='34' y1='80' x2='148' y2='80' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.1'/><line x1='34' y1='116' x2='148' y2='80' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.1'/><line x1='172' y1='80' x2='286' y2='44' stroke='#C98A1F' stroke-width='1.1'/><line x1='172' y1='80' x2='286' y2='80' stroke='#C98A1F' stroke-width='1.1'/><line x1='172' y1='80' x2='286' y2='116' stroke='#C98A1F' stroke-width='1.1'/><line x1='104' y1='36' x2='104' y2='124' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><line x1='216' y1='36' x2='216' y2='124' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><circle cx='160' cy='80' r='18' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.15' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='160' y='83' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>Event</text><text x='32' y='30' fill='#B5462F' font-size='9'>Threats</text><text x='288' y='30' fill='#C98A1F' font-size='9' text-anchor='end'>Consequences</text><text x='104' y='140' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>preventive</text><text x='216' y='140' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>recovery</text></svg>
Figure.caption: Bowtie: preventive barriers stop the causes (left); recovery barriers limit the consequences (right).

Source: IEC 31010 - bowtie analysis

Core Meaning: A property of systems that gain capability and improve from stressors, volatility, and disorder rather than merely resisting them.
Expansion: Coined by Nassim Taleb, antifragility goes beyond resilience: a resilient system withstands shocks and returns to baseline, while an antifragile system actually benefits and grows stronger from a degree of stress, error, and uncertainty. In project and agile contexts it argues for small, frequent experiments and fast feedback so teams improve through controlled exposure to failure.
Contrast: Resilience - resilience is the ability to absorb disruption and recover to the prior state, whereas antifragility means improving beyond the prior state because of the disruption.
Example: A team that runs frequent small releases learns from each minor failure, becoming antifragile compared with one chasing a single flawless big-bang launch.
Source: Taleb - Antifragile

Core Meaning: The practice of business users building applications using low-code or no-code platforms without formal software development skills.
Expansion: Citizen development empowers non-IT employees, called citizen developers, to create applications on approved low-code and no-code platforms governed by IT. PMI has promoted it as a way to accelerate solution delivery and digital transformation, while stressing the governance needed to manage security, quality, and shadow-IT risks.
Contrast: Shadow IT - shadow IT is unsanctioned technology built outside governance, whereas citizen development is sanctioned, governed, business-led app building.
Example: A finance analyst builds an approval-workflow app on a no-code platform under the IT-approved citizen development program.
Source: PMI Citizen Developer

Core Meaning: A centralized team that provides leadership, best practices, standards, and support for a discipline across an organization.
Expansion: A Center of Excellence concentrates expertise in a discipline - such as agile, project management, or citizen development - to define standards, share knowledge, provide training, and raise maturity organization-wide. It promotes consistency and capability building rather than executing the work of individual projects itself.
Contrast: PMO - a PMO governs and supports project delivery and portfolio decisions, while a Center of Excellence focuses on building competence and standards in a specific discipline.
Example: An agile Center of Excellence publishes coaching guides and certifies internal Scrum Masters to standardize practice across teams.
Source: organizational center of excellence model

Core Meaning: A cognitive bias where an initial piece of information disproportionately influences subsequent judgments and estimates.
Expansion: Anchoring bias occurs when an early reference point - such as a first cost figure or schedule guess - anchors later thinking, so adjustments remain insufficiently far from that anchor. It distorts estimating and negotiation, for example when a stakeholder's casual deadline becomes the fixed expectation regardless of analysis.
Contrast: Confirmation Bias - anchoring fixes judgment to an initial value, while confirmation bias filters evidence to support a pre-existing belief.
Example: After a sponsor mentions a budget figure offhand, all later estimates cluster near it despite scope suggesting a higher cost.
Source: Tversky and Kahneman - anchoring and adjustment

Core Meaning: The systematic tendency to overestimate favorable outcomes and underestimate costs, durations, and risks.
Expansion: Optimism bias leads planners to expect things to go better than they realistically will, producing underestimated budgets and schedules and underweighted risks. It is a root cause of the planning fallacy and is often countered with reference class forecasting, which grounds estimates in the outcomes of similar past projects.
Contrast: Planning Fallacy - optimism bias is the broad tendency toward rosy expectations, while the planning fallacy is its specific manifestation in underestimating task time and cost.
Example: A team confident in a three-month delivery ignores that every similar past project took six, exhibiting optimism bias.
Source: Kahneman - Thinking Fast and Slow

Core Meaning: The error of continuing an endeavor because of already-spent resources that cannot be recovered, rather than future value.
Expansion: The sunk cost fallacy occurs when prior investments of money, time, or effort irrationally justify further commitment, even when objective analysis says to stop. Sound decisions should weigh only future costs and benefits, because sunk costs are gone regardless. The fallacy frequently drives organizations to keep funding failing projects.
Contrast: Sunk cost - a sunk cost is simply an unrecoverable past expenditure, while the sunk cost fallacy is the decision error of letting it drive future choices.
Example: A steering committee keeps funding a doomed system because abandoning it would waste the large sum already spent.
Source: behavioral economics - sunk cost fallacy

Core Meaning: The tendency to increase investment in a failing course of action to justify prior decisions.
Expansion: Escalation of commitment describes decision makers doubling down on a losing path rather than cutting losses, often to avoid admitting a mistake or because of sunk costs and ego. It explains why troubled projects receive more resources instead of being cancelled, and it is countered by independent reviews and clear kill criteria.
Contrast: Sunk Cost Fallacy - the sunk cost fallacy is the reasoning error about past spending, while escalation of commitment is the resulting behavior of pouring in still more resources.
Example: A sponsor approves yet another budget increase for a late project to avoid conceding the original plan failed.
Source: Staw - escalation of commitment research

Core Meaning: A cognitive bias in which people with low competence overestimate their ability while experts may underestimate theirs.
Expansion: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how those least skilled in an area lack the metacognition to recognize their own gaps, inflating their self-assessment, while highly competent people often assume tasks are equally easy for others and underrate their relative skill. It cautions project managers to validate self-reported confidence with objective evidence.
Contrast: Impostor Syndrome - Dunning-Kruger has the unskilled overrating themselves, whereas impostor syndrome has capable people doubting their genuine competence.
Example: A junior developer confidently promises a quick fix, unaware of the complexity an expert would immediately flag.
Source: Dunning and Kruger - unskilled and unaware

Core Meaning: A psychological pattern of doubting one's accomplishments and fearing being exposed as a fraud despite evident competence.
Expansion: Impostor syndrome is the persistent internal experience of believing one is not as capable as others perceive, attributing success to luck rather than skill. In teams it can suppress contributions from capable members and is addressed through psychological safety, mentoring, and recognition.
Contrast: Dunning-Kruger Effect - impostor syndrome is competent people underrating themselves, the inverse of low performers overrating their ability.
