Example: A skilled engineer hesitates to volunteer expertise, privately fearing colleagues will discover she does not belong.
Source: Clance and Imes - impostor phenomenon

Core Meaning: The principle that adding manpower to a late software project makes it later.
Expansion: Articulated by Fred Brooks in The Mythical Man-Month, Brooks's Law observes that new people require ramp-up time and training from existing staff, and that added headcount multiplies communication paths, so reinforcing a delayed project often slows it further. It warns against treating effort and progress as simply interchangeable.
Contrast: Crashing - crashing adds resources to shorten a schedule and can work on partitionable work, while Brooks's Law warns that on complex, late, interdependent work adding people backfires.
Example: A manager assigns five new developers to a slipping release, but onboarding and coordination overhead pushes delivery even later.
Source: Brooks - The Mythical Man-Month

Core Meaning: A sense-making framework that sorts situations into clear, complicated, complex, chaotic, and disordered domains to guide the appropriate response.
Expansion: Created by Dave Snowden, Cynefin helps leaders match their approach to the nature of the problem. Clear or obvious problems have known best practices; complicated ones need analysis or expertise, that is good practice; complex ones require safe-to-fail experiments to let solutions emerge; chaotic ones demand immediate action to establish order; and disorder is not yet knowing which domain applies.
Deep Dive: The framework's power is in prescribing different decision approaches per domain: in Clear you sense-categorize-respond, in Complicated you sense-analyze-respond, in Complex you probe-sense-respond, and in Chaotic you act-sense-respond. Predictive, plan-driven methods fit the clear and complicated domains, while agile, iterative, and emergent methods suit the complex domain where cause and effect are only clear in hindsight. A key danger is the cliff between Clear and Chaotic: over-applying rigid best practice to a complacent clear domain can trigger sudden collapse into chaos. Cynefin thus underpins tailoring and hybrid delivery decisions.
Contrast: Stacey Matrix - both classify uncertainty, but Stacey plots agreement against certainty on two axes, while Cynefin offers five named domains each with a distinct decision heuristic.
Example: A project manager treats a regulatory report as Complicated and hires experts, but treats a novel product feature as Complex and runs small experiments.

Figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 205' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='20' y='16' width='140' height='88' fill='#2F6F5E' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='160' y='16' width='140' height='88' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='20' y='104' width='140' height='88' fill='#B5462F' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='160' y='104' width='140' height='88' fill='#C98A1F' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><text x='90' y='50' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Complex</text><text x='90' y='66' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>probe-sense-respond</text><text x='230' y='50' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Complicated</text><text x='230' y='66' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>sense-analyse-respond</text><text x='90' y='142' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Chaotic</text><text x='90' y='158' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>act-sense-respond</text><text x='230' y='142' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Clear</text><text x='230' y='158' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>sense-categorise-respond</text><circle cx='160' cy='104' r='23' fill='none' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><text x='160' y='107' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>Disorder</text></svg>
Figure.caption: Cynefin: match the response to the kind of problem; Disorder is not yet knowing which domain you are in.

Source: Snowden - Cynefin framework

Core Meaning: A model that plots the degree of agreement against the degree of certainty to recommend simple, complicated, complex, or chaotic management approaches.
Expansion: The Stacey matrix, from Ralph Stacey, positions a decision on two axes: how much stakeholders agree on what to do, and how certain the cause-and-effect knowledge is. Combinations near agreement and certainty suit traditional plan-driven control, while areas of low agreement or low certainty call for collaborative, adaptive, or experimental approaches. It is widely used to justify selecting agile over predictive methods.
Contrast: Cynefin Framework - the Stacey matrix uses two continuous axes of agreement and certainty, whereas Cynefin presents discrete domains each with its own response strategy.
Example: Because stakeholders disagree on requirements and the technology is unproven, the team places the work in the complex zone and chooses an adaptive approach.
Source: Stacey - Stacey matrix

Core Meaning: PMI's framework of three skill domains project professionals need: ways of working, power skills, and business acumen.
Expansion: The PMI Talent Triangle defines the balanced skill set modern project professionals require. Its current three sides are Ways of Working, covering technical and methodological skills; Power Skills, covering interpersonal and leadership abilities; and Business Acumen, covering strategic and organizational understanding. PMI uses it to structure continuing certification education and professional development.
Contrast: Power skills - power skills are one side of the triangle, the interpersonal abilities, while the Talent Triangle is the complete three-part competency framework.
Example: A PMP earns PDUs across all three Talent Triangle sides to maintain certification and round out their capabilities.
Source: PMI Talent Triangle

Core Meaning: A project selection technique that ranks options by scoring them against weighted criteria.
Expansion: A scoring model, or weighted scoring model, evaluates candidate projects or solutions by rating each against defined criteria and multiplying by criterion weights to produce a comparable total score. It is a benefit-measurement method that makes selection transparent and reduces bias in prioritization.
Contrast: Decision matrix - a scoring model is typically used to prioritize and select among projects, while a decision matrix is the general grid tool that can support many kinds of multi-criteria choices.
Example: A portfolio board scores five proposals on strategic fit, ROI, and risk, then funds the two with the highest weighted totals.
Source: PMBOK Guide - project selection methods

Core Meaning: A planning approach where work is scheduled backward from a target milestone based on what downstream activities actually need.
Expansion: Pull scheduling, common in Lean and the Last Planner System, builds a plan by starting from a completion milestone and having teams define handoffs based on what the next activity requires, pulling work rather than pushing it from a central master schedule. It increases reliability and ownership because those doing the work commit to their own handoffs.
Contrast: Critical path method - CPM pushes a centrally computed forward-pass schedule, while pull scheduling derives commitments backward from the milestone based on downstream need.
Example: In a construction pull-planning session, trades work backward from the handover date, each committing to the predecessors the next trade needs.
Source: Lean Construction - Last Planner System

Core Meaning: A model of skill acquisition describing progression through five stages from novice to expert.
Expansion: The Dreyfus model of skill acquisition holds that learners pass through novice, advanced beginner, competent, proficient, and expert stages. Novices rely on rigid rules and context-free guidance, while experts act intuitively from deep tacit experience. It informs how project managers coach, delegate, and set expectations based on an individual's stage.
Contrast: Shu-Ha-Ri - the Dreyfus model details five graded stages of competence, while Shu-Ha-Ri offers three stages framed around the learner's relationship to rules.
Example: A coach gives a novice tester explicit checklists but lets a proficient tester rely on judgment, applying the Dreyfus model.
Source: Dreyfus and Dreyfus - skill acquisition model

Core Meaning: A hierarchy showing how data becomes information, then knowledge, and finally wisdom.
Expansion: The DIKW pyramid models increasing context and value: Data are raw facts, Information is data given meaning and structure, Knowledge is information applied with experience, and Wisdom is knowing how and when to act on that knowledge. It underpins knowledge management and explains why raw project metrics must be interpreted before they can guide decisions.
Contrast: Knowledge management - knowledge management is the discipline of capturing and sharing knowledge, while DIKW is the conceptual hierarchy describing how data ascends toward wisdom.
Example: Sensor readings as data become a defect-rate report as information, guide a process fix as knowledge, and inform when to release as wisdom.

Figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 185' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><polygon points='150,18 36,172 264,172' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.3'/><line x1='121' y1='58' x2='179' y2='58' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.7'/><line x1='92' y1='96' x2='208' y2='96' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.7'/><line x1='64' y1='134' x2='236' y2='134' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.7'/><text x='150' y='45' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Wisdom</text><text x='150' y='81' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Knowledge</text><text x='150' y='118' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Information</text><text x='150' y='156' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Data</text></svg>
Figure.caption: DIKW: raw data becomes wisdom as context, experience, and judgement are added.

Source: Ackoff - DIKW hierarchy

Core Meaning: The five-phase Six Sigma improvement cycle: Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, and Control.
Expansion: DMAIC is the structured Six Sigma method for improving an existing process. Define frames the problem and goals, Measure baselines current performance, Analyze finds root causes, Improve implements and verifies solutions, and Control sustains the gains. It is data-driven and aims to reduce variation and defects.
Contrast: Deming Cycle - both are improvement cycles, but DMAIC is a rigorous, metrics-heavy Six Sigma method for existing processes, while the Deming Cycle is a simpler general-purpose plan-do-check-act loop.
Example: A team uses DMAIC to cut invoice errors, measuring the baseline rate, analyzing causes, piloting a fix, and adding controls to hold the gain.

existing process, then sustain and repeat</text></svg>
Figure.caption: DMAIC: the Six Sigma cycle for improving an existing process.

Source: Six Sigma - DMAIC methodology

Core Meaning: The rate at which a unit must be completed to meet customer demand, calculated as available time divided by demand.
Expansion: Takt time, from Lean manufacturing, sets the pace of production by dividing available working time by customer demand over that period, giving the maximum time allowed per unit to keep up with demand. Used in location-based and Lean scheduling, it synchronizes flow and exposes bottlenecks where work exceeds the takt.
Contrast: Cycle Time - takt time is the demand-driven target pace you must meet, while cycle time is the actual time a unit takes to complete.
Example: With 8 productive hours and demand for 16 units a day, takt time is 30 minutes, so each station must finish a unit every half hour.
Source: Lean manufacturing - takt time

Core Meaning: The tendency to underestimate the time, cost, and risk of future actions despite knowing that similar past efforts overran.
Expansion: The planning fallacy, identified by Kahneman and Tversky, is the systematic underestimation of how long tasks will take and how much they will cost, even when people know that comparable past projects exceeded their plans. It stems from taking an inside view focused on the specifics of the current plan; the remedy is an outside view using reference class forecasting.
Contrast: Optimism Bias - optimism bias is the general tendency toward favorable expectations, while the planning fallacy is its specific form applied to time and cost estimates.
Example: Despite every past audit taking six weeks, the team again commits to four, falling into the planning fallacy.
Source: Kahneman and Tversky - planning fallacy

Core Meaning: A model describing five sequential stages - forming, storming, norming, performing, and adjourning - that teams typically move through as they develop.
Expansion: Bruce Tuckman proposed that groups mature through forming (orientation and dependence on the leader), storming (conflict over roles and approach), norming (cohesion and agreed ways of working), and performing (autonomous high output), with adjourning later added for disbandment. Progress is not strictly linear, since a change in membership or scope can send a team back to an earlier stage. The model helps leaders treat early conflict as normal and tailor their support to each stage.
Deep Dive: Tuckman published the first four stages in 1965 and added adjourning (sometimes called mourning) with Mary Ann Jensen in 1977. Leadership emphasis typically shifts from directive in forming and storming toward facilitative and delegating in performing, which links the model to situational leadership. Storming is the stage teams most often get stuck in, and skipping it by suppressing disagreement tends to surface the unresolved conflict later. Because the stages are descriptive rather than guaranteed, a reorganisation, new member, or major scope change can cause regression, so the PM should re-establish norms when the team composition shifts.
Contrast: Drexler-Sibbet Team Performance Model - Tuckman describes five descriptive stages of group maturity, whereas Drexler-Sibbet maps seven stages each framed by a specific question the team must resolve.
Example: A new project team argues over task ownership and approach in its first weeks (storming) before settling into smooth, self-directed delivery (performing).

Figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='24' y1='170' x2='300' y2='170' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='24' y1='170' x2='24' y2='24' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><polygon points='24,20 21,28 27,28' fill='currentColor'/><polygon points='304,170 296,167 296,173' fill='currentColor'/><text x='300' y='185' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='end'>time</text><text x='16' y='30' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 16 30)'>performance</text><rect x='34' y='134' width='52' height='24' rx='3' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.08' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.1'/><rect x='92' y='112' width='52' height='24' rx='3' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.1'/><rect x='150' y='90' width='52' height='24' rx='3' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.12' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.1'/><rect x='208' y='60' width='52' height='24' rx='3' fill='#2F6F5E' fill-opacity='0.14' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.1'/><rect x='262' y='34' width='52' height='24' rx='3' fill='#C98A1F' fill-opacity='0.16' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.1'/><text x='60' y='149' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Forming</text><text x='118' y='127' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Storming</text><text x='176' y='105' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Norming</text><text x='234' y='75' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Performing</text><text x='288' y='49' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>Adjourning</text></svg>
Figure.caption: Performance grows through the stages; Adjourning disbands the team.

Source: Tuckman - stages of group development

Core Meaning: A motivation theory arranging human needs in five ascending levels, where lower needs must be largely met before higher ones drive behaviour.
Expansion: Abraham Maslow ordered needs as physiological, safety, social or belonging, esteem, and self-actualisation, arguing that a substantially satisfied need stops motivating and attention shifts upward. People pursue higher-order growth needs only once lower deficiency needs are reasonably secure. For project leaders this implies that pay and job security must be addressed before recognition and challenging work will motivate.
Deep Dive: Maslow distinguished deficiency needs (the lower four), which people are driven to fill when lacking, from the growth need of self-actualisation, which expands as it is fed. The hierarchy is a general tendency rather than a rigid staircase, and people can pursue several levels at once or prioritise a higher need over a lower one. Maslow later added cognitive, aesthetic, and transcendence needs, and the model has been criticised for limited cross-cultural validity. On projects it pairs well with Herzberg and McClelland: secure the basics, then enrich the work to reach esteem and self-actualisation.
Contrast: Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory - Maslow ranks all needs on one ascending ladder, whereas Herzberg splits factors into hygiene factors that prevent dissatisfaction and motivators that create satisfaction.
Example: A PM ensures a relocated team member has stable pay and housing (lower needs) before expecting recognition and stretch goals to motivate them.

Figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='58,148 242,148 260,180 40,180' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.05' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.1'/><polygon points='76,116 224,116 242,148 58,148' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.08' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.1'/><polygon points='94,84 206,84 224,116 76,116' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.11' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.1'/><polygon points='112,52 188,52 206,84 94,84' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.14' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.1'/><polygon points='130,20 170,20 188,52 112,52' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.1'/><text x='150' y='168' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Physiological</text><text x='150' y='136' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Safety</text><text x='150' y='104' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Belonging</text><text x='150' y='72' fill='currentColor' font-size='9.5' text-anchor='middle'>Esteem</text><text x='150' y='33' fill='currentColor' font-size='7' text-anchor='middle'>Self-</text><text x='150' y='44' fill='currentColor' font-size='7' text-anchor='middle'>actualisation</text></svg>
Figure.caption: Lower needs are met first; self-actualisation sits at the top.

Source: Maslow - A Theory of Human Motivation (hierarchy of needs)

Core Meaning: A motivation theory distinguishing hygiene factors that prevent dissatisfaction from motivators that actively create satisfaction.
Expansion: Frederick Herzberg found that two separate sets of factors govern attitudes at work: hygiene factors such as salary, company policy, working conditions, and supervision cause dissatisfaction when poor but do not motivate when good, while motivators such as achievement, recognition, the work itself, responsibility, and growth drive genuine satisfaction. Improving hygiene only moves people from dissatisfied to neutral, so lasting motivation requires enriching the work itself. The two are independent dimensions rather than opposite ends of one scale.
Contrast: Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs - Maslow places all needs on a single ascending hierarchy, whereas Herzberg separates them into two independent categories with different effects on motivation.
Example: A PM lifts a stalled team's morale not just by fixing pay complaints (hygiene) but by giving members ownership of meaningful features (motivators).

Figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='40' y1='42' x2='260' y2='42' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><polygon points='36,42 44,38 44,46' fill='currentColor'/><polygon points='264,42 256,38 256,46' fill='currentColor'/><line x1='150' y1='36' x2='150' y2='48' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><text x='40' y='30' fill='#B5462F' font-size='9' text-anchor='start'>Dissatisfaction</text><text x='150' y='30' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>Neutral</text><text x='260' y='30' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='9' text-anchor='end'>Satisfaction</text><rect x='34' y='66' width='118' height='94' rx='4' fill='#B5462F' fill-opacity='0.07' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='158' y='66' width='108' height='94' rx='4' fill='#2F6F5E' fill-opacity='0.08' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><text x='93' y='86' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>Hygiene factors</text><text x='93' y='100' fill='currentColor' font-size='7.5' text-anchor='middle'>prevent dissatisfaction</text><text x='93' y='120' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>Salary, policies,</text><text x='93' y='132' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>security, conditions,</text><text x='93' y='144' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>supervision</text><text x='212' y='86' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>Motivators</text><text x='212' y='100' fill='currentColor' font-size='7.5' text-anchor='middle'>create satisfaction</text><text x='212' y='120' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>Achievement,</text><text x='212' y='132' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>recognition, growth,</text><text x='212' y='144' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>responsibility</text></svg>
Figure.caption: Hygiene factors prevent dissatisfaction; motivators create satisfaction.

Source: Herzberg - two-factor theory (motivation-hygiene theory)

Core Meaning: A model identifying five conflict-handling modes based on how assertive and how cooperative a person chooses to be.
Expansion: Kenneth Thomas and Ralph Kilmann mapped conflict responses on two axes - assertiveness (concern for one's own goals) and cooperativeness (concern for others' goals) - yielding five modes: competing, collaborating, compromising, avoiding, and accommodating. No single mode is best, since the right choice depends on the stakes, the relationship, and the time available. Collaborating seeks a win-win that fully satisfies both parties, while compromising splits the difference.
Contrast: Compromising versus collaborating - compromising is a partial give-and-take where each side concedes something, whereas collaborating works for a full win-win that meets both parties' needs.
Example: A PM uses collaborating to resolve a genuine technical disagreement between two leads, but uses avoiding for a trivial issue not worth the time.
Source: Thomas-Kilmann - conflict mode instrument

Core Meaning: A framework defining quality management as three interconnected processes - quality planning, quality control, and quality improvement.
Expansion: Joseph Juran held that managing for quality rests on three processes: quality planning (designing products and processes to meet customer needs), quality control (monitoring operations and holding the gains), and quality improvement (raising performance to new levels by attacking chronic problems). Juran also defined quality as fitness for use, emphasising the customer's real needs over mere conformance to specification. The trilogy mirrors how organisations manage finances through budgeting, cost control, and cost reduction.
Contrast: Crosby's Zero Defects - Juran defines quality as fitness for use and accepts an economic balance point, whereas Crosby defines quality as conformance to requirements with an absolute goal of zero defects.
Example: A PM plans quality metrics up front (planning), inspects deliverables against them (control), and runs retrospectives to cut recurring defects (improvement).
Source: Juran - Juran on Planning for Quality (quality trilogy)

Core Meaning: A quality philosophy holding that the performance standard should be zero defects, achieved by preventing problems rather than inspecting them out.
Expansion: Philip Crosby argued that quality means conformance to requirements, that the only acceptable performance standard is zero defects, and that quality is free because preventing defects costs less than the rework, scrap, and warranty claims they cause. He stressed building quality in through prevention and doing it right the first time rather than relying on detection. Defects are not inevitable but stem from inattention to requirements and process.
Contrast: Juran's Quality Trilogy - Crosby targets an absolute zero-defect standard through conformance and prevention, whereas Juran frames quality as fitness for use managed through planning, control, and improvement.
Example: A PM invests in design reviews and automated testing so defects are prevented early, arguing the prevention cost is lower than late rework.
Source: Crosby - Quality Is Free (zero defects)

Core Meaning: A motivation model arguing that for complex work, intrinsic drivers - autonomy, mastery, and purpose - outperform external rewards.
Expansion: Daniel Pink synthesised behavioural research to argue that carrot-and-stick rewards work for simple routine tasks but can undermine performance on creative, cognitively demanding work. Lasting motivation instead comes from three intrinsic elements: autonomy (control over how, when, and with whom one works), mastery (the urge to get better at something that matters), and purpose (serving a cause larger than oneself). For knowledge teams, leaders should provide direction and good conditions rather than rely on bonuses.
Contrast: Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory - Pink centres motivation on autonomy, mastery, and purpose for creative work, whereas Herzberg distinguishes hygiene factors from motivators across work generally.
Example: A PM motivates a development team by giving them latitude over technical decisions, time to deepen skills, and a clear line of sight to the customer's mission.
Source: Pink - Drive (autonomy, mastery, purpose)

Core Meaning: The observation that a system's design tends to mirror the communication structure of the organisation that builds it.
Expansion: Melvin Conway observed that any organisation designing a system will produce a design whose structure copies the organisation's own communication patterns. In practice, teams that communicate poorly tend to build poorly integrated interfaces, while clean, modular architectures emerge from well-bounded, well-communicating teams. This insight motivates the inverse Conway manoeuvre, deliberately shaping team structure to encourage the desired architecture.
Contrast: Inverse Conway manoeuvre - Conway's law describes how organisation shapes system design, whereas the inverse manoeuvre deliberately restructures teams to drive a target architecture.
Example: A program splits one large component team into service-aligned squads so the software naturally evolves into cleanly separated services.
Source: Conway - How Do Committees Invent? (Conway's law)

Core Meaning: A model identifying nine behavioural roles people tend to adopt in a team, grouped into action, people, and thinking categories.
Expansion: Meredith Belbin's research identified nine clusters of behaviour that contribute to team success: action-oriented roles (Shaper, Implementer, Completer Finisher), people-oriented roles (Coordinator, Teamworker, Resource Investigator), and thinking-oriented roles (Plant, Monitor Evaluator, Specialist). Most people have two or three preferred roles, and balanced teams cover the full range rather than duplicating the same strengths. Each role carries both a contribution and an allowable weakness the team should tolerate.
Contrast: Tuckman's Stages of Team Development - Belbin classifies the behavioural roles individuals play, whereas Tuckman describes the stages a whole team passes through over time.
Example: A PM staffing a team notices it has three Plants generating ideas but no Completer Finisher, so recruits someone strong on follow-through.
Source: Belbin - Management Teams: Why They Succeed or Fail

Core Meaning: A model mapping seven stages teams move through when creating and then sustaining high performance.
Expansion: Allan Drexler and David Sibbet describe team development as seven stages: orientation (why am I here?), trust building (who are you?), goal clarification (what are we doing?), commitment (how will we do it?), implementation (who does what, when, and where?), high performance (the wow stage), and renewal (why continue?). The first four stages create the team and the last three sustain it, with each stage posing a question that must be resolved before the team advances. Unresolved early questions tend to resurface as problems later.
Contrast: Tuckman's Stages of Team Development - Drexler-Sibbet defines seven stages each framed by a question to resolve, whereas Tuckman describes five broader stages of group maturity.
Example: A PM facilitating a new team works explicitly through purpose and trust before assigning tasks, following the Drexler-Sibbet sequence.
Source: Drexler and Sibbet - Team Performance Model

Core Meaning: An agile prioritisation method that sequences work by dividing each item's cost of delay by its size or duration.
Expansion: Weighted Shortest Job First, popularised by the Scaled Agile Framework, prioritises a backlog by calculating cost of delay divided by job size or duration, then doing the highest-scoring items first. Cost of delay typically combines user and business value, time criticality, and risk reduction or opportunity enablement. Because it favours high-value, short jobs, WSJF maximises the economic benefit delivered per unit of effort when capacity is constrained.
Contrast: MoSCoW prioritisation - WSJF produces an economic ranking from cost of delay over size, whereas MoSCoW sorts items into must, should, could, and won't have categories without a numeric ratio.
Example: A product owner scores backlog epics by cost of delay over effort and schedules a small, time-critical, high-value feature ahead of a large one.
Source: Reinertsen / SAFe - Weighted Shortest Job First

Core Meaning: The original situational leadership model prescribing four styles - telling, selling, participating, and delegating - matched to follower maturity.
Expansion: Paul Hersey and Ken Blanchard's original model holds that leaders should adapt their style to a follower's readiness or maturity for a task by balancing task behaviour and relationship behaviour. As maturity rises, the leader moves from telling (high task, low relationship) to selling, to participating, and finally to delegating (low task, low relationship). There is no single best style, since effectiveness depends on diagnosing each follower's competence and willingness.
Contrast: Situational Leadership II - the original Hersey-Blanchard model uses telling, selling, participating, and delegating against follower maturity, whereas Blanchard's later SLII relabels the styles as directing, coaching, supporting, and delegating and frames readiness as development level (competence and commitment).
Example: A PM tells a brand-new technician exactly what to do, but delegates outcomes to an experienced and willing engineer.

Figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='60' y='28' width='105' height='72' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.08' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='165' y='28' width='105' height='72' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.12' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='60' y='100' width='105' height='72' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.05' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='165' y='100' width='105' height='72' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><text x='112' y='60' fill='currentColor' font-size='9.5' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>S3</text><text x='112' y='73' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Supporting</text><text x='217' y='60' fill='currentColor' font-size='9.5' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>S2</text><text x='217' y='73' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Coaching</text><text x='112' y='132' fill='currentColor' font-size='9.5' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>S4</text><text x='112' y='145' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Delegating</text><text x='217' y='132' fill='currentColor' font-size='9.5' font-weight='bold' text-anchor='middle'>S1</text><text x='217' y='145' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>Directing</text><line x1='48' y1='172' x2='48' y2='24' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><polygon points='48,20 45,28 51,28' fill='currentColor'/><line x1='60' y1='184' x2='274' y2='184' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><polygon points='278,184 270,181 270,187' fill='currentColor'/><text x='40' y='100' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 40 100)'>Supportive behaviour</text><text x='167' y='200' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>Directive behaviour</text></svg>
Figure.caption: Match style to follower readiness: directive vs supportive behaviour.

Source: Hersey and Blanchard - situational leadership theory

Core Meaning: An organisational model asserting that effectiveness requires aligning seven interdependent elements, with shared values at the centre.
Expansion: Developed at McKinsey by Tom Peters and Robert Waterman, the 7-S framework holds that organisational performance depends on the alignment of seven elements: strategy, structure, and systems (the hard elements) plus shared values, skills, style, and staff (the soft elements). The elements are interconnected, so changing one requires realigning the others, and shared values sit at the centre tying them together. It is often used to diagnose why a change effort is stalling.
Contrast: SWOT analysis - the 7-S framework checks internal alignment across seven organisational elements, whereas SWOT scans strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats including the external environment.
Example: After a reorganisation, a PMO uses 7-S to check that new structures, systems, and skills still align with the firm's strategy and shared values.
Source: Peters and Waterman / McKinsey - 7-S framework

Core Meaning: A learning model describing how people progress from unconscious incompetence to unconscious competence as they acquire a skill.
Expansion: The four stages of competence model describes learning as movement through unconscious incompetence (not knowing what you lack), conscious incompetence (aware of the gap), conscious competence (able but only with deliberate effort), and unconscious competence (the skill has become second nature). Recognising a learner's current stage lets a coach pitch support appropriately, building awareness first, then practice, then automaticity. The hardest step is often moving people from unconscious to conscious incompetence.
Contrast: Dreyfus model of skill acquisition - the four stages of competence track awareness and automaticity across four steps, whereas the Dreyfus model describes five levels from novice to expert focused on judgement and intuition.
Example: A new team member moves from not realising they misuse a tool (unconscious incompetence) to using it fluently without thinking (unconscious competence) through coaching.
Source: Conscious competence learning model (four stages of competence)

Core Meaning: An agile practice in which two developers work together at one workstation, one writing code while the other reviews in real time.
Expansion: Pair programming, a core Extreme Programming practice, has two developers collaborate on the same task at a single workstation: the driver writes the code while the navigator reviews each line, thinks ahead, and catches issues immediately. The pair switch roles frequently to stay fresh and share understanding. Although two people produce one stream of code, the continuous review tends to improve quality, spread knowledge, and reduce defects and key-person risk.
Contrast: Code review - pair programming reviews code continuously as a co-located pair writes it, whereas a code review inspects completed work asynchronously after it is submitted.
Example: Two developers pair on a tricky payment module so defects are caught as they are written and both fully understand the code afterwards.
Source: Beck - Extreme Programming Explained (pair programming)

Core Meaning: The portion of cost of quality spent because work fails to meet requirements, covering internal and external failure costs.
Expansion: Cost of nonconformance is the money spent as a result of failing to meet quality requirements, and it forms one half of the cost of quality. It comprises internal failure costs such as rework and scrap found before delivery, and external failure costs such as warranty claims, liabilities, recalls, and lost reputation found after delivery. It contrasts with the cost of conformance - prevention and appraisal spending - and the central argument is that investing in conformance up front is cheaper than paying for nonconformance later.
Contrast: Cost of conformance - cost of nonconformance is money lost to failures and rework, whereas cost of conformance is money spent on prevention and appraisal to avoid those failures.
Example: A project incurs cost of nonconformance when a delivered feature fails in production and the team pays for emergency fixes and customer credits.
Source: PMBOK Guide - cost of quality (cost of nonconformance)

Core Meaning: The internal and external drivers that energize, direct, and sustain a person's effort toward goals.
Expansion: Motivation explains why team members commit effort and persist on project work. Practice distinguishes intrinsic motivation, such as autonomy, mastery, and purpose, from extrinsic motivation, such as pay, recognition, and deadlines. Several theories from Maslow, Herzberg, and McClelland guide how leaders create the conditions for engagement. Sustaining motivation supports productivity, quality, and retention across a project team.
Contrast: Engagement — motivation is the internal drive to act, while engagement is the observable involvement and commitment that results from it.
Example: A project manager rotates a developer onto a challenging new module to renew their sense of mastery and keep motivation high during a long delivery.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition — Team Performance Domain

Core Meaning: The degree to which a project team collectively achieves its goals in output, quality, and collaboration.
Expansion: Team performance reflects how well members work together to deliver results, not just the sum of individual contributions. It is shaped by shared goals, trust, clear roles, and effective communication, and it usually improves as a team matures through stages of development. In PMBOK terms it is cultivated within the Team performance domain, where leaders build a cohesive, high-performing team.
Contrast: Team Performance Domain — team performance is the outcome being pursued, whereas the Team Performance Domain is the PMBOK area of activities that produces it.
Example: After introducing daily coordination and a team charter, a software team raises its velocity and reduces rework, both signs of improving team performance.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition — Team Performance Domain

Core Meaning: The activity of defining how the project schedule will be developed, managed, and controlled.
Expansion: Schedule planning establishes the approach, tools, and rules for building and maintaining the schedule before detailed sequencing and estimating begin. It covers selecting a scheduling method, defining activity and milestone conventions, and setting control thresholds for variance. In predictive work it produces a schedule management plan, while in adaptive work it favours rolling wave and lookahead techniques.
Contrast: Schedule development — schedule planning sets how scheduling will be done, while schedule development is the act of producing the actual dated schedule.
Example: Early in a construction project, the team agrees to use the critical path method with monthly rebaselining, documenting these choices as the schedule plan.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Project Schedule Management

Core Meaning: The defined assignment of who does what and who answers for outcomes on a project.
Expansion: Roles and responsibilities clarify the functions individuals perform and the work they own, reducing ambiguity, gaps, and overlap. A role describes a position such as tester or sponsor, while a responsibility is the specific work or decision tied to it. Tools such as a responsibility assignment matrix make these expectations explicit and support accountability.
Contrast: Accountability — responsibility concerns who performs the work, while accountability concerns the single role that must answer for the outcome.
Example: A team charter lists the product owner as responsible for prioritising the backlog and the Scrum Master as responsible for removing impediments.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Plan Resource Management

Core Meaning: The process of developing an approximation of the monetary resources needed to complete project work.
Expansion: Cost estimating quantifies the likely cost of activities, work packages, and the overall project to support budgeting and decisions. Estimators draw on techniques such as analogous, parametric, and bottom-up estimating, and they express results with a stated accuracy range. Estimates are refined as the project progresses from rough order of magnitude toward definitive figures.
Contrast: Cost budgeting — cost estimating produces the expected cost of individual elements, while budgeting aggregates those estimates into an authorized cost baseline.
Example: Using parametric rates per square metre, a quantity surveyor estimates the cost of a new office fit-out before the budget is set.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Estimate Costs

Core Meaning: A structured comparison of competing options to choose the best balance among conflicting objectives or constraints.
Expansion: Trade-off analysis weighs options against criteria such as cost, schedule, scope, quality, and risk when no single choice is best on all dimensions. It makes the give-and-take explicit, for example accepting higher cost to shorten duration. Techniques such as weighted criteria and multicriteria decision analysis support a defensible, transparent decision.
Contrast: Decision matrix — a decision matrix is the scoring tool, while trade-off analysis is the broader reasoning about what is gained and given up across options.
Example: Facing a tight deadline, a team analyses the trade-off between adding contractors at extra cost and reducing scope, and chooses to defer two low-value features.
Source: PMBOK Guide — multicriteria decision analysis

Core Meaning: An evaluation of how an organization, process, or capability operates today as a baseline for change.
Expansion: A current state assessment documents existing processes, performance, pain points, and capabilities before defining a future state. It establishes the baseline that exposes gaps and justifies a proposed solution or change. In business analysis it informs needs assessment and the business case by grounding decisions in present reality.
Contrast: Future state — the current state assessment describes how things work now, while the future state describes the desired condition to be achieved.
Example: Before automating invoicing, an analyst maps the manual approval steps, cycle times, and error rates to capture the current state.
Source: PMI Guide to Business Analysis — current state assessment

Core Meaning: The deliberate exchange of project information with stakeholders to build understanding and support.
Expansion: Stakeholder communication tailors the content, channel, frequency, and tone of information to each audience's needs and influence. It supports engagement by keeping stakeholders informed, managing expectations, and surfacing concerns early. A communications management plan and analysis of requirements guide who receives what, when, and how.
Contrast: Stakeholder engagement — communication is the act of conveying information, while engagement is the broader effort to involve and influence stakeholders over time.
Example: A project manager sends executives a concise monthly dashboard while giving the operations team detailed weekly working sessions.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Manage Communications and Stakeholder Engagement

Core Meaning: A program managed as a component within a larger program.
Expansion: A subprogram is a grouping of related projects and activities coordinated under, and contributing benefits to, a parent program. It allows a large program to be decomposed so that each part has dedicated governance while remaining aligned to the overall program outcome.
Contrast: Subproject — a subproject is a smaller piece of a single project, while a subprogram is itself a program nested within a larger program.
Example: A national digital health program runs a records subprogram and a payments subprogram, each with its own program manager reporting to the program director.
Source: The Standard for Program Management

Core Meaning: The process of choosing which programs and projects to fund and pursue to best meet strategic objectives.
Expansion: Portfolio selection evaluates candidate initiatives against strategic fit, value, risk, cost, and resource capacity to form a balanced portfolio. It uses scoring models, benefit measurement, and constrained optimization to rank and choose investments. The aim is to maximize value and alignment rather than to optimize any single project in isolation.
Contrast: Project selection methods — project selection methods are the techniques applied, while portfolio selection is the organization-wide decision about the optimal mix of investments.
Example: A steering board uses a weighted scoring model to select five of twelve proposed initiatives that best fit strategy within the annual budget.
Source: The Standard for Portfolio Management

Core Meaning: The activity of specifying how a chosen solution will be structured and built to meet requirements.
Expansion: Solution design translates approved requirements into a concrete description of components, interfaces, data, and behaviour that will satisfy the need. It bridges the gap between what is required and how it will be delivered, balancing constraints such as cost, quality, and feasibility. Designs are often validated through prototypes or reviews before full construction.
Contrast: Solution architecture — solution design specifies detailed components and behaviour, while solution architecture defines the higher-level structure and key decisions that frame that design.
Example: After requirements are agreed, an analyst and engineer design the screens, data model, and integrations for a new customer portal.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A specific task carried out to implement a planned risk response.
Expansion: A risk action is the concrete step taken to enact a response strategy such as mitigation or avoidance, often assigned to a risk action owner with a due date. Tracking these actions turns risk plans into executed work and keeps exposure within tolerance.
Contrast: Risk response — the risk response is the chosen strategy, while a risk action is an individual task that delivers that strategy.
Example: To mitigate a supplier delay, the team logs a risk action to qualify a second vendor by the end of the month.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Implement Risk Responses

Core Meaning: The high-level structure of a solution, defining its major components, interfaces, and guiding design decisions.
Expansion: Solution architecture describes how the parts of a system fit together and interact to meet business and technical requirements. It sets the key structural decisions, standards, and constraints that detailed design and construction must follow. A solution architect balances functional needs against quality attributes such as scalability, security, and maintainability.
Contrast: Enterprise architecture — solution architecture covers one solution or initiative, while enterprise architecture spans the structure of the whole organization.
Example: An architect defines that a new ordering platform will use a cloud-hosted microservices design with an API gateway and a shared identity service.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The completion and formal settlement of a procurement or contract.
Expansion: Procurement closure confirms that contracted work is complete and acceptable, settles open claims and payments, and formally ends the agreement. It includes verifying deliverables, finalizing records, releasing retention or bonds, and capturing lessons learned. Proper closure protects both parties legally and preserves a clean audit trail.
Contrast: Project closure — procurement closure ends a specific contract, while project closure concludes the entire project after all contracts are settled.
Example: After the contractor finishes the building and defects are resolved, the team confirms acceptance, releases final payment, and closes the contract.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Close Procurements

Core Meaning: How well a process delivers its intended outputs against measures such as quality, time, and cost.
Expansion: Process performance describes the actual results a process produces, judged against defined targets, control limits, or capability requirements. It is assessed using metrics and quality tools such as control charts to distinguish normal variation from problems. Understanding performance drives improvement and keeps the process stable and predictable.
Contrast: Process capability — process performance is what a process currently achieves, while process capability is its inherent ability to meet specifications when stable.
Example: A control chart shows the order-fulfilment process averaging two days with rising variation, prompting a review of process performance.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Manage Quality and process analysis

Core Meaning: A systematic pattern of deviation from rational judgment that distorts decisions and estimates.
Expansion: Cognitive bias is a predictable error in thinking that arises from mental shortcuts, emotions, or how information is framed. In projects it skews estimates, risk assessments, and stakeholder decisions through effects such as anchoring, optimism, and confirmation bias. Awareness, diverse input, and structured techniques help reduce its impact.
Contrast: Groupthink — a cognitive bias is an individual reasoning error, while groupthink is a group dynamic where the desire for harmony suppresses dissent.
Example: A team anchored on its first estimate of three weeks resists evidence the work will take six, illustrating anchoring bias in planning.
Source: Behavioral decision theory (Tversky and Kahneman)

Core Meaning: The defined plan for how a solution's performance and value will be measured after delivery.
Expansion: A solution evaluation approach specifies what to measure, the methods and metrics to use, and when evaluation will occur to judge whether a solution delivers expected value. It links measures of effectiveness and performance to the original business need. Agreeing the approach early ensures the right data is collected to support later decisions about the solution.
Contrast: Solution evaluation — the approach is the plan defining how value will be assessed, while solution evaluation is the act of carrying out that assessment.
Example: Before launch, an analyst agrees to evaluate a new portal by adoption rate, support tickets, and processing time at thirty and ninety days.
Source: PMI Guide to Business Analysis — Determine Solution Evaluation Approach

Core Meaning: A facilitated process in which a neutral third party helps disputing parties reach a voluntary agreement.
Expansion: Mediation uses an impartial mediator to guide conflicting parties toward a mutually acceptable resolution without imposing a decision. It is a form of alternative dispute resolution often used for contract disputes or team conflict, preserving relationships and avoiding costly litigation. The mediator manages the conversation, but the parties themselves own the outcome.
Contrast: Arbitration — in mediation the parties craft their own voluntary agreement, while in arbitration a neutral arbitrator imposes a binding decision.
Example: When a supplier and buyer disagree over scope, they engage a mediator who helps them negotiate a revised delivery schedule both accept.
Source: PMBOK Guide — conflict management and alternative dispute resolution

Core Meaning: The work of combining separate components or systems so they function together as a unified whole.
Expansion: Systems integration connects hardware, software, data, and processes so that interfaces work correctly and the combined system meets requirements. It addresses compatibility, data exchange, and end-to-end behaviour that individual components cannot guarantee alone. Integration is typically verified through staged testing before the full system is accepted.
Contrast: Integration test — integration testing is the verification activity, while systems integration is the broader engineering effort of making components work together.
Example: A program integrates a new billing engine with the CRM and payment gateway so an order flows automatically from sale to invoice.
Source: Systems engineering practice (INCOSE)

Core Meaning: A testable assumption proposed as the basis for an experiment to learn whether it holds true.
Expansion: A hypothesis states a clear, falsifiable expectation, such as a belief that a feature will increase customer retention. In adaptive and lean approaches, teams frame work as hypotheses and run small experiments to validate or invalidate them before scaling. This reduces waste by replacing assumptions with evidence.
Contrast: Assumption — an assumption is taken as true without proof, while a hypothesis is explicitly framed to be tested and potentially disproved.
Example: A product team hypothesizes that one-click checkout will lift conversion by ten percent and runs an A/B test to confirm it.
Source: Lean Startup (Eric Ries) — hypothesis-driven experimentation

Core Meaning: Meeting present needs through project outcomes without compromising environmental, social, and economic value over the long term.
Expansion: Sustainability in project management considers the long-term environmental, social, and economic effects of how a project is delivered and what it produces. It extends success beyond cost, schedule, and scope to include responsible resource use and lasting benefits. The triple bottom line of people, planet, and profit is a common framing for balancing these concerns.
Contrast: Sustainable pace — sustainability concerns long-term environmental and social responsibility, while sustainable pace concerns a workload a team can maintain over time.
Example: A facilities project specifies recycled materials and energy-efficient systems to lower lifetime emissions and operating costs.
Source: Triple Bottom Line (Elkington) and PMI sustainability practice

Core Meaning: The effect a project, product, or process has on the natural environment across its life cycle.
Expansion: Environmental impact captures consequences such as emissions, energy and water use, waste, and effects on ecosystems arising from a project and its deliverables. Assessing it helps teams reduce harm, meet regulations, and choose lower-impact options. Tools such as life cycle assessment quantify impact from sourcing through disposal.
Contrast: Social impact — environmental impact concerns effects on the natural world, while social impact concerns effects on people and communities.
Example: An infrastructure team conducts an environmental impact assessment that leads them to reroute construction away from a protected wetland.
Source: Life Cycle Assessment practice (ISO 14040)

Core Meaning: The ability to understand an organization and its environment well enough to make decisions that drive value.
Expansion: Business acumen is the knowledge of how an organization creates value, including its strategy, finances, customers, and operating environment. It is one of the three sides of the PMI Talent Triangle, alongside ways of working and power skills. Project leaders use it to align delivery with strategic objectives and to make sound trade-offs that support the business case.
Contrast: Power skills — business acumen is strategic and organizational understanding, while power skills are interpersonal abilities such as communication and leadership.
Example: A project manager reprioritizes features after recognizing a market shift that threatens the product's revenue case.
Source: PMI Talent Triangle — Business Acumen

Core Meaning: Interpersonal and behavioral abilities that enable a project leader to work effectively with people.
Expansion: Power skills, sometimes called soft skills, include communication, leadership, collaboration, empathy, and conflict management. They form one side of the PMI Talent Triangle alongside ways of working and business acumen. PMI emphasizes them because delivery success increasingly depends on influencing stakeholders and building high-performing teams, not only on technical mastery.
Contrast: Business acumen — power skills are interpersonal abilities for working with people, while business acumen is the strategic understanding of the organization.
Example: A project manager defuses a tense sponsor meeting through active listening and clear framing, drawing on strong power skills.
Source: PMI Talent Triangle — Power Skills

Core Meaning: The date on which work on a schedule activity actually began.
Expansion: The actual start date records when an activity truly started, as opposed to when it was planned or scheduled to start. Comparing actual and planned start dates supports progress tracking, variance analysis, and schedule forecasting.
Contrast: Early start date — the early start date is the earliest an activity could begin per the schedule model, while the actual start date is when it really began.
Example: An activity planned to start Monday actually began Wednesday, so the project manager records Wednesday as its actual start date and assesses the delay.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — actual start date

Core Meaning: Confidence in the reliability, competence, and integrity of others that enables open collaboration.
Expansion: Trust is the willingness to rely on others based on belief in their honesty, ability, and good intent. On project teams it underpins candid communication, psychological safety, and effective delegation, allowing people to raise problems and take ownership. Leaders build it through consistency, transparency, and follow-through, and it is central to servant leadership.
Contrast: Transparency — transparency is openly sharing information, which is one behaviour that helps build trust, the broader confidence people place in one another.
Example: Because the team trusts its lead to shield them from blame, members openly flag a slipping milestone early rather than hiding it.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition — leadership and team development

Core Meaning: A person, role, or external system that interacts with a solution to achieve a goal.
Expansion: In requirements and use case modeling, an actor represents an external entity, such as a user role or another system, that exchanges information with the solution. Actors define who or what the system serves, framing its boundaries and interactions.
Contrast: Persona — an actor is an abstract role in a model, while a persona is a detailed, fictional profile of a specific user used to guide design.
Example: In a use case for an ATM, the Customer and the Bank network are actors that interact with the withdrawal function.
Source: PMI Guide to Business Analysis — use case modeling

Core Meaning: Project teams whose members are distributed across multiple countries, cultures, and time zones.
Expansion: Global teams bring together members from different geographies, languages, and cultures to deliver a shared project. They offer access to broad talent and follow-the-sun working but introduce challenges in communication, coordination, and cultural alignment. Success depends on clear working agreements, cultural awareness, and reliable collaboration tools.
Contrast: Virtual team — a virtual team is any team that works mainly through technology rather than in person, while global teams add the dimension of crossing countries and cultures.
Example: A product built by engineers in Berlin, Bangalore, and Boston relies on overlapping hours and a shared backlog to keep the global team aligned.
Source: PMBOK Guide — virtual teams and cultural considerations

Core Meaning: A visual model showing data entities and the relationships among them.
Expansion: An entity relationship diagram depicts entities such as customers and orders, their attributes, and how they relate, including cardinality such as one-to-many. It is a core data-modeling tool used to define and communicate the structure of information a solution must store.
Contrast: Data flow diagram — an entity relationship diagram models how data is structured and related, while a data flow diagram models how data moves through processes.
Example: An analyst draws an entity relationship diagram showing that one Customer can place many Orders, each containing many Line Items.
Source: Data modeling practice (Chen entity-relationship model)

Core Meaning: Indirect costs that support project or organizational work but are not tied to a single activity.
Expansion: Overhead covers ongoing indirect costs such as facilities, administration, utilities, and management that cannot be traced directly to one deliverable. It is allocated across projects or activities and must be included when building realistic budgets and rates.
Contrast: Direct costs — direct costs are attributable to a specific activity or deliverable, while overhead is shared indirect cost spread across the work.
Example: A consultancy adds an overhead rate covering office rent and HR to each consultant's billable hour when pricing a project.
Source: Cost accounting practice — indirect and overhead costs

Core Meaning: A situation where a person's private interests could improperly influence their professional judgment or duties.
Expansion: A conflict of interest arises when competing loyalties, such as personal gain or relationships, could bias decisions made on behalf of an organization or stakeholders. Under the PMI Code of Ethics, practitioners must proactively disclose such conflicts and avoid acting on them. Managing conflicts of interest protects fairness, trust, and integrity in decisions like procurement and hiring.
Contrast: Conflict — a conflict is a disagreement between parties, while a conflict of interest is a clash between a person's duty and their private interests.
Example: A project manager discloses that a bidding vendor is owned by a relative and recuses themselves from the source selection decision.
Related Terms: Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct
Source: PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct

Core Meaning: The agile practice of regularly examining work and outcomes and adjusting based on what is learned.
Expansion: Inspect and adapt is a core principle of empirical, agile delivery in which teams frequently review the product and their process and make changes accordingly. It relies on transparency so that inspection reveals real status, and it is enacted through events such as reviews and retrospectives. The practice drives continuous improvement and keeps work aligned to changing needs.
Contrast: Continuous improvement — inspect and adapt is the specific empirical cycle of frequent review and adjustment in agile, while continuous improvement is the broader ongoing pursuit of better processes.
Example: In each sprint retrospective the team inspects its workflow and adapts by adding a code review step to cut defects.
Source: Scrum Guide — empiricism (inspect and adapt)

Core Meaning: A practice where the whole team shares responsibility for the work product rather than individuals owning parts.
Expansion: Collective ownership means any team member can improve any part of the shared work, and the team as a whole is accountable for its quality. Originating in Extreme Programming as collective code ownership, it reduces bottlenecks and single points of failure and spreads knowledge across the team. It depends on shared standards, trust, and practices such as pairing to keep quality consistent.
Contrast: Self-organizing team — collective ownership is shared responsibility for the work product, while a self-organizing team is one that decides for itself how to do the work.
Example: Any developer on the team can refactor a module they did not write because the codebase is collectively owned.
Source: Extreme Programming (Kent Beck) — collective code ownership

Core Meaning: The process of identifying quality requirements and standards for the project and documenting how to meet them.
Expansion: Plan Quality Management determines the quality requirements and standards that apply to the project and its deliverables and defines how compliance will be demonstrated. Its main output is the quality management plan, which guides later assurance and control work. Planning quality early helps embed prevention over inspection and avoids costly rework.
Contrast: Control Quality — Plan Quality Management defines the standards and approach, while Control Quality measures deliverables against those standards.
Example: For a medical device project, the team documents applicable regulatory standards and test criteria in the quality management plan.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Plan Quality Management

Core Meaning: A written record of what was discussed, decided, and assigned during a meeting.
Expansion: Meeting minutes capture key points, decisions, and action items with owners and due dates, providing an authoritative record for participants and absentees. They support accountability and follow-up and become part of the project's documentation and audit trail.
Contrast: Decision log — meeting minutes record the full proceedings of a meeting, while a decision log is a focused running record of decisions made across the project.
Example: After a steering committee meeting, the coordinator circulates minutes listing the approved budget change and three follow-up actions.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The activity of collecting stakeholder needs and conditions that a solution must satisfy.
Expansion: Requirements gathering is the common term for eliciting and capturing what stakeholders need from a product or project. It uses techniques such as interviews, workshops, observation, and document analysis to surface explicit and implicit needs. The collected requirements are then analysed, documented, and validated before becoming the basis for scope.
Contrast: Requirements elicitation — elicitation is the formal PMI term for drawing out needs, while requirements gathering is the everyday synonym for the same activity.
Example: An analyst runs workshops with finance and sales staff to gather requirements for a new reporting tool.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Disciplined reasoning that analyzes evidence and assumptions to reach sound, objective judgments.
Expansion: Critical thinking is the deliberate evaluation of information, assumptions, and arguments to form well-founded conclusions rather than accepting claims at face value. In projects it strengthens estimating, risk analysis, and problem solving by exposing bias and weak evidence. PMI treats it as a power skill that underpins effective decisions and tailoring.
Contrast: Problem solving — critical thinking is the disciplined evaluation of information and assumptions, while problem solving is the broader process of finding and applying a solution.
Example: Faced with an optimistic vendor estimate, the project manager critically tests its assumptions against historical data before accepting it.
Source: PMI Talent Triangle — Power Skills (critical thinking)

Core Meaning: A structured review of a contracted seller's delivery, quality, and compliance against the agreed terms of the contract.
Expansion: Seller performance evaluation is part of controlling procurements, where the buyer assesses how well a vendor meets cost, schedule, quality, and scope obligations. Findings inform decisions about payments, corrective action, contract continuation, and the seller's eligibility for future work. It typically draws on inspections, audits, and procurement performance reviews documented across the contract life cycle.
Contrast: Differs from source selection, which evaluates sellers before award; seller performance evaluation judges actual delivery after award.
Example: A project manager rates a software vendor each quarter on defect rates and on-time releases, using the scores to justify renewing the support contract.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A seller's priced offer to supply specified goods or services, usually given in response to a request for quotation.
Expansion: A quotation states the price, and often the terms and validity period, at which a seller will provide standard, well-defined items. Buyers compare quotations from multiple sellers when price is the dominant selection factor.
Contrast: Differs from a proposal, which addresses complex needs and includes technical approach as well as price; a quotation is primarily a price response.
Example: The procurement team requests quotations from three suppliers for 500 identical laptops and selects the lowest compliant price.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: Establishing and applying consistent rules, methods, or specifications so that work and outputs become uniform and repeatable.
Expansion: Standardization reduces variation by defining a single agreed way to perform a task or produce a deliverable, which improves predictability and quality. In project and operational settings it underpins economies of scale, easier training, and reliable measurement.
Contrast: Opposite of customization, which tailors each output to a specific case at the cost of repeatability.
Example: A PMO standardizes status report templates across all projects so executives can compare performance consistently.
Source: ISO 9000 quality management

Core Meaning: The periods, defined by a calendar, during which resources are available to perform project work.
Expansion: Working time is set through project and resource calendars that specify workdays, shifts, holidays, and non-working periods. Scheduling tools use it to convert activity durations into calendar dates and to calculate resource availability.
Contrast: Differs from elapsed time, which counts all calendar time including non-working periods.
Example: Because the resource calendar shows no working time on weekends, a five-day task starting Thursday finishes the following Wednesday.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: An evaluation of how capable and consistent an organization's processes are against a defined maturity model.
Expansion: A maturity assessment rates current practices across defined levels, from ad hoc to optimized, to reveal capability gaps. The results guide a roadmap of improvements in governance, methods, and competence. Organizations repeat assessments over time to track progress and benchmark against peers.
Contrast: Differs from a project audit, which checks compliance of a single project; maturity assessment judges the organization's overall capability.
Example: Using a project management maturity model, an organization scores at level two and creates a plan to reach level three within two years.
Source: OPM3 Organizational Project Management Maturity Model

Core Meaning: The degree to which an organization is prepared to adopt, absorb, and sustain a change produced by a project.
Expansion: Organisational readiness considers whether people, processes, culture, and systems can accept a new solution without unacceptable disruption. It is assessed through stakeholder analysis, capability checks, and change impact reviews before deployment. Low readiness signals the need for training, communication, or phased rollout to protect benefit realization.
Contrast: Differs from solution readiness, which asks whether the deliverable itself is complete; organisational readiness asks whether the receiving organization can use it.
Example: Before launching a new ERP system, the team surveys departments and delays go-live for two units that lack trained super-users.
Source: Managing Change in Organizations: A Practice Guide (PMI)

Core Meaning: A planned timetable specifying when and how much will be paid to a seller over the course of a contract.
Expansion: A payment schedule ties disbursements to milestones, deliverables, or elapsed time so cash outflow is predictable and linked to value received. It is a key term of the procurement agreement and feeds the project cash flow forecast.
Contrast: Differs from a cost baseline, which authorizes total spend; a payment schedule governs the timing of payments to a specific seller.
Example: The construction contract releases 20 percent on mobilization and the remainder against four completed inspection milestones.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: The methods used to settle disagreements between contracting parties over claims, performance, or interpretation of terms.
Expansion: Dispute resolution covers the spectrum from informal negotiation through mediation and arbitration to litigation. Contracts usually specify the preferred sequence and forum so that conflicts are handled predictably. Effective resolution preserves the working relationship and avoids costly delays to delivery.
Contrast: Differs from claims administration, which logs and quantifies claims; dispute resolution is the process of actually settling them.
Example: When a subcontractor disputes a withheld payment, the parties follow the contract clause and resolve it through mediation rather than court.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: The ongoing management of a contract during execution to ensure both parties meet their obligations.
Expansion: Contract administration includes monitoring seller performance, managing changes, processing payments, maintaining records, and handling claims. It ensures the buyer receives what was agreed while protecting legal and commercial interests. Disciplined administration provides the documented basis for acceptance, disputes, and contract closure.
Contrast: Differs from contract negotiation, which sets the terms before signing; contract administration manages the relationship after award.
Example: The project manager keeps a complete file of change orders and inspection results so the final contract closeout is uncontested.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: The confidence stakeholders hold that the project team will act with competence, honesty, and consistency on their interests.
Expansion: Stakeholder trust is built through transparent communication, reliable follow-through, and demonstrated stewardship, and it lowers resistance and speeds decisions. Once damaged it is slow to rebuild, so the team protects it through credibility and ethical conduct.
Contrast: Differs from stakeholder engagement, which is the activity of involving stakeholders; trust is the relational quality that engagement aims to build.
Example: By delivering on every small commitment early in the project, the manager earns the sponsor's trust and is granted more autonomy.
Source: The Standard for Project Management

Core Meaning: A portfolio that sits within and is governed by a larger parent portfolio in the organizational hierarchy.
Expansion: A subsidiary portfolio groups related programs and projects under a higher-level portfolio, allowing strategy and resources to be cascaded and rolled up across organizational levels. It enables large enterprises to balance investments locally while aligning to enterprise objectives.
Contrast: Differs from a subsidiary program, which is a single coordinated effort; a subsidiary portfolio is a collection managed for strategic balance.
Example: A global firm runs a parent portfolio with subsidiary portfolios for each region, each aligned to the same corporate strategy.
Source: The Standard for Portfolio Management (PMI)

Core Meaning: A mathematical optimization technique that finds the best outcome for a linear objective subject to linear constraints.
Expansion: Linear programming models a decision as variables, a linear objective function such as minimizing cost, and constraints such as limited resources. Solvers identify the variable values that optimize the objective without violating any constraint. In project management it supports resource leveling, make-or-buy choices, and schedule optimization.
Contrast: Differs from heuristic scheduling, which applies rules of thumb; linear programming computes a mathematically optimal solution within its model.
Example: A planner uses linear programming to assign limited crews across tasks so total project cost is minimized while all deadlines are met.
Source: Operations research and management science

Core Meaning: The structured process of verifying and documenting that installed systems function as designed before handover to operations.
Expansion: Commissioning tests equipment and integrated systems against design intent and acceptance criteria, often progressing from component checks to full operational trials. It confirms safety, performance, and readiness for use, and produces records that support the operations team. Successful commissioning is frequently a precondition for final acceptance and benefit realization.
Contrast: Differs from construction completion, which means the asset is built; commissioning proves the built asset actually works as intended.
Example: After a new water treatment plant is built, the team runs commissioning trials and only then hands it to the operator.
Source: Construction and engineering practice

Core Meaning: A seller engaged by a prime contractor to perform part of the work the prime has contracted to deliver.
Expansion: A subcontractor holds a contract with the prime contractor rather than directly with the buyer, and the prime remains accountable for the overall result. Managing subcontractors involves flow-down of requirements, quality control, and coordination of interfaces.
Contrast: Differs from a prime contractor, who is directly accountable to the buyer; a subcontractor answers to the prime.
