Example: The main builder hires an electrical subcontractor for wiring while remaining responsible to the client for the whole building.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: Construction work performed on the project location to prepare and develop the ground and surrounding area.
Expansion: Site work includes clearing, grading, excavation, utilities, drainage, and access that ready a location for the main structures. It is often an early and weather-sensitive part of the construction schedule that shapes later work.
Contrast: Differs from building work, which erects the structure itself; site work prepares the land and infrastructure around it.
Example: Before foundations are poured, crews complete site work by grading the lot and installing storm drainage.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The use of software tools to execute predefined tests against a product automatically and report the results.
Expansion: Automated testing runs repeatable checks such as unit, integration, and regression tests without manual effort, giving fast feedback on quality. It is central to continuous integration, where every change is verified before it is merged. Automation frees people for exploratory work and reduces the cost of frequent releases.
Contrast: Differs from manual testing, where a person executes each test step; automated testing executes scripted checks at machine speed.
Example: Each code commit triggers an automated test suite that blocks the merge if any regression test fails.
Source: Agile Practice Guide

Core Meaning: The benefit or worth that a project's outcomes deliver to a particular stakeholder or stakeholder group.
Expansion: Stakeholder value reflects how well outcomes meet the needs and expectations of those affected, and it can be financial, social, or experiential. Recognizing that different stakeholders value different things helps the team prioritize scope and trade-offs.
Contrast: Differs from business value, which centers on the sponsoring organization's gain; stakeholder value spans the full range of affected parties.
Example: A new clinic system creates stakeholder value for nurses through faster charting, distinct from the financial value it gives the hospital.
Source: The Standard for Project Management

Core Meaning: A short, time-boxed daily event where the development team synchronizes work and plans the next day toward the sprint goal.
Expansion: The Daily Scrum is held at the same time and place each day and is limited to about fifteen minutes. The team inspects progress toward the sprint goal and adapts its plan, surfacing impediments for later resolution. It is for the developers, keeping coordination self-organized rather than a status report to managers.
Contrast: Differs from a sprint review, which inspects the product increment with stakeholders; the Daily Scrum inspects daily progress within the team.
Example: Each morning the developers spend fifteen minutes agreeing what to tackle next and flagging a blocked integration task.
Source: The Scrum Guide

Core Meaning: Planning that proceeds in short, iterative cycles, progressively refining detail as requirements and conditions become clearer.
Expansion: Adaptive planning replaces a single fixed plan with frequent replanning as the team learns from delivered increments and feedback. Near-term work is planned in detail while longer-term work is held at a coarse level until needed. This embraces change and reduces the cost of decisions made under uncertainty.
Contrast: Differs from predictive planning, which fixes scope and schedule up front; adaptive planning keeps the plan deliberately responsive to change.
Example: The team plans only the next two iterations in detail and revises the roadmap after each customer demo.
Source: Agile Practice Guide

Core Meaning: A quality principle that it is better to design and build quality in than to find and fix defects after the fact.
Expansion: Prevention over inspection holds that investing in good process, training, and design avoids defects, which is cheaper than detecting and reworking them later. It shifts cost from appraisal and failure toward prevention within the cost of quality. The principle underlies practices such as standardized procedures, mistake-proofing, and early testing.
Contrast: Contrasts with an inspection-led approach that relies on catching defects at the end; prevention stops defects from occurring.
Example: Rather than inspecting every finished part, the team improves the machining setup so defects rarely arise in the first place.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: The boundaries of the solution that will satisfy a business need, defining what the solution will and will not include.
Expansion: Solution scope describes the capabilities, features, and boundaries of the chosen solution, linking the business need to the work required to deliver it. It guides requirements, design, and acceptance and helps prevent scope creep.
Contrast: Differs from project scope, which is the work to deliver the solution; solution scope is the characteristics of the solution itself.
Example: The solution scope for a portal includes self-service billing but excludes mobile apps, which are deferred to a later release.
Source: BABOK Guide (IIBA)

Core Meaning: The business analysis activity of defining metrics and assessing how well a delivered solution achieves its intended value.
Expansion: Measuring solution performance establishes the indicators, targets, and data sources used to judge whether a solution delivers expected benefits in operation. Results reveal shortfalls, risks, and opportunities to enhance value. The activity bridges delivery and benefits realization by checking outcomes against the original need.
Contrast: Differs from validating requirements, which confirms requirements deliver value on paper; this measures the live solution's actual results.
Example: Six months after launch, the analyst tracks processing time and error rates to confirm the new system met its target gains.
Source: BABOK Guide (IIBA)

Core Meaning: The deep integration of digital technology across an organization that reshapes how it operates and delivers value.
Expansion: Digital transformation goes beyond adopting tools to rethinking processes, business models, and culture around digital capabilities such as automation, data, and connected services. It is typically delivered through a portfolio of interdependent projects and sustained change management. Success is judged by improved customer value and operational performance, not technology deployment alone.
Contrast: Differs from digitization, which simply converts analog information to digital form; transformation changes how the organization works and competes.
Example: A retailer reorganizes around real-time data and online fulfillment, changing roles and processes rather than just adding a website.
Source: Digital transformation management practice

Core Meaning: Methods for settling contractual disputes outside of court, such as negotiation, mediation, and arbitration.
Expansion: Alternative dispute resolution, or ADR, offers faster and less adversarial routes than litigation to resolve claims and disagreements. Contracts often mandate a sequence such as negotiation, then mediation, then binding arbitration. ADR helps preserve commercial relationships and limits the cost and delay of formal legal action.
Contrast: Differs from litigation, which resolves disputes through the courts; ADR settles them through private, agreed mechanisms.
Example: A contract clause requires the parties to attempt mediation, and if that fails, to use binding arbitration instead of suing.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: The general rise in prices over time that reduces the purchasing power of money.
Expansion: Inflation matters in project finance because future costs and contract prices may exceed today's estimates, so estimators apply escalation factors and contingency. It also affects discount rates used to evaluate long-term project economics.
Contrast: Differs from currency exchange risk, which arises from cross-border rate movements; inflation erodes value within a single currency over time.
Example: A multiyear construction estimate adds an annual escalation allowance so the budget keeps pace with rising material prices.
Source: Economics and cost estimating practice

Core Meaning: The capacity to recognize one's own emotions, strengths, limitations, and their effect on others.
Expansion: Self-awareness is a foundational element of emotional intelligence and a key power skill for project leaders. It enables a manager to regulate reactions, seek feedback, and adapt their leadership style to the situation. Greater self-awareness improves decision quality, conflict handling, and the credibility a leader holds with the team.
Contrast: Differs from social awareness, which reads others' emotions; self-awareness is insight into one's own internal state.
Example: Noticing that stress makes him terse, a project manager pauses before difficult conversations to lead more calmly.
Source: Emotional intelligence literature

Core Meaning: A representation of an organization's capabilities, value streams, processes, and structure that links strategy to execution.
Expansion: Business architecture describes what an organization does and how it is organized to create value, independent of specific systems. It provides a blueprint that aligns initiatives to strategy and reveals capability gaps and overlaps. Project and program teams use it to ensure changes fit the wider operating model.
Contrast: Differs from technology architecture, which describes the supporting platforms; business architecture describes capabilities and value, not technology.
Example: Before approving projects, an enterprise maps its capabilities so each initiative targets a clear gap in the business architecture.
Source: TOGAF (The Open Group)

Core Meaning: The structuring and organization of information and data so it can be found, understood, and used effectively.
Expansion: Information architecture defines how data entities, content, and their relationships are arranged, labeled, and navigated across systems. It supports consistent data definitions and usable interfaces, and it is a domain within enterprise architecture.
Contrast: Differs from technology architecture, which covers platforms and infrastructure; information architecture covers the data and content layer.
Example: An enterprise team defines shared customer data definitions so every system uses one consistent information architecture.
Source: TOGAF (The Open Group)

Core Meaning: The arrangement of hardware, software platforms, networks, and infrastructure that supports an organization's systems.
Expansion: Technology architecture specifies the technical components and standards on which applications and data run, and how they interconnect. As a domain of enterprise architecture it ensures solutions are scalable, secure, and consistent with technology strategy.
Contrast: Differs from information architecture, which structures data and content; technology architecture defines the underlying platforms and infrastructure.
Example: The architecture board mandates a standard cloud platform so new projects build on a common technology architecture.
Source: TOGAF (The Open Group)

Core Meaning: Acting honestly, ethically, and consistently in line with values and commitments, even when it is difficult.
Expansion: Integrity requires project professionals to be truthful, keep promises, avoid conflicts of interest, and hold themselves accountable for their conduct. It is a core value of the profession and the basis of stakeholder trust and credibility.
Contrast: Differs from compliance, which follows external rules; integrity holds to ethical principles even where no rule compels it.
Example: Discovering an estimating error in the team's favor, the manager discloses it to the client rather than staying silent.
Source: PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct

Core Meaning: Checking that documented requirements are correct, complete, consistent, and of sufficient quality to be used.
Expansion: Requirements verification reviews the requirements themselves against quality criteria such as clarity, testability, and freedom from conflict. It is typically done through peer review, inspection, and checklists before the requirements are baselined. Catching defects in the requirements early prevents costly rework downstream.
Contrast: Differs from requirements validation, which confirms requirements deliver stakeholder value; verification confirms the requirements are well-formed.
Example: A peer review flags two ambiguous requirements as untestable, and they are reworded before the baseline is set.
Source: BABOK Guide (IIBA)

Core Meaning: The action taken to address a current issue so that its impact on the project is contained or resolved.
Expansion: An issue response assigns an owner and a course of action to a problem that is already affecting the project, with a target resolution date tracked in the issue log. Unlike a planned risk response, it reacts to an event that has already occurred.
Contrast: Differs from a risk response, which is prepared for a possible future event; an issue response deals with a problem that is already happening.
Example: When a key supplier misses a delivery, the manager logs an issue and the response is to expedite an alternate source.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The agreed split between buyer and seller of cost savings or overruns under an incentive contract.
Expansion: A share ratio, often written as a buyer or seller percentage such as 80/20, determines how a difference between target and actual cost is divided. It motivates the seller to control costs because they keep a portion of any savings and bear a portion of any overrun.
Contrast: Differs from a fixed fee, which does not change with cost; the share ratio deliberately ties seller profit to cost performance.
Example: Under an 80/20 share ratio, a 10,000 underrun returns 8,000 to the buyer and 2,000 to the seller as added fee.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: Estimating the magnitude of a software effort using measures of functionality or relative complexity.
Expansion: Software sizing produces a measure of size, such as function points, lines of code, or story points, that is then converted into effort and duration estimates. Sizing supports budgeting, planning, and productivity comparison across projects. Relative sizing techniques like story points are common in agile, while function point analysis is widely used in predictive contexts.
Contrast: Differs from effort estimation, which yields person-hours; sizing first quantifies the magnitude of what is to be built.
Example: The team assigns story points to backlog items, then uses past velocity to forecast how many sprints the release will take.
Source: Software estimation practice (Function Point Analysis)

Core Meaning: A scheduled checkpoint at which the team and stakeholders assess whether a significant milestone has been achieved and whether work should proceed as planned.
Expansion: A milestone review evaluates completed work, deliverable acceptance, and readiness to continue against the criteria set for that milestone. It commonly informs go or no-go decisions and may trigger rebaselining or escalation to governance. Because it is tied to a specific high-importance schedule event rather than a routine cadence, its findings carry weight for forecasts and funding. The review typically produces decisions, action items, and updated risk and schedule information.
Contrast: Phase gate — a phase gate formally authorizes transition between life cycle phases, whereas a milestone review can occur at any significant scheduled point without ending a phase.
Example: On completing detailed design, the team holds a milestone review to confirm drawings are approved before releasing long-lead procurement.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition (Schedule discussion)

Core Meaning: A contract instrument that authorizes a discrete package of services under the terms of an existing indefinite-delivery indefinite-quantity master contract.
Expansion: A task order defines the scope, period of performance, and funding for a specific tranche of labor or services without renegotiating the underlying master agreement. It lets buyers issue work incrementally as needs emerge while pricing and terms stay fixed at the master-contract level. Task orders are common in government and large professional-services procurements. Each order can carry its own deliverables and acceptance criteria.
Contrast: Delivery order — a delivery order procures supplies or goods under the same master contract, whereas a task order procures services or labor.
Example: A program office issues a task order against its engineering master contract to staff a six-month requirements study.
Source: Procurement management practice (indefinite-delivery contracting)

Core Meaning: A contract instrument that orders a defined quantity of supplies or goods under the terms of an existing indefinite-delivery indefinite-quantity master contract.
Expansion: A delivery order calls off products against pricing and terms already agreed in the parent contract. It speeds repeat purchasing because commercial conditions are settled at the master-contract level and only quantities and dates vary. Delivery orders are widely used for materials and equipment in framework arrangements. Each order specifies quantities, delivery dates, and destinations.
Contrast: Task order — a task order authorizes services or labor, whereas a delivery order authorizes tangible supplies.
Example: The buyer places a delivery order for 200 servers under its hardware master contract.
Source: Procurement management practice (indefinite-delivery contracting)

Core Meaning: The disciplined practice of running small, controlled tests to validate assumptions and reduce uncertainty before committing to a full solution.
Expansion: In adaptive and lean delivery, experimentation treats uncertain features or strategies as testable propositions checked cheaply against real users or data. Teams design minimal tests, measure outcomes, and use the evidence to decide whether to persevere or change course. This lowers the cost of being wrong and accelerates learning where ambiguity is high. Experimentation underpins practices such as split testing and the minimum viable product.
Contrast: Big design up front — up-front design commits to a complete solution before building, whereas experimentation defers commitment until evidence accumulates.
Example: A product team releases two pricing pages to a small user segment to learn which converts better before a full rollout.
Source: Agile Practice Guide (PMI and Agile Alliance)

Core Meaning: A running record of significant decisions made during a project, capturing the choice, its rationale, owner, and date.
Expansion: A decision log preserves why key choices were made so the team can revisit reasoning, onboard new members, and avoid relitigating settled questions. Entries typically note the options considered, the decision maker, affected stakeholders, and any constraints or assumptions. It supports transparency and accountability and feeds lessons learned at closure. Though widely used, it is a practitioner artifact rather than a named PMI project document.
Contrast: Issue log — an issue log tracks open problems needing resolution, whereas a decision log records choices already concluded and their justification.
Example: The project manager records the decision to adopt a cloud database, with rationale and approvers, in the decision log.
Source: Project documentation practice; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A short, shared statement of the single most important outcome a team commits to achieving within an iteration.
Expansion: An iteration goal gives a timebox coherence by naming the value the team intends to deliver, helping members prioritize and make trade-offs when work competes. It aligns the iteration backlog to a purpose rather than a loose list of items, and it provides a clear basis for the review. The goal is negotiated at planning and remains stable through the iteration even if specific tasks change. In Scrum this is expressed as the Sprint goal.
Contrast: Product vision — a product vision describes long-term direction for the whole product, whereas an iteration goal scopes one short timebox.
Example: The team sets an iteration goal to enable guest checkout end to end, then selects backlog items that serve it.
Source: Agile Practice Guide (PMI and Agile Alliance)

Core Meaning: A deliberate change in strategy or direction made in response to validated evidence that the current approach is not working.
Expansion: Originating in lean startup thinking, a pivot is a structured course correction that keeps the underlying vision while changing the product, customer, or business-model hypothesis being tested. It is triggered by learning, not whim, and is distinguished from minor adjustments by the scale of the change. Pivoting early, before heavy investment, limits sunk cost and preserves options. The alternative to pivoting is to persevere on the current path.
Contrast: Scope creep — scope creep is uncontrolled, unplanned expansion of scope, whereas a pivot is an intentional, evidence-based redirection.
Example: After data shows businesses, not consumers, adopt the tool, the team pivots its target market and repositions the product.
Source: Lean Startup (Eric Ries)

Core Meaning: Knowledge about customers or solutions that is confirmed through real evidence from experiments rather than opinion or assumption.
Expansion: Validated learning measures progress by what a team has demonstrably proven about value and demand, not by features shipped. Teams state a hypothesis, build the smallest test that can refute it, and use observed behavior or metrics to accept or reject it. This evidence then drives the decision to persevere or pivot. It reframes early uncertainty as a series of answerable questions instead of a fixed plan to execute.
Contrast: Output-based progress — counting delivered features assumes value, whereas validated learning requires evidence that the value is real.
Example: A landing-page test confirms that 8 percent of visitors will pre-order, giving the team validated learning to fund the build.
Source: Lean Startup (Eric Ries)

Core Meaning: The practice of using existing knowledge and creating new knowledge to achieve project objectives and contribute to organizational learning.
Expansion: Project knowledge management covers both explicit knowledge that can be codified and tacit knowledge held in experience and relationships. It relies on knowledge-sharing methods such as communities of practice, work shadowing, and discussions, alongside documentation tools. The aim is to prevent loss of know-how when people leave and to reuse insight across the project and organization. PMI frames the related effort as managing project knowledge throughout execution.
Contrast: Lessons learned — lessons learned capture retrospective insight at points in time, whereas project knowledge management is the ongoing flow and reuse of knowledge.
Example: The team runs regular knowledge-sharing sessions and maintains a register so tacit insight is captured before specialists roll off.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition (Manage Project Knowledge)

Core Meaning: A contractual obligation in which one party agrees to compensate another for specified losses, damages, or liabilities.
Expansion: Indemnity clauses allocate the financial consequences of defined risks between buyer and seller, shifting responsibility for losses such as third-party claims, infringement, or negligence. They are a core mechanism for risk transfer in procurement and are often paired with insurance and limitation-of-liability provisions. Negotiating indemnities involves balancing protection against the price and willingness of the other party to accept exposure. Poorly bounded indemnities can create large contingent liabilities.
Contrast: Contingency reserve — a contingency reserve is money set aside internally for identified risks, whereas indemnity transfers loss to another contracting party.
Example: The supplier agrees to indemnify the client against third-party intellectual-property claims arising from the delivered software.
Source: Contract and procurement management practice

Core Meaning: The sequence of stages an asset passes through from conception and acquisition, through operation and maintenance, to disposal or decommissioning.
Expansion: The asset life cycle situates a project within the longer existence of what it creates, reminding teams that delivery is only one phase before extended operation and eventual retirement. Decisions made during design and build, such as material choices and maintainability, drive cost and value across the whole life. Total cost of ownership and benefits realization are assessed over this full span rather than at handover alone. The view links project outputs to operational outcomes.
Contrast: Project life cycle — the project life cycle ends at closure and handover, whereas the asset life cycle continues through years of operation and disposal.
Example: A bridge project evaluates maintenance and inspection costs across a 100-year asset life cycle, not just construction cost.
Source: Asset management practice (aligned with ISO 55000)

Core Meaning: The discipline of planning, sequencing, and timing construction activities to coordinate labor, materials, equipment, and site access.
Expansion: Construction scheduling translates the project plan into a time-phased sequence that respects physical dependencies, resource limits, and site logistics such as access and weather. It typically relies on network methods and is updated continuously as field progress and changes occur. Coordination across many trades and subcontractors makes timing and handoffs especially critical. Effective schedules drive procurement, cash flow, and claims management on the project.
Contrast: Resource leveling — resource leveling adjusts a schedule to fit resource limits, whereas construction scheduling is the broader domain practice of building and maintaining the timeline.
Example: The contractor sequences foundation, framing, and roofing with a network schedule so each trade has cleared predecessors before mobilizing.
Source: Construction domain practice; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The management of stock levels of materials and goods to balance availability against the cost of holding excess.
Expansion: Inventory control sets reorder points, order quantities, and buffers so that materials are available when needed without tying up cash in surplus stock. In lean thinking, excess inventory is treated as waste that hides problems and slows flow. Techniques range from just-in-time replenishment to safety-stock calculations driven by demand variability and lead time. On projects it affects procurement timing, site storage, and the risk of shortages stalling work.
Contrast: Capacity planning — capacity planning matches available resource capability to demand, whereas inventory control manages physical stock levels of materials.
Example: A site manager uses just-in-time delivery to keep only two days of rebar on site, reducing storage and damage.
Source: Lean and operations management practice

Core Meaning: The routine, ongoing operational activities of an organization, as distinct from the temporary, change-driven work of a project.
Expansion: Business-as-usual refers to repetitive operations that sustain current value delivery, such as running services, processing transactions, and maintaining systems. Projects exist to change business-as-usual by creating new capabilities, which are then absorbed into operations at handover. Distinguishing the two matters for funding, resourcing, and governance, since they are managed and measured differently. A frequent transition risk is operations being unready to absorb a project output.
Contrast: Project work — project work is temporary and unique to produce change, whereas business-as-usual is continuous operational activity.
Example: Once the new billing system goes live, its day-to-day running becomes business-as-usual for the operations team.
Source: Operations management practice

Core Meaning: An earned value convention that credits zero percent of an activity until it is finished, then 100 percent on completion.
Expansion: The 0/100 rule is a conservative earned value technique for assigning credit to work in progress, recognizing value only when an activity is fully complete. It avoids subjective in-progress estimates and is best suited to short activities that start and finish within a single reporting period. Applying it to long activities understates progress, so it is matched to small work packages. It is one of several fixed-formula rules used to measure earned value.
Contrast: 50/50 rule — the 50/50 rule credits half the value at start, whereas the 0/100 rule credits nothing until completion.
Example: A two-day inspection task earns no value until it is signed off, then books its full budgeted value.
Source: Practice Standard for Earned Value Management (PMI)

Core Meaning: An earned value convention that credits 50 percent of an activity when it starts and the remaining 50 percent when it completes.
Expansion: The 50/50 rule is a fixed-formula earned value technique that recognizes partial progress without requiring subjective percent-complete estimates. Booking half the value at start gives early credit for work under way, while withholding the rest until completion guards against overstating progress. It suits activities spanning about two reporting periods. Other split rules such as 25/75 follow the same logic with different weightings.
Contrast: 0/100 rule — the 0/100 rule gives no credit until completion, whereas the 50/50 rule recognizes half the value as soon as work begins.
Example: On starting the data-migration task, the team books 50 percent of its budget and the rest only when migration is verified.
Source: Practice Standard for Earned Value Management (PMI)

Core Meaning: The methods used to quantify how much of a planned scope of work has been accomplished at a point in time.
Expansion: Progress measurement converts physical accomplishment into objective figures that feed earned value, forecasting, and reporting. Methods include fixed-formula rules, weighted milestones, units completed, and physical percent complete, chosen to fit the nature of each work package. Sound progress measurement minimizes subjective judgment so that schedule and cost performance reflect reality. Weak measurement, such as guessing percent complete, distorts forecasts and hides slippage.
Contrast: Effort expended — recording hours spent measures input consumed, whereas progress measurement quantifies output actually accomplished.
Example: For piping, the team measures progress by linear meters installed against the total planned, rather than by hours worked.
Source: Practice Standard for Earned Value Management (PMI)

Core Meaning: The activity of defining how project and product scope will be defined, validated, and controlled throughout the project.
Expansion: Scope planning establishes the approach and procedures for elaborating requirements, building the scope statement and work breakdown structure, and managing changes. Its output, a scope management plan, guides how scope will be verified with stakeholders and protected against uncontrolled growth. Decisions here depend on the chosen life cycle, since predictive and adaptive approaches treat scope differently. It precedes and frames the detailed work of defining the scope.
Contrast: Scope definition — scope planning decides how scope will be handled, whereas scope definition produces the actual detailed scope statement.
Example: Early on, the team agrees in a scope management plan that requirements will be elaborated progressively in a rolling backlog.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition (Plan Scope Management)

Core Meaning: The PMI foundational standard that defines the principles guiding effective project management, published alongside the PMBOK Guide.
Expansion: The Standard for Project Management presents a principles-based view of the discipline, articulating twelve guiding principles and a system for value delivery rather than a prescriptive set of processes. It is an American National Standard and forms the normative companion to the PMBOK Guide, which provides supporting practices. The shift to principles reflects recognition that approaches must adapt to context. It anchors how value, stewardship, and outcomes are framed across predictive and adaptive work.
Contrast: PMBOK Guide — the PMBOK Guide offers detailed practices and methods, whereas the Standard for Project Management states the higher-level principles they serve.
Example: A team cites the principle of focusing on value from the Standard for Project Management to justify reprioritizing the backlog.
Source: The Standard for Project Management (PMI, 2021)

Core Meaning: The exposure of a project to the chance that actual costs will differ unfavorably from the planned budget.
Expansion: Cost risk arises from uncertain quantities, rates, productivity, scope change, and market conditions that can push spending above the baseline. It is assessed during estimating and risk analysis and is buffered with contingency reserves sized to the uncertainty.
Contrast: Schedule risk — schedule risk concerns uncertainty in timing and duration, whereas cost risk concerns uncertainty in the money required.
Example: Volatile steel prices create cost risk, so the team adds a contingency reserve sized by quantitative analysis.
Source: Project cost and risk management practice

Core Meaning: The ordering of requirements or backlog items by value, urgency, and dependency to decide what is delivered first.
Expansion: Scope prioritization makes explicit trade-offs about which features deliver the most value given limited time and resources. Techniques such as MoSCoW categorization, value-versus-effort ranking, and weighted scoring help stakeholders agree an order. In adaptive delivery it is continuous, keeping the most valuable work at the top of the backlog as understanding evolves. Good prioritization protects outcomes when the full scope cannot be completed within constraints.
Contrast: Scope definition — scope definition establishes what is in and out of scope, whereas scope prioritization sequences the in-scope items by importance.
Example: Facing a fixed date, the product owner uses MoSCoW to mark login as a must and social sharing as a could.
Source: Agile backlog management practice

Core Meaning: The structured growth of an individual's skills, experience, and responsibilities over the course of their working path.
Expansion: On projects, career development connects assignments and stretch opportunities to people's longer-term professional goals, supporting motivation and retention. It includes setting development objectives, providing feedback, and arranging training, coaching, or new responsibilities. Project managers contribute by giving team members meaningful work and recognizing growth even within temporary engagements. It complements organizational talent processes and links to team development.
Contrast: Team building — team building strengthens how a group works together now, whereas career development advances an individual's longer-term professional path.
Example: The project manager gives a junior analyst lead responsibility for reporting as a planned step in their career development.
Source: Resource and team development practice

Core Meaning: The ongoing pursuit of knowledge and skill improvement by individuals and teams as a regular part of work.
Expansion: Continuous learning embeds reflection and adjustment into delivery so that experience translates into better performance over time. At team level it shows up in retrospectives, knowledge sharing, and acting on lessons learned; at individual level in deliberate skill building. It is a foundation of agility and of a learning organization that adapts to change. The practice depends on psychological safety that makes it safe to surface mistakes and experiment.
Contrast: One-time training — a single course delivers a fixed skill at a point in time, whereas continuous learning is a sustained, iterative habit.
Example: Each retrospective ends with one improvement action, making continuous learning a built-in part of every iteration.
Source: Continuous improvement and learning organization practice

Core Meaning: The framework of authority, decision rights, and oversight that guides and controls a program toward its intended benefits.
Expansion: Program governance defines the bodies, roles, and processes through which a program is sponsored, funded, monitored, and held accountable. It sets decision gates, escalation paths, and the criteria for continuing, changing, or stopping component projects in service of strategic benefits. A governance board or steering committee typically provides direction and resolves cross-project issues. Effective governance aligns the program with organizational strategy and manages its risk and value over time.
Contrast: Project governance — project governance oversees a single project's delivery, whereas program governance coordinates multiple components toward shared benefits.
Example: The program board meets at each stage gate to confirm benefits remain achievable before releasing the next tranche of funding.
Source: The Standard for Program Management (PMI)

Core Meaning: The set of knowledge and skills concerned with the methods, tools, and techniques used to deliver projects effectively.
Expansion: Technical project management covers competencies such as scheduling, estimating, scope and risk management, and earned value, alongside the domain-specific knowledge needed to direct the work. In PMI's earlier Talent Triangle it formed one leg beside leadership and strategic and business management, emphasizing that delivery rigor complements people and business skills. The current framing broadens this leg toward ways of working that span predictive and adaptive approaches. It is the disciplined craft of running the project itself.
Contrast: Power skills — power skills are interpersonal and leadership capabilities, whereas technical project management is the hard methods and tools of delivery.
Example: A manager applies technical project management by building a resource-loaded schedule and tracking it with earned value.
Source: PMI Talent Triangle

Core Meaning: The 2021 edition of PMI's foundational reference, which shifted from a process-based model to twelve principles and eight performance domains.
Expansion: The seventh edition marked a major reorientation, replacing the knowledge-area and process structure of earlier editions with a principles-based, outcome-focused model that spans predictive, hybrid, and adaptive approaches. It is bound with the Standard for Project Management and centers on a system for value delivery and tailoring. Performance domains describe areas of activity such as stakeholders, team, and delivery, rather than prescribed processes. It reflects the profession's move toward flexibility over prescription.
Contrast: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — the sixth edition organized practice by knowledge areas and processes, whereas the seventh edition organizes it by principles and performance domains.
Example: Studying for certification, a candidate maps each principle in the PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition to scenarios on the exam.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition (PMI, 2021)

Core Meaning: A specific, measurable result the project is undertaken to achieve, against which success is assessed.
Expansion: Project objectives translate broad business goals into concrete targets for scope, schedule, cost, quality, and outcomes that the team commits to deliver. Well-formed objectives are clear and measurable so that completion and success can be judged unambiguously. They guide planning, prioritization, and trade-off decisions and connect deliverables to the benefits the organization seeks. Objectives are typically baselined and used to evaluate performance at closure.
Contrast: Deliverable — a deliverable is a tangible product or result produced, whereas a project objective is the measurable end state that deliverables are meant to achieve.
Example: A project objective to cut order-processing time from five days to one defines the target the new system must meet.
Source: PMBOK Guide and The Standard for Project Management

Core Meaning: A chronological record that traces the history of changes, decisions, or transactions so they can be reconstructed and verified.
Expansion: An audit trail captures who did what and when, providing the evidence needed to demonstrate that processes were followed and to investigate problems. In configuration and change management it links each baseline change to its request, approval, and implementation. It supports accountability, compliance, and quality audits, and helps resolve disputes by showing how a state was reached. Maintaining a complete, tamper-evident trail is essential in regulated environments.
Contrast: Change log — a change log summarizes the changes themselves, whereas an audit trail preserves the full traceable record of how each change was raised, approved, and applied.
Example: During a quality audit, the team uses the audit trail to show every approval behind the released configuration.
Source: Configuration and quality management practice

Core Meaning: The interpersonal ability to shape the views, decisions, or actions of others without relying on formal authority.
Expansion: Influencing is a core power skill for project managers, who often must align stakeholders and mobilize resources they do not directly control. It draws on credibility, relationships, reciprocity, and persuasive framing rather than positional power. Effective influencing depends on understanding others' interests and communicating in terms of shared value. It is central to negotiation, conflict resolution, and securing commitment across a matrix organization.
Contrast: Directing — directing relies on positional authority to assign work, whereas influencing gains commitment through persuasion and relationships.
Example: Lacking authority over a shared team, the manager influences its lead by linking the request to that team's own goals.
Source: PMI Talent Triangle (power skills)

Core Meaning: Repetitive operational activity that sustains an organization's existing functions, as opposed to temporary project work.
Expansion: Ongoing work has no defined end and continues to deliver routine value, such as production, support, and administration. It is distinguished from projects, which are temporary endeavors that produce unique outputs and then close, often handing their results into ongoing work.
Contrast: Project work — project work is temporary and unique, whereas ongoing work is continuous and repetitive operational activity.
Example: After the platform launches, monitoring and routine maintenance become ongoing work for the operations team.
Source: The Standard for Project Management (projects versus operations)

Core Meaning: The process of developing a detailed description of the project and product to be delivered.
Expansion: Scope definition elaborates requirements and constraints into a clear scope statement that states what is included and excluded. It establishes the shared understanding that the work breakdown structure, estimates, and acceptance criteria depend on. Boundaries set here are the reference against which change requests and scope creep are judged. Defining scope well early reduces ambiguity, rework, and disputes over deliverables.
Contrast: Scope planning — scope planning decides how scope will be handled, whereas scope definition produces the detailed scope statement itself.
Example: The team writes a scope statement listing the three modules in scope and explicitly excluding mobile, to prevent later creep.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition (Define Scope)

Core Meaning: The process of estimating the types and quantities of people, equipment, and materials needed to perform each scheduled activity.
Expansion: Activity resource estimating determines what resources, in what amounts, are required to complete the work, providing the basis for both schedule and cost estimates. It considers resource availability, skill levels, and the productivity assumptions behind each activity. Outputs feed the resource breakdown structure and resource calendars and inform leveling and capacity decisions. Inaccurate resource estimates ripple into unrealistic durations and budgets.
Contrast: Activity duration estimating — duration estimating predicts how long an activity takes, whereas resource estimating determines what and how much resource the activity needs.
Example: For excavation, the team estimates two operators and one excavator over three days, recorded against the activity.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition (Estimate Activity Resources)

Core Meaning: The process of determining whether available resource capacity matches the demand of planned work over time.
Expansion: Capacity planning compares the supply of people, skills, and equipment against the workload across projects and periods to spot shortfalls or excess. It informs decisions to hire, contract, resequence work, or adjust commitments before bottlenecks materialize. In multi-project settings it underpins portfolio resourcing and protects teams from chronic overload. It draws on resource estimates, calendars, and forecasts to keep demand within sustainable limits.
Contrast: Resource leveling — resource leveling reschedules activities to fit current limits, whereas capacity planning sizes overall supply against future demand.
Example: Reviewing the pipeline, the manager finds testing demand exceeds capacity next quarter and arranges contract testers in advance.
Source: Resource management practice

Core Meaning: The planned process of safely retiring an asset, system, or facility from active service at the end of its useful life.
Expansion: Decommissioning covers shutting down, dismantling, disposing of, or repurposing an asset while managing safety, environmental, data, and regulatory obligations. It is often itself a project, with its own scope, risks, and stakeholders, and may be planned far in advance as part of the asset life cycle. Activities include data migration or destruction, contract closeout, and site remediation. Overlooking decommissioning leaves residual liabilities and stranded costs.
Contrast: Closeout — project closeout formally ends a delivery effort, whereas decommissioning retires the operational asset that delivery produced.
Example: When the legacy data center is replaced, a decommissioning plan governs secure data wiping and safe equipment disposal.
Source: Project and asset closure practice

Core Meaning: A risk identification technique that examines the assumptions underlying a plan to expose those that are unsound or could fail.
Expansion: Assumptions analysis tests the validity, completeness, and stability of the assumptions a project depends on, since an invalid assumption is a latent source of risk. By questioning what has been taken for granted about scope, resources, environment, or stakeholders, the team surfaces threats and opportunities for the risk register. It also distinguishes assumptions from constraints and flags those needing confirmation. Recording assumptions in a log makes them visible for ongoing review.
Contrast: Constraint — a constraint is a known limitation imposed on the project, whereas an assumption is something taken to be true that assumptions analysis then tests for risk.
Example: Reviewing the assumption that a key vendor can deliver in four weeks, the team finds it unconfirmed and logs the risk.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition (risk identification technique)

Core Meaning: Uncertainty about whether and how a technical solution can be achieved, arising from novelty or unproven approaches.
Expansion: Technical uncertainty reflects gaps in knowledge about feasibility, design, or performance that cannot be resolved by planning alone and must be reduced through investigation. It is a key driver of project risk and a signal that adaptive, iterative approaches with experimentation may fit better than fixed plans. High technical uncertainty argues for prototypes, spikes, and incremental learning to retire the unknowns early. It is distinct from uncertainty about requirements or markets.
Contrast: Requirements uncertainty — requirements uncertainty concerns doubt about what to build, whereas technical uncertainty concerns doubt about how to build it.
Example: Unsure a new algorithm can meet latency targets, the team runs a time-boxed spike to reduce the technical uncertainty before committing.
Source: Risk management and adaptive delivery practice

Core Meaning: Evaluating a positive risk to determine how its likelihood or benefit can be increased.
Expansion: Opportunity analysis examines identified opportunities, which are positive risks, to assess their probability, potential benefit, and the response strategies that could capture them. It feeds decisions on whether to exploit, enhance, share, or accept an opportunity. Practitioners weigh the cost of pursuing an opportunity against the value it would add to project objectives.
Contrast: Unlike threat analysis, which seeks to reduce or avoid negative outcomes, opportunity analysis seeks to increase the likelihood or impact of beneficial events.
Example: A team finds a cheaper material that could cut costs, and opportunity analysis quantifies the savings and the steps needed to exploit it.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: Practices that protect the physical and psychological wellbeing of people involved in or affected by the project.
Expansion: Health and safety covers the policies, controls, and regulatory compliance that prevent harm to workers, stakeholders, and the public during project work. It is a core resource-management and ethical responsibility, especially in construction, manufacturing, and field operations.
Contrast: Distinct from quality management, which focuses on the product meeting requirements rather than on protecting people from harm.
Example: A construction project enforces hard-hat zones and logs near-miss incidents to meet occupational safety rules.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: Examining the schedule model to evaluate its logic, durations, and feasibility.
Expansion: Schedule model analysis investigates the network of activities, dependencies, durations, and constraints to confirm the schedule is realistic and to surface risks such as negative float or over-allocated resources. Techniques include critical path analysis, what-if scenario analysis, and resource optimization. The analysis supports forecasting completion dates and testing the impact of proposed changes.
Contrast: Unlike simple progress tracking, which records what has happened, schedule model analysis interrogates the underlying model to predict outcomes and test alternatives.
Example: A planner runs a what-if scenario in the schedule model to see whether fast-tracking two activities recovers a slipped milestone.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: An uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has a positive or negative effect on one or more project objectives.
Expansion: Project risk is uncertainty that matters to objectives, spanning both threats, which are negative, and opportunities, which are positive. PMI distinguishes individual project risks, which are discrete events, from overall project risk, which is the effect of uncertainty on the project as a whole. Managing project risk means identifying, analyzing, prioritizing, and responding to these uncertainties throughout the life cycle. Effective handling aligns responses with the risk appetite and thresholds of the organization.
Deep Dive: PMI separates project risk into two levels. Individual project risks are discrete uncertain events, each managed in the risk register with its own probability, impact, owner, and response. Overall project risk is the cumulative effect of all uncertainty plus interactions, and it is communicated to sponsors as a single exposure. Analysis ranges from qualitative prioritization using a probability and impact matrix to quantitative methods such as expected monetary value and Monte Carlo simulation. Responses are matched to the risk appetite and risk thresholds of the organization, with contingency reserves covering known risks and management reserves held for emergent ones. Treating both threats and opportunities keeps the process balanced rather than purely defensive.
Contrast: A risk differs from an issue, because a risk is a future uncertainty whereas an issue is a condition already affecting the project.
Example: A vendor might deliver late, a threat, or a new tool might speed delivery, an opportunity, and both are project risks tracked in the risk register.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: Determining the logical order and dependencies among activities to build the schedule.
Expansion: Sequencing identifies and documents the relationships between project activities so that work is performed in a feasible order. It uses dependency types such as finish-to-start, along with leads and lags, often visualized with a precedence diagram. Correct sequencing is the foundation for calculating the critical path and overall duration.
Contrast: Sequencing arranges the order of work, whereas estimating determines how long each activity takes, and both are needed before the schedule can be modeled.
Example: Foundation work must finish before framing begins, so the planner sets a finish-to-start dependency between them.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: In earlier Scrum, the cross-functional group responsible for delivering a usable increment each Sprint.
Expansion: The Development Team was the self-organizing, cross-functional set of professionals who did the work of building the increment in Scrum. It had no sub-titles or internal hierarchy and was accountable as a whole for the output of each Sprint. The 2020 Scrum Guide replaced the term with Developers, folding them into a single Scrum Team to remove the sense of a team within a team.
Contrast: Unlike a traditional project team led by a manager who assigns tasks, the Development Team self-organized to decide how to turn Backlog items into an increment.
Example: A Scrum Development Team of five engineers and a designer collectively committed to delivering the Sprint Backlog.
Source: Scrum Guide

Core Meaning: The Scrum Team members who commit to creating any aspect of a usable increment each Sprint.
Expansion: Developers are the people on the Scrum Team accountable for creating a plan for the Sprint, instilling quality, adapting their plan daily, and holding each other accountable as professionals. The term covers anyone doing the work of building the product, not only programmers. Introduced in the 2020 Scrum Guide, it replaced Development Team to emphasize one unified team.
Contrast: Developers differ from the Product Owner and Scrum Master, who hold distinct accountabilities for value and process within the same Scrum Team.
Example: On a marketing product, the Developers include a copywriter, a designer, and an analyst who together produce the Sprint increment.
Source: Scrum Guide

Core Meaning: A technique for choosing the method and criteria used to evaluate and select sellers.
Expansion: Source selection analysis reviews how proposals will be weighed before solicitation, balancing factors such as price, quality, technical capability, and risk. It produces a selection method such as least cost, qualifications only, quality-based, or fixed budget. The chosen approach shapes the evaluation criteria included in procurement documents.
Contrast: Unlike make-or-buy analysis, which decides whether to procure at all, source selection analysis decides how to pick among competing sellers once buying is chosen.
Example: For a complex IT build, the team uses a quality-based method so technical merit outweighs the lowest bid.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A concise description of the issue or need a project is intended to address.
Expansion: A problem statement articulates the gap between the current state and a desired state, framing why the project is warranted. It anchors the business case and keeps the team focused on solving the right problem rather than jumping to solutions.
Contrast: Distinct from a solution statement, which describes how the problem will be solved rather than the problem itself.
Example: A bank writes that customers abandon loan applications because approval takes ten days, defining the problem before designing a faster process.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: The collective acquisition of knowledge and skill that improves how a team works together.
Expansion: Team learning is the process by which a group reflects, shares insight, and adapts its practices to perform better over time. It underpins continuous improvement and is reinforced through retrospectives, knowledge sharing, and a psychologically safe environment.
Contrast: Unlike individual training, which builds one person's competence, team learning builds shared understanding and collective capability.
Example: After each iteration the team holds a retrospective, turning lessons into agreed changes for the next cycle.
Source: Senge The Fifth Discipline; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A decision checkpoint at the end of a phase where the project is assessed before continuing.
Expansion: A gate review, also called a phase gate, is a formal evaluation held at the boundary between life-cycle phases to decide whether to proceed, adjust, hold, or terminate the project. Decision-makers examine deliverables, business justification, risk, and readiness against defined criteria. Gates protect the organization from continuing to fund work that no longer makes sense.
Contrast: Unlike a routine status meeting, a gate review carries an authority decision that can stop the project, not merely report progress.
Example: At the design gate, sponsors confirm the prototype meets criteria before releasing funding for production.
