Figure.caption: Phases (boxes) are separated by gates (diamonds) where the project is continued or stopped.

Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: Conditions outside the organization that influence the project but lie beyond its direct control.
Expansion: The external environment includes market, regulatory, economic, social, and technological factors that shape project constraints and opportunities. It is a subset of enterprise environmental factors that the team must monitor and adapt to rather than command.
Contrast: Distinct from the internal environment of organizational culture and assets, which the organization can shape more directly.
Example: New data-privacy regulation in the external environment forces the team to redesign how the product stores customer records.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: The arithmetic average of a set of values, used in estimating and quality analysis.
Expansion: The mean is the sum of values divided by their count, giving a central measure of a data set. In project work it appears in PERT expected-duration estimates and as the centerline of a control chart.
Contrast: Unlike the median, which is the middle value, the mean is sensitive to extreme values that can skew it.
Example: Averaging optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic estimates with PERT weighting yields a mean activity duration.
Source: Statistical method applied in estimating and quality; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The point in time when project work is planned or authorized to begin.
Expansion: The project start date marks the beginning of the project timeline, against which durations and milestones are measured. It is set during schedule development and may be driven by an imposed constraint such as a contract or funding date.
Contrast: Distinct from the project finish date, which marks planned completion, and together they bound the overall duration.
Example: A contract requires work to commence on the first of March, fixing the project start date as a constraint.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: The complete set of items or events from which a sample may be drawn for inspection.
Expansion: In statistical quality control the population is the entire group of units under consideration, such as all parts in a production run. Because inspecting every item is often impractical, a representative sample is taken to infer the quality of the population.
Contrast: A population is the whole, whereas a sample is a subset selected to represent it.
Example: All ten thousand printed circuit boards in a batch form the population from which fifty are sampled for testing.
Source: Statistical method applied in quality control; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A subset of a population selected to draw conclusions about the whole.
Expansion: A sample is a representative portion of a population chosen for inspection or measurement so that quality can be assessed without examining every item. Sample size and selection method affect how confidently results can be generalized.
Contrast: A sample is a part, whereas the population is the entire set it is meant to represent.
Example: Pulling fifty boards from a run of ten thousand to test for defects creates the sample.
Source: Statistical method applied in quality control; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An interdisciplinary approach to designing, integrating, and managing complex systems over their life cycle.
Expansion: Systems engineering focuses on defining customer needs and required functionality early, then ensuring all components integrate into a coherent whole that meets requirements. It emphasizes requirements management, interface control, verification, and validation across the life cycle of the system. The discipline is common in engineering-heavy domains such as aerospace, defense, and large infrastructure.
Contrast: Unlike project management, which governs cost, schedule, and stakeholders, systems engineering governs the technical integrity and integration of the product itself.
Example: On a satellite program, systems engineers manage interfaces between the payload, power, and communications subsystems.
Source: INCOSE Systems Engineering Handbook

Core Meaning: A test that confirms a deliverable meets agreed criteria and is fit for acceptance by the customer.
Expansion: An acceptance test evaluates a completed deliverable against predefined acceptance criteria to verify it satisfies requirements and is ready for handover. Passing the test is often the trigger for formal acceptance, payment, or release. It is typically performed or witnessed by the customer or end users.
Contrast: Unlike unit or system testing, which verify internal correctness, an acceptance test validates that the deliverable meets the stated needs of the customer.
Example: Before going live, end users run an acceptance test confirming the payroll system processes a full cycle correctly.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A developmental relationship in which an experienced person guides the growth of a less experienced one.
Expansion: Mentoring is a longer-term, relationship-based form of support in which a mentor shares experience, perspective, and advice to help a mentee develop professionally. In projects it builds team capability, transfers tacit knowledge, and supports succession. It is a key interpersonal skill for leaders developing their teams.
Contrast: Mentoring differs from coaching, because mentoring is broader and experience-driven over time while coaching is more targeted at improving specific performance or skills.
Example: A senior project manager mentors a new team lead over a year, advising on stakeholder politics and career choices.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: Organized activities that build the knowledge, skills, and competencies of team members.
Expansion: Learning and development encompasses training, mentoring, and on-the-job growth that raise individual and team capability. In resource management it improves performance, engagement, and retention while closing skill gaps needed for the project.
Contrast: Distinct from team building, which strengthens relationships and cohesion rather than individual competence.
Example: The plan funds cloud-certification training so engineers gain the skills the new platform of the project requires.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A Lean practice where a cost target drives the design rather than the design driving the cost.
Expansion: Target value design sets an allowable cost, the target value, up front based on customer value and then steers design decisions to meet it. Cross-functional teams design to the target, treating cost as a constraint to design within rather than an outcome to be estimated afterward. It originated in Lean construction to reduce waste and deliver value within budget.
Contrast: Unlike traditional design-then-estimate, which prices a finished design and risks overruns, target value design fixes the budget first and shapes the design to fit.
Example: Given a budget for a hospital wing, the team designs each system to its allotted target cost rather than designing freely and pricing later.
Source: Lean Construction Institute; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The person responsible for guiding the technical direction and quality of a project's solution.
Expansion: A technical lead owns architectural and engineering decisions, mentors the team on technical matters, and ensures the solution meets technical requirements. The role bridges project management and hands-on delivery without necessarily holding formal people-management authority.
Contrast: Distinct from the project manager, who owns scope, schedule, and stakeholders rather than the technical design.
Example: The technical lead chooses the system architecture and reviews critical code while the project manager tracks the plan.
Source: Common project role; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Requirements specifying the technical attributes a solution must possess.
Expansion: Technical requirements define how a solution must be built or perform, covering attributes such as performance, security, interfaces, standards, and reliability. They translate stakeholder and functional needs into engineering specifications that designers and testers can verify. Clear technical requirements reduce ambiguity and rework during build and acceptance.
Contrast: Technical requirements differ from functional requirements, because functional requirements state what the solution must do while technical requirements state the technical conditions it must satisfy.
Example: A web platform must support 5,000 concurrent users and encrypt data at rest, both of which are technical requirements.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A contractual ceiling on the amount a buyer will pay regardless of actual costs incurred.
Expansion: A not-to-exceed limit caps the total a seller can bill, limiting the financial exposure of the buyer even on cost-reimbursable or time-and-materials work. Costs above the ceiling are typically borne by the seller unless the buyer authorizes a change.
Contrast: Unlike a firm fixed price, which sets a single agreed price, a not-to-exceed limit allows variable billing up to a maximum.
Example: A consulting contract bills hourly but stops at a not-to-exceed limit of 200k for the engagement.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: The effect a project has on people, communities, and society beyond its direct outputs.
Expansion: Social impact captures how a project changes the wellbeing, livelihoods, equity, or environment of affected communities and stakeholders. Considering it supports responsible delivery and aligns with sustainability and stakeholder expectations in the business environment.
Contrast: Distinct from financial benefit, which measures monetary return, social impact measures broader human and community outcomes.
Example: A rural road project assesses social impact such as improved school access alongside its cost and schedule.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: The quantity of work moved through a process or released together at one time.
Expansion: Batch size is the amount of work or number of items processed as a single unit before being passed downstream. Lean and agile thinking favor small batch sizes because they shorten feedback cycles, reduce work in progress, and surface problems sooner. Large batches delay feedback and amplify the cost of defects.
Contrast: Unlike a one-piece flow that moves single items continuously, large batch sizes accumulate work before handing it off, increasing cycle time.
Example: Releasing software in small weekly increments rather than a yearly mega-release reduces batch size and risk.
Source: Lean and agile practice; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The estimated time still needed to finish an in-progress activity.
Expansion: Remaining duration is the time required to complete the unfinished portion of an activity from the status date forward. It is used to update the schedule model and forecast completion as work progresses.
Contrast: Distinct from actual duration, which records time already spent, remaining duration looks forward to what is left.
Example: An activity planned for ten days is half done at day six, so its remaining duration is revised to four days.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A document recording the intentions and shared understanding of two or more parties, usually non-binding.
Expansion: A memorandum of understanding sets out the roles, expectations, and intended cooperation among parties before or instead of a binding contract. It signals commitment and aligns understanding but generally lacks the legal enforceability of a formal agreement. Such memoranda are common between partner organizations, agencies, or internal divisions.
Contrast: Unlike a contract, which creates legally enforceable obligations, a memorandum of understanding usually expresses intent without binding the parties.
Example: Two universities sign a memorandum of understanding to share research facilities before negotiating detailed contractual terms.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A risk that was unforeseeable at planning time and arises from changing conditions during the project.
Expansion: An emergent risk is one that could not be identified in advance because it stems from new, evolving, or previously unknown circumstances. Such risks are the unknown-unknowns that escape traditional identification and demand resilience and adaptive response. Management reserves and a responsive risk process help absorb their impact.
Contrast: Unlike a known risk that is identified and analyzed in advance, an emergent risk surfaces unexpectedly and cannot be planned for individually.
Example: A sudden global supply shortage that no one anticipated becomes an emergent risk midway through the project.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A mutually agreed resolution of outstanding claims and issues when closing a procurement.
Expansion: A negotiated settlement is the agreement that resolves disputes, claims, and open items so a contract can be closed fairly to both parties. It is the preferred way to settle differences without resorting to litigation or arbitration.
Contrast: Unlike alternative dispute resolution through arbitration, a negotiated settlement is reached directly by the parties rather than imposed by a third party.
Example: Buyer and seller negotiate a final payment that settles all change-order claims and closes the contract.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: Quality sampling that measures a characteristic on a continuous scale rather than as pass or fail.
Expansion: Variables sampling assesses quality by measuring a quantifiable attribute, such as length or weight, and recording the actual value on a continuous scale. It yields more information per sample than attribute sampling and supports analysis of how far results deviate from target. It is used when the degree of conformance, not just conformance itself, matters.
Contrast: Unlike attributes sampling, which records a simple conforms or does-not-conform result, variables sampling captures the measured degree of conformance.
Example: Measuring the exact diameter of sampled bolts in millimeters is variables sampling, versus merely judging each bolt good or bad.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: The negotiated reference price under an incentive contract, used to share cost variances.
Expansion: Target price is the agreed reference price in an incentive contract, derived from target cost plus target fee. The final price is adjusted from it using a sharing ratio that splits cost overruns or underruns between buyer and seller.
Contrast: Distinct from a firm fixed price, which never changes, target price moves within limits based on actual performance against the target cost.
Example: With a target cost of 100k and a fee of 10k, the target price is 110k before applying the sharing ratio.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A formally documented request to modify a project document, deliverable, or baseline.
Expansion: A requested change is a proposal to alter scope, schedule, cost, or other project elements, submitted for evaluation through integrated change control. It must be assessed and approved or rejected before the change is implemented.
Contrast: Distinct from an approved change, which has passed through change control, a requested change is still awaiting a decision.
Example: A stakeholder submits a requested change to add a reporting feature, which the change control board then reviews.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A dedicated shared space where a project team co-locates to collaborate intensively.
Expansion: A war room is a central physical space where team members work together, post information radiators, and make rapid decisions. Co-locating in this way improves communication, builds team identity, and speeds problem solving. The concept is also described as a tight matrix when used to gather a dispersed team.
Contrast: Unlike distributed or virtual teams that rely on digital tools, a war room concentrates people in one place to maximize face-to-face interaction.
Example: The launch team takes over a conference room as a war room for the final two weeks before go-live.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: The count of potential communication paths in a team, calculated as n times n minus 1 divided by 2.
Expansion: The number of communication channels measures how many distinct two-way links exist among stakeholders, using the formula n(n-1)/2 where n is the number of people. It illustrates how communication complexity grows rapidly as a team enlarges, informing the need for structured communication planning. Adding people increases channels far faster than it increases headcount.
Contrast: Unlike headcount, which grows linearly, the number of communication channels grows roughly with the square of the team size.
Example: A team of five has 10 channels, but adding one more member raises it to 15.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: Examining market conditions, suppliers, and trends to inform project and procurement decisions.
Expansion: Market analysis studies the availability, capabilities, and pricing of suppliers and the broader competitive landscape before make-or-buy and sourcing decisions. It helps the team understand options, risks, and opportunities in the external environment.
Contrast: Distinct from make-or-buy analysis, which decides whether to procure, market analysis informs that decision by surveying what the market offers.
Example: Before issuing a tender, the team performs market analysis to learn which vendors can supply the specialized component.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: Selecting and documenting the products and attributes that make up a configuration baseline.
Expansion: Configuration identification is the configuration-management activity of choosing which deliverables and documents are configuration items and labeling their characteristics and baselines. It establishes the items whose changes will be controlled and tracked throughout the project. Clear identification underpins the ability to manage versions and verify what has been delivered.
Contrast: Distinct from configuration status accounting, which records and reports the state of those items, identification first defines what they are.
Example: The team designates the requirements specification, design, and source code as configuration items with version labels.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: An event or circumstance that signals a risk is about to occur or has occurred.
Expansion: A trigger condition is an early-warning indicator that a particular risk is materializing, prompting the team to enact its planned response. Defining triggers during risk planning allows timely action rather than reaction after impact.
Contrast: Distinct from the risk itself, which is the uncertain event, the trigger condition is the observable sign that it is happening.
Example: A supplier missing two consecutive interim deadlines is the trigger condition to activate the backup-vendor response.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A decision-making style in which a single authority figure makes the choice alone without group input or consensus.
Expansion: Autocratic decision making concentrates authority in one decision maker, typically the project manager or sponsor, who selects a course of action without seeking team agreement. It is fast and decisive, which suits crises, low-stakes choices, or situations where one person holds the relevant expertise. The trade-off is reduced buy-in and the loss of the diverse perspectives that group methods provide.
Contrast: Consensus decision making seeks broad agreement from the whole group, whereas autocratic decision making bypasses group input for speed.
Example: A project manager unilaterally halts work after a safety incident rather than convening the team to debate the response.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: A defined boundary at which two system components, products, or organizations connect and exchange data, signals, or materials.
Expansion: A system interface specifies how separate parts of a larger system interact, including the inputs each side provides, the outputs it expects, and the protocols governing the exchange. Identifying and managing interfaces is central to systems integration, because many defects and risks in complex projects arise where components meet rather than within them. Interface management defines, documents, and controls these connections across teams, vendors, and subsystems.
Contrast: A component performs internal functions, while the interface governs only the connection between components.
Example: An integration team documents the interface contract between a payment service and the order-management system before either is built.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: The presence of varied backgrounds, perspectives, identities, and experiences within a project team or stakeholder group.
Expansion: Diversity refers to differences among team members across dimensions such as culture, gender, discipline, cognitive style, and experience. PMI positions diversity as a driver of innovation and better decisions, because varied viewpoints reduce blind spots and groupthink. Realizing its benefits requires an inclusive environment where differing perspectives are actively sought and respected.
Contrast: Diversity is the mix of differences present, while inclusion is the practice of making those differences feel valued and heard.
Example: A program deliberately staffs its design team with members from different regions and disciplines to surface a wider range of user needs.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: Any framework that explains what drives people to act, persist, and perform, used to inform how project leaders engage teams.
Expansion: Motivation theories provide models for understanding why individuals exert effort, such as Maslow's hierarchy of needs, Herzberg's two-factor theory, McGregor's Theory X and Theory Y, and McClelland's theory of needs. Project leaders draw on these models to design recognition, autonomy, and growth opportunities that sustain engagement. The theories distinguish factors that merely prevent dissatisfaction from those that actively energize performance.
Contrast: Intrinsic motivation arises from internal satisfaction, while many classical motivation theories also weigh extrinsic rewards such as pay.
Example: A manager applies Herzberg's model by improving recognition and challenge rather than only raising pay to lift a stalled team.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: A contractual promise by a seller that a deliverable will meet specified standards or perform as agreed for a defined period.
Expansion: A warranty is an assurance embedded in a contract that goods, services, or work will conform to stated requirements and remain fit for purpose over a warranty period. If the deliverable fails within that period, the warranty obligates the seller to repair, replace, or otherwise remedy the defect, often at no cost to the buyer. Warranties allocate post-delivery quality risk to the seller and are a key term negotiated in procurement.
Contrast: A warranty is a specific contractual condition about performance, while a guarantee is a broader assurance that may be informal or promotional.
Example: A construction contract includes a one-year warranty obliging the contractor to fix structural defects discovered after handover.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: The process of reaching agreement on the terms, price, and conditions of a contract between buyer and seller.
Expansion: Procurement negotiation clarifies and settles the structure, requirements, pricing, schedule, and responsibilities of a contract before it is signed. It aims for a fair agreement that protects both parties while securing value for the buyer and a viable arrangement for the seller. Skilled negotiators prepare objectives, understand each side's interests, and seek terms that reduce downstream disputes.
Contrast: Source selection chooses which seller to engage, while negotiation settles the terms with the chosen seller.
Example: A project team negotiates payment milestones and warranty length with a preferred vendor before awarding the contract.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: A visual representation of the sequence of steps, inputs, and outputs that make up a process.
Expansion: A process map depicts how work flows through a series of activities, showing the order of steps, decision points, handoffs, and the inputs and outputs at each stage. It is used to understand, communicate, and improve processes by exposing bottlenecks, redundancies, and sources of variation. Process mapping is a foundational technique in quality and continuous-improvement work.
Contrast: A SIPOC gives a high-level summary of suppliers and customers, while a process map details the internal steps between them.
Example: A team maps its change-request workflow and discovers two approval steps that add delay without adding value.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: A high-level process summary that captures Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, and Customers on a single diagram.
Expansion: SIPOC is a Lean Six Sigma tool that frames a process at a glance by naming its suppliers, the inputs they provide, the major process steps, the outputs produced, and the customers who receive them. It is typically created early in an improvement effort to establish scope and shared understanding before detailed analysis. By forcing teams to identify customers and suppliers explicitly, it clarifies boundaries and accountability.
Contrast: SIPOC stays at a summary altitude of five to seven steps, while a detailed process map decomposes each step.
Example: A Six Sigma team builds a SIPOC for the invoicing process to agree on its start and end points before measuring defects.
Source: Lean Six Sigma; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The principle that a sum of money is worth more now than the same sum in the future because of its earning potential.
Expansion: Time value of money holds that money available today can be invested to earn returns, so a future cash flow must be discounted to express its present worth. This principle underpins financial appraisal techniques used to select and justify projects, including net present value, discounted cash flow, and internal rate of return. It allows costs and benefits arriving at different times to be compared on a common basis.
Contrast: Simple payback ignores the timing of cash flows, while time value of money discounts each flow according to when it occurs.
Example: An analyst discounts a project's projected five-year benefits to present value before comparing it against alternatives.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: A cognitive bias in which one negative trait or impression unfairly colors the overall judgment of a person or thing.
Expansion: The horn effect occurs when a single unfavorable characteristic leads an observer to assume that other, unrelated qualities are also poor. In project settings it can distort performance appraisals, stakeholder assessments, and vendor evaluations, letting one weakness overshadow genuine strengths. Awareness and structured, criteria-based evaluation help counter it.
Contrast: The halo effect generalizes from a positive trait, while the horn effect generalizes from a negative one.
Example: A reviewer rates an engineer poorly across all skills because of one missed deadline, ignoring strong technical work.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: A formal evaluation of an individual's work performance against expectations over a defined period.
Expansion: A performance appraisal reviews how well a team member has met role expectations, objectives, and competencies, providing structured feedback and identifying development needs. On projects it supports staffing decisions, recognition, and growth planning, and may feed into functional managers' assessments in a matrix organization. Effective appraisals rely on observable evidence and clear criteria to reduce bias.
Contrast: An appraisal evaluates an individual, while a team performance assessment evaluates the group's effectiveness as a unit.
Example: A project manager provides input to an engineer's annual appraisal based on contributions during the project.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: The total calendar time between the start and finish of an activity, including non-working periods.
Expansion: Elapsed time measures duration on the calendar from start to end, counting weekends, holidays, and idle waiting alongside actual working time. It differs from effort, which counts only the labor expended, so a task needing two hours of work can have an elapsed time of several days if it waits in a queue.
Contrast: Effort is the work performed, while elapsed time is the wall-clock span over which it happens.
Example: A lab test requiring ten minutes of work has an elapsed time of three days because results take that long to return.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms

Core Meaning: The systematic elimination of activities and resources that consume effort without adding value for the customer.
Expansion: Waste reduction is the core aim of Lean thinking, targeting any activity that absorbs resources but creates no value, such as waiting, rework, overproduction, excess inventory, and unnecessary motion. By identifying and removing these wastes, teams shorten lead time, lower cost, and improve flow. The discipline relies on continuous observation of the value stream and incremental improvement.
Contrast: Cost cutting may remove value-adding capacity, while waste reduction removes only non-value-adding activity.
Example: A team eliminates a redundant approval queue, cutting cycle time without reducing quality.
Source: Lean; PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: A set of techniques used to plan, analyze, and improve quality and to display data about process performance and defects.
Expansion: This group of tools supports quality planning, assurance, and control by helping teams investigate causes, visualize relationships, and prioritize problems. It traditionally includes cause-and-effect diagrams, flowcharts, check sheets, Pareto charts, histograms, control charts, and scatter diagrams, alongside management and planning tools such as affinity diagrams and matrix diagrams. Selecting the right tool depends on whether the goal is to gather, organize, or analyze quality data.
Contrast: The tools analyze data and causes, while a quality audit independently checks compliance with processes.
Example: A team uses a Pareto chart and a cause-and-effect diagram together to find the dominant source of defects.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Sixth Edition

Core Meaning: A stakeholder who supports the project and stands to benefit from its success.
Expansion: A positive stakeholder views the project favorably and is likely to gain from its outcomes, so they tend to champion it, supply resources, and help remove obstacles. Engaging positive stakeholders well turns their support into active advocacy that can offset resistance elsewhere. Recognizing them helps the team build a coalition that sustains momentum.
Contrast: A positive stakeholder benefits from success and supports the project, while a negative stakeholder perceives harm and may resist it.
Example: A department head who will gain efficiency from a new system actively promotes its adoption to peers.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Sixth Edition

Core Meaning: The standards and measures used to judge whether a project, deliverable, or objective has been achieved successfully.
Expansion: Success criteria define in advance what a successful outcome looks like, translating objectives into measurable conditions against which results can be assessed. They may cover scope, quality, schedule, cost, benefits, and stakeholder satisfaction, and they guide decisions throughout delivery. Agreeing them early with sponsors and key stakeholders prevents later disputes about whether the project delivered.
Contrast: Acceptance criteria govern whether a specific deliverable is accepted, while success criteria judge the project or objective as a whole.
Example: A team agrees that success means launching within budget and achieving a defined adoption rate within three months.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: A contract under which a primary contractor delegates part of its contracted work to another party.
Expansion: A subcontract is an agreement in which a prime contractor engages a third party, the subcontractor, to perform a defined portion of the work the prime owes to the buyer. It lets the prime contractor obtain specialized capability or capacity while remaining accountable to the buyer for the whole scope. Managing subcontracts adds a layer of coordination, flow-down requirements, and risk that the prime must control.
Contrast: The prime contract binds the buyer and main contractor, while a subcontract binds the main contractor and a lower-tier supplier.
Example: A general contractor subcontracts the electrical scope to a specialist firm while remaining responsible to the client.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: The three-part structure used in PMI process descriptions to show what a process consumes, how it transforms it, and what it produces.
Expansion: Often abbreviated ITTO, this structure describes each project management process in terms of its inputs, the tools and techniques applied to them, and the resulting outputs. It was the organizing framework of the process-based PMBOK Guide editions through the Sixth Edition, showing how the outputs of one process become inputs to others. The Seventh Edition shifted toward principles and performance domains, reducing emphasis on the formal ITTO model.
Contrast: ITTO prescribes process mechanics, while the principles-based approach in PMBOK 7 emphasizes outcomes and tailoring.
Example: In the Develop Schedule process, the schedule network and estimates are inputs, the critical path method is a technique, and the schedule baseline is an output.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Sixth Edition

Core Meaning: A method of assigning relative importance to evaluation criteria so options can be scored and compared objectively.
Expansion: A weighting system applies numeric weights to each selection or evaluation criterion according to its importance, scores each option against the criteria, and combines the results into a weighted total. It is widely used in project selection and source selection to make multi-criteria decisions consistent and defensible. By making priorities explicit, it reduces the influence of bias and of any single dominant factor.
Contrast: A screening system applies pass-or-fail thresholds, while a weighting system produces comparative scores.
Example: A committee scores three vendors on cost, quality, and delivery using assigned weights to choose the best overall fit.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Sixth Edition

Core Meaning: A pure risk of loss that can be transferred to an insurer because it meets the conditions for coverage.
Expansion: An insurable risk is a threat of loss, with no possibility of gain, that an insurer is willing to cover because the loss is measurable, accidental, and spread across many similar exposures. Projects transfer such risks through insurance, paying a premium so the insurer bears the financial impact if the event occurs. Not all project risks are insurable, since speculative business risks generally fall outside coverage.
Contrast: Insurable risk involves only loss and can be transferred, while business risk carries the chance of gain or loss and is usually retained.
Example: A contractor buys insurance covering fire and equipment damage to transfer those insurable risks.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: A family of international standards defining requirements and principles for quality management systems.
Expansion: ISO 9000 is a set of standards published by the International Organization for Standardization that establishes the vocabulary and principles of quality management, with ISO 9001 specifying certifiable requirements for a quality management system. Organizations adopt it to demonstrate consistent processes, customer focus, and continual improvement. Projects operating under such a system align their quality processes with its requirements.
Contrast: ISO 9000 sets organization-wide quality-system standards, while the quality management plan applies quality to a specific project.
Example: A manufacturer pursues ISO 9001 certification to assure clients that its production processes meet a recognized standard.
Source: International Organization for Standardization (ISO 9000 family)

Core Meaning: A specific, enforceable statement that defines or constrains some aspect of how an organization operates.
Expansion: A business rule prescribes a policy, condition, or constraint that governs business behavior, such as how a discount is calculated or who may approve an expense. In requirements and solution work, business rules are captured so that processes and systems consistently enforce the organization's policies. They are maintained separately from procedures because a single rule may apply across many processes.
Contrast: A requirement states what a solution must do, while a business rule states a policy constraint the solution must enforce.
Example: A rule stating that orders over a set value require manager approval is built into a procurement system.
Source: PMI Guide to Business Analysis

Core Meaning: The accounting allocation of an asset's cost over its useful life to reflect its declining value.
Expansion: Depreciation spreads the cost of a tangible asset across the periods in which it provides benefit, recognizing that the asset loses value through use and age. It affects how project capital expenditures appear in financial statements and influences cost and benefit analyses used to justify projects. Methods include straight-line depreciation and accelerated approaches that recognize more expense in early years.
Contrast: Straight-line depreciation spreads cost evenly, while accelerated methods recognize larger amounts in the asset's early life.
Example: A company depreciates a new machine over ten years, expensing a portion of its cost each year.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: A project risk arising from uncertainty other than a discrete future event, namely variability or ambiguity.
Expansion: Non-event risk covers uncertainty that is not tied to a single identifiable event, comprising variability risk, where a known parameter could range across values, and ambiguity risk, arising from imperfect knowledge or understanding. It complements event risk, which concerns specific occurrences that may or may not happen. Recognizing non-event risk widens risk management beyond a list of possible events to include emergent and systemic uncertainty.
Contrast: Event risk is a discrete possible occurrence, while non-event risk stems from variability or ambiguity that has no single trigger.
Example: Uncertainty about how a never-used technology will behave is an ambiguity risk handled as non-event risk.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Sixth Edition

Core Meaning: An agile software development method emphasizing technical excellence, frequent feedback, and close customer collaboration.
Expansion: Extreme Programming, or XP, is an agile approach built on short development cycles and disciplined engineering practices that keep software flexible and high in quality. Its practices include pair programming, test-driven development, continuous integration, simple design, refactoring, and an on-site customer. XP aims to lower the cost of change so that requirements can evolve throughout delivery without degrading the codebase.
Contrast: Scrum focuses on team workflow and roles, while Extreme Programming prescribes specific engineering practices for how the code is built.
Example: A development team adopts XP, pairing on code and writing automated tests before each feature to sustain quality.
Source: Agile Practice Guide

Core Meaning: A shared, rigorously consistent vocabulary used by developers and domain experts to describe a system.
Expansion: Ubiquitous language is a practice from domain-driven design in which the whole team adopts one common set of terms, grounded in the business domain, for conversations, models, and code. By eliminating translation between business and technical wording, it reduces misunderstanding and keeps the software model aligned with real domain concepts. The language is refined continuously as understanding deepens.
Contrast: Technical jargon separates specialists from the business, while ubiquitous language unifies them around shared meaning.
Example: A team agrees that the word policy means exactly one concept across discussions, documentation, and source code.
Source: Domain-Driven Design; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An agile engineering principle of building the simplest solution that meets current requirements without speculative complexity.
Expansion: Simple design holds that a system should do everything required today and nothing more, avoiding features and abstractions added in anticipation of needs that may never arise. It is an Extreme Programming practice that keeps code easy to understand and cheap to change, relying on refactoring to evolve the design as real requirements emerge. The aim is to defer complexity until it is genuinely justified.
Contrast: Big design up front specifies the full architecture early, while simple design lets it emerge incrementally.
Example: A developer implements only the rules the current story requires, refactoring later when a new requirement actually appears.
Source: Extreme Programming; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The drive to perform an activity for the inherent satisfaction it provides rather than for external reward.
Expansion: Intrinsic motivation arises from internal factors such as interest, mastery, purpose, and autonomy, leading people to engage in work because they find it meaningful or enjoyable. In knowledge work and agile teams it is associated with higher creativity, persistence, and quality than reliance on external incentives alone. Leaders foster it by providing autonomy, opportunities to grow, and a clear sense of purpose.
Contrast: Intrinsic motivation comes from within the work itself, while extrinsic motivation comes from outside rewards such as pay or recognition.
Example: An engineer spends extra effort perfecting a tool because the problem fascinates her, not for a bonus.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: A deliberate arrangement of team types and their interaction patterns to optimize fast, sustainable software delivery.
Expansion: Team topology, from the Team Topologies model, organizes work around a small number of team types, such as stream-aligned, enabling, complicated-subsystem, and platform teams, and defines clear modes of interaction between them. The approach treats team structure and communication paths as a primary design concern, reflecting that system architecture tends to mirror organizational structure. The goal is to reduce cognitive load and dependencies so teams can deliver value independently.
Contrast: An org chart shows reporting lines, while a team topology defines value-flow and interaction patterns between teams.
Example: An organization sets up a platform team to provide self-service infrastructure, lowering the cognitive load on stream-aligned teams.
Source: Team Topologies; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The economic impact of postponing the delivery of value, expressed as value lost per unit of time.
Expansion: Cost of delay quantifies how much value is forgone for each period a feature or project is late, combining the value at stake with its urgency over time. It makes the economics of timing explicit so teams can prioritize work that loses the most value if delayed. Frameworks such as weighted shortest job first use cost of delay divided by job size to sequence a backlog.
Contrast: A cost overrun is spending above budget, while cost of delay is value lost from late delivery regardless of spend.
Example: A team prioritizes a feature whose market window closes soon because its cost of delay is highest.
Source: Scaled Agile Framework

Core Meaning: The ongoing ordering of backlog items so the most valuable work is done first.
Expansion: Backlog prioritisation arranges the product backlog so that items delivering the greatest value, or reducing the most risk, sit at the top ready to be pulled into the next iteration. The product owner continually re-ranks items as value, cost, dependencies, and new information change. Techniques such as MoSCoW, weighted shortest job first, and cost of delay inform the ordering.
Contrast: Backlog refinement clarifies and sizes items, while prioritisation decides their relative order.
Example: A product owner moves a high-value compliance feature to the top of the backlog ahead of cosmetic improvements.
Source: Agile Practice Guide

Core Meaning: A framework for applying agile and lean practices across many teams in a large enterprise.
Expansion: The Scaled Agile Framework, or SAFe, provides structured roles, events, and artifacts to coordinate multiple agile teams working toward shared business objectives. It organizes teams into an agile release train that plans and delivers together on a common cadence, and layers portfolio, program, and team concerns to align strategy with execution. SAFe is one of several approaches to scaling agile beyond a single team.
Contrast: Scrum guides one team, while the Scaled Agile Framework coordinates many teams and aligns them to enterprise strategy.
Example: An enterprise launches an agile release train of eight teams that plan together in quarterly increment planning.
Source: Scaled Agile Framework

Core Meaning: A development approach that helps individuals or teams improve performance by drawing out their own insight and capability.
Expansion: Coaching supports people in solving problems and building skills through questioning, feedback, and encouragement rather than by giving direct instructions. In project and agile environments it is a key behavior of servant leaders and agile coaches, who help teams become self-organizing and continuously improve. Effective coaching focuses on growth and ownership, adapting its intensity to the person's experience and confidence.
Contrast: Mentoring shares the mentor's own experience and advice, while coaching draws solutions out of the person being coached.
Example: An agile coach asks a team probing questions in a retrospective so they identify their own improvement actions.
Source: Agile Practice Guide

Core Meaning: A practice in which developers examine each other's source code to find defects and improve quality.
Expansion: Code review is the systematic inspection of code changes by one or more peers before they are merged, aimed at catching defects, improving design, and sharing knowledge across the team. It can be synchronous, as in pair programming, or asynchronous through pull-request comments, and it reinforces shared standards and collective ownership of the codebase. Beyond finding bugs, it spreads understanding and reduces single points of knowledge.
Contrast: Testing executes the code to detect failures, while code review reads the code to assess quality and correctness.
Example: A developer opens a pull request and a teammate comments on edge cases before approving the merge.
Source: Software engineering practice; not a formal PMI glossary entry
